title: From Language to Logic: A Theoretical Architecture for VLM-Grounded Safe Navigation
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, to be published in ICUAS 2026 conference proceedings
summary: We propose an architecture for integrating high-level, human-provided safety rules and operator-aligned semantic preferences into autonomous robot navigation in unstructured outdoor environments. In our approach, natural-language rules are translated into Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications that guide planning and navigation during runtime. Persistent, environment-centric rules and terrain preferences are grounded into a 2D cost map, while temporally dynamic requirements are expressed as STL specifications to be monitored during runtime. We hypothesize the use of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for zero-shot scene understanding, enabling mapping between human instructions, semantic features, and environmental constraints. Within this framework, we construct an illustrative navigation model that is designed to satisfy a set of STL-encoded specifications and soft operator preferences through formal satisfaction metrics embedded into environmental properties and runtime monitoring.

AI.tldr: An architecture that translates natural-language safety rules into STL constraints to guide autonomous outdoor navigation, grounding persistent rules in a 2D cost map, expressing dynamic requirements as STL for runtime monitoring, and using Vision-Language Models for zero-shot scene understanding, with an illustrative navigation model designed to satisfy STL specs and operator preferences.
AI.motivation: To ensure safety and operator-aligned semantics in unstructured outdoor robot navigation by combining high-level human instructions with formal runtime guarantees, leveraging environment-grounded costs and dynamic STL specifications for continuous verification.
AI.method: Translate natural-language rules into Signal Temporal Logic (STL) constraints; ground persistent, environment-centric rules into a 2D cost map; express temporally dynamic requirements as STL specifications to monitor during runtime; employ Vision-Language Models for zero-shot scene understanding to map human instructions, semantic features, and environmental constraints; construct an illustrative navigation model that satisfies STL-encoded specifications and soft operator preferences via formal satisfaction metrics embedded in environment properties and runtime monitoring.
AI.result: The work presents a framework and an illustrative model rather than empirical results. It demonstrates feasibility of encoding safety rules and operator preferences into STL-based runtime constraints and grounding, but lacks validated experiments or performance benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled way to integrate human safety guidelines and semantic preferences into autonomous navigation using STL and runtime monitoring, with grounding in a 2D cost map and semantic mapping via VLMs. Realization hinges on reliable NL-to-STL translation, robust perception, and scalable runtime verification; future work should include empirical evaluation, handling rule conflicts, and exploring perception/planning trade-offs.

title: Autonomous Laparoscope Control through Unified Mechanics-Based Representation of Multimodal Intraoperative Information
summary: Laparoscope-holding robots can provide surgeons with a stable laparoscopic field of view (FOV) and reduce the burden on human assistants. To maintain an ideal intraoperative FOV, the robot must continuously adjust the laparoscope pose according to intraoperative information. However, intraoperative multimodal signals, such as position, force/torque, and images, differ markedly in physical meaning and units, making it difficult to build a unified representation and to generate control commands that can be used directly for laparoscope control. To address this issue, we propose a laparoscope-holding robot control method based on unified mechanics modeling of multimodal information. First, we design mapping strategies for multiple intraoperative sources, including position, force/torque, and images, and unify them into an equivalent-wrench representation in the operational space. Then, using a task-priority scheme, we inject the wrenches into the task space and the null space, respectively, and synthesize laparoscope control commands via task-priority projection, thereby achieving consistent representation and coordinated fusion of multimodal information within a single framework. Finally, taking the intraoperative remote center of motion (RCM) position, force/torque sensor readings, and laparoscopic images as examples, we construct an RCM-constraint wrench to enforce the RCM geometric constraint and reduce the contact force at the trocar site, a laparoscope-manipulation wrench to enable compliant dragging, and an instrument-tracking wrench to achieve autonomous visual tracking of the instruments. Experiments on a surgical phantom and in vivo porcine trials demonstrate that the proposed method supports multi-task operation, including compliant laparoscope manipulation and autonomous instrument tracking, while maintaining the RCM constraint and reducing sustained trocar-site loading.

AI.tldr: Unified mechanics modeling of multimodal intraoperative signals to control a laparoscope-holding robot via equivalent-wrench representation and task-priority projection, enabling RCM constraints, compliant dragging, and autonomous instrument tracking.
AI.motivation: Intraoperative signals (position, force/torque, images) have different physical meanings and units, making unified control difficult. A single framework is needed to fuse multimodal data, maintain stable FOV, respect the remote center of motion (RCM), and reduce trocar loading.
AI.method: Map multiple intraoperative sources (position, force/torque, images) into an equivalent-wrench representation in the operational space. Apply a task-priority scheme to inject wrenches into the task space and null space, synthesizing control commands via task-priority projection. Construct and use specific wrenches: an RCM-constraint wrench to enforce RCM and reduce trocar contact, a laparoscope-manipulation wrench for compliant dragging, and an instrument-tracking wrench for autonomous visual tracking of instruments.
AI.result: Experimental validation on a surgical phantom and in vivo porcine trials demonstrates multi-task operation, maintenance of RCM constraint, reduced sustained trocar-site loading, and capability for compliant laparoscope manipulation and autonomous instrument tracking.
AI.conclusion: A unified multimodal wrench representation combined with task-priority projection provides consistent fusion of multimodal data and effective coordination for laparoscope control, improving FOV stability and enabling autonomous instrument tracking while respecting RCM and minimizing trocar loading.

title: Tightly-Coupled Estimation and Guidance for Robust Low-Thrust Rendezvous via Adaptive Homotopy
summary: Minimum-fuel low-thrust rendezvous guidance yields bang-bang control structures highly sensitive to estimation errors, sensor anomalies, and solver regularization, making aggressive closed-loop execution brittle for uncooperative proximity operations. This paper proposes a tightly-coupled estimation and guidance architecture where navigation confidence directly modulates the homotopy parameter of a receding-horizon indirect optimal control solver. Relative motion is modeled in the Clohessy-Wiltshire frame. The translational state is estimated via a linear Kalman filter augmented by a Multiple Tuning Factors (MTF) covariance inflation mechanism that suppresses suspicious innovation directions. A composite score from the normalized innovation and MTF activity is mapped online to the homotopy parameter, allowing the controller to relax toward a smoother, conservative regime when confidence degrades, and recover fuel-efficient bang-bang control as sensing improves. Numerical results under severe measurement degradation show fixed bang-bang guidance remains brittle; both plain-KF and MTF-KF fixed-epsilon controllers yield large terminal miss distances. Conversely, the proposed MTF-adaptive homotopy controller reduces terminal miss by roughly two orders of magnitude, from hundreds of meters to sub-meter levels, requiring only a moderate increase in control effort versus the open-loop fuel-optimal benchmark. A comparison indicates adaptive homotopy is the dominant robustness mechanism, while MTF provides additional accuracy and efficiency improvements. The receding-horizon implementation exhibits consistently fast and reliable solution times, supporting the practical online viability of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: MTF-adaptive homotopy in receding-horizon indirect optimal control for minimum-fuel low-thrust rendezvous; navigation-confidence controls homotopy; CW-frame dynamics; KF with MTF inflation; substantial robustness gains under degraded sensing; fast online solution; adaptive homotopy dominates robustness; MTF adds accuracy/efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness of bang-bang, fuel-optimal low-thrust rendezvous under estimation errors, sensor anomalies, and solver regularization by making guidance adapt to navigation confidence.
AI.method: Model relative motion in the Clohessy-Wiltshire frame. Estimate translational state with a linear Kalman filter augmented by a Multiple Tuning Factors (MTF) covariance inflation mechanism to suppress suspicious innovation directions. Compute a composite score from normalized innovation and MTF activity. Map this online to the homotopy parameter of a receding-horizon indirect optimal control solver, relaxing to smoother control when confidence degrades and recovering bang-bang as sensing improves.
AI.result: Under severe measurement degradation, fixed bang-bang guidance remains brittle; plain-KF and MTF-KF fixed-epsilon controllers yield large terminal miss distances. The proposed MTF-adaptive homotopy controller reduces terminal miss from hundreds of meters to sub-meter levels, with only a moderate increase in control effort compared to the open-loop fuel-optimal benchmark. Adaptive homotopy is the dominant robustness mechanism; MTF offers additional accuracy and efficiency. Recurring-horizon solver demonstrates consistently fast, reliable solution times, enabling online viability.
AI.conclusion: Tightly-coupled estimation–guidance with navigation-confidence-driven homotopy adaptation yields substantial robustness and accuracy gains for low-thrust rendezvous, with practical online performance; adaptive homotopy dominates robustness, while MTF inflation provides supplementary benefits.

title: LCM: Lossless Context Management
summary:
We introduce Lossless Context Management (LCM), a deterministic architecture for LLM memory that outperforms Claude Code on long-context tasks. When benchmarked using Opus 4.6, our LCM-augmented coding agent, Volt, achieves higher scores than Claude Code on the OOLONG long-context eval, including at every context length between 32K and 1M tokens.
  LCM may be considered both a vindication and extension of the recursive paradigm pioneered by Recursive Language Models (RLMs). Our results demonstrate that recursive context manipulation can outperform not just conventional LLMs, but frontier coding agents with native file-system access.
  LCM departs from RLM by decomposing symbolic recursion into two deterministic, engine-managed mechanisms: recursive context compression, in which a hierarchical summary DAG automatically compacts older messages while retaining lossless pointers to every original; and recursive task partitioning, in which engine-managed parallel primitives like LLM-Map replace model-written loops. This trade-off, analogous to the move from GOTO to structured control flow in program-ming language design, sacrifices maximal flexibility for termination guarantees, zero-cost continuity on short tasks, and lossless retrievability of all prior state.

AI.tldr: LCM presents a deterministic, memory-efficient architecture for LLMs that uses recursive context management to maintain lossless access to all past data and deterministic control flow; it outperforms Claude Code on long-context tasks (OOLONG) across 32K–1M tokens in Opus 4.6 with the Volt agent.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of traditional LLM memory and frontier coding agents on long contexts by offering guaranteed retrievability and termination, while preserving long-context performance.
AI.method: Two deterministic mechanisms: recursive context compression (hierarchical summary DAG preserving lossless pointers to originals) and recursive task partitioning (engine-managed parallel primitives like LLM-Map replacing model-writt en loops). Treats context management as explicit, engine-controlled processes, analogous to structured control flow replacing GOTO.
AI.result: Benchmarks show Volt with LCM achieves higher OOLONG scores than Claude Code across context lengths from 32K to 1M tokens on Opus 4.6; claims of outperformance of frontier coding agents with file-system access.
AI.conclusion: LCM validates and extends recursive language model concepts by delivering lossless memory with termination guarantees and structured control flow, trading flexibility for determinism and retrievability; implications for long-context LLM design and memory management; future work may explore scalability and broader task coverage.

title: Are Multimodal LLMs Ready for Clinical Dermatology? A Real-World Evaluation in Dermatology
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated promise on publicly available dermatology benchmarks. However, benchmark performance may not generalize to real-world dermatologic decision-making. To quantify this benchmark-to-bedside gap, we evaluated four open-weight MLLMs (InternVL-Chat v1.5, LLaVA-Med v1.5, SkinGPT4 and MedGemma-4B-Instruct) and one commercial MLLM (GPT-4.1) across three publicly available dermatology datasets and a retrospective multi-site hospital-based dermatology consultation cohort comprising 5,811 cases and 46,405 clinical images. Models were evaluated on two clinically relevant tasks: differential diagnosis generation and severity-based triage. Diagnostic performance was modest on public datasets and declined substantially in the real-world cohort. On public benchmarks, top-3 diagnostic accuracy reached 26.55% for the best open-weight model and 42.25% for GPT-4.1. On real-world consultation cases using images alone, top-3 diagnostic accuracy fell to 1.50%-13.35% among open-weight models and 24.65% for GPT-4.1. Incorporating clinical context improved performance across all models, increasing top-3 diagnostic accuracy up to 28.75% among open-weight models and 38.93% for GPT-4.1. However, model outputs were highly sensitive to incomplete or erroneous consultation context. For severity-based triage, models achieved moderate sensitivity (above 60%), suggesting potential utility for screening but insufficient reliability for clinical deployment. These findings demonstrate that benchmark performance substantially overestimates the real-world clinical capability of current dermatology MLLMs.

AI.tldr: Benchmark performance overestimates real-world dermatology capability of MLLMs; real-world accuracy drops substantially, though clinical context helps; triage shows moderate sensitivity but not reliable for deployment.
AI.motivation: Quantify the gap between benchmark (public datasets) and real-world bedside utility of multimodal LLMs in dermatology, comparing open-weight and commercial models across public datasets and a large real-world cohort.
AI.method: Evaluate four open-weight MLLMs (InternVL-Chat v1.5, LLaVA-Med v1.5, SkinGPT4, MedGemma-4B-Instruct) and one commercial MLLM (GPT-4.1) on three public dermatology datasets and a retrospective multi-site hospital-based cohort with 5,811 cases and 46,405 images. Two tasks: differential diagnosis generation and severity-based triage. Metrics include top-3 diagnostic accuracy; performance with images alone vs with clinical context; and sensitivity of triage. Also assess impact of incomplete/erroneous consultation context.
AI.result: Public benchmarks: top-3 accuracy 26.55% (best open-weight) vs 42.25% (GPT-4.1). Real-world cohort (images alone): 1.50–13.35% (open-weight) and 24.65% (GPT-4.1). With clinical context: up to 28.75% (open-weight) and 38.93% (GPT-4.1). Triages show moderate sensitivity (>60%), suggesting screening potential but not deployment-ready. Outputs are highly sensitive to quality of consultation context. Overall, benchmark performance substantially overestimates real-world clinical capability of dermatology MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Current dermatology MLLMs exhibit limited reliability for real-world clinical decision-making; benchmark-to-bedside gap is large. Contextual information improves performance but remains insufficient for deployment. Benchmark-focused evaluation should be complemented by real-world-data studies; triage utility is potential but requires caution.

title: Endogenous Regime Switching Driven by Scalar-Irreducible Learning Dynamics
summary: Achieving endogenous regime switching is crucial for the emergence of autonomous intelligence, yet remains a central challenge for existing machine learning frameworks, where such transitions are typically externally imposed. In this work, we introduce a classification that distinguishes scalar-reducible dynamics, which can be expressed as gradient flows driven by a scalar objective, from scalar-irreducible dynamics that cannot be reduced to such a form. While most existing machine learning systems operate within the scalar-reducible class, we demonstrate that scalar-irreducible dynamics naturally enable internally generated regime switching through feedback between fast dynamical variables and slow structural adaptation. Using a minimal dynamical model, we illustrate how this mechanism produces sustained endogenous regime transitions without external scheduling. Our results suggest a new dynamical paradigm for regime exploration and provide a potential route toward autonomous learning systems whose adaptive behavior is organized internally rather than externally prescribed.

AI.tldr: Classifies dynamics into gradient-flow (scalar-reducible) vs irreducible; scalar-irreducible dynamics enable endogenous regime switching via fast–slow feedback, shown in a minimal model, suggesting autonomous learning with internally organized adaptation.
AI.motivation: Endogenous regime switching is essential for autonomous intelligence but is not captured by current ML frameworks, which largely rely on scalar-reducible (gradient-flow) dynamics and external scheduling.
AI.method: Propose a taxonomy distinguishing scalar-reducible and scalar-irreducible dynamics; demonstrate with a minimal dynamical system how fast variables and slow structural adaptation couple to produce sustained regime transitions without external control.
AI.result: Demonstrates that scalar-irreducible dynamics can sustain endogenous regime transitions through internal feedback between fast and slow processes, while scalar-reducible dynamics lack autonomous switching; provides a potential dynamical paradigm for regime exploration.
AI.conclusion: Points toward autonomous learning systems whose adaptive behavior is organized internally, not externally prescribed, implying a shift in how regime changes are realized in AI systems.

title: HDFlow: Hierarchical Diffusion-Flow Planning for Long-horizon Tasks
summary: Recent advances in generative models have shown promise in generating behavior plans for long-horizon, sparse reward tasks. While these approaches have achieved promising results, they often lack a principled framework for hierarchical decomposition and struggle with the computational demands of real-time execution, due to their iterative denoising process. In this work, we introduce Hierarchical Diffusion-Flow (HDFlow), a novel hierarchical planning framework that optimally leverages the strengths of diffusion and rectified flow models to overcome the limitations of single-paradigm generative planners. HDFlow employs a high-level diffusion planner to generate sequences of strategic subgoals in a learned latent space, capitalizing on diffusion's powerful exploratory capabilities. These subgoals then guide a low-level rectified flow planner that generates smooth and dense trajectories, exploiting the speed and efficiency of ordinary differential equation (ODE)-based trajectory generation. We evaluate HDFlow on four challenging furniture assembly tasks in both simulation and real-world, where it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, we also showcase our method's generalizability on two long-horizon benchmarks comprising diverse locomotion and manipulation tasks. Project website: https://hdflow-page.github.io/

AI.tldr: HDFlow combines high-level diffusion-based subgoal planning with low-level rectified-flow trajectory generation to create a hierarchical planner for long-horizon tasks, improving performance on furniture assembly and other benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of principled hierarchical decomposition in diffusion-only planners and the computational burden of iterative denoising for real-time execution; leverage diffusion's exploration at a high level and flow's fast trajectory synthesis at a low level.
AI.method: A two-tier architecture where a diffusion-based planner operates in a learned latent space to produce strategic subgoals, which then guide a rectified flow-based planner that generates dense, smooth trajectories via ODE-based trajectory generation.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art methods on four furniture-assembly tasks in simulation and real-world settings; demonstrates generalization on two long-horizon benchmarks across locomotion and manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: HDFlow provides a principled hierarchical framework that unites diffusion and rectified flow to overcome limitations of single-paradigm planners, enabling efficient, long-horizon planning and execution.

title: On the Complexity of Minimum Riesz s-Energy Subset Selection in Euclidean and Ultrametric Spaces
summary: We study the computational complexity of exact cardinality-constrained minimum Riesz $s$-energy subset selection in finite metric spaces: given $n$ points, select $k<n$ points of minimum Riesz $s$-energy. The objective sums inverse-power pair interactions and therefore promotes well-separated subsets; as $s$ becomes large, it increasingly approaches a bottleneck criterion governed by the closest selected pair, linking it to minimum pairwise distance (MPD). Building on the general-metric NP-hardness result of Pereverdieva et al. (2025), we prove that NP-hardness persists for point sets in the Euclidean plane when $s$ is part of the input. In contrast, finite ultrametric spaces form an exact tractable regime: on rooted binary ultrametric trees with $n$ leaves, an optimal size-$k$ subset can be computed by dynamic programming in $O(nk^2)$ time. We also discuss the ordered one-dimensional Euclidean case, where the classical MPD objective admits simple dynamic programming, but the additive Riesz energy does not appear to allow the same state compression. Finally, we explain why one natural route to fixed-$s$ Euclidean hardness does not close: Fowler-style 3SAT gadgets, together with zeta-function bounds for far-field interactions, show why this approach still requires an exponent depending on $k$. Together, these results provide a compact complexity landscape for a natural diversity or dispersion objective, distinguishing Euclidean hardness, ultrametric tractability, and the ordered one-dimensional case.

AI.tldr: The abstract maps a natural combinatorial optimization problem (select k points with minimal Riesz s-energy) to a complexity landscape across metric spaces: Euclidean plane with s variable is NP-hard; ultrametrics permit efficient DP; ordered 1D Euclidean is in-between (MPD DP exists, Riesz energy resists compact DP); standard gadget-based reductions to fixed-s hardness in Euclidean face obstacles due to far-field interactions, implying exponent dependence on k.
AI.motivation: Understand the computational complexity of a dispersion/diversity objective (Riesz energy) across different metric spaces, clarifying where exact optimization is tractable and where hardness persists.
AI.method: Leverages known general-metric NP-hardness results (Pereverdieva et al. 2025) and adapts them to the Euclidean plane with s as part of the input; develops a dynamic programming algorithm for rooted binary ultrametric trees (O(nk^2)); discusses 1D ordered Euclidean case and why additive energy does not admit similar state compression; analyzes limitations of Fowler-style 3SAT gadget reductions for fixed-s hardness via zeta-function bounds for far-field interactions.
AI.result:
- NP-hardness persists in the Euclidean plane when s is part of the input. 
- Finite ultrametric spaces are tractable; optimal size-k subset on rooted binary trees can be computed in O(nk^2).
- In 1D ordered Euclidean case, MPD DP exists, but additive Riesz energy resists the same DP compression.
- Fixed-s hardness via standard gadget reductions remains unclosed due to needing an exponent depending on k, due to far-field interaction analysis.
AI.conclusion: The paper delineates a compact complexity landscape for exact cardinality-constrained Riesz-energy subset selection: Euclidean hardness with variable s, ultrametric tractability via DP, and intermediate behavior in ordered 1D; it also identifies intrinsic barriers to proving fixed-s hardness in Euclidean spaces using conventional gadget approaches.

title: Regularized Centered Emphatic Temporal Difference Learning
summary: Off-policy temporal-difference (TD) learning with function approximation faces a structural tradeoff among stability, projection geometry, and variance control. Emphatic TD (ETD) improves the off-policy projection geometry through follow-on emphasis, but the follow-on trace can have high variance. We revisit this tradeoff through Bellman-error centering. Although centering naturally removes a common drift term from TD errors, we show that a naive centered emphatic extension introduces an auxiliary coupling that can destroy the positive-definiteness of the ETD key matrix. We propose \emph{Regularized Emphatic Temporal-Difference Learning} (RETD), which preserves the follow-on trace and regularizes only the auxiliary centering recursion, corresponding to lifting the lower-right block of the coupled key matrix from \(1\) to \(1+c\). We derive the RETD core matrix, prove convergence under a conservative sufficient regularization condition, and evaluate the method on diagnostic linear off-policy prediction tasks. The experiments show that RETD avoids the instability of naive centered emphatic learning, preserves favorable emphatic geometry, and exhibits a robust intermediate regime for the regularization parameter \(c\) across the diagnostics.

AI.tldr: Regularized Emphatic TD (RETD) stabilizes off-policy TD with function approximation by regularizing the centering recursion, preserving follow-on traces and favorable projection geometry, with convergence guarantees and robust empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Off-policy TD with function approximation faces a stability/geometry/variance tradeoff. Emphatic TD (ETD) improves projection geometry via follow-on emphasis but can incur high variance; naive centering can introduce destabilizing coupling that harms positive-definiteness.
AI.method: Introduce Regularized Emphatic TD (RETD) that preserves the follow-on trace and only regularizes the auxiliary centering recursion, effectively lifting the lower-right block of the coupled key matrix from 1 to 1+c. Derive the RETD core matrix, establish convergence under a conservative regularization condition, and evaluate on diagnostic linear off-policy prediction tasks.
AI.result: RETD avoids the instability inherent in naive centered emphatic learning, preserves the favorable emphatic geometry, and exhibits a robust intermediate regime for the regularization parameter c across the diagnostic tasks.
AI.conclusion: RETD provides a stable, convergent off-policy TD algorithm with a geometry-preserving emphasis mechanism and practical robustness, offering a controlled trade-off between stability and variance through regularization.

title: MuCALD-SplitFed: Causal-Latent Diffusion for Privacy-Preserving Multi-Task Split-Federated Medical Image Segmentation
comment: Accepted to oral presentation and conference proceedings at IEEE International Conference on Image Processing (ICIP 2026), Finland
summary: Federated Learning enables decentralized training by aggregating model updates across clients without sharing raw data, while Split Federated Learning further partitions the model between clients and a server to reduce computation and communication at the client side. However, decentralized medical institutions rarely operate on a single shared task, making standard Federated and SplitFed collaborations poorly aligned with real clinical workflows. Multi-task FL extends these frameworks by allowing clients to handle different tasks, but often introduces instability and privacy vulnerabilities. This study proposes \textbf{MuCALD-SplitFed}, a multi-task SplitFed framework that integrates causal representation learning and latent diffusion. Experiments show MuCALD-SplitFed consistently improves segmentation, while baseline SplitFed fails to converge. The proposed approach further reduces information leakage at split points, mitigating reconstruction-based and membership inference attacks. Additionally, MuCALD SplitFed outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL and multi-task FL approaches. The code repository is: https://github.com/ChamaniS/MuCALD_SplitFed.

AI.tldr: MuCALD-SplitFed delivers a stable, privacy-preserving multi-task SplitFed framework by integrating causal representation learning and latent diffusion to improve medical image segmentation in decentralized settings; it converges where standard SplitFed does not and surpasses existing personalized FL and multi-task FL methods, with reduced leakage at split points.
AI.motivation: Clinical decentralized institutions often work on multiple related tasks; standard Federated Learning (FL) and SplitFed do not align well with such multi-task workflows and can suffer from instability and privacy vulnerabilities. A framework that supports multi-task learning with improved convergence and privacy is needed for realistic clinical deployment.
AI.method: Proposes MuCALD-SplitFed, a multi-task SplitFed architecture that integrates causal representation learning and latent diffusion. Training is performed across clients and a server with split model points, aiming to reduce information leakage at split points and enhance task-specific performance. The approach is evaluated on segmentation tasks, with comparisons to baseline SplitFed and other FL methods; code is provided in a public repository.
AI.result: MuCALD-SplitFed consistently improves segmentation performance, while the baseline SplitFed approach fails to converge. It also reduces information leakage at split points, mitigating reconstruction-based and membership inference attacks. The method outperforms state-of-the-art personalized FL and multi-task FL approaches.
AI.conclusion: MuCALD-SplitFed offers a viable, privacy-conscious solution for decentralized medical imaging that aligns with real clinical workflows by enabling stable multi-task learning, improved segmentation, and enhanced privacy at split points; it shows promise for broader adoption in heterogeneous clinical environments.

title: A Self-Attentive Meta-Optimizer with Group-Adaptive Learning Rates and Weight Decay
summary: Adaptive optimizers like AdamW apply uniform hyperparameters across all parameter groups, ignoring heterogeneous optimization dynamics across layers and modules. We address this limitation by proposing MetaAdamW - a new optimizer that integrates a self-attention mechanism to dynamically modulate per-group learning rates and weight decay. The modulation factors are produced by a lightweight Transformer encoder that operates on statistical features (gradient norms, momentum norms, correlations) extracted from each parameter group. To train the attention module, we introduce a meta-learning objective that combines gradient alignment, loss decrease, and generalization gap. A key novel contribution is the extension of homoscedastic uncertainty weighting (HUW) with task-specific priorities that directly scale the regularization terms - enabling domain knowledge to guide automatic loss balancing. Extensive experiments on five diverse tasks-time series forecasting (ETT), language modeling (WikiText-2), machine translation (Multi30k), image classification (CIFAR-10), and sentiment analysis (IMDB) - demonstrate that MetaAdamW consistently outperforms the standard AdamW baseline in terms of validation loss, accuracy, or perplexity. Depending on the task, MetaAdamW either reduces overall training time (by up to 17.11%) or improves performance (by up to 11.08%) while introducing only moderate overhead; in some cases, it can also mitigate issues of insufficient convergence caused by premature early stopping. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each component, including feature versions, grouping strategies, and the proposed priority-injected uncertainty weighting.

AI.tldr: MetaAdamW uses a transformer-based attention module to dynamically modulate per-group learning rates and weight decay, with uncertainty-weighted loss balancing, yielding consistent gains across diverse tasks with moderate overhead.
AI.motivation: Uniform hyperparameters across all parameter groups ignore heterogeneous optimization dynamics across layers; per-group dynamics necessitate adaptive mechanisms. Incorporating meta-learning and uncertainty weighting can tailor optimization to tasks and domains.
AI.method: Introduce MetaAdamW, where a lightweight Transformer encoder takes per-group statistics (gradient norms, momentum norms, correlations) to produce per-group learning-rate and weight-decay modulation factors. Train the attention module with a meta-learning objective that combines gradient alignment, loss decrease, and generalization gap. Extend homoscedastic uncertainty weighting with task-specific priorities to scale regularization terms. Evaluate across multiple tasks with various grouping strategies and ablations.
AI.result: MetaAdamW consistently outperforms AdamW on validation loss, accuracy, or perplexity across five tasks, with up to 17.11% training-time reduction and up to 11.08% performance gains; moderate overhead observed. Ablations confirm the effectiveness of feature choices, grouping strategies, and priority-injected uncertainty weighting.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, per-group optimization guided by a lightweight attention module and domain-aware loss balancing improves convergence and generalization across diverse domains, while offering configurable trade-offs between speed and accuracy.

title: Practical validation of synthetic pre-crash scenarios
summary: The representativeness of synthetic pre-crash scenarios is crucial for assessing the safety impact of Driving Automation Systems through virtual simulations. However, a gap remains in the robust evaluation of synthetic pre-crash scenarios' practical equivalence to their real-world counterparts; that is, whether they are similar enough for the intended assessment purpose. Conventional significance testing is inadequate, as it focuses on detecting differences rather than establishing practical equivalence. This study addresses the research gap by extending our previous work on a Bayesian Region of Practical Equivalence (ROPE)-based equivalence testing framework by introducing a binning-based approach to define appropriate statistics and equivalence criteria. Two binning-based statistics are proposed to measure practically meaningful distributional differences between datasets in the context of safety impact assessment. The framework's applicability is demonstrated through a case study, which tests the practical equivalence of two synthetic rear-end pre-crash datasets with a previously developed reference dataset in the context of the safety impact assessment of an Automatic Emergency Braking system. The results show that the framework provides informative quantitative assessments of practical equivalence as well as diagnostic insights into the divergence of datasets. Although the demonstration focuses on rear-end pre-crash scenarios, the framework is generic and extensible to broader validation contexts, providing an interpretable and principled basis for practical equivalence assessment across diverse synthetic data applications.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian ROPE-based equivalence testing framework, augmented with binning-based statistics, assesses practical equivalence between synthetic pre-crash scenarios and real-world references for safety impact assessment, demonstrated on rear-end pre-crash data for AEB; the approach is generic for broader validation.
AI.motivation: To ensure synthetic pre-crash scenarios are practically equivalent to real-world counterparts, enabling reliable virtual safety evaluations; conventional significance tests miss equivalence.
AI.method: Extend a Bayesian Region of Practical Equivalence (ROPE) framework with two binning-based statistics to quantify distributional differences; declare practical equivalence when statistics fall within ROPE; apply to a case study comparing two synthetic rear-end pre-crash datasets against a reference dataset for Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) safety impact assessment.
AI.result: The framework yields informative quantitative assessments of practical equivalence and diagnostic insights into dataset divergence; demonstrates usefulness for rear-end pre-crash scenarios and is generalizable to other validation contexts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed binning-based ROPE framework is generic and extensible to broader validation contexts, providing an interpretable, principled basis for practical equivalence assessment across diverse synthetic data applications in safety assessment.

title: Actionable Real-Time Modeling of Surgical Team Dynamics via Time-Expanded Interaction Graphs
comment: Accepted at Hybrid Human Artificial Intelligence (HHAI) 2026
summary: Surgical team performance arises from complex interactions between technical execution and non-technical skills, including communication and coordination dynamics. However, current surgical AI systems predominantly model visual workflow signals, lacking structured representations of intraoperative team interactions over time. We propose a real-time actionable approach for modeling surgical team dynamics using time-expanded interaction graphs, where team members are modeled as time-indexed nodes and communication exchanges define directed edges. This spatio-temporal expansion enables dynamic interaction modeling, while allowing efficient inference with a static graph neural network. The model predicts procedural efficiency as the deviation from the expected duration and supports real-time deployment. Beyond prediction, we perform a counterfactual analysis to identify minimal changes in communication structure and interpretable behavioral variables associated with improved predicted outcomes. Experiments on recorded surgical procedures show that structured modeling of team interactions improves early identification of prolonged interventions and provides coherent, actionable explanations. This work advances surgical AI toward real-time, team-aware, and actionable decision support in the operating room.

AI.tldr: Real-time modeling of surgical team dynamics using time-expanded interaction graphs improves early detection of prolonged interventions and provides actionable explanations, deployable via a static graph neural network.
AI.motivation: Current surgical AI overemphasizes visual workflow signals and lacks structured representations of intraoperative team interactions over time, hindering real-time, team-aware decision support.
AI.method: Model team members as time-indexed nodes and communication as directed edges in a time-expanded graph; enable dynamic interaction modeling with a static GNN for efficient inference; perform counterfactual analysis to identify minimal communication-structure changes and interpretable variables associated with better outcomes.
AI.result: Experiments on recorded surgeries show improved early identification of prolonged interventions and coherent, actionable explanations.
AI.conclusion: This approach advances surgical AI toward real-time, team-aware, and actionable decision support in the operating room.

title: Topology-Constrained Quantized nnUNet for Efficient and Anatomically Accurate 3D Tooth Segmentation
summary: We propose a topology-constrained quantized nnUNet framework for efficient and anatomically accurate 3D tooth segmentation, addressing the challenges of spatial distortion introduced by quantization in deep learning models. The proposed method integrates a novel tooth-specific topological loss into quantization-aware training, preserving critical anatomical structures such as tooth count, adjacency relationships, and cavity integrity while maintaining computational efficiency. The system employs an 8-bit quantized nnUNet backbone, where weights and activations are dynamically calibrated to minimize precision loss during inference. Furthermore, the topological loss combines connected-component analysis, adjacency consistency, and hole detection penalties, ensuring anatomical fidelity without modifying the underlying network architecture. The joint optimization objective harmonizes cross-entropy loss, quantization regularization, and topological constraints, enabling end-to-end training with gradient approximations for persistent homology terms. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces topological errors compared to conventional quantized models, achieving clinically plausible segmentations on dental CBCT scans. The method retains the hardware efficiency of integer-only inference, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained clinical environments. This work bridges the gap between computational efficiency and anatomical precision in medical image segmentation, offering a practical solution for real-world dental applications.

AI.tldr: 8-bit quantized nnUNet with a tooth-specific topological loss for efficient and anatomically faithful 3D tooth segmentation on CBCT, maintaining integer-only inference while preserving topology.
AI.motivation: To address quantization-induced spatial distortion and preserve critical dental anatomy under limited computational resources in clinical settings.
AI.method: Use 8-bit quantized nnUNet backbone with dynamic weight/activation calibration; introduce a tooth-specific topological loss combining connected components, adjacency consistency, and hole-detection penalties; enable end-to-end training with gradient approximations for persistent-homology terms; optimize a joint objective over cross-entropy, quantization regularization, and topological constraints.
AI.result: Significant reduction in topological errors compared with standard quantized models; clinically plausible segmentations on dental CBCT; retains hardware efficiency for integer-only inference.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges computational efficiency and anatomical precision in medical image segmentation, enabling practical deployment in resource-constrained dental clinics.

title: Transformation Categorization Based on Group Decomposition Theory Using Parameter Division
summary: Representation learning seeks meaningful sensory representations without supervision and can model aspects of human development. Although many neural networks empirically learn useful features, a principled account of what makes a representation "good" remains elusive. We study unsupervised categorization of transformations between pairs of inputs under algebraic constraints. Classical disentanglement favors mutually independent factors and fails when factors are coupled. Our prior Galois-theoretic approach decomposes a group via normal subgroups by learning a product of two transformations with one factor constrained to a normal subgroup, covering both commutative and non-commutative cases. That method, however, relied on auxiliary assumptions (e.g., motion and isometry restrictions) not required by decomposition theory, and ablations did not separate theory-based from auxiliary effects. We propose parameter division for a single transformation: we split its parameter into components, impose homomorphism constraints mapping the full transformation to one component, and identify the normal subgroup as the set of transformations when that component is fixed to the identity. This formulation drops the previous auxiliary assumptions and applies more broadly. We evaluate on image pairs involving rotation, translation, and scale; ablations show that group-decomposition constraints drive appropriate categorization.

AI.tldr: A principled unsupervised transformation categorization method using parameter division and normal-subgroup constraints within group decomposition, avoiding prior auxiliary assumptions, validated on rotation/translation/scale.
AI.motivation: Provide a general, theory-grounded criterion for disentangling transformation factors in representation learning; address coupling of factors and limits of classical disentanglement; extend Galois-theoretic decomposition to non-commutative cases.
AI.method: Split a single transformation's parameter into two components; map to one component via a homomorphism; define the normal subgroup as the set where the component equals identity; apply group-decomposition constraints; drop motion/isometry-specific assumptions; evaluate on image pairs under rotation, translation, scale; ablation studies to isolate effect.
AI.result: Constraints produce correct categorization of transformations; removal of auxiliary assumptions broadens applicability; ablations show necessity of decomposition constraints; good performance on tested transformations.
AI.conclusion: A broader, more principled framework for unsupervised factorization of transformations via group theory; demonstrates that parameter-wise decomposition and normal-subgroup structure suffice for effective disentanglement across both commutative and non-commutative groups.

title: Right Model, Right Time: Real-Time Cascaded-Fidelity MPC for Bipedal Walking
comment: Accepted to IEEE ICRA 2026 Workshop "2cnd Workshop on Frontiers of Optimization for Robotics"
summary: This paper presents a multi-phase whole-body model predictive control approach for bipedal walking, combining a detailed whole-body model in the near horizon with a simplified single-rigid-body model in the later prediction steps. This reduces computational complexity while retaining prediction capabilities. The resulting nonlinear optimal control problem is solved using sequential quadratic programming (SQP) in acados. Using a prior specified contact schedule and a target walking speed, the controller optimizes joint torques without depending on prior selected foot step locations. The controller is validated in MuJoCo simulation on the 18-DoF bipedal robot HyPer-2

AI.tldr: Multi-phase whole-body MPC for bipedal walking with a detailed near-horizon model and a simplified later horizon, solved as a nonlinear OCP via SQP in acados, using fixed contact schedule and target speed, eliminating the need for footstep priors; validated in MuJoCo on 18-DoF HyPer-2.
AI.motivation: To achieve accurate, predictive whole-body control of bipedal walking while keeping real-time computational demands manageable by reducing model complexity in the prediction horizon.
AI.method: Develop a multi-phase MPC that combines a detailed whole-body model in the near horizon with a simplified single-rigid-body model in later steps. Formulate a nonlinear optimal control problem and solve it with sequential quadratic programming (SQP) using the acados framework. Use a predefined contact schedule and target walking speed, and optimize joint torques without relying on pre-selected footstep locations.
AI.result: The approach reduces computational load while preserving predictive capability and is validated in MuJoCo on the 18-DoF bipedal robot HyPer-2.
AI.conclusion: A tractable, accurate whole-body walking controller is achievable by phase-based model simplification within MPC, enabling efficient optimization without footstep priors and demonstrated in simulation on HyPer-2.

title: ANDRE: An Attention-based Neuro-symbolic Differentiable Rule Extractor
summary: Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) aims to learn interpretable first-order rules from data, but existing symbolic and neuro-symbolic approaches struggle to scale to noisy and probabilistic settings. Classical ILP relies on discrete combinatorial rule search and is brittle under uncertainty, while differentiable ILP methods typically depend on predefined rule templates or inaccurate fuzzy operators that suffer from vanishing gradients or poor approximation of logical structure when reasoning over probabilistic predicate valuations. This paper proposes an Attention-based Neuro-symbolic Differentiable Rule Extractor (ANDRE), a novel ILP framework that learns first-order logic programs by optimizing over a continuous rule space with attention-based logical operators. ANDRE replaces both rule templates and logical operators with fully differentiable, attention-driven conjunction and disjunction operators that approximate logical min-max semantics, enabling accurate, stable, and interpretable reasoning over probabilistic data. By softly selecting, negating, or excluding predicates within each rule, ANDRE supports flexible rule induction while preserving symbolic structure. Extensive experiments on classical ILP benchmarks, large-scale knowledge bases, and synthetic datasets with probabilistic predicates and noisy supervision demonstrate that ANDRE achieves competitive or superior predictive performance while reliably recovering correct symbolic rules under uncertainty. In particular, ANDRE remains robust to moderate label noise, substantially outperforming existing differentiable ILP methods in both rule extraction quality and stability.

AI.tldr: ANDRE is an attention-based differentiable ILP framework that learns first-order rules in a continuous space using differentiable, attention-driven logical operators, robust to noise and probabilistic data, achieving competitive performance and improved rule extraction.
AI.motivation: Classical ILP relies on discrete combinatorial rule search and is fragile under uncertainty; differentiable ILP often depends on fixed templates or fuzzy operators that can suffer from vanishing gradients and poor logical-structure approximation in probabilistic settings.
AI.method: ANDRE introduces a fully differentiable rule extractor that uses attention-based conjunction and disjunction operators to approximate min–max semantics, soft-predicate selection/negation/exclusion, and a continuous rule space for end-to-end optimization without rigid templates.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on classical ILP benchmarks, large-scale knowledge bases, and synthetic probabilistic datasets shows competitive or superior predictive performance and, importantly, robust and accurate rule recovery under uncertainty, outperforming existing differentiable ILP approaches in rule extraction quality and stability.
AI.conclusion: ANDRE provides a scalable, interpretable neuro-symbolic ILP framework that jointly optimizes soft, attention-driven logical operators and predicate choices to achieve reliable rule induction in noisy or probabilistic environments.

title: Anatomy of a failure: When, how, and why deep vision fails in scientific domains
summary: Mirroring its ubiquity in popular media and all human activities, the use of deep learning (DL) is rapidly growing in scientific imaging modalities. However, unlike everyday RGB pictures, pixels encode precise physicochemical properties in scientific imaging across potentially thousands of channels. While DL is well validated on human-centric RGB perceptual tasks, its effectiveness for scientific imaging remains uncertain. Here, we show that the naive application of DL frameworks to scientific images can lead to critical failures. We evaluate the use of DL for pathology, comparing RGB images of stained tissue with the quantitative and information-rich biochemical signatures of infrared (IR) imaging. Despite this informational advantage, DL models trained on IR data paradoxically underperform. We investigate this discrepancy to find that IR data priors interact poorly with the simplicity bias of DL, causing models to collapse to one-dimensional predictions. This constitutes a catastrophic DL failure because the model's representational capacity remains largely unused, while furthermore raising AI safety concerns and undermining the advantages of such scientific modalities. Notably, this problem persists even with state-of-the-art DL robustification strategies, which are primarily designed and validated for RGB imagery and thus inherit the same prior-bias mismatch. This work establishes a framework for understanding the limitations of generic DL in science and advocates for the study of modality-specific failure modes to guide the development of specialized, safe AI algorithms.

AI.tldr: DL on high-channel scientific IR imaging can fail due to modality priors and DL simplicity bias, causing 1D prediction collapse; standard robustness methods do not fix this; calls for modality-aware, safe AI design.
AI.motivation: Scientific imaging encodes rich physicochemical information across many channels; naive DL may underutilize data, reduce reliability, and raise safety concerns; understanding modality-specific failure modes is essential for safe AI in science.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across modalities (RGB stained tissue vs infrared imaging); analysis of priors, representation, and prediction behavior; assessment of robustification strategies; development of a framework for modality-specific failures.
AI.result: DL models trained on IR data underperform compared to RGB despite richer information; priors interact with DL simplicity bias causing collapse to near-1D predictions; robustification strategies designed for RGB do not remedy this; failure persists.
AI.conclusion: Advocate modality-aware model design and safety-focused AI for scientific imaging; propose a framework to study and mitigate modality-specific failure modes, guiding development of specialized, safe AI algorithms.

title: Structured Progressive Knowledge Activation for LLM-Driven Neural Architecture Search
summary: This paper focuses on a key challenge in Neural Architecture Search (NAS): integrating established architectural knowledge while exploring new designs under expensive evaluations. Large language models (LLMs) are a promising assistant for NAS because they can translate rich architectural and coding priors into executable code edits. However, in practice, seemingly local revisions often propagate into non-local behavioral and performance shifts because a single edit can inadvertently couple multiple interacting functional factors, a phenomenon we refer to as functional entanglement. To make LLM knowledge usable under such entanglement, we propose Structured Progressive Knowledge Activation (SPARK), which activates relevant priors by explicitly selecting the functional factor to modify and conditioning the edit on that factor. This factor-conditioned editing reduces entangled side effects and yields more targeted, reliable architecture modifications. On CLRS-DFS, SPARK achieves a 28.1x sample-efficient architecture evolution speedup and yields a 22.9 percent relative improvement in OOD accuracy.

AI.tldr: SPARK proposes factor-conditioned editing to mitigate functional entanglement when using LLMs for NAS, improving efficiency and OOD performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs can leverage architectural priors for NAS, but local edits can cause non-local adverse effects due to entanglement; a mechanism is needed to target edits to specific functional factors.
AI.method: Introduce Structured Progressive Knowledge Activation (SPARK) that explicitly selects the functional factor to modify and conditions edits on that factor during LLM-guided architecture edits.
AI.result: Demonstrates 28.1x sample-efficient architecture evolution speedup and 22.9% relative improvement in OOD accuracy on CLRS-DFS dataset.
AI.conclusion: Factor-conditioned, factor-aware editing reduces entangled side effects, enabling more reliable architecture modifications and practical gains in NAS with LLM assistance.

title: Active Contact Sensing for Robust Robot-to-Human Object Handover
summary: Robot-to-human object handover is an essential skill for robot assistants, from serving drinks at home to passing surgical tools in the operating room. We expect robots to perform handover robustly -- to release the object only after a firm human grasp while ignoring incidental touches. Existing passive-sensing methods struggle to generalize across diverse objects and human behaviors, as they lack informative perturbations to disambiguate different contact conditions, such as firm grasp versus incidental touch. We propose an active sensing approach for robust handovers: the robot applies information-gathering motions and senses the resulting human-applied forces to infer the contact state. A firm grasp produces forces in multiple directions, while an accidental touch does not. To capture this distinction, we model the contact state with a Bayesian linear model: a distribution over piecewise-linear mappings from robot motions to human-applied forces. This model enables firm grasp detection and active information gathering. In experiments with 12 participants and 30 diverse rigid objects, our method achieved a 97.5% success rate -- over 30% higher than two common baselines.

AI.tldr: Active sensing with a Bayesian piecewise-linear model detects firm grasp vs incidental touch in robot-to-human handover, achieving 97.5% success across 12 participants and 30 objects, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Robust robot-to-human handover requires reliable detection of a firm grasp; passive sensing lacks generalization across diverse objects and human behaviors, making handovers error-prone.
AI.method: Robot performs information-gathering motions and senses human-applied forces. The contact state is modeled with a Bayesian linear model: a distribution over piecewise-linear mappings from robot motions to human-applied forces, enabling firm-grasp detection and active information gathering.
AI.result: Experiment with 12 participants and 30 diverse rigid objects yielded 97.5% success, about 30% higher than two common baselines.
AI.conclusion: Active sensing via a Bayesian piecewise-linear contact model improves robustness of robot-to-human handover by enabling reliable detection of firm grasp across varied objects and users, and by eliciting informative contacts.

title: Pro$^2$Assist: Continuous Step-Aware Proactive Assistance with Multimodal Egocentric Perception for Long-Horizon Procedural Tasks
summary: Procedural tasks with multiple ordered steps are ubiquitous in daily life. Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have enabled personal assistants that support daily activities. However, existing systems primarily provide reactive guidance triggered by user queries, or limited proactive assistance for isolated short-term events rather than long-horizon procedural tasks. In this work, we introduce Pro$^2$Assist, a step-aware proactive assistant that continuously tracks fine-grained task progress and reasons over the user's evolving state to provide timely assistance throughout tasks. Pro$^2$Assist leverages multimodal data from augmented reality (AR) glasses to achieve motion-based perception. It then extracts step-oriented procedural context from multi-scale temporal dynamics and task-specific expert knowledge. Based on both sensory input and procedural context, Pro$^2$Assist performs continuous reasoning to infer user needs and display timely assistance on AR glasses. We evaluate Pro$^2$Assist using a dataset curated from public sources and a real-world dataset collected on our testbed with AR glasses. Extensive evaluations show that Pro$^2$Assist outperforms the best-performing baselines by over 21% in procedural action understanding accuracy, and it achieves up to 2.29x the proactive timing accuracy of baselines. A user study with 20 participants further shows that 90% find Pro$^2$Assist useful, indicating its effectiveness for real-world procedural assistance.

AI.tldr: Pro^2Assist is a step-aware proactive AR-assisted procedural task assistant that continuously tracks fine-grained progress using motion-based perception from AR glasses, reasons over evolving state with multi-scale temporal dynamics and expert knowledge, and provides timely, proactive guidance. Evaluations show substantial improvements over baselines in procedural action understanding and proactive timing; a user study reports high usefulness.
AI.motivation: Procedural tasks with long horizons are common; current systems are reactive or limited to short-term events, lacking continuous, proactive support that tracks progress and evolving state.
AI.method: Proposed system integrates AR-glasses-based motion perception to extract step-oriented procedural context, leverages multi-scale temporal dynamics and task-specific expert knowledge to perform continuous reasoning about user needs, and displays proactive guidance on AR glasses.
AI.result: Quantitatively, surpasses best baselines by over 21% in procedural action understanding accuracy and up to 2.29x in proactive timing accuracy. User study with 20 participants shows 90% found Pro^2Assist useful.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and effectiveness of step-aware proactive assistance for long-horizon procedures, with strong quantitative gains and positive user reception; supports AR-based continuous procedural support and indicates potential for broader deployment and future improvements.

title: Disentangled Learning Improves Implicit Neural Representations for Medical Reconstruction
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for medical imaging via physics-informed unsupervised learning. Classical INRs optimize an entire network from scratch for each subject, leading to inefficient training and suboptimal imaging quality. Recent initialization-based approaches attempt to inject population priors into pre-trained networks, yet they rely on high-quality images and often suffer from catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning. We present DisINR, a novel INR framework that explicitly disentangles shared and subject-specific representations. DisINR introduces a shared encoder-decoder pair and subject-specific encoders, whose features are jointly decoded for image reconstruction. By integrating differentiable forward models, it pre-trains the shared modules directly from limited raw measurements, removing the need for pre-acquired high-quality images. During test-time adaptation, only the subject-specific encoder is optimized, while the shared pair remains frozen, effectively preserving learned priors. Extensive evaluations on three representative medical imaging tasks show that DisINR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art INRs in both reconstruction accuracy and efficiency.

AI.tldr: DisINR introduces a disentangled INR framework with a shared encoder–decoder and subject-specific encoders; it pre-trains shared modules from raw measurements and only tunes subject-specific encoders at test time, yielding improved reconstruction quality and efficiency over prior INRs.
AI.motivation: To address inefficiencies and quality limits of classical INRs in medical imaging, which require per-subject training and can forget prior information during fine-tuning; leverage population priors without relying on high-quality images.
AI.method: A shared encoder–decoder pair is complemented by subject-specific encoders. Features are jointly decoded. Differentiable forward models enable pre-training from limited raw measurements. During testing, only the subject-specific encoder is optimized while the shared modules remain fixed, preserving priors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three medical imaging tasks show that DisINR surpasses state-of-the-art INRs in reconstruction accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: DisENTangled representation learning for INRs, with pre-training on raw measurements and test-time adaptation restricted to subject-specific encoders, effectively preserves priors while enabling subject-specific reconstruction and improves performance and efficiency.

title: ReflectDrive-2: Reinforcement-Learning-Aligned Self-Editing for Discrete Diffusion Driving
summary: We introduce ReflectDrive-2, a masked discrete diffusion planner with separate action expert for autonomous driving that represents plans as discrete trajectory tokens and generates them through parallel masked decoding. This discrete token space enables in-place trajectory revision: AutoEdit rewrites selected tokens using the same model, without requiring an auxiliary refinement network. To train this capability, we use a two-stage procedure. First, we construct structure-aware perturbations of expert trajectories along longitudinal progress and lateral heading directions and supervise the model to recover the original expert trajectory. We then fine-tune the full decision--draft--reflect rollout with reinforcement learning (RL), assigning terminal driving reward to the final post-edit trajectory and propagating policy-gradient credit through full-rollout transitions. Full-rollout RL proves crucial for coupling drafting and editing: under supervised training alone, inference-time AutoEdit improves PDMS by at most $0.3$, whereas RL increases its gain to $1.9$. We also co-design an efficient reflective decoding stack for the decision--draft--reflect pipeline, combining shared-prefix KV reuse, Alternating Step Decode, and fused on-device unmasking. On NAVSIM, ReflectDrive-2 achieves $91.0$ PDMS with camera-only input and $94.8$ PDMS in a best-of-6 oracle setting, while running at $31.8$ ms average latency on NVIDIA Thor.

AI.tldr: Proposes ReflectDrive-2, a discrete-token diffusion planner for autonomous driving with in-place trajectory editing (AutoEdit), trained via a two-stage process (perturbation recovery) and full decision–draft–reflect RL, achieving strong PDMS and low latency on NAVSIM with a reflective decoding stack for efficiency.
AI.motivation: Enable fast, editable trajectory planning in autonomous driving by representing plans as discrete tokens that can be revised in-place, reducing reliance on separate refinement networks and enabling end-to-end improvement through RL.
AI.method: 1) Train with structure-aware perturbations of expert trajectories to recover the original path. 2) Fine-tune a full decision--draft--reflect pipeline using reinforcement learning with a terminal driving reward, propagating gradients through full-rollout transitions. 3) Develop an efficient reflective decoding stack (shared-prefix KV reuse, Alternating Step Decode, fused on-device unmasking) for quality and speed. 4) Evaluate on NAVSIM with camera input and best-of-6 oracle setting.
AI.result: AutoEdit with supervised training alone yields modest gains (PDMS up to +0.3). Incorporating full-rollout RL yields a larger gain (+1.9). ReflectDrive-2 achieves 91.0 PDMS (camera-only) and 94.8 PDMS (best-of-6 oracle) on NAVSIM, with an average latency of 31.8 ms on NVIDIA Thor.
AI.conclusion: The discrete-token diffusion and in-place editing framework, coupled with RL fine-tuning and an efficient reflective decoding stack, yields high-performance, low-latency autonomous driving planning (PDMS) and effective drafting–editing coupling; AutoEdit benefits substantially from end-to-end RL.

title: Temporal Reasoning Is Not the Bottleneck: A Probabilistic Inconsistency Framework for Neuro-Symbolic QA
summary: Despite significant advances, large language models (LLMs) continue to exhibit brittle performance on complex temporal reasoning tasks. This failure mode is widely attributed to inherent deficits in autoregressive logical deduction. In this paper, we challenge this prevailing narrative, demonstrating that temporal reasoning is not the fundamental bottleneck; rather, the locus of failure lies in unstructured text-to-event representation. We introduce a novel neuro-symbolic question-answering framework governed by a Probabilistic Inconsistency Signal (PIS) that explicitly isolates perceptual errors from reasoning failures. By lifting unstructured text into explicit event graphs and interval constraints, our architecture strictly decouples semantic extraction from a symbolic reasoning engine. To robustly detect structural breaks, the PIS elegantly unifies symbolic credal intervals with epistemic neural uncertainty extracted via Evidential Deep Learning on LLM hidden states. Empirical evaluations reveal a striking paradigm shift: when provided with correct structural representations, our system's explicit proof traces achieve perfect 1.0 accuracy (4000/4000) and strictly zero false positives/negatives on temporal arithmetic benchmarks. On broader, noise-injected QA settings, the framework maintains a competitive 75.1\% accuracy while enabling deterministic, step-level failure localization. Ultimately, by isolating the representation bottleneck from the reasoning substrate, this work reframes temporal QA from an algorithmic reasoning challenge to a structural alignment problem, charting a verifiable path forward for reliable neuro-symbolic AI.

AI.tldr: Neuro-symbolic QA with a Probabilistic Inconsistency Signal (PIS) that isolates perceptual errors from reasoning by lifting text to explicit event graphs and interval constraints; achieves perfect proof traces with correct structure and strong performance on noisy temporal QA.
AI.motivation: To address brittle temporal reasoning in LLMs, which is often attributed to autoregressive deduction failures. The work argues the bottleneck is unstructured text-to-event representation and proposes a framework to separate semantic extraction from symbolic reasoning using a probabilistic inconsistency signal.
AI.method: Convert unstructured text into explicit event graphs with interval constraints; decouple semantic extraction from a symbolic reasoning engine; use Evidential Deep Learning to extract epistemic uncertainty from LLM hidden states; unify symbolic credal intervals with epistemic uncertainty via PIS to detect inconsistencies and localize failures; provide explicit proof traces.
AI.result: When structural representations are correct, the system achieves 1.0 accuracy (4000/4000) with zero false positives/negatives on temporal arithmetic benchmarks; in noisy QA settings, it maintains 75.1% accuracy and enables deterministic, step-level failure localization.
AI.conclusion: Reframes temporal QA as a structural alignment problem; decouples representation from reasoning to enable verifiable, reliable neuro-symbolic AI, offering a clear path to robust temporal reasoning.

title: Densification and forecasting of Sentinel-2 time series from multimodal SAR and Optical satellite data using deep generative models
summary: Optical satellite image time series are extensively used in many Earth observation applications, including agriculture, climate monitoring, and land surface analysis. However, clouds and swath edges result in irregular sampling along the temporal dimension, limiting continuous monitoring. To address this issue, a growing body of work has focused on temporal densification and reconstruction of satellite image time series, with the objective of filling missing or cloud-contaminated observations within the temporal extent of the available data. While these approaches improve temporal continuity, they are inherently restricted to the reconstruction of the gaps within the observed time periods, and do not address the prediction of future observations. This work proposes a probabilistic deep learning framework for the densification and forecasting of Sentinel-2 time series by generating optical images at arbitrary past or future dates. The approach leverages multimodal satellite data by jointly exploiting Sentinel-2 optical and Sentinel-1 SAR observations. Unlike most existing works, we propose to focus on the uncertainty of the generated images. Experimental results demonstrate effective densification and forecasting, on sparse and temporally misaligned time series.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic deep learning framework for densifying and forecasting Sentinel-2 time series by fusing Sentinel-2 optical and Sentinel-1 SAR data, with a focus on uncertainty quantification for generated images at arbitrary past/future dates.
AI.motivation: Clouds and swath edges cause irregular temporal sampling in optical satellite time series, hindering continuous monitoring. Existing densification methods fill gaps within observed windows but do not predict future observations; uncertainty is often neglected.
AI.method: A probabilistic deep learning model that jointly exploits multimodal data (Sentinel-2 optical and Sentinel-1 SAR) to generate optical images at arbitrary dates (past or future) and quantify uncertainty in the predictions.
AI.result: Experiments show the method can densify and forecast time series effectively for sparse and temporally misaligned data, with an emphasis on reliable uncertainty estimation.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables continuous monitoring by generating plausible images at arbitrary dates with quantified uncertainty, leveraging multimodal data to improve temporal predictions beyond observed periods.

title: Continual Distillation of Teachers from Different Domains
summary: Deep learning models continue to scale, with some requiring more storage than many large-scale datasets. Thus, we introduce a new paradigm: Continual Distillation (CD), where a student learns sequentially from a stream of teacher models without retaining access to earlier teachers. CD faces two challenges: teacher training data is unavailable, and teachers have varying expertise. We show that external unlabeled data enables Unseen Knowledge Transfer (UKT), allowing the student to acquire information from domains not present in the training data, while known to the teacher. We also show that sequential distillation causes Unseen Knowledge Forgetting (UKF) when transferred knowledge is lost after training on later teachers. To better trade off between UKT and UKF, we propose Self External Data Distillation (SE2D), a method that preserves logits on external data to stabilize learning across heterogeneous teachers. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that SE2D reduces UKF and improves cross-domain generalization. The code and implementation for this work are publicly available at: https://github.com/Nicolas1203/continual_distillation.

AI.tldr: Continual Distillation (CD) trains a student from a sequence of teacher models without accessing earlier teachers or training data. External unlabeled data enables Unseen Knowledge Transfer (UKT) but sequential distillation risks Unseen Knowledge Forgetting (UKF); Self External Data Distillation (SE2D) preserves logits on external data to stabilize learning across heterogeneous teachers, reducing UKF and improving cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Scaling models requires large storage and data, motivating a paradigm where a student learns from a stream of teacher models without retaining past teachers or data. External unlabeled data can enable transfer to domains unseen by the training data, while heterogeneous teacher expertise necessitates stable, cross-domain learning.
AI.method: Formulate Continual Distillation (CD) with a stream of teachers. Identify UKT and UKF as core challenges. Propose SE2D (Self External Data Distillation) that preserves logits on external data to stabilize learning across heterogeneous teachers. Evaluate on multiple benchmarks, compare UKT/UKF, and report improved cross-domain generalization.
AI.result: SE2D reduces Unseen Knowledge Forgetting (UKF) and enhances cross-domain generalization. External unlabeled data enables Unseen Knowledge Transfer (UKT) to learn from domains not present in training data but known to teachers. The approach achieves improved performance across benchmarks and code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: SE2D offers a practical solution for continual distillation with heterogeneous teachers and external data, balancing knowledge transfer to unseen domains while mitigating forgetting of previously transferred knowledge; it improves cross-domain robustness and generalization in sequential teacher settings.

title: From Reach to Insert: Tactile-Augmented Precision Assembly under Sub-Millimeter Tolerances
summary: High-precision assembly frequently involves tight-tolerance insertions, where even slight pose errors can cause jamming or excessive interaction forces, making robust and safe insertion policies difficult to obtain. This paper proposes a tactile-augmented two-stage method that combines Imitation Learning (IL) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) for precision insertion tasks. In the first stage, IL learns a reaching policy with position generalization that grasps the peg and brings it to the vicinity of the target region. In the second stage, RL executes the insertion and enables recovery from failures during contact-rich interactions. To better exploit tactile feedback, we introduce tactile group sampling to increase coverage of critical contact segments during training, and design a tactile critic to more accurately evaluate policy values, improving insertion performance while maintaining low contact forces. We conduct systematic experiments across five hole geometries and three clearance settings. Results show that our method substantially improves insertion performance across all settings; under the most challenging 0.05\,mm clearance, it achieves a 67\% success rate while keeping contact forces low, reducing the maximum interaction force by 60\% and torque by 44\%, thereby validating both effectiveness and safety for precision assembly.

AI.tldr: A two-stage tactile-augmented framework that uses imitation learning for reaching and reinforcement learning for insertion, enhanced by tactile group sampling and a tactile critic. Demonstrates improved precision insertion across multiple geometries and clearances, notably 0.05 mm clearance with 67% success and substantial reductions in contact forces and torque.
AI.motivation: High-precision assembly with tight-tolerance insertions suffers from jamming and large contact forces due to pose errors. Robust and safe insertion policies require effective use of tactile feedback and learning to handle contact-rich interactions.
AI.method: Stage 1: Imitation Learning to learn a reaching policy with position generalization to grasp the peg and approach the target region. Stage 2: Reinforcement Learning for insertion and recovery from contact failures.
