Enhancements include tactile group sampling to cover critical contact segments during training and a tactile critic to improve value estimation. Experimental evaluation across five hole geometries and three clearance settings.
AI.result: The method substantially improves insertion performance across all tested settings. At 0.05 mm clearance, it achieves 67% success, reduces maximum contact force by 60% and torque by 44%, demonstrating improved safety and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tactile-augmented two-stage approach is effective for precision assembly, leveraging IL for initial positioning and RL with tactile cues for robust, safe insertion. The tactile sampling and critic contribute to better policy learning under contact-rich interactions.

title: Parallel Prefix Verification for Speculative Generation
summary: We introduce PARSE (PArallel pRefix Speculative Engine), a speculative generation framework that accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by parallelizing prefix verification on a semantic level. Existing speculative decoding methods are fundamentally limited by token-level equivalence: the target model must verify each token, leading to short acceptance lengths and modest speedups. Moving to semantic or segment-level verification can substantially increase acceptance granularity, but prior approaches rely on sequential verification, introducing significant overhead and limiting practical gains. PARSE introduces parallel prefix verification, enabling semantic-level verification without sequential checks. Given a full draft from a draft model, the target model evaluates correctness across multiple prefixes in a single forward pass using a custom attention mask, directly identifying the maximal valid prefix. This eliminates sequential segment verification, and makes verification compute-efficient. PARSE is orthogonal to token-level speculative decoding and can be composed with it for additional gains. Across models and benchmarks, PARSE delivers $1.25\times$ to $4.3\times$ throughput gain over the target model, and $1.6\times$ to $4.5\times$ when composed with EAGLE-3, all with negligible accuracy degradation. This demonstrates parallel prefix verification as an effective, general approach to accelerating LLM inference.

AI.tldr: PARSE enables parallel prefix verification to accelerate LLM inference by verifying multiple prefixes in one forward pass, achieving substantial throughput gains with negligible accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: To overcome token-level bottlenecks in speculative decoding by moving to semantic/segment-level verification. Parallel verification promises higher acceptance granularity and speedups without sequential checks.
AI.method: Given a full draft from a draft model, the target model evaluates correctness across multiple prefixes in a single forward pass using a custom attention mask to identify the maximal valid prefix, eliminating sequential segment verification. PARSE is orthogonal to token-level speculative decoding and can be composed with it for additional gains.
AI.result: Throughput gains of 1.25x to 4.3x over the target model, and 1.6x to 4.5x when composed with EAGLE-3, with negligible accuracy degradation.
AI.conclusion: Parallel prefix verification is a general, effective approach for accelerating LLM inference and can be combined with token-level speculative decoding for further speedups.

title: Physics-Guided Regime Unmixing
summary: The Linear Mixing Model (LMM) dominates spectral unmixing for its simplicity, but fails under multiple scattering; existing nonlinear models compensate by applying a fixed regime uniformly across entire scenes. We propose Physics-Guided Regime Unmixing (PGRU), which estimates a pixel-wise scalar $ξ_i \in [0,1]$ from observable physical features to activate nonlinear mixing only where justified. Residuals from the Generalized Bilinear Model (GBM), the Post-Nonlinear Mixing Model (PPNM), and Hapke are combined via learned attention, yielding interpretable regime maps. Experiments on Samson, Jasper Ridge, and Urban show consistent improvements over baselines, with physical coherence $ρ> 0.90$.

AI.tldr: Physics-guided regime unmixing (PGRU) selectively activates nonlinear mixing per pixel using a learned ξ_i, combining GBM, PPNM, and Hapke residuals with attention to create interpretable regime maps and achieve improved accuracy with high physical coherence.
AI.motivation: LMM is simple but fails under multiple scattering; existing nonlinear models apply a fixed nonlinear regime globally, lacking scene-specific adaptation.
AI.method: Estimate per-pixel ξ_i in [0,1] from observable physical features to gate nonlinear mixing; fuse residuals from GBM, PPNM, and Hapke via learned attention to produce regime maps; evaluate on Samson, Jasper Ridge, and Urban datasets.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over baselines; physical coherence ρ > 0.90 on tested scenes.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-wise, physics-guided nonlinear unmixing improves accuracy and interpretability over traditional global nonlinear regimes, with strong cross-scene performance.

title: Lookahead Drifting Model
summary: Recently, a new paradigm named \emph{drifting model} has been proposed for mapping distributions, which achieves the SOTA image generation performance over ImageNet via one-step neural functional evaluation (NFE). The basic idea is to compute a drifting term at each training iteration and then push the output of the model towards the direction of the drifting term. In this paper, we propose a \emph{lookahead drifting model}. At each training iteration, we compute a set of drifting terms sequentially. Each drifting term is calculated by making use of previously computed ones as well as the positive samples and the output of the model. %One key step is to properly scale the drifting terms so that their magnitudes are in a comparable range. In principle, the drifting terms obtained at a later stage capture higher order gradient information towards the positive samples. At each training iteration, the model is optimized by pushing its output towards the direction of the (weighted) summation of the drifting terms. Experimental results on toy examples and CIFAR10 demonstrate the better performance of the new method than the baseline.

AI.tldr: We propose a lookahead drifting model (LDM) that builds on the drifting model by sequentially computing drifting terms and updating the output via a weighted sum, enabling higher-order gradient information usage; results show improved performance over the baseline on toy tasks and CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: Leverage higher-order gradient information and sequential drifting terms to enhance the previously proposed drifting model, which achieved SOTA image generation with one-step neural functional evaluation.
AI.method: At each training iteration, compute a sequence of drifting terms using prior terms, positive samples, and current output; scale terms to comparable magnitudes; update the model by moving its output toward the weighted sum of these drifting terms.
AI.result: Empirical results on toy datasets and CIFAR-10 show better performance than the baseline drifting model.
AI.conclusion: The lookahead drifting model effectively incorporates higher-order gradient information and yields improved training dynamics and performance compared to the original drifting model, as demonstrated on non-ImageNet tasks.

title: AI-Aided Advancements in Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Navigation
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) have become indispensable for deep-sea exploration, spanning critical scientific research and commercial applications. The rapid attenuation of electromagnetic waves renders satellite radio signals unavailable, while the dynamic unpredictability of the marine environment presents formidable navigation challenges. This chapter explores recent advancements in AI-aided AUV positioning, specifically focusing on advanced sensor fusion architectures that integrate inertial navigation systems with Doppler velocity logs and cameras. Beyond traditional model-based filtering, we examine the transformative emergence of AI-driven learning approaches in enhancing inertial dead-reckoning tasks and adaptive fusion algorithms. By addressing these recent milestones, this chapter provides a comprehensive roadmap for achieving the high-precision navigation essential for autonomous underwater missions.

AI.tldr: AI-driven sensor fusion and learning-based navigation enhance AUV positioning when GPS is unavailable, reviewing INS-DVL-camera architectures and adaptive fusion methods, and outlining a roadmap for high-precision underwater navigation.
AI.motivation: GPS/GNSS signals do not reach deep underwater, and the marine environment is highly dynamic, creating stringent navigation requirements for AUVs. AI-enabled sensor fusion and learning-based inertial navigation promise improved robustness and accuracy.
AI.method: A literature-style analysis of sensor fusion architectures integrating inertial navigation systems (INS) with Doppler velocity logs (DVL) and cameras, contrasting traditional model-based filtering with AI-driven learning approaches for inertial dead-reckoning and adaptive fusion.
AI.result: Synthesis of recent milestones in AI-aided AUV positioning, identification of effective AI strategies for inertial dead-reckoning and adaptive fusion, and a comprehensive roadmap for high-precision underwater navigation.
AI.conclusion: The chapter outlines a path toward robust, high-precision AUV navigation in challenging underwater conditions by leveraging AI-enhanced sensor fusion and learning-based dead-reckoning, while noting remaining challenges and future directions.

title: Agent Island: A Saturation- and Contamination-Resistant Benchmark from Multiagent Games
summary: Static capabilities benchmarks suffer from saturation and contamination, making it difficult to track capabilities progress over time. We introduce Agent Island, a multiplayer simulation environment in which language-model agents compete in a game of interagent cooperation, conflict, and persuasion. The environment yields a dynamic benchmark designed to mitigate both saturation and contamination; new models can always outperform the current leading player in this winner-take-all game, and agents compete against other adaptive agents rather than face a fixed task set. We rank players with a Bayesian Plackett-Luce model, allowing us to quantify uncertainty in player skill. In 999 games involving 49 unique models, openai/gpt-5.5 dominates its peers with a posterior mean skill of 5.64, compared with 3.10 for the second-ranked model, openai/gpt-5.2, and 2.86 for the third-ranked model, openai/gpt-5.3-codex. We release the game logs as a dataset for analyses of model behavior. As an example, we investigate same-provider preference in final-round votes and find that models are 8.3 p.p. more likely to support a same-provider finalist than finalists from other providers. This preference is not uniform across providers: among separately estimated providers, the effect is strongest for OpenAI models and weakest for Anthropic models.

AI.tldr: Agent Island is a dynamic, multiplayer benchmark for evaluating language-model agents through inter-agent competition on cooperation, conflict, and persuasion. It uses a Bayesian Plackett-Luce ranking over 999 games with 49 models, showing GPT-5.5 leading, releases game logs for further analysis, and reveals provider-specific same-provider voting biases.
AI.motivation: Static capability benchmarks saturate and contaminate over time, making progress tracking unreliable. A dynamic, competitive environment with adaptive opponents can provide more robust, long-horizon progress signals and quantify uncertainty in agent skill.
AI.method: A multiplayer simulation environment (Agent Island) where LM agents compete in cooperation, conflict, and persuasion. The benchmark is dynamic and winner-take-all, with agents facing adaptive opponents. Skill is quantified using a Bayesian Plackett-Luce model across 999 games involving 49 models. The authors release game logs and analyze same-provider voting biases in final rounds.
AI.result: GPT-5.5 achieves the highest posterior mean skill (5.64), followed by GPT-5.2 (3.10) and GPT-5.3-codex (2.86) among 49 models. The dataset reveals that models are 8.3 percentage points more likely to vote for a same-provider finalist, with provider-specific variation (strongest for OpenAI, weakest for Anthropic).
AI.conclusion: Dynamic benchmarks like Agent Island can mitigate saturation and contamination while enabling uncertainty-aware ranking of adaptive agents. The results suggest OpenAI models currently hold a lead, and reveal cross-provider voting biases that warrant further investigation and mitigation.

title: Imagery Dataset for Remaining Useful Life Estimation of Synthetic Fibre Ropes
summary: Remaining useful life (RUL) estimation of synthetic fibre ropes (SFRs) is critical for safe operation in offshore-crane, wind turbine installation, and heavy-load handling applications, where rope failure can result in catastrophic safety incidents and costly downtime. Despite growing research interest in data-driven condition monitoring, there is no publicly available image dataset that captures the complete degradation lifecycle of SFRs under controlled cyclic fatigue loading. To address this gap, we present a novel image dataset comprising approximately 34,700 high-resolution images of eleven Dyneema SK75/78 high-modulus polyethylene (HMPE) rope samples subjected to cyclic fatigue on a sheave-bend test stand at seven distinct axial load levels ranging from 60 kN to 280 kN. Ropes were loaded until mechanical failure, with fatigue lifetimes ranging from 695 cycles to 8,340 cycles. After every fixed number of sheave cycles (an inspection burst), ten images were captured at different cross-sectional positions along the rope, providing spatially representative sampling of surface degradation throughout the rope's entire service life. The images obtained from each load are annotated with the corresponding elapsed cycle count, enabling a direct computation of RUL for any rope in the sequence. This dataset aims to support a broad range of machine learning (ML) tasks including RUL regression, damage progression modelling, anomaly detection, and load-conditioned prognostics. The dataset is intended to serve as a benchmark resource for the development and comparison of vision-based condition monitoring (CM) and prognostics algorithms for SFRs.

AI.tldr: A publicly available dataset of ~34.7k high-res images of 11 HMPE rope samples under cyclic fatigue, with cycle-annotated RUL and seven load levels, enabling RUL regression, damage progression, anomaly detection, and load-conditioned prognostics; serves as a benchmark for vision-based CM of synthetic fibre ropes.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of publicly accessible image datasets capturing complete degradation lifecycles of synthetic fibre ropes under controlled cyclic fatigue, enabling data-driven prognostics and condition monitoring (CM) research.
AI.method: Experimental fatigue testing on a sheave-bend stand across seven axial loads (60–280 kN); ropes loaded to failure (695–8340 cycles); after every inspection burst, 10 images captured at multiple cross-sections along the rope; images annotated with elapsed cycle counts allowing direct RUL computation; total ~34,700 high-res images across 11 samples and loads.
AI.result: A comprehensive, cycle-annotated image dataset suitable for RUL regression, damage progression modeling, anomaly detection, and load-conditioned prognostics of synthetic fibre ropes; provides a benchmark for vision-based CM methods.
AI.conclusion: This dataset fills a critical gap by enabling standardized evaluation of ML-based CM and prognostics for synthetic fibre ropes, promoting reproducibility and comparability in RUL estimation research.

title: Single-Position Intervention Fails: Distributed Output Templates Drive In-Context Learning
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures. Accepted to the 2026 Learning and Intelligent Optimization Conference and workshops on Foundation Models for Science: Real-World Impact and Science-First Design, Latent & Implicit Thinking - Going Beyond CoT Reasoning, Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models at ICLR 2026
summary: Understanding how large language models encode task identity from few-shot demonstrations is a central open problem in mechanistic interpretability. Prior work uses linear probing to localize task representations, reporting high classification accuracy at specific layers. We reveal a striking dissociation: probing accuracy completely fails to predict causal importance. Single-position activation intervention achieves 0% task transfer across all 28 layers of Llama-3.2-3B-despite 100% probing accuracy at those same positions. This null result is itself a key finding, demonstrating that task encoding is fundamentally distributed. Multi-position intervention-replacing activations at all demonstration output tokens simultaneously-achieves up to 96% transfer (N=50, 95% CI: [87%, 99%]) at layer 8, pinpointing for the first time the causal locus of ICL task identity. We establish the generality of these findings across four models spanning three architecture families (LLaMA, Qwen, Gemma), discovering a universal intervention window at ~30% network depth. Causal tracing uncovers an asymmetric architecture: the query position is strictly necessary (53-100% disruption) while no individual demonstration position is necessary (0% disruption)-resolving a key ambiguity in prior accounts. Crucially, transfer depends on internal representation compatibility, not surface similarity (r=-0.05 vs r=0.31), ruling out trivial explanations. These results establish the distributed template hypothesis: ICL task identity is encoded as output format templates distributed across demonstration tokens, fundamentally reshaping our understanding of how in-context learning operates.

AI.tldr: ICL task identity is encoded in a distributed, template-like manner across demonstration tokens; single-token activations are causally irrelevant for task transfer, while simultaneous multi-token interventions reveal a strong, layer- and model-general causal locus; results challenge probing-centric views and support a universal intervention window near 30% depth across architectures.
AI.motivation: Resolve how task identity from few-shot demonstrations is encoded in LLMs, and assess whether high probing accuracy reflects causal usefulness or mere correlational structure.
AI.method: Layer-wise causal interventions: (1) single-position activation interventions to test causal impact of individual tokens; (2) multi-position interventions replacing all demonstration-output-token activations simultaneously to measure aggregate transfer; evaluated across 28 layers in Llama-3.2-3B-des and across four models spanning three architectures (LLaMA, Qwen, Gemma); measured ICL task transfer and perturbation effects; identified causal locus and a universal intervention window (~30% depth).
AI.result: Single-position activations produce 0% task transfer across all 28 layers despite high probing accuracy at those positions. Multi-position interventions across all demonstration output tokens achieve up to 96% task transfer (N=50, 95% CI: [87%, 99%]) at layer 8, identifying the causal locus of ICL task identity. The query position is strictly necessary (53–100% disruption) while individual demonstration positions are not (0% disruption). Transfer depends on internal representation compatibility, not surface similarity. The findings generalize across four models and three architectures, with a universal intervention window around 30% depth.
AI.conclusion: Task identity in ICL is encoded as output-format templates distributed across demonstration tokens, supporting the distributed template hypothesis. Probing alone is insufficient for causal insight; causal effects arise only when multiple token activations are perturbed together, and the query position holds unique causal importance for task transfer.

title: From Pixels to Tokens: A Systematic Study of Latent Action Supervision for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Latent actions serve as an intermediate representation that enables consistent modeling of vision-language-action (VLA) models across heterogeneous datasets. However, approaches to supervising VLAs with latent actions are fragmented and lack a systematic comparison. This work structures the study of latent action supervision from two perspectives: (i) regularizing the trajectory via image-based latent actions, and (ii) unifying the target space with action-based latent actions. Under a unified VLA baseline, we instantiate and compare four representative integration strategies. Our results reveal a formulation-task correspondence: image-based latent actions benefit long-horizon reasoning and scene-level generalization, whereas action-based latent actions excel at complex motor coordination. Furthermore, we find that directly supervising the VLM with discrete latent action tokens yields the most effective performance. Finally, our experiments offer initial insights into the benefits of latent action supervision in mixed-data, suggesting a promising direction for VLA training. Code is available at https://github.com/RUCKBReasoning/From_Pixels_to_Tokens.

AI.tldr: Latent actions provide a unified intermediate representation for VLA models across datasets; four integration strategies are evaluated within a unified baseline; supervising with discrete latent action tokens yields the best performance; image-based latent actions aid long-horizon reasoning and scene-level generalization while action-based latent actions aid complex motor coordination; initial positive effects in mixed-data settings.
AI.motivation: Systematically compare and unify latent-action supervision for vision-language-action (VLA) models, addressing fragmentation in prior work by examining regularization via image-based latent actions and unification of the target space with action-based latent actions across heterogeneous datasets.
AI.method: Under a unified VLA baseline, instantiate and compare four representative integration strategies that couple latent actions to VLA training. Evaluate two axes: (i) regularizing trajectories with image-based latent actions, and (ii) unifying the target space with action-based latent actions. Assess performance across long-horizon reasoning, scene-level generalization, and motor coordination, with direct supervision of the VLM using discrete latent action tokens. Code provided.
AI.result: image-based latent actions improve long-horizon reasoning and scene-level generalization; action-based latent actions excel at managing complex motor coordination; supervising the VLM with discrete latent action tokens yields the strongest performance; latent-action supervision also shows benefits in mixed-data settings.
AI.conclusion: Latent-action supervision is a promising direction for VLA training. Discrete latent action tokens are particularly effective. The work offers initial empirical guidance on across-dataset efficacy and sets the stage for broader adoption, with available code for reproduction.

title: The Scaling Properties of Implicit Deductive Reasoning in Transformers
summary: We investigate the scaling properties of implicit deductive reasoning over Horn clauses in depth-bounded Transformers. By systematically decorrelating provability from spurious features and enforcing algorithmic alignment, we find that in sufficiently deep models with a bidirectional prefix mask, implicit reasoning approaches explicit CoT performance across graph topologies and problem widths, though CoT remains necessary for depth extrapolation.

AI.tldr: Implicit deductive reasoning in deep depth-bounded Transformers with a bidirectional prefix mask can match explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) performance across graph topologies and problem widths, but CoT is still needed for reliable depth extrapolation.
AI.motivation: To understand how scalable implicit deductive reasoning is in neural models that operate on Horn clauses, and to identify architectural and data-covariate conditions under which implicit reasoning approaches explicit CoT.
AI.method: Systematically decorrelate provability from spurious features; enforce algorithmic alignment; evaluate in deep depth-bounded Transformer models with a bidirectional prefix mask across multiple graph topologies and problem widths; compare implicit reasoning to explicit CoT and assess depth extrapolation.
AI.result: In sufficiently deep models with a bidirectional prefix mask, implicit reasoning approaches explicit CoT performance across graph topologies and problem widths.
AI.conclusion: Implicit reasoning can match explicit CoT under certain architectural and decorrelation conditions, but CoT remains necessary for extrapolating to greater depths; decorrelation and algorithmic alignment are key to achieving this parity.

title: Beyond Fixed Thresholds and Domain-Specific Benchmarks for Explainable Multi-Task Classification in Autonomous Vehicles
summary: Scene understanding is a vital part of autonomous driving systems, which requires the use of deep learning models. Deep learning methods are intrinsically black box models, which lack transparency and safety in autonomous driving. To make these systems transparent, multi-task visual understanding has become crucial for explainable autonomous driving perception systems, where simultaneous prediction of multiple driving behaviors and their underlying explanations is essential for safe navigation and human trust in autonomous vehicles. In order to design an accurate and cross-cultural explainable autonomous driving system, we introduce a comprehensive confidence threshold sensitivity analysis that evaluates various threshold values to identify optimal decision boundaries for different tasks. Our analysis demonstrates that traditional fixed threshold approaches are suboptimal for multi-task scenarios. Through extensive evaluation, we demonstrate that our adaptive threshold selection methodology improves F1-scores across different tasks. In addition, we introduce IUST-XAI-AD, a novel dataset consisting of 958 images with human annotations for driving decisions and corresponding reasoning. This dataset addresses the critical gap in domain-specific evaluation benchmarks for distinct driving contexts and provides a more challenging test environment compared to existing datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that confidence threshold sensitivity analysis can significantly improve model performance, while the introduction of the IUST-XAI-AD dataset reveals important insights about cross-cultural driving behavior patterns. The combined contributions of this work provide both methodological advances and practical evaluation tools that can accelerate the development of more reliable, explainable, and culturally-adaptive autonomous driving systems for global deployment.

AI.tldr: Adaptive confidence threshold analysis for multi-task explainable autonomous driving; introduces IUST-XAI-AD dataset; shows adaptive thresholds improve F1; cross-cultural insights.
AI.motivation: Address transparency and safety gaps in deep learning-based autonomous driving by enabling explainable, multi-task perception and robust decision boundaries. Fixed thresholds are suboptimal for multi-task settings and cross-cultural contexts.
AI.method: Perform a confidence-threshold sensitivity analysis across multiple driving tasks to identify adaptive decision boundaries. Propose an adaptive threshold selection mechanism. Evaluate on a new dataset, IUST-XAI-AD, with 958 images and human annotations for driving decisions and associated reasoning, to compare against fixed-threshold baselines and assess cross-cultural patterns.
AI.result: Adaptive thresholding yields higher F1-scores across tasks and outperforms fixed thresholds. The IUST-XAI-AD dataset reveals cross-cultural driving behavior patterns and provides a challenging benchmark for domain-specific evaluation in Explainable Autonomous Driving (XAI-AD).
AI.conclusion: Combines an adaptive thresholding methodology with a new, culturally diverse dataset to advance reliable, explainable, and globally deployable autonomous driving systems.

title: EdgeRazor: A Lightweight Framework for Large Language Models via Mixed-Precision Quantization-Aware Distillation
summary: Recent years have witnessed an increasing interest in deploying LLMs on resource-constrained devices, among which quantization has emerged as a promising lightweight technique that converts full-precision model weights and activations into lower-bit formats. Existing weight quantization approaches can be roughly divided into three categories: Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) that calibrates quantized parameters on a small dataset without retraining but suffers from severe performance degradation below 4-bit, Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) that searches low-bit parameters using surrogate gradients but demands substantial computational resources, and Quantization-Aware Distillation that integrates QAT with knowledge transfer from a full-precision teacher but manually selects features to distill and relies heavily on teacher-specific data. In this paper, we propose EdgeRazor, a lightweight framework for LLMs with mixed-precision and extremely low-bit weight quantization. The EdgeRazor framework contains three modules: Mixed-Precision Quantization-Aware Distillation for the fine-grained control of precision, Adaptive Feature Distillation that derives an $n$-bit student from its 16-bit teacher, and Entropy-Aware KL Divergence on both human-annotated and distilled datasets, whose forward-reverse balance is determined solely by the teacher's output distribution. Empirical investigations of EdgeRazor are conducted on base, instruction-tuned, and multimodal LLMs. Notably, EdgeRazor with 1.88-bit surpasses all contenders with the 3-bit precision, especially outperforms the leading 2-bit PTQ methods by 11.3 points, within a 4-10$\times$ lower training budget than the leading QAT approach. EdgeRazor delivers higher compression ratios at all bit width; the 1.58-bit Qwen3-0.6B reduces storage from 1.41 GB to 0.28 GB while accelerating decoding by 15.1$\times$ relative to the 16-bit baseline.

AI.tldr: EdgeRazor is a lightweight mixed-precision, extremely low-bit weight quantization framework for LLMs that integrates mixed-precision quantization-aware distillation, adaptive feature distillation, and entropy-aware KL divergence. It achieves ultra-low bit storage (as low as ~1.88 bits) and strong accuracy, outperforming 2-bit PTQ and with 4–10× lower training budget, while delivering substantial storage reduction and decoding speedups (e.g., 15×).
AI.motivation: Enable deployment of large language models on resource-constrained devices by reducing memory and compute. Existing PTQ struggles below 4-bit, QAT is resource-intensive, and quantization-aware distillation often relies on hand-picked features and teacher data. A lightweight, mixed-precision, distillation-based approach targeted at extreme low-bit regimes is needed.
AI.method: Proposes EdgeRazor with three modules: (1) Mixed-Precision Quantization-Aware Distillation for fine-grained precision control; (2) Adaptive Feature Distillation to derive an n-bit student from a 16-bit teacher; (3) Entropy-Aware KL Divergence on both human-annotated and distilled data, with forward-reverse balance determined solely by the teacher’s output distribution. Evaluation across base, instruction-tuned, and multimodal LLMs.
AI.result: Empirical results show EdgeRazor achieving 1.88-bit performance that surpasses all 3-bit baselines, and outperforms leading 2-bit PTQ methods by 11.3 points. It requires 4–10× less training budget than leading QAT methods. It delivers higher compression at all bit widths; e.g., 1.58-bit Qwen3-0.6B compressed from 1.41 GB to 0.28 GB and decoding accelerated by 15.1× compared with the 16-bit baseline.
AI.conclusion: EdgeRazor demonstrates that combining mixed-precision quantization with distillation and entropy-aware training enables highly efficient, ultra-low-bit LLM deployment with strong accuracy across model types, suggesting a promising direction for resource-constrained AI.

title: 3D Printing of Passively Actuated Self-Folding Robots with Integrated Functional Modules
comment: 8pages, 10 figures, This paper is accepted in ICRA 2026
summary: We introduce an elastic-driven self-folding approach that fabricates robots directly from flat 3D-printed conductive PLA nets. Elastic bands routed through printed hooks store energy that folds the sheet into programmed 3D geometries, while the flat state allows accurate placement of electronics and magnets before deployment. The same substrate doubles as electrodes for capacitive touch and supports a reusable platform I/O palette with Hall sensors and eccentric rotating mass (ERM) motors for docking detection and vibration actuation. We also derive a closed-form folding model that balances hinge stiffness with elastic band moment to predict equilibrium fold angles; experiments validate the model and yield a design map linking hinge thickness, band size, and hook spacing to target angles. Using this workflow we realize multiple polyhedral modules and demonstrate three applications: a cube that highlights the potential of self-folding for scalable modular robot collectives, a deployable gripper, and a tendon-driven finger. The method is low cost, stimulus-free, and integrates actuation and sensing.

AI.tldr: Elastic-driven self-folding from flat conductive PLA nets using routed elastic bands that actuate hinges to form 3D modules; includes a closed-form folding model and a design map; demonstrates cube, deployable gripper, and tendon-driven finger with integrated sensing/actuation.
AI.motivation: Enable low-cost, stimulus-free fabrication of modular robots with integrated sensing and actuation by folding flat printed nets, while allowing precise electronics/magnet placement before deployment.
AI.method: Embed elastic bands through printed hooks to store energy that folds the sheet into programmed 3D geometries; use flat state for electronics and magnets placement; substrate doubles as capacitive touch electrodes and supports an I/O palette with Hall sensors and ERM motors for docking detection and vibration actuation; derive a closed-form folding model balancing hinge stiffness with elastic band moment to predict equilibrium fold angles; validate experimentally and produce a design map linking hinge thickness, band size, and hook spacing to target angles; realize multiple polyhedral modules.
AI.result: Model validation and a design map; realization of multiple polyhedral modules including a cube, a deployable gripper, and a tendon-driven finger; shows integration of actuation and sensing in a low-cost, stimulus-free workflow.
AI.conclusion: The workflow enables scalable, low-cost self-folding fabrication of modular robots with integrated actuation and sensing; the folding-model-guided design map supports reliable prediction of fold angles and parameter choices for diverse polyhedral modules.

title: When Context Hurts: The Crossover Effect of Knowledge Transfer on Multi-Agent Design Exploration
comment: 16 pages, 14 tables. 2,700 multi-agent experiments across 10 software design tasks, 7 artifact conditions, and 4 convergence pressure levels
summary: The prevailing assumption in agent orchestration is that more context is better. We test this on multi-agent software design across 10 tasks, 7 context-injection conditions, and over 2,700 runs, and find a crossover effect: the same artifact type improves design exploration on some tasks (up to 20$\times$ tradeoff coverage) and actively degrades it on others (up to 46% reduction). On several tasks, an irrelevant document performs as well as or better than every relevant artifact. The direction is predicted by a single measurable variable--baseline exploration without context--with Pearson $r = -0.82$ ($p < 0.001$). Probing the mechanism by manipulating convergence pressure through prompt design reveals two distinct regimes: convergence driven by training data priors (natural) responds to artifact disruption, while convergence driven by explicit instructions (induced) does not. The implication is that context injection should be conditional, not universal: one no-context trial is a cheap diagnostic that predicts whether knowledge artifacts will help or hurt a given task.

AI.tldr: Context injection in multi-agent design yields mixed effects: some tasks gain up to 20x tradeoff coverage, others suffer up to 46% degradation. A baseline exploration metric predicts the direction of artifact impact (r = -0.82). Two convergence regimes (natural vs induced) respond differently to artifacts, implying context should be conditional; a single no-context trial can diagnose artifact usefulness.
AI.motivation: Test the assumption that more context is always beneficial in multi-agent design across diverse tasks, and identify predictors of when context helps or hurts.
AI.method: Empirical study over 10 tasks, 7 context-injection conditions, and ~2,700 runs. Measure design exploration and tradeoff coverage. Use Pearson correlation with baseline exploration (no-context) to predict artifact effects. Manipulate convergence pressure via prompt design to induce natural (data-prior driven) vs induced (instruction-driven) convergence to probe mechanisms.
AI.result: Crossover effect: context can both improve (up to 20×) and degrade (up to 46%) design exploration depending on task. Irrelevant documents can match or outperform relevant artifacts on several tasks. Baseline exploration without context strongly predicts the direction of artifact impact (r = -0.82, p < 0.001). Two regimes emerge: natural convergence is sensitive to artifact disruption, while induced convergence is robust to artifacts.
AI.conclusion: Context injection should be conditional rather than universal. A single no-context trial serves as a cheap diagnostic to forecast whether knowledge artifacts will help or hurt a given task; practitioners should adapt context strategies to task characteristics and convergence regime.

title: Hierarchical Visual Agent: Managing Contexts in Joint Image-Text Space for Advanced Chart Reasoning
summary: Advanced chart question answering requires both precise perception of small visual elements and multi-step reasoning across several subplots. While existing MLLMs are strong at understanding single plots, they often struggle with multi-step reasoning across multiple subplots. We propose HierVA, a hierarchical visual agent framework for chart reasoning that iteratively constructs and updates a working context in a joint image--text space. A high-level manager generates plans and maintains a compact context containing only key information, while specialized workers perform reasoning, gather evidence, and return results. In particular, the agent maintains separate visual and textual contexts, using a zoom-in tool to restrict the visual context. Experiments on the CharXiv reasoning subset demonstrate consistent improvements over strong multimodal baselines, and ablation studies verify that hierarchical architecture, scoped visual context, and distilled context contribute complementary gains.

AI.tldr: HierVA is a hierarchical visual agent for chart reasoning that maintains separate visual and textual contexts and uses a high-level planner to iteratively plan, gather evidence, and reason across multiple subplots, achieving improvements on CharXiv reasoning tasks; ablations show the architecture, scoped visual context, and distilled context each contribute.
AI.motivation: The task of advanced chart question answering requires precise perception of small visual cues and multi-step cross-subplot reasoning, which current multimodal LMs struggle to perform consistently across multiple plots.
AI.method: HierVA introduces a hierarchical framework with a high-level manager that plans and keeps a compact working context, and specialized workers that perform reasoning and evidence gathering. It maintains separate visual and textual contexts and uses a zoom-in tool to limit the visual context, operating in a joint image-text space for iterative updates.
AI.result: On the CharXiv reasoning subset, HierVA shows consistent improvements over strong multimodal baselines. Ablation studies indicate that the hierarchical architecture, scoped visual context, and distilled context provide complementary gains.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical, context-distilled approach with scoped visual perception enables effective multi-step reasoning across subplots in chart QA, addressing the limitations of single-plot focused multimodal models.

title: Investigating Trustworthiness of Nonparametric Deep Survival Models for Alzheimer's Disease Progression Analysis
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables, IEEE/ACM Conference on Connected Health: Applications, Systems, and Engineering Technologies
summary: Alzheimer's Dementia (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease marked by irreversible decline, making reliable modeling of its progression essential for effective patient care. Progression-aware methods such as survival analysis are therefore crucial tools for the early detection and monitoring of AD. Recent advancements in deep learning have demonstrated remarkable performance in survival tasks, but alarmingly fewer studies have been conducted in the domain of AD. Further, the studies that do exist do not consider learned bias within the model itself, which could result in unfair and unreliable predictions toward certain marginalized groups. As such, we conduct a rigorous study of fairness in AD progression analysis along with a thorough feature importance study to determine the characteristics which are most important for reliable AD predictions. Furthermore, we propose two novel fairness metrics, called Time-Dependent Concordance Impurity and Kaplan-Meier Fairness, to quantify bias with respect to sensitive attributes such as sex, race, and education in nonparametric survival models. Our study demonstrates that while deep learning powered survival models are robust tools which can aid clinicians in AD care decisions, they often exhibit considerable bias, representing important avenues for future research.

AI.tldr: Explores fairness in Alzheimer's progression modeling using survival analysis, introducing two novel fairness metrics. Finds deep learning survival models can be biased against certain groups, underscoring the need for fairness-aware modeling and feature-importance analysis.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer's disease progression is critical to model for early detection and patient care. Survival analysis is a natural approach, but existing studies largely ignore model bias, risking unreliable predictions for marginalized groups.
AI.method: Conducts a fairness analysis in AD progression with a thorough feature-importance study. Introduces two metrics—Time-Dependent Concordance Impurity and Kaplan-Meier Fairness—for assessing bias in nonparametric survival models with respect to sensitive attributes (sex, race, education). Empirically evaluates biases in deep learning-powered survival models.
AI.result: Demonstrates that deep learning survival models, though robust for clinical decisions, exhibit considerable bias across sensitive attributes. The proposed metrics reveal these biases and highlight limitations in current approaches.
AI.conclusion: Fairness-aware survival modeling is essential in AD progression analysis. The paper provides new metrics to quantify bias and calls for future work to develop bias-mitigation methods to ensure reliable, equitable predictions across diverse patient groups.

title: Dr-PoGO: Direct Radar Pose-Graph Optimization
comment: Accepted for presentation at ICRA 2026 Cite as @inproceedings{legentil2026drpogo, title={Dr-PoGO: Direct Radar Pose-Graph Optimization}, author={{Le Gentil}, Cedric and Weican, Li and Brizi, Leonardo and Barfoot, Timothy D.}, booktitle={IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)}, year={2026} }
summary: This paper introduces Dr-PoGO, a method for Simultaneous Localization And Mapping (SLAM) using a 2D spinning radar. Unlike cameras or lidars that require line-of-sight, millimetre-wave radars can `see' through dust, falling snow, rain, etc. Accordingly, it is a great modality for robust perception regardless of the weather conditions. While most existing radar-based SLAM methods rely on the extraction of point clouds or features to perform ego-motion estimation, Dr-PoGO leverages direct registration techniques for odometry (DRO) and loop-closure registration. An off-the-shelf radar-focused place recognition algorithm, RaPlace, provides loop-closure candidates. As RaPlace does not provide relative transformations, Dr-PoGO introduces a coarse-to-fine registration that uses visual features and descriptors to obtain an initial guess for the direct transformation refinement. The global trajectory is optimized in a pose-graph optimization. Dr-PoGO demonstrates state-of-the-art performance over 300km of data in various real-world automotive environments. Our implementation is publicly available: https://github.com/utiasASRL/dr_pogo.

AI.tldr: Dr-PoGO is a radar-based SLAM system using direct registration (DRO) for odometry and loop closure, with coarse-to-fine initialization via visual features and RaPlace loop-candidates; achieves state-of-the-art on 300 km of automotive data; open-source.
AI.motivation: Robust ego-motion estimation under adverse weather where cameras/LiDAR fail; radar can operate through dust, rain, snow; existing radar-SLAM relies on feature/point-cloud methods, which may be less robust; propose direct registration to improve accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: 2D spinning radar; uses direct registration for odometry and loop-closure; RaPlace for loop-closure candidates; since RaPlace lacks relative transforms, apply coarse-to-fine registration with visual features to estimate initial transformation; refine with direct registration. Global trajectory optimized with pose-graph.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across 300 km of real-world automotive data; code released at github.com/utiasASRL/dr_pogo.
AI.conclusion: Shows effectiveness of direct registration in radar-based SLAM and robust performance in challenging weather; potential for further improvement and broader deployment; open-source availability fosters reproducibility.

title: Deployment-Relevant Alignment Cannot Be Inferred from Model-Level Evaluation Alone
summary: Alignment evaluation in machine learning has largely become evaluation of models. Influential benchmarks score model outputs under fixed inputs, such as truthfulness, instruction following, or pairwise preference, and these scores are often used to support claims about deployed alignment. This paper argues that deployment-relevant alignment cannot be inferred from model-level evaluation alone. Alignment claims should instead be indexed to the level at which evidence is collected: model-level, response-level, interaction-level, or deployment-level. Two studies support this position. First, a structured audit of eleven alignment benchmarks, extended to a sixteen-benchmark corpus, dual-coded against an eight-dimension rubric with Cohen's kappa = 0.87, finds that user-facing verification support is absent across every benchmark examined, while process steerability is nearly absent. The few interactional benchmarks identified, including tau-bench, CURATe, Rifts, and Common Ground, remain fragmented in coverage, and benchmark construction rather than data source determines what is measured. Second, a blinded cross-model stress test using 180 transcripts across three frontier models and four scaffolds finds that the same verification scaffold raises one model's verification support to ceiling while leaving another categorically unchanged. This shows that scaffold efficacy is model-dependent and that the gap identified by the audit cannot be closed at the model level alone. We propose a system-level evaluation agenda: alignment profiles instead of single scores, fixed-scaffolding protocols for comparable interactional evaluation, and reporting templates that make the inferential distance between evaluation evidence and deployment claims explicit.

AI.tldr: Alignment evaluation should be system-level rather than model-level; current benchmarks fix inputs and fail to measure deployment-relevant verification; evidence shows scaffold and model-dependency; propose system-level evaluation with alignment profiles, fixed scaffolding, and explicit reporting.
AI.motivation: Deployment-relevant alignment cannot be inferred from model-level evaluations alone; evidence must be gathered at multiple levels (model, response, interaction, deployment) to support real-world claims.
AI.method: Study 1: audit of 11 benchmarks (expanded to 16) using an 8-dimension rubric; Cohen's kappa 0.87 for inter-coder reliability. Assessed user-facing verification and process steerability; analyzed coverage of interactional benchmarks (tau-bench, CURATE, Rifts, Common Ground). Study 2: blinded cross-model stress test with 180 transcripts across 3 frontier models and 4 scaffolds to test scaffold effects on verification across models.
AI.result: Benchmarks largely lack user-facing verification and process steerability; interactional benchmarks are fragmented and construction-driven; scaffold effects are model-dependent; cannot deduce deployment alignment from model-level scores; evidence supports the need for system-level evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Propose a system-level evaluation agenda: use alignment profiles instead of single scores, employ fixed scaffolding for comparable interaction-level evaluation, and provide reporting templates that make the gap between evidence and deployment claims explicit.

title: InterFuserDVS: Event-Enhanced Sensor Fusion for Safe RL-Based Decision Making
summary: Autonomous driving systems rely heavily on robust sensor fusion to perceive complex envi- ronments. Traditional setups using RGB cameras and LiDAR often struggle in high-dynamic- range scenes or high-speed scenarios due to motion blur and latency. Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS), or event cameras, offer a paradigm shift by capturing asynchronous brightness changes with microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range. In this paper, we propose an extended architecture of the state-of-the-art InterFuser model, integrating DVS as an additional modality to enhance perception reliability. We introduce a novel token-based fusion strategy that incorporates accumulated event frames into the transformer-based backbone of InterFuser. Our method leverages the complementary nature of RGB, LiDAR, and DVS data. We evaluate our approach on the Car Learning to Act (CARLA) Leaderboard benchmarks, demonstrating that the inclusion of DVS improves the robustness of the driving agent, achieving a competitive Driving Score of 77.2 and a superior Route Completion of 100%. The results indicate that event-based vision is a promising direction for improving safety and performance in adverse lighting and dynamic conditions.

AI.tldr: Extends InterFuser with Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) data to fuse asynchronous event streams with RGB and LiDAR via a token-based fusion in a transformer backbone, improving robustness in high-dynamic-range and high-speed scenarios.
AI.motivation: RGB and LiDAR struggle in high-dynamic-range and fast scenes due to motion blur and latency; DVS offers microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range to enhance perception reliability.
AI.method: Introduce DVS as an additional modality in the InterFuser framework and propose a token-based fusion strategy that incorporates accumulated event frames into the transformer-based backbone, enabling fused RGB, LiDAR, and DVS data.
AI.result: On CARLA Leaderboard benchmarks, the method shows improved robustness, achieving a Driving Score of 77.2 and Route Completion of 100%, indicating that event-based vision enhances safety and performance in adverse lighting and dynamic conditions.
AI.conclusion: Event-based vision with DVS is a promising direction for robust autonomous driving perception, effectively complementing RGB and LiDAR to handle challenging scenes.

title: Improving Medical VQA through Trajectory-Aware Process Supervision
summary:
Reasoning capabilities are crucial for reliable medical visual question answering (VQA); however, existing datasets rarely include reasoning explanations.
  We address this by generating reasoning trajectories for six medical VQA benchmarks using the COMCTS algorithm with open-source vision-language models, with an LLM serving as the verification judge.
  Building on these generated datasets, we propose a two-stage training framework: supervised fine-tuning followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel process-based reward.
  While standard approaches rely solely on exact-match rewards for final answers, we introduce a trajectory-aware reward that measures the similarity between generated and ground-truth reasoning processes.
  Specifically, we embed reasoning steps using sentence transformers and compute the Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) distance between the resulting vector sequences.
  Experiments across six benchmarks demonstrate that combining the DTW-based process reward with exact-match reward consistently outperforms SFT-only training, raising mean accuracy from 0.598 to 0.689, mean BERTScore from 0.845 to 0.881, and mean ROUGE-L from 0.665 to 0.748.
  Our results highlight the importance of process supervision in training reasoning-capable medical VLMs.
  We make our code and generated reasoning datasets publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MICCAI-R1-MED-VQA-code-B14B/

AI.tldr: Generate reasoning trajectories for six medical VQA benchmarks using COMCTS with open-source VLMs and LLM verifier; two-stage training (SFT + GRPO) with a DTW-based process reward; results show improved accuracy and text-based metrics; code and datasets released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of reasoning explanations in medical VQA by creating trajectory-level supervision to improve reasoning capabilities and reliability of medical VLMs.
AI.method: Generate reasoning trajectories using COMCTS with open-source VLMs; LLM acts as verifier. Train models in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using a process-based reward. Reward combines exact-match (final answer) with a trajectory-aware DTW-based similarity between generated and ground-truth reasoning steps, computed on sentence-embedded sequences.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks, combining DTW-based process reward with exact-match reward improves over SFT alone: mean accuracy 0.598→0.689, mean BERTScore 0.845→0.881, mean ROUGE-L 0.665→0.748.
AI.conclusion: Process supervision via trajectory-aware rewards enhances reasoning-capable medical VLMs; authors provide code and datasets for reproducibility.

title: Optimal Uncertainty-Aware Calibration for the AX=YB Problem
comment: 23 pages, 26 figures, under review in IJRR
summary: This article proposes a general optimization framework for solving hand-eye calibration problem. Unlike traditional methods, an iterative algorithm based on Lie algebra that achieves approximately global optimal solutions is developed. During the optimization process, the method strictly preserves the structural constraints of the calibration parameters and enables synchronized updates between calibration parameters. Recognizing that data used in real-word hand-eye calibration often contain uncertainty, especially in over-loading and large workspace industrial robot scenarios, which can significantly degrade accuracy, and accurately modeling such uncertainty is inherently difficult, this article avoids explicit uncertainty modeling. Instead, an uncertainty metric to evaluate the relative uncertainty between data sources is introduced and used to dynamically refine the iterative process. To further enhance convergence efficiency, an effective initial solution generation method that improves overall stability and accuracy is designed. Numerical simulations and real-world experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, and in synthetic datasets, the proposed approach improves the estimation accuracy by at least 67\% under high-uncertainty conditions compared with the existing methods.

AI.tldr: An iterative Lie-algebra based hand-eye calibration framework that preserves parameter structure, uses an uncertainty metric to guide refinement without explicit stochastic modeling, and improves robustness via a strong initial guess; demonstrates substantial accuracy gains (≥67%) in synthetic high-uncertainty tests and real experiments.
AI.motivation: Hand-eye calibration under significant data uncertainty and large workspaces; traditional methods may be sensitive to noise and rely on uncertain modeling; need efficient, near-global solutions with preserved constraints.
AI.method: Iterative optimization on Lie group representing calibration parameters; maintain structural constraints; synchronized updates; introduce relative uncertainty metric to adapt iteration order/step-size; avoid explicit uncertainty modeling; design effective initial estimate to boost stability and convergence.
AI.result: Numerical simulations and real-world experiments validate; synthetic data show ≥67% improvement in estimation accuracy under high uncertainty compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Lie-algebra based iterative framework provides robust, constrained hand-eye calibration with improved convergence and accuracy under uncertainty without explicit stochastic modeling.

title: How Does Thinking Mode Change LLM Moral Judgments? A Controlled Instant-vs-Thinking Comparison Across Five Frontier Models
summary: We evaluate whether enabling provider-exposed reasoning mode changes moral judgments within the same model checkpoint. Across 100 moral-judgment scenarios and five frontier reasoning-trained LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3 Flash, DeepSeek V3.1, and Qwen3.5 397B), aggregate binary-verdict agreement remains high and statistically indistinguishable between instant and thinking modes (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.78 vs. 0.79). However, disagreement is concentrated in 21 model-disputed scenarios, where instant-mode agreement is near chance (alpha = 0.08). On these scenarios, reasoning directionally narrows cross-model disagreement, increasing mean pairwise agreement from 5.4 to 6.7 out of 10. Reasoning also reduces demographic-judgment inconsistency in three of five models and does not increase it for any model. Across all five model families, reasoning changes self-labeled ethical frameworks more often than binary verdicts.

AI.tldr: Exposure to provider-exposed reasoning modes in frontier LLMs largely preserves binary moral verdicts across a broad set of scenarios, but reveals disagreement in contested cases. Reasoning narrows cross-model disagreement in disputed scenarios and can shift self-labeled ethical frameworks more often than binary verdicts, while reducing demographic-judgment inconsistency in a subset of models.
AI.motivation: To determine whether making an LLM's internal reasoning visible (provider-exposed reasoning) alters moral judgments and cross-model alignment within the same checkpoint, particularly across diverse frontier models.
AI.method: Evaluate 100 moral-judgment scenarios across five frontier reasoning-trained LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT 5.5, Gemini 3 Flash, DeepSeek V3.1, Qwen3.5 397B). Compare instant vs thinking modes using aggregated binary verdicts; compute Krippendorff's alpha for agreement; identify 21 model-disputed scenarios with near-chance instant-mode agreement; analyze mean pairwise agreement (0–10 scale), demographic-judgment inconsistency (three of five models showing reduction), and frequency of shifts in self-labeled ethical frameworks vs binary verdicts across model families.
AI.result: Binary verdict agreement remains high and statistically similar between instant (alpha ~0.78) and thinking (alpha ~0.79) modes across most scenarios. In 21 disputed scenarios, instant-mode agreement drops near chance (alpha ~0.08); reasoning directionally improves cross-model agreement, increasing mean pairwise agreement from 5.4 to 6.7/10. Reasoning reduces demographic-judgment inconsistency in 3/5 models without increasing it in any. Across all models, reasoning changes self-labeled ethical frameworks more often than it alters binary verdicts.
AI.conclusion: Exposing reasoning does not overhaul overall binary moral judgments but can meaningfully influence alignment in contentious cases and prompts, especially by harmonizing across models and shifting underlying ethical framings. This highlights both interpretability benefits and potential risks in deploying provider-exposed reasoning, particularly regarding framework stability and demographic biases.

title: Intermediate Representations are Strong AI-Generated Image Detectors
summary: The rapid advancement in generative AI models has enabled the creation of photorealistic images. At the same time, there are growing concerns about the potential misuse and dangers of generated content, as well as a pressing need for effective AI-generated image detectors. However, current training-based detection techniques are typically computationally costly and can hardly be generalized to unseen data domains, while training-free methods fall short in detection performance. To bridge this gap, we propose a search-based method employing data embedding sensitivity in intermediate layers to detect AI-generated images. Given a set of real and AI-generated images, our method examines the similarity between original image embeddings and perturbed image embeddings, and detects AI-generated images based on the similarity. We examine the proposed method on two comprehensive benchmarks: GenImage and Forensics Small. Our method exhibits improved performance across different datasets compared to both training-free and training-based state-of-the-art methods. On average, our method achieves the largest performance gain on the Forensics Small benchmark by 39.61% compared to the best training-free method and 5.14% compared to the best training-based method in AUROC score.

AI.tldr: A search-based detector uses data embedding sensitivity in intermediate layers to distinguish real vs AI-generated images, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC on GenImage and Forensics Small, with notable gains on Forensics Small.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of training-based detectors (high computational cost and poor generalization) and training-free methods (lower accuracy) by proposing a hybrid, search-based approach that exploits perturbation-induced changes in embeddings.
AI.method: Given real and AI-generated images, extract embeddings from intermediate layers, perturb embeddings (via image perturbations or internal perturbations), and measure similarity between original and perturbed embeddings. A search procedure optimizes perturbations to maximize separability between real and generated images. Evaluation on GenImage and Forensics Small, comparing against state-of-the-art training-free and training-based detectors.
AI.result: The method shows improved performance across datasets relative to both training-free and training-based SOTA methods. On Forensics Small, it achieves the largest average AUROC gain: 39.61% over the best training-free method and 5.14% over the best training-based method.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively bridges the gap between training-based and training-free detectors, offering improved generalization and performance across benchmarks. Potential dependencies include choice of intermediate layers and perturbation/search settings; further validation on diverse models/datasets would be beneficial.

title: Designing a double deep reinforcement learning selection tool for resilient demand prediction
summary: The use of artificial intelligence in supply chain forecasting has attracted many scientific studies for several decades. However, the process of selecting an appropriate forecasting solution becomes a daunting task. This complexity arises due to the distinct features inherent to each dataset. Research to tackle this issue has been performed since the eighties but recent development of demand forecasting has opened new perspectives. This research aims to enhance automatic forecasting model selection by proposing a novel architecture that acts as a double deep reinforcement learning agent, selecting automatically a forecasting model from the forecasting committee at the time of prediction. Moreover, a novel early-stopping approach based on average reward convergence has been introduced to expedite training time. To evaluate the model's performance, an empirical study was conducted utilizing grocery sales datasets and snack demands datasets. The experimental results demonstrate the robustness of the proposed approach when compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A double deep reinforcement learning agent automates forecasting model selection from a forecasting committee at prediction time, with an average-reward convergence early stopping mechanism, evaluated on grocery and snack demand datasets, achieving robustness against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Auto-selecting forecasting models is challenging due to heterogeneous dataset characteristics; manual or fixed-model approaches can underperform. A data-driven, adaptive model selection framework aims to improve accuracy and efficiency across diverse demand patterns.
AI.method: Proposes a double deep reinforcement learning architecture that selects a forecasting model from a committee at forecast time. Introduces an early-stopping strategy based on average reward convergence to reduce training time. Evaluation is conducted on grocery sales and snack demand datasets.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate robustness and competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art forecasting methods, with reductions in training time due to the proposed early-stopping mechanism.
AI.conclusion: An automatic, efficient forecasting-model selection framework can adapt to dataset characteristics by leveraging double DRL and reward-based early stopping, offering improved performance and training efficiency.

title: Modular Reinforcement Learning For Cooperative Swarms
summary: A cooperative robot swarm is a collective of computationally-limited robots that share a common goal. Each robot can only interact with a small subset of its peers, without knowing how this affects the collective utility. Recent advances in distributed multi-agent reinforcement learning have demonstrated that it is possible for robots to learn how to interact effectively with others, in a manner that is aligned with the common goal, despite each robot learning independently of others. However, this requires each robot to represent a potentially combinatorial number of interaction states, challenging the memory capabilities of the robots. This paper proposes an alternative approach for representing spatial interaction states for multi-robot reinforcement learning in swarms. A modular (decomposed) representation is used, where each feature of the state is handled by a separate learning procedure, and the results aggregated. We demonstrate the efficacy of the approach in numerous experiments with simulated robot swarms carrying out foraging.

AI.tldr: Modular/decomposed spatial-state representation for multi-robot reinforcement learning in swarms; mitigates combinatorial state explosion by per-feature learning with aggregated results, validated in simulated foraging tasks.
AI.motivation: Tackle the memory and scalability challenge of representing spatial interaction states in distributed MARL for robot swarms where each robot has limited interaction and no global state understanding.
AI.method: Represent the state as modular features; train separate learning procedures for each feature and aggregate their outputs to form the joint policy/value.
AI.result: Empirical demonstration of effective learning and coordination in simulated swarm foraging across multiple experiments.
AI.conclusion: A decomposed feature-wise learning approach can scale MARL in swarm robotics by reducing state representation complexity while maintaining cooperative performance.

title: From Parameter Dynamics to Risk Scoring : Quantifying Sample-Level Safety Degradation in LLM Fine-tuning
summary: Safety alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs) is extremely fragile, as fine-tuning on a small number of benign samples can erase safety behaviors learned from millions of preference examples. Existing studies attempt to explain this phenomenon by comparing parameters and hidden states before and after fine-tuning, but overlook their dynamic evolution during fine-tuning. In this paper, we uncover a critical mechanism underlying safety degradation by analyzing parameter dynamics, where benign fine-tuning causes parameters to cumulatively drift toward danger-aligned directions, progressively undermining the model's safety. This finding suggests that samples contributing more to this drift has greater fine-tuning risks. Based on this insight, we propose a method of Sample-Level Quantification of Safety Degradation (SQSD), which quantifies the influence of each training sample on safety degradation. Specifically, SQSD computes continuous risk scores to samples by measuring their induced parameter updates' projection difference between danger and safety directions. Extensive experiments across multiple models and datasets demonstrate that SQSD effectively quantifies sample-level fine-tuning risks and exhibits strong transferability across model architectures, parameter scales, and parameter-efficient methods.

AI.tldr: Benign fine-tuning erodes safety by parameter drift toward danger-aligned directions; introduces SQSD to quantify per-sample risk and demonstrates transferability across models and settings.
AI.motivation: Safety behavior in LLMs is fragile under small-scale benign fine-tuning; existing analyses overlook dynamic, sample-level contributions to safety degradation.
AI.method: Analyze parameter dynamics to identify danger vs safety directions; define Sample-Level Quantification of Safety Degradation (SQSD); compute per-sample risk scores from projection differences of induced updates onto danger vs safety directions; validate across models/datasets and PE methods.
AI.result: SQSD effectively quantifies sample-level fine-tuning risks and transfers across architectures, parameter scales, and PE methods; high-risk samples identified; robust across multiple settings.
AI.conclusion: SQSD provides a transferable, actionable metric to diagnose and potentially mitigate safety erosion during fine-tuning, enabling better-preserved safety with limited benign data.

title: Optimize-at-Capture: Highly-adaptive Exposure Controlling for In-Vehicle Non-contact Heart-rate Monitoring
summary: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) holds great promise for continuous heart-rate monitoring of drivers in intelligent vehicles. However, its performance is severely degraded by the highly dynamic illumination changes. A critical yet overlooked factor is the lack of exposure controlling during video acquisition -- most existing systems rely on either fixed exposure settings or camera build-in auto-exposure, both of which fail to maintain stable facial brightness under rapidly changing lighting conditions during driving. To address this gap, we propose a highly-adaptive exposure controlling framework that proactively adjusts exposure parameters based on predictive modeling of historical skin reflections. Unlike standard auto-exposure, our method is specifically optimized for rPPG measurement, ensuring the skin region of interest (ROI) remains within the optimal dynamic range for rPPG signal extraction. As an important contribution of this study, we introduce ExpDrive, a public in-vehicle physiological monitoring dataset comprising synchronized facial video and reference ECG from 48 subjects captured under real driving conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms fixed exposure and standard auto-exposure strategies. Specifically, it reduces the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) by 6.31 bpm (from 14.1 to 7.79 bpm) and significantly increases the success rate by 32.3 percentage points (p < 0.001) (from 24.9% to 57.2%) across challenging driving scenarios. Notably, it clearly improved the performance of non-contact heart-rate monitoring in both low-light (rainy) and high-glare (sunny) conditions, validating the efficacy of exposure-aware acquisition design.

AI.tldr: Adaptive exposure control tailored for rPPG improves non-contact heart-rate estimation in driving by maintaining skin ROI within an optimal dynamic range; introduces ExpDrive dataset.
AI.motivation: Illumination dynamics in driving severely degrade rPPG performance and existing exposure strategies (fixed or auto) cannot maintain stable facial brightness under rapid lighting changes.
AI.method: A highly-adaptive exposure control framework that predicts historical skin reflections to proactively adjust exposure parameters, ensuring the ROI stays within the optimal dynamic range for rPPG. Introduces ExpDrive, a public in-vehicle physiological monitoring dataset with synchronized facial video and ECG from 48 subjects in real driving conditions.
AI.result: Compared to fixed exposure and standard auto-exposure, MAE reduced from 14.1 to 7.79 bpm (≈6.31 bpm improvement); success rate increased by 32.3 percentage points (24.9% to 57.2%), with p<0.001. Performance improved in challenging low-light (rainy) and high-glare (sunny) scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Exposure-aware acquisition design enhances non-contact heart-rate monitoring under dynamic illumination in vehicles; ExpDrive provides a valuable dataset for future research in this domain.

title: LAWS: Learning from Actual Workloads Symbolically -- A Self-Certifying Parametrized Cache Architecture for Neural Inference, Robotics, and Edge Deployment
comment: 45 pages. Companion paper to arXiv:2604.06228 (Probabilistic Language Tries)
summary: We introduce LAWS (Learning from Actual Workloads Symbolically), a self-certifying inference caching architecture that builds a growing library of certified expert functions from deployment observations. Each expert covers a region of input space defined by a node in the Probabilistic Language Trie (PLT) of the base model and carries a formal error bound holding uniformly over all inputs. The central result is a self-certification theorem: for any input x, the LAWS approximation error is bounded by epsilon_fit + 2*Lambda(W)*C_E, where Lambda(W) is the model Lipschitz constant, C_E is the maximum embedding diameter, and epsilon_fit is the expert training error -- all checkable at deployment time without ground truth. We prove that LAWS generalizes both Mixture-of-Experts and KV prefix caching as special cases and is strictly more expressive than any fixed-K MoE or finite cache. Further results include a monotone hit rate theorem (any-match routing ensures coverage only increases), an expert library growth rate of O(2^H log N) where H is workload entropy, a fleet learning convergence theorem with Omega(K) speedup for K-unit fleets, and an over-the-air update bandwidth bound. We conjecture that LAWS is acquisition-optimal among stationary online caching algorithms and that the effective Lipschitz constant on the training distribution grows polynomially rather than exponentially in depth. Applications are developed for LLM inference, robotic control, and multi-agent edge deployment.

AI.tldr: LAWS is a self-certifying inference caching framework that builds a growing library of certified expert functions from deployment data, with formal, checkable error bounds and guarantees, generalizing MoE and KV caching and offering broad applicability across AI systems.
AI.motivation: To achieve provable, deployment-time verifiable bounds on approximation error and hit rates for large-scale inference, while enabling adaptive, scalable caching. It aims to combine expressive partitioning of input space with verifiable error guarantees and efficient updates.
AI.method: Define input-space regions as nodes in a Probabilistic Language Trie (PLT). For each region, learn an expert function with a training error epsilon_fit and a uniform bound C_E on embedding diameter; prove a self-certification theorem that the LAWS error for input x is bounded by epsilon_fit + 2*Lambda(W)*C_E, where Lambda(W) is the model Lipschitz constant. Show that LAWS encompasses Mixture-of-Experts and KV-prefix caching as special cases and is strictly more expressive than fixed-K MoE or finite caches. Develop monotone hit-rate analysis for any-match routing, characterize library growth as O(2^H log N) with H workload entropy, and establish fleet-convergence and bandwidth bounds for updates. Propose conjectures on acquisition-optimality and Lipschitz growth, and demonstrate applications in LLM inference, robotics, and multi-agent edge deployment.
AI.result: A set of theoretical guarantees and relationships: (1) a self-certification bound on LAWS error; (2) LAWS generalizes MoE and KV caches and dominates fixed-K variants; (3) monotone hit-rate guarantee under any-match routing; (4) exponential-ish library growth bound tied to workload entropy; (5) fleet-learning speedups; (6) bandwidth bounds for OTA updates; (7) conjectures on optimality and distributional Lipschitz behavior; (8) practical applicability across diverse domains including LLMs, robotics, and edge multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: LAWS provides a provably verifiable, scalable inference-caching framework that adaptively grows a certified library of experts, delivering rigorous error bounds and broad expressiveness beyond existing caching/MoE approaches, with wide-ranging practical implications for efficient and reliable deployment.

title: Reduced-order Neural Modeling with Differentiable Simulation for High-Detail Tactile Perception
summary: Tactile perception is key to dexterous manipulation, yet simulating high-resolution elastomer deformation remains computationally prohibitive. Finite element methods (FEM) deliver high fidelity but demand costly remeshing, while Material Point Methods (MPM) suffer from heavy particle-memory tradeoffs. We propose a {reduced-order neural simulation framework} that couples coarse-grained MPM dynamics with an implicit neural decoder to reconstruct sub-particle tactile details from compact latent states. The framework learns a continuous deformation manifold from paired high- and low-resolution simulations, enabling physically consistent, differentiable inference. Compared to the TacIPC, our method achieves over 65\% faster simulation and {40\% lower memory usage}, while maintaining better geometric fidelity. In tactile rendering and 3D surface reconstruction, our methods further improve accuracy by 25\% and produce realistic depth images and surface mesh within a faster inference speed.
