These results demonstrate that the proposed reduced-order neural model enables high-detail, physically grounded tactile simulation with substantial efficiency gains for robotic interaction and optimization.

AI.tldr: A reduced-order neural simulation framework couples coarse MPM with an implicit neural decoder to reconstruct sub-particle tactile details, enabling fast, memory-efficient, differentiable tactile simulation with preserved fidelity.
AI.motivation: Tactile simulation of high-resolution elastomer deformation is computationally expensive. FEM offers fidelity but needs remeshing; MPM incurs heavy memory. A compact, differentiable surrogate is desirable for real-time robotics.
AI.method: Learn a continuous deformation manifold from paired high- and low-resolution simulations and integrate a neural decoder with coarse-grained MPM dynamics to reconstruct sub-particle details. The approach enables differentiable inference and physically consistent results.
AI.result: Achieves ~65% faster simulation and ~40% lower memory usage than TacIPC, while maintaining better geometric fidelity. Improves tactile rendering and 3D surface reconstruction accuracy by ~25%, yielding realistic depth images and surface meshes at faster inference speeds.
AI.conclusion: A reduced-order neural model can deliver high-detail, physically grounded tactile simulation with substantial efficiency gains, benefiting robotic interaction and optimization.

title: SensingAgents: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework for Robust IMU Activity Recognition
summary: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) sensors is a cornerstone of mobile health, smart environments, and human-computer interaction. However, current deep learning-based HAR models often struggle with heavy reliance on labeled data, position-specific ambiguity, and a lack of transparent reasoning. Inspired by the advanced agents framework, which emulates a collaborative agent using Large Language Models (LLMs), we propose SensingAgents, a novel multi-agent system for robust IMU activity recognition. SensingAgents organizes LLM-powered agents into specialized roles: a group of Analyst Agents for position-specific sensor analysis (arm, wrist, belt, pocket), a pair of Advocate Agents that resolves sensor conflicts through dynamic and static dialectical debates, and a Decision Agent that ensures reliability under sensor drift or failure. Evaluation on the Shoaib dataset demonstrates that SensingAgents significantly outperforms state-of-the-art single-agent and multi-agent LLM models, achieving an accuracy of 79.5% in a zero setting--29% higher than existing agent models and 9.4% higher than deep learning baselines--particularly in complex scenarios where multi-sensor data is conflicting or noisy. Our work highlights the potential of multi-agent collaborative reasoning for advancing the robustness and interpretability of ubiquitous sensing systems.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent LLM-based framework, SensingAgents, elevates IMU HAR by assigning Analyst, Advocate, and Decision roles; achieves 79.5% accuracy in zero-shot on Shoaib dataset, outperforming single-agent LLMs and DL baselines, especially in noisy/conflicting sensor data.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of deep learning HAR: heavy labeled data requirements, position-specific ambiguity, and lack of transparent reasoning; leverage collaborative, interpretable reasoning of LLM-powered agents to improve robustness in ubiquitous sensing.
AI.method: Architectural design with Analyst Agents for position-specific sensor analysis (arm, wrist, belt, pocket); Advocate Agents conducting dialectical debates to resolve conflicts; a Decision Agent ensuring reliability under sensor drift or failure; zero-shot evaluation on the Shoaib dataset with multi-sensor inputs.
AI.result: 79.5% accuracy in zero-shot; ~29% higher than existing agent models and ~9.4% higher than DL baselines; robustness in complex scenarios with conflicting/noisy multi-sensor data.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of multi-agent collaborative reasoning with LLMs to enhance robustness and interpretability of HAR and broader ubiquitous sensing systems, with promising generalization under sensor drift or failure.

title: Detecting Deepfakes via Hamiltonian Dynamics
summary: Driven by the rapid development of generative AI models, deepfake detectors are compelled to undergo periodic recalibration to capture newly developed synthetic artifacts. To break this cycle, we propose a new perspective on deepfake detection: moving from static pattern recognition to dynamical stability analysis. Specifically, our approach is motivated by physics-inspired priors: we hypothesize that natural images, as products of dissipative physical processes, tend to settle near stable, low-energy equilibria. In contrast, generative models optimize for statistical similarity to real images but do not explicitly enforce structural constraints such as geometric smoothness, leaving deepfakes more likely to occupy unstable, high-energy states. To operationalize this, we introduce Hamiltonian Action Anomaly Detection (HAAD), comprising three contributions: \textbf{i)} We model the image latent manifold as a potential energy surface. Under this hypothesis, real images are expected to produce basin-like low-energy responses, whereas fake images are more likely to induce high-potential, high-gradient responses. \textbf{ii)} We employ Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics as a stability probe. By releasing latent states from rest, samples near stable regions remain bounded, while high-gradient samples produce larger trajectory responses. \textbf{iii)} We quantify these dynamic behaviors through two trajectory statistics, \ie, Hamiltonian action and energy dissipation. Extensive experiments show that HAAD outperforms evaluated state-of-the-art baselines on challenging cross-dataset transfer benchmarks, supporting a physics-inspired stability prior for digital forensics.

AI.tldr: HAAD introduces a physics-inspired deepfake detector that treats real images as low-energy basins on a latent potential surface and fake images as high-energy, high-gradient states. It uses Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics as a stability probe and two trajectory statistics (Hamiltonian action and energy dissipation) to distinguish real from fake images, achieving superior cross-dataset transfer performance.
AI.motivation: Address the need for robust, generalizable deepfake detection beyond static pattern recognition by leveraging physics-inspired priors: natural images arise from dissipative processes and settle into stable equilibria, while generative models optimize similarity without enforcing structural smoothness.
AI.method: 1) Model the image latent manifold as a potential energy surface. 2) Use Hamiltonian-inspired dynamics by releasing latent states from rest to probe stability (stable regions stay bounded; unstable/high-gradient regions yield larger trajectories). 3) Quantify dynamics with trajectory statistics: Hamiltonian action and energy dissipation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HAAD outperforms evaluated state-of-the-art baselines on challenging cross-dataset transfer benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A physics-inspired stability prior is effective for digital forensics, and HAAD provides robust cross-domain deepfake detection by focusing on dynamical stability properties rather than static artifacts.

title: FlatASCEND: Autoregressive Clinical Sequence Generation with Continuous Time Prediction and Association-Based Pharmacological Testing
summary: Autoregressive models can predict clinical events, but generating patient-conditioned multi-step trajectories that respond to intervention tokens and testing whether those responses preserve known pharmacological associations has received limited attention. We present FlatASCEND, a 14.5M-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model using flat composite tokens and a zero-inflated log-normal time head. Standard distributional metrics (Jaccard 0.889-0.954) do not distinguish FlatASCEND from trivial baselines; the model's value lies in conditional generation from patient-specific prefixes. A prompt-shuffle ablation shows patient-specific conditioning amplifies mechanistic pharmacological effects (2.0-2.2x for steroid to glucose, diuretic to potassium) while leaving confounding-driven associations unchanged (0.9x for insulin to glucose). An incident-user framework assesses directional consistency against prior pharmacological knowledge on MIMIC-IV (N=500 per comparison): 4/10 recover correct mechanistic directions, 2 reproduce treatment-context associations, 4 are incorrect (9/10 significant, Wilcoxon p<0.05). This pattern - partial recovery under residual confounding - is consistent with learned observational associations without causal distinction. Direct preference optimisation with surrogate reward destroys all correct associations (3/3 to 0/3), illustrating reward exploitation when reward and evaluation share an outcome domain. Generative evidence is strongest for short-horizon ICU data; outpatient temporal fidelity is weaker (median 10 vs 154 days on INSPECT), and zero-shot cross-site transfer degrades without adaptation.

AI.tldr: FlatASCEND demonstrates conditional generation of patient-specific, multi-step clinical trajectories and reveals that while conditioning amplifies some pharmacological mechanisms, causal interpretability remains limited due to residual confounding; standard metrics fail to detect nontrivial differences, and reward-based optimization can exploit evaluation signals.
AI.motivation: To enable patient-conditioned generation of clinical trajectories that respond to intervention tokens and to evaluate whether such generation preserves known pharmacological associations, addressing limitations of existing evaluation metrics and causal inference in observational data.
AI.method: 14.5M-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model (FlatASCEND) using flat composite tokens and a zero-inflated log-normal time head. Evaluations include a prompt-shuffle ablation to measure patient-specific conditioning effects, an incident-user framework on MIMIC-IV (N=500 per comparison) to assess directional pharmacological consistency, and direct preference optimization with a surrogate reward. Cross-site transfer and outpatient long-horizon fidelity are also examined (INSPECT, ICU vs outpatient; zero-shot transfer).
AI.result: Standard distributional metrics (Jaccard 0.889-0.954) fail to distinguish FlatASCEND from baselines. Conditioning amplifies mechanistic pharmacological effects (2.0–2.2x: steroid→glucose, diuretic→potassium) while confounding-driven associations persist (0.9x: insulin→glucose). Incident-user evaluation shows 4/10 correct mechanistic directions, 2/10 repeating treatment-context, 4/10 incorrect (9/10 significant, Wilcoxon p<0.05). Direct surrogate-reward optimization destroys all correct associations (3/3→0/3). Stronger evidence in short-horizon ICU data; outpatient fidelity weaker (median 10 vs 154 days on INSPECT); zero-shot cross-site transfer degrades without adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Conditional generation can reflect pharmacological mechanisms under limited, short-horizon data but causal interpretation is hindered by residual confounding. Reward-based optimization can exploit evaluation signals, undermining alignment with known pharmacology. Future work should emphasize causal evaluation, robust confounding control, longer-horizon fidelity, and effective cross-site adaptation to improve causal validity and generalization.

title: Driver-WM: A Driver-Centric Traffic-Conditioned Latent World Model for In-Cabin Dynamics Rollout
summary: Safe L2/L3 driving automation requires anticipating human-in-the-loop reactions during shared-control transitions. While most driving world models forecast the external environment, in-cabin intelligence remains strictly recognition-oriented and lacks multi-step rollout capabilities for driver dynamics. We introduce Driver-WM, a driver-centric latent world model that rolls out in-cabin dynamics causally conditioned on out-cabin traffic context. This formulation unifies physical kinematics forecasting with auxiliary behavioral and emotional semantic recognition. Operating in a compact latent space constructed from frozen vision-language features, Driver-WM adopts a dual-stream architecture to separately encode external traffic and internal driver states. These streams are directionally coupled via a gated causal injection mechanism, which uses a learned vector gate to modulate external contextual perturbations while strictly enforcing temporal causality. Evaluations on a multi-task assistive driving benchmark demonstrate that Driver-WM yields robust long-horizon geometric forecasting for reactive high-motion maneuvers and improves semantic alignment for both driver and traffic states. Finally, the explicit external-to-internal conditioning allows for controlled test-time interventions to systematically analyze mechanism responses.

AI.tldr: Driver-WM: a driver-centric latent world model that rolls out in-cabin dynamics conditioned on out-cabin traffic context, enabling long-horizon forecasting and controlled interventions.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in in-cabin intelligence for driving, moving beyond recognition to multi-step rollouts of driver dynamics in shared-control contexts; unify physical forecasting with driver behavior and emotion semantics.
AI.method: Dual-stream latent world model with frozen vision-language features; separate encoding of external traffic and internal driver states; gated causal injection to propagate external perturbations into the driver state stream while enforcing temporal causality; external-to-internal conditioning enables test-time interventions.
AI.result: Robust long-horizon geometric forecasting for reactive high-motion maneuvers; improved semantic alignment for driver and traffic states; enables controlled test-time interventions to analyze mechanism responses.
AI.conclusion: External-context conditioning of in-cabin dynamics improves forecasting accuracy and interpretability; the model supports multi-task, driver-aware planning and mechanistic analysis through interventions.

title: AuditRepairBench: A Paired-Execution Trace Corpus for Evaluator-Channel Ranking Instability in Agent Repair
comment: 25 pages, 8 figures, NeurIPS 2026 Evaluation and D Directions Track
summary: Agent-repair leaderboards reorder under evaluator reconfiguration, and a measurable share of the reordering is produced by methods that consult evaluator-derived signal during internal selection of candidate repairs. We document this failure mode on a public leaderboard and release AuditRepairBench, a paired-execution trace corpus of 576,000 registered cells (96,000 executed) that operationalizes evaluator-channel-blocking ranking instability within a declared observability boundary. A modular screening architecture decides pathway-blocking through four interchangeable implementations, a learned influence proxy, a rule-based channel-exposure ratio that uses no trained model, a counterfactual sensitivity proxy, and a sparse human-audit proxy, combined into a screening posterior that feeds a cell-level flip functional, a set-valued label, a stratified system score, and a set-valued leaderboard. The resource is supported by mechanism-anchored validation on an 80-case source-level channel-surgery subset, an independent-discovery protocol under which two annotator groups separated from the pipeline developers discover coupling patterns blinded to the screening design and the frozen ensemble attains pooled AUROC 0.83 on their 79 cases, implementation robustness, uncertainty propagation that raises 95% coverage from 0.81 to 0.95, and forward transfer with pooled community-evaluator Spearman \r{ho} = 0.65. Screening-guided blinding patches reduce rank displacement by 55--74% (mean 62%) at fewer than 50 lines of code, whereas random channel blinding produces at most 7% reduction and generic retraining at most 13%. AuditRepairBench-Lite, a rule-only configuration on a 12,000-cell subset, preserves the leaderboard at Kendall τ = 0.88 under twenty-four GPU-hours and is the primary release artifact at 42 GB.

AI.tldr: AuditRepairBench reveals evaluator-channel leakage as a failure mode in agent-repair leaderboards; provides large paired-execution trace corpus and modular screening architecture to detect and mitigate ranking instability; reports robust validation, forward transfer, and patch effectiveness; includes a lighter, rule-only variant (AuditRepairBench-Lite) as a primary artifact.
AI.motivation: Mitigate and quantify leaderboard reordering caused by evaluator-derived signals influencing internal candidate selection, by creating a traceable benchmark and modular screening suite to block or reduce such leakage and to enable robust evaluation of agent repair methods.
AI.method: Construct a large paired-execution trace corpus (576k cells, 96k executed) to operationalize evaluator-channel-blocking ranking instability. Develop a modular screening architecture with four interchangeable components (learned influence proxy, rule-based channel-exposure ratio, counterfactual sensitivity proxy, sparse human-audit proxy) combined into a screening posterior. Use a cell-level flip functional, set-valued labels, stratified system score, and a set-valued leaderboard. Validate on an 80-case source-level channel-surgery subset, plus an independent-discovery protocol with blinded annotator groups to assess coupling patterns. Measure pooled AUROC, coverage, and forward transfer (Spearman). Compare screening-guided blinding against random blinding and generic retraining. Release AuditRepairBench-Lite (12,000-cell subset) as a rule-only configuration.
AI.result: Audited leaderboards show significant ranking instability due to evaluator-derived signaling; screening patches reduce rank displacement by 55–74% (mean 62%) with fewer than 50 lines of code; random blinding yields at most 7% reduction and retraining at most 13%. Independent evaluation yields pooled AUROC 0.83, 95% coverage 0.95, and Spearman 0.65 for forward transfer. AuditRepairBench-Lite maintains leaderboard stability (Kendall τ = 0.88) on 12,000 cells within ~24 GPU-hours; artifact size ~42 GB.
AI.conclusion: Evaluator-channel-blocking screening is an effective and efficient defense against leaderboard leakage in agent-repair evaluations. AuditRepairBench provides a scalable benchmark and actionable variants (including a lightweight rule-only configuration) to support robust, observable, and transferable evaluation outcomes.

title: UAV as Urban Construction Change Monitor: A New Benchmark and Change Captioning Model
summary: Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning (RSICC) aims to generate spatially grounded natural language descriptions of scene evolution from bi-temporal imagery, moving beyond binary change masks toward semantic-level understanding. However, existing methods rely on implicit feature differencing without explicitly modeling structured change semantics, and struggle to reconcile the conflicting representation demands of change detection and caption generation. In addition, current benchmarks provide limited coverage of high-resolution urban construction scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose PTNet, a prototype-guided task-adaptive framework for joint change captioning and detection. PTNet explicitly models structured change semantics through a learnable prototype bank that guides cross-temporal interaction, disentangles task-specific representations via multi-head gating, and injects detection-derived spatial priors into caption generation, enabling coherent semantic correspondence while preserving fine-grained spatial sensitivity. Furthermore, we construct UCCD, a large-scale UAV-based benchmark comprising 9,000 high-resolution image pairs and 45,000 annotated sentences for urban construction monitoring. Extensive experiments on UCCD and WHU-CDC demonstrate that PTNet consistently outperforms existing methods. The dataset and source code are publicly available at https://github.com/G124556/ptnet.

AI.tldr: PTNet is a prototype-guided, task-adaptive framework for joint RS change captioning and detection, introducing a learnable prototype bank, multi-head gating to separate change-detection and captioning representations, and detection-informed spatial priors for accurate, semantically grounded captions; accompanied by a UAV-based large-scale UCCD dataset (9k image pairs, 45k sentences) and released code, showing superior results on UCCD and WHU-CDC.
AI.motivation: Existing RS change captioning methods rely on implicit feature differencing and struggle to reconcile the conflicting demands of change detection and caption generation. High-resolution urban construction scenarios lack diverse benchmarks, limiting evaluation of semantic-level change understanding.
AI.method: PTNet uses a learnable prototype bank to explicitly model structured change semantics and guide cross-temporal interaction. It employs multi-head gating to disentangle task-specific representations for captioning and detection, and injects detection-derived spatial priors into caption generation to maintain semantic alignment with fine-grained spatial details.
AI.result: PTNet consistently outperforms existing methods on two benchmarks, UCCD and WHU-CDC, demonstrating improved semantic-grounded and spatially sensitive change captions and better change detection.
AI.conclusion: A prototype-guided, task-adaptive framework with a large UAV-based dataset (UCCD) and code release advances RS change captioning/detection; the approach improves structured change understanding and spatial coherence between detection and captioning.

title: Sparse Autoencoder Decomposition of Clinical Sequence Model Representations: Feature Complexity, Task Specialisation, and Mortality Prediction
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have been applied to large language models and protein language models, but not systematically to electronic health record (EHR) foundation models. We train TopK SAEs on FlatASCEND, a 14.5-million-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model, at all 10 residual stream extraction points on INSPECT (outpatient) and MIMIC-IV (ICU). SAE decomposition reveals progressive abstraction across transformer depth: layer-0 features are near-perfect token detectors (45.7% singleton), while layer-6 features span approximately 30 token types across multiple clinical categories (0.5% singleton). Under full-sequence simple linear probes, SAE features outperform dense representations for discrete event prediction (mortality) while dense representations outperform for continuous magnitude prediction (length of stay) - a probe-level representational phenomenon that does not extend to clinically relevant leakage-safe windows, where dense representations match or exceed SAE features across all tested settings (eICU-CRD 48-hour AUC: SAE 0.871 versus dense 0.880; base model zero-shot, SAE dictionaries trained on eICU activations; MIMIC-IV: 0.836 versus 0.914; INSPECT 1-year/3-year: 0.697 versus 0.800). A delta-mode intervention method reduces SAE perturbation noise by 86x, enabling cleaner feature-level experiments, though the resulting perturbation effects are larger than random controls in 3 of 4 conditions but not formally significant. Feature reproducibility across random seeds is 21%, and individual features should be interpreted as illustrative rather than stable.

AI.tldr: Systematically applying TopK sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to an EHR foundation model reveals depth-wise abstraction, mixed task performance (discrete vs. continuous), and limited feature stability; perturbation noise can be reduced but interpretability remains cautious due to reproducibility issues and non-significant effects in several tests.
AI.motivation: Understand the geometry and utility of SAE-based representations in EHR foundation models, to assess whether sparse, interpretable features can outperform dense features for clinical predictions and under leakage-safe constraints.
AI.method: Train TopK SAEs on FlatASCEND (14.5M-parameter autoregressive clinical sequence model) at 10 residual stream extraction points on INSPECT and MIMIC-IV. Analyze layer-wise features (layer-0 near perfect token detectors; layer-6 spanning ~30 token types across clinical categories). Use full-sequence simple linear probes to compare SAE vs dense representations on discrete event prediction (mortality) and continuous magnitude prediction (length of stay). Evaluate leakage-safe windows where dense features often match/exceed SAE. Apply delta-mode perturbation to reduce noise and assess reproducibility across seeds. Cross-dataset evaluations include eICU-CRD, MIMIC-IV, and INSPECT.
AI.result: SAE features show progressive abstraction with depth. Layer-0 features are strong token detectors; layer-6 captures diverse token types. Under full-sequence probes, SAE outperforms dense representations for discrete event prediction (mortality); dense representations outperform for LOS. In leakage-safe windows, dense features equal or exceed SAE across settings (eICU-CRD 48h AUC: SAE 0.871 vs dense 0.880; MIMIC-IV: 0.836 vs 0.914; INSPECT 1y/3y: 0.697 vs 0.800). Delta-mode reduces SAE perturbation noise by 86x, but perturbation effects are larger than random controls in 3 of 4 conditions (not statistically significant). Feature reproducibility across seeds is 21%, so individual features are illustrative rather than stable.
AI.conclusion: SAEs provide interpretable, depth-aware representations that can outperform dense features for certain discrete tasks but not for all objectives, particularly continuous outcomes and leakage-safe settings. The low reproducibility and lack of consistent significance caution against over-interpreting individual SAE features; further work should target stability, significance, and integration with leakage-safe evaluation for robust clinical deployment.

title: LineRides: Line-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Bicycle Robot Stunts
comment: Published in IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters (RA-L), 2026
summary: Designing reward functions for agile robotic maneuvers in reinforcement learning remains difficult, and demonstration-based approaches often require reference motions that are unavailable for novel platforms or extreme stunts. We present LineRides, a line-guided learning framework that enables a custom bicycle robot to acquire diverse, commandable stunt behaviors from a user-provided spatial guideline and sparse key-orientations, without demonstrations or explicit timing. LineRides handles physically infeasible guidelines using a tracking margin that permits controlled deviation, resolves temporal ambiguity by measuring progress via traveled distance along the guideline, and disambiguates motion details through position- and sequence-based key-orientations. We evaluate LineRides on the Ultra Mobility Vehicle (UMV) and show that the policy trained with our methods supports seamless transitions between normal driving and stunt execution, enabling five distinct stunts on command: MiniHop, LargeHop, ThreePointTurn, Backflip, and DriftTurn.

AI.tldr: LineRides is a line-guided RL framework that enables a custom bicycle robot to learn diverse, commandable stunt behaviors from a user-provided spatial guideline and sparse key-orientations, without demonstrations. It uses a tracking margin to tolerate infeasible guidelines, measures progress by traveled distance along the guideline to resolve temporal ambiguity, and uses position- and sequence-based key-orientations to disambiguate motion. Evaluated on the Ultra Mobility Vehicle (UMV), it enables seamless transitions between normal driving and five stunts: MiniHop, LargeHop, ThreePointTurn, Backflip, and DriftTurn.
AI.motivation: Reward design for agile robotic maneuvers in reinforcement learning is challenging; demonstration-based approaches require reference motions that may be unavailable for novel platforms or extreme stunts.
AI.method: LineRides introduces a line-guided learning framework that relies on a user-provided spatial guideline and sparse key-orientations, omitting demonstrations and explicit timing. It handles infeasible guidelines with a tracking margin, resolves temporal ambiguity by using progress along the guideline, and disambiguates motion via position- and sequence-based key-orientations. Evaluation is conducted on the Ultra Mobility Vehicle (UMV).
AI.result: The trained policy enables seamless transitions between normal driving and stunt execution, supporting five distinct on-command stunts: MiniHop, LargeHop, ThreePointTurn, Backflip, and DriftTurn.
AI.conclusion: LineRides provides a practical, demonstration-free approach to learning diverse stunt behaviors through minimal spatial guidance, accommodating temporal and feasibility ambiguities while yielding controllable on-demand maneuvers.

title: Budget-aware Auto Optimizer Configurator
summary: Optimizer states occupy massive GPU memory in large-scale model training. However, gradients in different network blocks exhibit distinct behaviors, such as varying directional stability and scale anisotropy, implying that expensive optimizer states are not universally necessary and using a global optimizer is often memory-inefficient. We propose the Budget-Aware Optimizer Configurator (BAOC) to reduce memory cost by assigning suitable optimizer configurations to individual blocks under given budgets. Specifically, BAOC samples gradient streams to derive statistical metrics that quantify the potential performance risk of applying cheaper configurations (e.g., low precision or removing momentum). It then solves a constrained allocation problem to minimize total risk under memory and time budgets, selecting a budget-feasible configuration for each block. Experiments across vision, language, and diffusion workloads demonstrate that BAOC maintains training quality while significantly reducing the memory usage of optimizer states. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BAOC-45C6.

AI.tldr: BAOC achieves per-block optimizer configuration under budgets to reduce memory usage while preserving training quality.
AI.motivation: Optimizer states consume massive GPU memory in large-scale training; gradients exhibit block-wise heterogeneity in stability and scale, making global low-precision or momentum elimination suboptimal.
AI.method: BAOC samples gradient streams to estimate per-block performance risk for cheaper configurations, then solves a constrained allocation problem to minimize total risk under memory/time budgets, assigning a budget-feasible configuration per block.
AI.result: Experiments on vision, language, and diffusion tasks show significant optimizer memory reduction with maintained training quality compared to full-precision per-block optimizers; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Budget-aware configuration is effective for memory-efficient optimization in heterogeneous networks and can adapt to various workloads; potential generalization to other optimizer components.

title: Evaluation Cards for XAI Metrics
comment: 7 pages. Accepted at the 5th XAI4CV Workshop, CVPR 2026 (non-archival)
summary: The evaluation of explainable AI (XAI) methods is affected by a lack of standardization. Metrics are inconsistently defined, incompletely reported, and rarely validated against common baselines. In this paper, we identify transparency of evaluation reporting as a central, under-addressed problem. We propose the XAI Evaluation Card, a documentation template analogous to model cards, designed to accompany any study that introduces an XAI evaluation metric. The card covers explicit declaration of target properties, grounding levels, metric assumptions, validation evidence, gaming risks, and known failure cases. We argue that adopting this template as a community norm would reduce evaluation fragmentation, support meta-analysis, and improve accountability in XAI research.

AI.tldr: Proposes XAI Evaluation Card to standardize reporting of XAI evaluation metrics.
AI.motivation: Current XAI evaluation lacks standardization; metrics are inconsistently defined, incompletely reported, and infrequently validated against baselines; transparency is needed for comparability and accountability.
AI.method: Introduce a documentation template (XAI Evaluation Card) analogous to model cards; specify sections such as target properties, grounding levels, metric assumptions, validation evidence, gaming risks, and known failure cases; argues for community adoption to guide reporting.
AI.result: A proposed standard/template to accompany XAI evaluation metrics; expected to reduce fragmentation, enable meta-analysis, and improve accountability in XAI research.
AI.conclusion: Adoption of the XAI Evaluation Card would improve transparency and rigor in XAI metric reporting.

title: Confronting Label Indeterminacy in Automated Bail Decisions
comment: This manuscript has been accepted for presentation as a short paper at the 21st International Conference of AI & Law in Singapore, June 8 to 12 of 2026
summary: Bail decisions present a fundamental challenge for data-driven decision support systems. When bail is denied, the counterfactual outcome of whether the defendant would have appeared in court remains unobserved. As a result, historical bail data embed structural label indeterminacy: future decisions are influenced by past decisions whose outcomes are only partially knowable. Building automated systems on such data risks introducing bias and reinforcing feedback loops. This raises a core question for machine-learning systems intended to assist judicial actors: how should cases in which bail was denied be treated during model development? In a case study of bail decisions from the Unified Judicial System of Pennsylvania, we evaluate five contemporary approaches to handling label indeterminacy across three machine learning models, including a novel label imputation method motivated by the dynamics of bail decisions. Each method relies on unverifiable assumptions, yet all influence the models' predictive behaviour, sometimes even more so than the choice of model itself. Explainable AI analysis further reveals that these effects extend to the models' internal decision-making processes as well. Finally, we consider the notion of label indeterminacy from a legal perspective and assess the legitimacy of these approaches in the context of bail decision-making.

AI.tldr: Evaluates how to handle label indeterminacy when bail decisions are modeled, comparing five methods (including a novel imputation) across three ML models using Pennsylvania court data; finds that label-handling choices can dominate model choice and affect explainable AI analyses; discusses legal legitimacy.
AI.motivation: To mitigate bias and feedback from unobserved counterfactual outcomes in bail data and guide responsible deployment of ML in judicial decision-support.
AI.method: Empirical study on PA Unified Judicial System bail decisions; assess five approaches to label indeterminacy; apply to three ML models; propose a novel imputation method; use Explainable AI to inspect internal decisions; evaluate legal legitimacy.
AI.result: All approaches rest on unverifiable assumptions and substantially influence predictions, sometimes more than model selection; imprint on internal model reasoning; AI explanations reveal effects of label choices; policy/legal considerations discussed.
AI.conclusion: Label indeterminacy is a core challenge; no single superior approach; emphasize transparent assumptions and legal alignment; call for principled, robust handling and further work on integrating legal standards.

title: ConsisVLA-4D: Advancing Spatiotemporal Consistency in Efficient 3D-Perception and 4D-Reasoning for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models primarily focus on mapping 2D observations to actions, but exhibit notable limitations in spatiotemporal perception and reasoning: 1) spatial representations often rely on additional sensors, introducing substantial computational overhead; 2) visual reasoning is typically limited to future-frame prediction, lacking alignment with the instruction-grounded scene and thus compromising spatiotemporal consistency. To address these challenges, we propose ConsisVLA-4D, a unified and efficient framework that enhances spatiotemporal consistency in 3D perception and 4D reasoning. Specifically, we design: 1) CV-Aligner, which ensures cross-view object semantic consistency by filtering instruction-relevant regions and aligning object identities across multiple viewpoints; 2) CO-Fuser, which guarantees cross-object spatial geometric consistency by eliminating spatial relation ambiguities between objects across views using compact latent representations. Building upon these, we introduce 3) CS-Thinker to achieve cross-scene spatiotemporal consistency as actions unfold. It learns implicit knowledge of local dynamics from object-semantic tokens of CV-Aligner and global depth from geometric tokens of CO-Fuser, thereby enhancing efficient visual reasoning under scene variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, benefiting from its efficient spatiotemporal consistency design, ConsisVLA-4D achieves 21.6% and 41.5% performance improvements, along with 2.3-fold and 2.4-fold inference speedups compared to OpenVLA on the LIBERO benchmark and real-world platforms, respectively.ConsisVLA-4D is open-sourced and publicly available at

AI.tldr: ConsisVLA-4D is a unified framework that enforces spatiotemporal consistency in Vision-Language-Action models for 3D perception and 4D reasoning, via CV-Aligner, CO-Fuser, and CS-Thinker, yielding substantial accuracy and speed improvements.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models rely on 2D-to-action mapping and often require extra sensors, leading to high computational overhead and poor spatiotemporal alignment, particularly for instruction-grounded scenes and dynamic environments. There is a need for cross-view, cross-object, and cross-scene consistency in 3D/4D VLA.
AI.method: Introduce three modules: CV-Aligner filters instruction-relevant regions and aligns object identities across viewpoints; CO-Fuser resolves spatial relation ambiguities across views using compact latent representations to guarantee cross-object geometric consistency; CS-Thinker learns implicit local dynamics from object-semantic tokens (from CV-Aligner) and global depth tokens (from CO-Fuser) to enable cross-scene spatiotemporal reasoning as actions unfold.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows 21.6% and 41.5% performance improvements versus OpenVLA on LIBERO and real-world platforms, respectively, and 2.3x and 2.4x inference speedups; framework is open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: ConsisVLA-4D demonstrates efficient spatiotemporal consistency for VLA tasks and sets a path toward resource-efficient 3D/4D reasoning in real-world contexts.

title: Reward-Decomposed Reinforcement Learning for Immersive Video Role-Playing
summary:
Text-based role-playing models can imitate character styles, yet they often fail to reflect a scene's atmosphere and evolving tension, both essential for immersive applications such as Virtual Reality (VR) games and interactive narratives. We study video-grounded role-playing dialogue and introduce EBM-RL (Eye-Brain-Mouth Reinforcement Learning), a decoupled GRPO-based framework that explicitly separates observation ([perception]), reasoning ([think]), and utterance ([answer]). This structure promotes human-like sensory grounding by compelling the model to first attend to visual cues, then form internal interpretations, and finally generate context-appropriate dialogue.
  EBM-RL integrates four complementary rewards: (i) CLIP-based scene-text alignment to improve ambiance and emotion; (ii) a Perceptual-Cognitive reward that encourages [perception] and [think] processes that increase the likelihood of the reference response; (iii) answer accuracy to ensure faithfulness; and (iv) a dense format reward to enforce the desired structured output.
  Extensive experiments demonstrate that EBM-RL substantially outperforms text-only role-playing baselines and larger-scale vision-language models on our immersive role-playing benchmark, delivering simultaneous gains in visual-atmosphere consistency and character authenticity. Beyond the role-playing domain, EBM-RL also exhibits strong zero-shot generalization: without any additional fine-tuning, it consistently improves performance on out-of-domain VideoQA benchmarks. We additionally release an open-source dataset for video-grounded role-playing dialogue.

AI.tldr: Introduces EBM-RL, a decoupled Eye-Brain-Mouth RL framework for video-grounded role-playing dialogue that explicitly separates perception, thinking, and utterance. Utilizes four rewards (CLIP-based scene-text alignment, perceptual-cognitive reward, answer accuracy, and a dense format reward) to improve ambiance, atmosphere, and character authenticity, achieving strong performance over baselines and good zero-shot VideoQA generalization; releases dataset.
AI.motivation: Text-based role-playing models struggle to capture scene atmosphere and dynamic tension. A human-like grounding process—attending to visuals, forming internal interpretations, and generating context-appropriate dialogue—could improve immersion and fidelity in VR games and interactive narratives.
AI.method: A decoupled GRPO-based framework named EBM-RL (Eye-Brain-Mouth Reinforcement Learning) that enforces a perception-think-answer loop. Training integrates four rewards: (i) CLIP-based scene-text alignment for ambiance, (ii) Perceptual-Cognitive reward to encourage perception and thinking aligned with the reference response, (iii) answer accuracy for faithfulness, and (iv) a dense format reward to enforce structured output.
AI.result: EBM-RL substantially outperforms text-only baselines and larger vision-language models on an immersive role-playing benchmark, yielding improvements in visual-atmosphere consistency and character authenticity. Demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization on out-of-domain VideoQA benchmarks without extra fine-tuning. A dataset for video-grounded role-playing dialogue is released.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly grounding perception, reasoning, and utterance in a decoupled framework enhances immersive, character-consistent dialogue and generalizes beyond the target domain, suggesting the approach is broadly beneficial for vision-language grounding in interactive narrative settings.

title: Structured 3D Latents Are Surprisingly Powerful: Unleashing Generalizable Style with 2D Diffusion
summary: 3D asset generation plays a pivotal role in fields such as gaming and virtual reality, enabling the rapid synthesis of high-fidelity 3D objects from a single or multiple images. Building on this capability, enabling style-controllable generation naturally emerges as an important and desirable direction. However, existing approaches typically rely on style images that lie within or are similar to the training distribution of 3D generation models. When presented with out-of-distribution (OOD) styles, their performance degrades significantly or even fails. To address this limitation, we introduce $\textbf{DiLAST}$: 2D Diffusion-based Latent Awakening for 3D Style Transfer. Specifically, we leverage a pretrained 2D diffusion model as a teacher to provide rich and generalizable style priors. By aligning rendered views with the target style under diffusion-based guidance, our method optimizes the structured 3D latent representation for stylization. We observe that this limitation stems not from insufficient model capacity, but from the underutilization of structured 3D latents, which are inherently expressive. Despite being trained on comparatively limited data, 3D generation models can leverage 2D diffusion guidance to steer denoising toward specific directions in latent space, thereby producing diverse, OOD styles. Extensive experiments across diverse data and multiple 3D generation backbones demonstrate the effectiveness and plug-and-play nature of our approach.

AI.tldr: A 2D diffusion teacher guides 3D style transfer by aligning rendered views with a target style in the 3D latent space, enabling out-of-distribution stylistic control without retraining 3D models.
AI.motivation: Overcome poor performance of 3D style transfer when target styles are out-of-distribution relative to the 3D generator's training data; reveal that structured 3D latents are underutilized and can be steered via 2D priors.
AI.method: Use a pretrained 2D diffusion model as a teacher to provide style priors; optimize a structured 3D latent representation by aligning rendered views with the target style under diffusion-guided denoising; the approach is plug-and-play across backbones and emphasizes latent awakening in 3D representations.
AI.result: Demonstrates effective OOD style transfer across diverse data and multiple 3D backbones; produces diverse stylizations guided by 2D diffusion without requiring large 3D style datasets; validates the plug-and-play nature and generality of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion guidance from 2D models can steer 3D latent optimizations to express new, out-of-distribution styles; structured 3D latents are sufficiently expressive when guided by rich 2D priors, enabling data-efficient, versatile 3D style transfer.

title: A Physics-Aware Framework for Short-Term GPU Power Forecasting of AI Data Centers
summary: AI data centers experience rapid fluctuations in power demand due to the heterogeneity of computational tasks that they have to support. For example, the power profile of inference and training of large language models (LLMs) is quite distinct and big divergences can result in the instability of the underlying electricity grid. In this paper we propose, to the best of our knowledge, the first physics-informed DLinear time-series model that can accurately forecast power utilization of an AI data center 5-80 minutes (short-term forecasting) into the future. The physics, based on a multi-node lumped thermal resistance-capacitance (RC) network consistent with Newton's law of cooling, is captured using newly derived time-dependent ordinary differential equations (ODE) that separately models and interlinks power consumption with the GPU compute and memory utilization and temperature. The resulting model, that we refer to as PI-DLinear, trained and evaluated on a real AI data center dataset and is not only more accurate than the state-of-the-art (SOTA) models tested, but the forecast profile respects the underlying physics under power throttling and load transient events. Relative to the SOTA transformer-based and non-transformer-based models, improvements in forecasting accuracy (averaged across all look-back and prediction windows) range from 0.782%-39.08% for MSE, 0.993%-51.82% for MAE, and 0.370%-22.28% for RMSE.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed DLinear time-series model (PI-DLinear) forecasts AI data center power usage 5-80 minutes ahead, combining a multi-node lumped RC thermal network with time-dependent ODEs, achieving higher accuracy than SOTA transformer-based/non-transformer models on real data.
AI.motivation: Address power demand fluctuations in AI data centers and their impact on grid stability; enable short-term power forecasts that respect physical constraints.
AI.method: Integrate a physics-based RC network (Newton's cooling) into the DLinear framework via time-dependent ODEs linking GPU compute/memory utilization and temperature; train on real data; evaluate against transformer-based and non-transformer baselines; forecast windows 5-80 minutes; evaluate using MSE, MAE, RMSE.
AI.result: PI-DLinear is more accurate than SOTA models across metrics; improvements: 0.782%-39.08% (MSE), 0.993%-51.82% (MAE), 0.370%-22.28% (RMSE); forecast respects physics under throttling and load transients.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed modeling improves short-term power forecasting for AI data centers; promising for grid stability; potential for broader adoption with RC-based cooling dynamics; further work on generalization and robustness.

title: When Life Gives You BC, Make Q-functions: Extracting Q-values from Behavior Cloning for On-Robot Reinforcement Learning
summary: Behavior Cloning (BC) has emerged as a highly effective paradigm for robot learning. However, BC lacks a self-guided mechanism for online improvement after demonstrations have been collected. Existing offline-to-online learning methods often cause policies to replace previously learned good actions due to a distribution mismatch between offline data and online learning. In this work, we propose Q2RL, Q-Estimation and Q-Gating from BC for Reinforcement Learning, an algorithm for efficient offline-to-online learning. Our method consists of two parts: (1) Q-Estimation extracts a Q-function from a BC policy using a few interaction steps with the environment, followed by online RL with (2) Q-Gating, which switches between BC and RL policy actions based on their respective Q-values to collect samples for RL policy training. Across manipulation tasks from D4RL and robomimic benchmarks, Q2RL outperforms SOTA offline-to-online learning baselines on success rate and time to convergence. Q2RL is efficient enough to be applied in an on-robot RL setting, learning robust policies for contact-rich and high precision manipulation tasks such as pipe assembly and kitting, in 1-2 hours of online interaction, achieving success rates of up to 100% and up to 3.75x improvement against the original BC policy. Code and video are available at https://pages.rai-inst.com/q2rl_website/

AI.tldr: Q2RL introduces Q-Estimation and Q-Gating to convert behavioral cloning into an efficient offline-to-online RL framework. It derives a Q-function from a BC policy with limited interaction, then gates between BC and RL actions by Q-values to collect RL data, achieving strong online performance on manipulation benchmarks with modest interaction time.
AI.motivation: Offline BC lacks a self-guided online improvement mechanism and standard offline-to-online methods often disrupt previously learned good actions due to distribution shift. The work aims to enable sample-efficient online RL while preserving the benefits of BC.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) Q-Estimation: extract a Q-function from a BC policy using a few environment interactions; (2) Q-Gating: during data collection, choose actions from BC or RL policy based on their Q-values to collect informative samples for RL training.
AI.result: Across D4RL and robomimic manipulation tasks, Q2RL outperforms state-of-the-art offline-to-online baselines in success rate and time-to-convergence. Demonstrated practical online learning on-robot for contact-rich, high-precision tasks (pipe assembly, kitting) with 1–2 hours of online interaction, achieving up to 100% success and up to 3.75x improvement versus the original BC policy.
AI.conclusion: Q2RL provides a practical, data-efficient offline-to-online framework that leverages Q-based gating to combine BC and RL strengths, yielding robust manipulation policies with limited online interaction. Code and video are available for replication.

title: AgentTrust: Runtime Safety Evaluation and Interception for AI Agent Tool Use
summary:
Modern AI agents execute real-world side effects through tool calls such as file operations, shell commands, HTTP requests, and database queries. A single unsafe action, including accidental deletion, credential exposure, or data exfiltration, can cause irreversible harm. Existing defenses are incomplete: post-hoc benchmarks measure behavior after execution, static guardrails miss obfuscation and multi-step context, and infrastructure sandboxes constrain where code runs without understanding what an action means.
  We present AgentTrust, a runtime safety layer that intercepts agent tool calls before execution and returns a structured verdict: allow, warn, block, or review. AgentTrust combines a shell deobfuscation normalizer, SafeFix suggestions for safer alternatives, RiskChain detection for multi-step attack chains, and a cache-aware LLM-as-Judge for ambiguous inputs.
  We release a 300-scenario benchmark across six risk categories and an additional 630 independently constructed real-world adversarial scenarios. On the internal benchmark, the production-only ruleset achieves 95.0% verdict accuracy and 73.7% risk-level accuracy at low-millisecond end-to-end latency. On the 630-scenario benchmark, evaluated under a patched ruleset and not claimed as zero-shot, AgentTrust achieves 96.7% verdict accuracy, including about 93% on shell-obfuscated payloads. AgentTrust is released under the AGPL-3.0 license and provides a Model Context Protocol server for MCP-compatible agents.

AI.tldr: AgentTrust is a runtime safety layer that intercepts AI agent tool calls, classifying actions as allow, warn, block, or review using deobfuscation, SafeFix suggestions, RiskChain detection, and a cache-aware LLM-based judge; benchmarked with high verdict accuracy and low latency; released under AGPL-3.0 with MCP compatibility.
AI.motivation: Post-hoc benchmarks, static guardrails, and restricted execution environments fail to prevent unsafe real-world actions by AI agents due to obfuscated or multi-step contexts; there is a need for a proactive, interpretable, runtime safety mechanism.
AI.method: Intercept tool calls before execution; apply a shell deobfuscation normalizer; generate SafeFix safer alternatives; detect multi-step attack chains with RiskChain; use a cache-aware LLM-as-Judge for ambiguous cases; evaluate on a 300-scenario six-risk-category internal benchmark and an additional 630 adversarial scenarios; provide MCP-compatible Model Context Protocol server.
AI.result: Internal benchmark with production-only ruleset achieves 95.0% verdict accuracy and 73.7% risk-level accuracy at low end-to-end latency. On the 630-scenario benchmark with a patched ruleset (not zero-shot), verdict accuracy is 96.7%, with ~93% accuracy on shell-obfuscated payloads. The work is released under AGPL-3.0 and includes a Model Context Protocol server for MCP-compatible agents.
AI.conclusion: AgentTrust offers effective runtime safety for real-world AI agents, handling obfuscated and multi-step attacks with interpretable indicators and low latency, suitable for deployment in MCP-enabled ecosystems.

title: Joint Semantic Token Selection and Prompt Optimization for Interpretable Prompt Learning
summary: Vision-language models such as CLIP achieve strong visual-textual alignment, but often suffer from overfitting and limited interpretability when adapted through continuous prompt learning. While discrete prompt optimization improves interpretability, it usually depends on large external models, leading to high computational costs and limited scalability. In this paper, we propose Interpretable Prompt Learning (IPL), a hybrid framework that alternates between discrete semantic token selection and continuous prompt optimization. Specifically, IPL formulates semantic token selection as an approximate submodular optimization problem, encouraging tokens that are both human-understandable and semantically diverse. It further adopts an alternating optimization strategy to integrate discrete token selection with continuous prompt tuning, improving interpretability while preserving adaptability to downstream tasks. Our framework is plug-and-play, allowing seamless integration with existing prompt learning methods. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that IPL consistently improves both interpretability and accuracy across five representative prompt learning methods, providing an effective and scalable extension to existing frameworks.

AI.tldr: IPL interleaves discrete semantic token selection with continuous prompt optimization to enhance interpretability and accuracy in CLIP-like prompt learning, via approximate submodular selection and alternating optimization; plug-and-play with existing methods.
AI.motivation: Continuous prompt learning in vision-language models often overfits and lacks interpretability; discrete prompts offer interpretability but are costly and hard to scale. A hybrid, scalable approach is desired that preserves adaptability.
AI.method: Formulate semantic token selection as an approximate submodular optimization problem to choose diverse, human-understandable tokens. Use an alternating optimization strategy to integrate discrete token selection with continuous prompt tuning. The framework is plug-and-play with existing prompt-learning methods.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show IPL consistently improves interpretability and accuracy across five representative prompt-learning methods, demonstrating its effectiveness and scalability as an extension to existing frameworks.
AI.conclusion: IPL provides a flexible, scalable framework that improves interpretability without sacrificing performance, enabling seamless integration with existing prompt-learning pipelines.

title: RetentiveKV: State-Space Memory for Uncertainty-Aware Multimodal KV Cache Eviction
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models face severe challenges in computational efficiency and memory consumption due to the substantial expansion of the visual KV cache when processing long visual contexts. Existing KV cache compression methods typically rely on the "persistence of importance" hypothesis to prune tokens. However, this approach proves fragile in multimodal settings due to two key issues: 1) Visual tokens display "deferred importance," initially exhibiting low salience but becoming pivotal during later decoding, which can lead to premature eviction. 2) Discrete pruning disrupts the inherent spatial continuity of visual cues. To address these challenges, we propose RetentiveKV, an entropy-driven KV cache optimization method that reformulates KV eviction from "discrete context truncation" to "continuous memory evolution" based on State Space Models. Our method leverages information entropy to quantify the information potential of low-attention tokens and integrates tokens scheduled for eviction into a continuous state space through entropy-guided state transitions, enabling their dynamic reactivation when semantic relevance arises during subsequent decoding. Extensive experiments on multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that RetentiveKV achieves 5.0 times KV cache compression and 1.5 times decoding acceleration.

AI.tldr: RetentiveKV introduces entropy-driven, continuous memory evolution for KV cache in multimodal LLMs to address long visual contexts. It replaces discrete pruning with state-space-based eviction guided by information entropy, enabling reactivation of evicted tokens and achieving substantial KV compression and speedups.
AI.motivation: Current KV cache pruning assumes persistent token importance; in multimodal settings visual tokens exhibit deferred importance and spatial continuity is disrupted by discrete pruning, leading to inefficient memory usage and degraded performance on long visual contexts.
AI.method: Model the KV cache as a continuous state-space and perform entropy-guided eviction. Tokens with low instantaneous attention but potential future relevance are integrated into the state space, allowing transition-based reactivation when semantic relevance returns. This shifts eviction from discrete truncation to continuous memory evolution using State Space Models and information entropy as a guiding metric.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multimodal benchmarks show 5.0× KV cache compression and 1.5× decoding acceleration, with maintained or improved performance.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-driven, continuous KV cache management effectively reduces memory and compute requirements for multimodal LLMs while handling deferred importance and preserving spatial cues, demonstrating practical acceleration on long visual-context tasks.

title: A Closed-Form Dual-Barrier CBF Safety Filter for Holonomic Robots on Incrementally Built Occupancy Grid Maps
summary: We present a dual-barrier control barrier function (CBF) safety filter for real-time, safety-critical velocity control of holonomic robots operating in incrementally built occupancy grid maps. As a robot explores an unknown environment, unmapped regions introduce irreducible uncertainty, since obstacle geometry beyond the explored frontier is unknown, making entry into such regions a source of collision risk, especially with front-facing sensors. To address this, we enforce two constraints: avoidance of mapped obstacles and restriction from unexplored regions. Both constraints are derived analytically from the occupancy grid's signed distance field, yielding a closed-form safety filter that requires only a small linear system solve per cycle. On resource-constrained platforms such as the Raspberry Pi, where SLAM and planning already consume significant compute, the low overhead of the proposed filter preserves resources. An adaptive gain schedule relaxes the frontier constraint in information-rich regions and tightens it in well-mapped areas, improving exploration efficiency while maintaining safety. The filter operates in velocity space as a minimally invasive correction and composes with arbitrary nominal controllers, including learning-based methods. Hardware flight experiments on a PX4-controlled quadrotor demonstrate zero collisions across multiple indoor runs.

AI.tldr: A dual-barrier CBF safety filter for real-time velocity control of holonomic robots in incrementally built occupancy grids, enforcing mapped-obstacle avoidance and frontier restriction via a closed-form, low-overhead filter that adapts by information density; validated on a PX4 quadrotor with zero collisions.
AI.motivation: Enable safe, real-time velocity control in unknown, partially explored environments where obstacle geometry beyond the frontier is uncertain, causing collision risk for front-facing sensors; reduce computational load on resource-constrained platforms while maintaining safety.
AI.method: Derive two safety constraints from the occupancy grid's signed distance field: one preventing entry into mapped obstacles and a second restricting motion into unexplored regions. Formulate these as barrier functions yielding a closed-form velocity filter solvable by a small linear system per cycle. Introduce an adaptive gain schedule that relaxes frontier constraints in information-rich areas and tightens them when mapped areas dominate. The filter operates in velocity space and composes with arbitrary nominal controllers.
AI.result: A computationally light safety filter with per-cycle small linear-system solve, suitable for embedded platforms; hardware flight experiments on a PX4-controlled quadrotor reported zero collisions across multiple indoor runs, demonstrating practical safety and resource efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The dual-barrier CBF framework provides an effective, low-overhead safety mechanism for real-time velocity control in uncertain, partially mapped environments, with adaptive behavior based on information density and compatibility with various nominal controllers.

title: DecodingTrust-Agent Platform (DTap): A Controllable and Interactive Red-Teaming Platform for AI Agents
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed across diverse domains to automate complex workflows through long-horizon and high-stakes action executions. Due to their high capability and flexibility, such agents raise significant security and safety concerns. A growing number of real-world incidents have shown that adversaries can easily manipulate agents into performing harmful actions, such as leaking API keys, deleting user data, or initiating unauthorized transactions. Evaluating agent security is inherently challenging, as agents operate in dynamic, untrusted environments involving external tools, heterogeneous data sources, and frequent user interactions. However, realistic, controllable, and reproducible environments for large-scale risk assessment remain largely underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce the DecodingTrust-Agent Platform (DTap), the first controllable and interactive red-teaming platform for AI agents, spanning 14 real-world domains and over 50 simulation environments that replicate widely used systems such as Google Workspace, Paypal, and Slack. To scale the risk assessment of agents in DTap, we further propose DTap-Red, the first autonomous red-teaming agent that systematically explores diverse injection vectors (e.g., prompt, tool, skill, environment, combinations) and autonomously discovers effective attack strategies tailored to varying malicious goals. Using DTap-Red, we curate DTap-Bench, a large-scale red-teaming dataset comprising high-quality instances across domains, each paired with a verifiable judge to automatically validate attack outcomes. Through DTap, we conduct large-scale evaluations of popular AI agents built on various backbone models, spanning security policies, risk categories, and attack strategies, revealing systematic vulnerability patterns and providing valuable insights for developing secure next-generation agents.

AI.tldr: A controllable red-teaming framework (DTap) for AI agents with an autonomous attacker (DTap-Red) and a large-scale benchmark (DTap-Bench) across 14 domains to assess and improve agent security.
AI.motivation: High-stakes AI agents are vulnerable to adversarial manipulation; realistic, controllable, and reproducible risk assessment environments are scarce.
AI.method: Develop the DTap platform spanning 14 real-world domains and 50 simulation environments; introduce DTap-Red, an autonomous red-teaming agent that probes injection vectors (prompt, tool, skill, environment, combinations); curate DTap-Bench with high-quality attack instances and verifiable judges; evaluate popular agents across security policies, risk categories, and attack strategies.
AI.result: First large-scale, controllable red-teaming framework for AI agents; autonomous attacker discovers effective attack strategies; creates a curated benchmark with verifiable attack outcomes; reveals systematic vulnerability patterns across domains and backbones.
AI.conclusion: DTap enables scalable, reproducible risk assessment of AI agents, yielding actionable insights to build secure, next-generation agents and informing security policy development.

title: Ground4D: Spatially-Grounded Feedforward 4D Reconstruction for Unstructured Off-Road Scenes
summary: Feedforward Gaussian Splatting has recently emerged as an efficient paradigm for 4D reconstruction in autonomous driving. However, in unstructured off-road scenes, its performance degrades due to high-frequency geometry, ego-motion jitter, and increased non-rigid dynamics. These factors introduce conflicting Gaussian observations across timestamps, leading to either over-smoothed renderings or structural artifacts. To address this issue, we propose Ground4D, a spatially-grounded 4D feedforward framework for pose-free off-road reconstruction. The key idea is to resolve temporal conflicts through spatially localized conditioning. Specifically, we introduce voxel-grounded temporal Gaussian aggregation, which partitions the canonical Gaussian space into spatial voxels and performs query-conditioned temporal attention within each voxel. Intra-voxel softmax normalization ensures that temporal selectivity and spatial occupancy become mutually reinforcing rather than conflicting. We furthermore introduce surface normal cues as auxiliary geometric guidance to regularize the geometry of Gaussian primitives. Extensive experiments on ORAD-3D and RELLIS-3D demonstrate that Ground4D consistently outperforms existing feedforward methods in reconstruction quality and generalizes zero-shot to unseen off-road domains. Project page and code:https://github.com/wsnbws/Ground4D.

AI.tldr: Ground4D proposes a spatially grounded 4D feedforward framework that uses voxel-level temporal Gaussian aggregation with query-conditioned attention and intra-voxel softmax normalization, augmented by surface normal cues, to improve pose-free off-road 4D reconstruction and zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: In unstructured off-road scenes, high-frequency geometry, ego-motion jitter, and non-rigid dynamics create conflicting observations across timestamps in feedforward Gaussian splatting, causing over-smoothing or artifacts.
AI.method: Partition the canonical Gaussian space into spatial voxels and perform query-conditioned temporal attention within each voxel; apply intra-voxel softmax normalization to align temporal selectivity with spatial occupancy; incorporate surface normal cues as auxiliary geometric guidance to regularize Gaussian primitives.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on ORAD-3D and RELLIS-3D shows Ground4D outperforms existing feedforward methods in reconstruction quality and generalizes zero-shot to unseen off-road domains.
AI.conclusion: Spatially localized conditioning via voxel-grounded temporal aggregation and normal regularization effectively resolves temporal conflicts in 4D off-road reconstruction, enabling robust, pose-free, and zero-shot capable performance.

title: A Regulatory Governance Framework for AI-Driven Financial Fraud Detection in U.S. Banking: Integrating OCC, SR 11-7, CFPB, and FinCEN Compliance Requirements for Model Development, Validation, and Monitoring Lifecycles
comment: 38 pages, submitted to Cogent Business & Management (Taylor & Francis), currently under peer review
summary: U.S. financial institutions deploying AI-based fraud detection face a fragmented compliance landscape spanning four regulatory frameworks -- OCC Bulletin 2011-12, SR 11-7, the CFPB AI circular, and FinCEN BSA/SAR requirements -- with no integrated governance life cycle connecting these requirements to model development, validation, and monitoring practice. This paper presents the Regulatory Governance Framework for AI-Driven Financial Fraud Detection (RGF-AFFD), a three-tier governance architecture empirically anchored in a multi-study empirical program. Using the IEEE-CIS dataset (590,540 transactions) and ULB benchmark (284,807 transactions), we benchmark six architectures including an LSTM+XGBoost ensemble, and conduct ablation, temporal drift, SHAP interpretability, and BISG fairness analyses. The LSTM+XGBoost ensemble achieves ROC-AUC of 0.9289 (F1: 0.6360) with a benefit-cost ratio of 6:1. XGBoost demonstrates the strongest temporal stability (delta-AUC = -0.0017 versus -0.0626 for LSTM). The RDT-FG Regulatory Digital Twin meta-model translates metrics into four regulator-specific health scores and a composite Regulatory Fitness Index for continuous compliance monitoring. The RGF-AFFD is the first integrated deployment blueprint to simultaneously satisfy OCC, SR 11-7, CFPB, and FinCEN requirements, supported by a community bank implementation vignette and four evidence-based policy recommendations.

AI.tldr: A three-tier Regulatory Governance Framework for AI-driven financial fraud detection (RGF-AFFD) integrating OCC Bulletin 2011-12, SR 11-7, CFPB AI circular, and FinCEN BSA/SAR; empirical evaluation using IEEE-CIS and ULB data; validates six architectures including LSTM+XGBoost; introduces Regulatory Digital Twin for monitoring and four policy recommendations.
AI.motivation: To resolve fragmented regulatory requirements and governance gaps in AI fraud detection, enabling continuous compliance through an integrated lifecycle.
AI.method: Three-tier governance architecture; multi-study empirical program; benchmark six architectures on two large datasets; perform ablation, temporal drift, SHAP, BISG fairness; develop RDT-FG Regulatory Digital Twin meta-model to map metrics to regulator-specific health scores; provide deployment blueprint and policy recommendations.
AI.result: LSTM+XGBoost ensemble achieves ROC-AUC 0.9289, F1 0.6360; BCR 6:1; XGBoost shows strongest temporal stability (delta-AUC -0.0017 vs -0.0626 for LSTM); four health scores and a Regulatory Fitness Index; first integrated deployment satisfying OCC, SR 11-7, CFPB, FinCEN; includes bank vignette and four policy recommendations.
AI.conclusion: RGF-AFFD constitutes a practical, integrated governance blueprint enabling compliant AI fraud detection across major regulatory regimes, with measurable performance, stability, interpretability, fairness analyses, and continuous monitoring.

title: Constraint-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Based on Dynamic Decoupled Spherical Radial Squashing
summary: When deploying reinforcement learning policies to physical robots, actuator rate constraints -- hard limits on how fast each joint can move per control step -- are unavoidable. These limits vary substantially across joints due to differences in motor inertia, power bandwidth, and transmission stiffness, creating pronounced heterogeneity that existing methods fail to handle geometrically: the per-joint feasible region forms a high-dimensional box in action-increment space, yet QP projection and spherical parameterization methods impose isotropic ball-shaped constraints, exponentially under-covering the true feasible set as heterogeneity grows. This paper proposes Dynamic Decoupled Spherical Radial Squashing (DD-SRad), which resolves this mismatch by computing a position-adaptive radius independently for each actuator, achieving tight alignment with the true per-joint feasible region. DD-SRad satisfies per-step hard constraints with probability~1, preserves well-conditioned gradients throughout training, and admits exact policy gradient backpropagation with zero runtime solver overhead. MuJoCo benchmark experiments demonstrate the highest task return at zero constraint violation -- matching the unconstrained upper bound -- with 30%--50% improvement in constraint-space coverage over spherical baselines. High-fidelity IsaacLab simulations with Unitree H1 and G1 humanoid robots confirm end-to-end optimality parameterized directly from official joint specifications, validating a systematic pathway from hardware datasheets to safe deployment.

AI.tldr: A per-joint adaptive radius squashing method (DD-SRad) for actuator-rate-constrained RL, aligning action-feasible sets with per-joint constraints; achieves hard per-step constraints with probability 1, preserves gradients, enables exact policy gradients with no solver overhead, and improves constraint-space coverage and task performance in simulated and hardware-ready settings.
AI.motivation: Actuator rate limits create heterogeneous, high-dimensional, per-joint feasible regions that are poorly captured by isotropic ball constraints used by QP or spherical projections, leading to constraint violations and under-explored action space as heterogeneity grows.
AI.method: Dynamic Decoupled Spherical Radial Squashing (DD-SRad) computes a position-adaptive radius independently for each actuator, aligning the constraint envelope with the per-joint feasible region. It ensures per-step hard constraints with probability 1, maintains well-conditioned gradients, and supports exact policy gradient backpropagation with zero runtime solver overhead.
AI.result: MuJoCo benchmarks show the highest task return with zero constraint violation, matching unconstrained upper bound, and a 30–50% improvement in constraint-space coverage over spherical baselines. High-fidelity IsaacLab simulations with Unitree H1 and G1 validate optimality parameterized from official joint specifications, supporting a practical path from hardware datasheets to safe deployment.
AI.conclusion: DD-SRad resolves the mismatch between the true per-joint feasible set and isotropic constraint assumptions, enabling safe, efficient policy learning under heterogenous actuator limits and providing a principled hardware-to-deployment pipeline.

title: Strat-Reasoner: Reinforcing Strategic Reasoning of LLMs in Multi-Agent Games
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in certain reasoning tasks, they struggle in multi-agent games where the final outcome depends on the joint strategies of all agents. In multi-agent games, the non-stationarity of other agents brings significant challenges on the evaluation of the reasoning process and the credit assignment over multiple reasoning steps. Existing single-agent reinforcement learning (RL) approaches and their multi-agent extensions fail to address these challenges as they do not incorporate other agents in the reasoning process. In this work, we propose Strat-Reasoner, a novel RL-based framework that improves LLMs' strategic reasoning ability in multi-agent games. We introduce a novel recursive reasoning paradigm where an agent's reasoning also integrates other agents' reasoning processes. To provide effective reward signals for the intermediate reasoning sequences, we employ a centralized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) comparison module to evaluate the reasoning quality. Finally, we compute an accurate hybrid advantage and develop a group-relative RL approach to optimize the LLM policy. Experimental results show that Strat-Reasoner substantially improves strategic abilities of underlying LLMs, achieving 22.1\% average performance improvements across various multi-agent games.

AI.tldr: A recursive, multi-agent reasoning framework (Strat-Reasoner) for LLMs that integrates other agents' reasoning, uses a centralized CoT-based reward signal, and group-relative RL to improve strategic performance in multi-agent games, achieving 22.1% average gains.
AI.motivation: In multi-agent games, non-stationarity of other agents hinders accurate reasoning evaluation and credit assignment. Existing single-agent RL and some multi-agent extensions fail to incorporate other agents’ reasoning into the decision process, limiting strategic capabilities.
AI.method: Proposes a recursive reasoning paradigm where an agent’s reasoning incorporates other agents’ reasoning. Introduces a centralized Chain-of-Thought (CoT) comparison module to evaluate reasoning quality and provide rewards for intermediate steps. Computes an accurate hybrid advantage and adopts a group-relative RL objective to optimize the LLM policy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows Strat-Reasoner substantially improves strategic abilities of underlying LLMs, with a 22.1% average performance improvement across multiple multi-agent games.
AI.conclusion: Strat-Reasoner effectively enhances LLM strategic reasoning in multi-agent settings through recursive reasoning that includes other agents’ thoughts, a centralized CoT-based reward signal, and group-relative RL optimization, yielding notable performance gains.

title: A cross-modal network for facial expression recognition
comment: Published in IEEE Transactions on Image Processing 2026
summary: Deep neural networks enriched with structural information have been widely employed for facial expression recognition tasks. However, these methods often depend on hierarchical information rather than face property to finish expression recognition. In this paper, we propose a cross-modal network with strong biological and structural information for facial expression recognition (CMNet). CMNet can respectively learn expression information via face symmetry on a whole face, left and right half faces to extract complementary facial features.
