To prevent negative effect of biological and structural information fusion, a salient facial information refinement module can obtain salient facial expression information to improve stability of an obtained facial expression classifier. To reduce reliance on unilateral facial features, a half-face alignment optimization mechanism is designed to align obtained expression information of learned left and right half faces. Our experimental results demonstrate that CMNet outperforms several novel methods, i.e., SCN and LAENet-SA for facial expression recognition. Codes can be obtained at https://github.com/hellloxiaotian/CMNet.

AI.tldr: A cross-modal network CMNet leverages facial symmetry across whole, left, and right halves with a salient information refinement module and half-face alignment to improve facial expression recognition, achieving superior results over SCN and LAENet-SA; code is publicly available.
AI.motivation: To exploit intrinsic biological and structural properties of faces (symmetry) for robust expression recognition, addressing potential over-reliance on hierarchical/global features.
AI.method: CMNet processes whole-face and left/right half-face information to learn complementary features. It includes a salient facial information refinement module to extract salient expression cues and a half-face alignment optimization mechanism to align left and right half-face features, reducing reliance on unilateral features. Fusion combines the modalities in a cross-modal framework.
AI.result: CMNet outperforms several recent methods (e.g., SCN and LAENet-SA) on facial expression recognition tasks; code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Integrating symmetry-based global and regional facial cues with refinement and alignment mechanisms yields improved recognition performance, suggesting robustness to variations and potential for wider applicability in expression analysis.

title: Balanced Aggregation: Understanding and Fixing Aggregation Bias in GRPO
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a central paradigm for improving reasoning and code generation in large language models, and GRPO-style training is widely adopted for its simplicity and effectiveness. However, an important design choice remains underexplored: how token-level policy gradient terms are aggregated within each sampled group. Standard GRPO uses sequence aggregation, while recent work has advocated token aggregation as a better alternative. We show that these two rules induce different optimization biases: token aggregation introduces sign-length coupling, while sequence aggregation implicitly downweights longer responses through sequence-level equal weighting. To address this tension, we propose \textbf{Balanced Aggregation (BA)}, a simple drop-in replacement that computes token-level means separately within the positive and negative subsets and then combines them with sequence-count-based weights. Experiments with Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Qwen3-1.7B on DAPO-17k and Polaris, evaluated on six reasoning and coding benchmarks, show that BA consistently improves training stability and final performance over standard token and sequence aggregation. Our analysis further shows that the relative effectiveness of token and sequence aggregation is largely governed by response-length variation and the positive-negative length gap, highlighting aggregation as a critical design dimension in GRPO-style RLVR.

AI.tldr: Balanced Aggregation (BA) for GRPO-style RLVR mitigates biases from token vs sequence gradient aggregation, improving stability and performance across multiple models/datasets by computing token-level means separately for positive/negative groups and weighting by sequence counts.
AI.motivation: The design choice of how token-level policy gradients are aggregated in GRPO-style RLVR (token vs sequence aggregation) induces distinct optimization biases, impacting training stability and final performance. A simple, effective aggregation that mitigates these biases is desirable.
AI.method: Introduce Balanced Aggregation (BA): compute token-level means separately within positive and negative response subsets, then combine using sequence-count-based weights; BA is a drop-in replacement for existing aggregation rules.
AI.result: BA consistently outperforms standard token and sequence aggregation in training stability and final performance. Experiments on Qwen2.5-Math-7B and Qwen3-1.7B (DAPO-17k and Polaris) across six reasoning and coding benchmarks show gains. The paper also analyzes that aggregation effectiveness depends on response-length variation and the positive-negative length gap, highlighting aggregation as a key design dimension.
AI.conclusion: Aggregation strategy is a critical design dimension in GRPO-style RLVR. BA offers a simple, effective remedy to bias introduced by token vs sequence aggregation, delivering stable training and better final results across varied tasks and models.

title: LEGO: LoRA-Enabled Generator-Oriented Framework for Synthetic Image Detection
summary: The rapid advancement of generative technologies has made synthetic images nearly indistinguishable from real ones, thereby creating an urgent need for robust detectors to counter misinformation. However, existing methods mainly rely on universal artifact features that are shared across multiple generators. We observe that as the diversity of generators increases, the overlap of these common features gradually decreases. This severely undermines model generalization. In contrast, focusing only on unique artifacts tends to cause overfitting to specific forgery patterns. To address this challenge, we propose LEGO (LoRA-Enabled Generator-Oriented Framework). The core mechanism of LEGO employs an MLP to modulate multiple LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) blocks, each pretrained to capture the unique artifacts of a specific generator, followed by attention-based feature fusion. Unlike conventional methods that seek a single universal solution, LEGO delegates unique artifact extraction to specialized LoRA modules by dividing its training procedure into two stages. Each LoRA module is individually trained on a single-generator dataset to learn generator-specific representations, then MLP and attention layers are trained on mixed datasets to dynamically regulate the contribution of each module. Benefiting from its modular yet robust design, LEGO can be naturally extended by incorporating new LoRA modules for adaptation to newly emerging next-generation datasets, while still achieving substantially better performance than prior SOTA methods with fewer than 30,000 training images, less than 10% of their training data, and only 5 epochs in each training stage.

AI.tldr: LEGO introduces a modular, generator-oriented detection framework that uses per-generator LoRA modules guided by an MLP to fuse features, enabling robust detection with limited data and fast training; scalable to new generators.
AI.motivation: Conventional detectors rely on universal artifact features that become less effective as generator diversity grows, leading to poor generalization. A balance between generator-specific artifacts and generalization is needed.
AI.method: Two-stage training: (1) train each LoRA module on data from a single generator to capture unique artifacts; (2) train an MLP and attention fusion on mixed data to regulate contributions from each module. Each module is pretrained and then integrated; the system is modular, enabling new LoRA blocks for new generators.
AI.result: Outperforms prior SOTA with substantially less data (under 30k images, <10% of baseline) and only 5 epochs per stage. Extensible by adding new LoRA modules; robust fusion via attention.
AI.conclusion: LEGO offers a scalable, data-efficient detector that generalizes across generator diversity via generator-oriented modularity, with potential for rapid adaptation to emerging synthetic-generation technologies.

title: Validity-Calibrated Reasoning Distillation
summary: Reasoning distillation aims to transfer multi-step reasoning capabilities from large language models to smaller, more efficient ones. While recent methods have shown promising gains, they typically rely on static teacher-student hierarchies and frame distillation as trajectory imitation. This is misaligned with the structure of reasoning, where intermediate steps are often locally under-specified: global correctness constrains the final answer, but does not uniquely determine each intermediate move. We propose validity-calibrated reasoning distillation, a framework that treats reasoning distillation as a problem of local learning-signal allocation rather than path alignment. Instead of enforcing token-level imitation, we compare the student's and teacher's proposed next-step actions under the same prefix and use their relative local validity to modulate the strength of the distillation update. This yields a dynamic, context-dependent supervision mechanism that preserves the teacher's structural guidance while adapting update strength to local reasoning quality. Across mathematical reasoning, code generation, and instruction-following benchmarks, our method consistently outperforms strong distillation baselines. These results indicate that effective LLM reasoning distillation is governed not by rigid trajectory imitation, but by principled, locally calibrated allocation of learning signal.

AI.tldr: Proposes validity-calibrated reasoning distillation, a local, signal-allocation approach to distilling multi-step reasoning from LLMs, rather than trajectory imitation. Uses comparison of next-step proposals under the same prefix and modulates updates by local validity to provide dynamic supervision.
AI.motivation: Traditional distillation relies on static teacher-student hierarchies and token-level trajectory imitation. In reasoning tasks, intermediate steps are under-specified; global correctness does not determine each local move. A need exists for a dynamic, locally calibrated supervision signal during distillation.
AI.method: Treats distillation as local learning-signal allocation. For each prefix, compare the teacher's and student's proposed next steps; assess their local validity and use that to modulate the distillation update strength, producing context-dependent supervision that preserves teacher structure while adapting to reasoning quality.
AI.result: Empirically, the approach yields consistent improvements over strong distillation baselines on math reasoning, code generation, and instruction-following benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Effective LLM reasoning distillation is governed by principled, locally calibrated learning signals rather than rigid trajectory imitation, enabling more flexible and accurate transfer of reasoning capabilities.

title: CRAFT: Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Driving Policies
summary: Open-loop imitation learning has advanced modern autonomous driving policy architectures, but closed-loop deployment remains vulnerable to policy-induced distribution shift. Existing post-training paradigms exhibit fundamental trade-offs: closed-loop RL fine-tuning provides grounded feedback from executed actions but is constrained by the sparsity of informative events, whereas counterfactual fine-tuning provides dense supervision over candidate futures but inherits bias from imperfect future estimates. We introduce Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (CRAFT), an on-policy framework that formulates closed-loop post-training as proxy-residual optimization. CRAFT uses group-normalized counterfactual advantages as a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages and aligns this proxy with the closed-loop world through grounded residual correction from interaction-critical events. To stabilize adaptation, CRAFT regularizes the online policy toward an EMA teacher via asymmetric KL self-distillation. Theoretically, CRAFT decomposes the real closed-loop policy gradient into proxy and residual terms under the same visited-state distribution, reducing residual variance with an aligned proxy while mitigating proxy bias through grounded residual approximation. Empirically, CRAFT achieves the strongest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures. Ablations, scaling behavior, stability analyses, and transfer results further validate the complementary roles of dense counterfactual proxy and grounded residual correction. Project page: https://currychen77.github.io/CRAFT.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is an on-policy post-training method that combines dense counterfactual proxies with grounded residual corrections to improve closed-loop imitation learning, achieving strong gains on Bench2Drive with stable, bias-controlled fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Addresses policy-induced distribution shift in closed-loop deployment for open-loop imitation learning. Balances sparse feedback from online RL fine-tuning with biased, dense supervision from counterfactuals; seeks data-efficient, stable post-training with theoretical grounding.
AI.method: Proposes Counterfactual-to-Interactive Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (CRAFT): on-policy proxy-residual optimization using group-normalized counterfactual advantages as a dense proxy for real closed-loop advantages; aligns proxy with actual closed-loop through grounded residual correction from interaction-critical events; stabilizes adaptation by asymmetric KL self-distillation toward an EMA teacher; provides a theoretical decomposition of the real closed-loop gradient into proxy and residual terms under the same visited-state distribution to reduce residual variance and mitigate proxy bias.
AI.result: Theoretically, real policy gradient = proxy term + residual term under the same state distribution, enabling variance reduction and bias mitigation. Empirically, CRAFT yields the strongest closed-loop gains on Bench2Drive across hierarchical planning, vision-language-action, and vocabulary-scoring architectures; ablations and transfer analyses validate the complementary roles of dense proxy and grounded residual corrections, along with scaling and stability assessments.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT delivers robust closed-loop post-training improvement by fusing dense counterfactual supervision with grounded residual learning, achieving significant performance gains and demonstrating stable transfer across architectures; the approach clarifies how to balance proxy bias with interactive correction in on-policy fine-tuning.

title: A Foundation Model for Zero-Shot Logical Rule Induction
comment: Camera-ready version accepted at IJCAI 2026, with full appendices
summary: Inductive Logic Programming (ILP) learns interpretable logical rules from data. Existing methods are transductive: their learned parameters are bound to specific predicates and require retraining for each new task. We introduce Neural Rule Inducer (NRI), a pretrained model for zero-shot rule induction. Rather than encoding literal identities, NRI represents literals using domain-agnostic statistical properties such as class-conditional rates, entropy, and co-occurrence, which generalize across variable identities and counts without retraining. The model consists of a statistical encoder and a parallel slot-based decoder. Parallel decoding preserves the permutation invariance of logical disjunction; an autoregressive decoder would instead impose an arbitrary clause order. Product T-norm relaxation makes rule execution differentiable, allowing end-to-end training on prediction accuracy alone. We evaluate NRI on rule recovery, robustness to label noise and spurious correlations, and zero-shot transfer to real-world benchmarks, and we believe this work opens up the possibility of foundation models for symbolic reasoning. Code and the reference checkpoint are available at https://github.com/phuayj/neural-rule-inducer.

AI.tldr: NRI presents zero-shot rule induction via domain-agnostic statistical literals, enabling generalization without retraining through a parallel slot-based decoder and differentiable rule execution; aims to enable foundation-model-like symbolic reasoning.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of transductive ILP where learned rules and parameters are tied to specific predicates, necessitating retraining for new tasks. Seek robust, scalable, zero-shot transfer to new domains while maintaining interpretability.
AI.method: Encode literals as domain-agnostic statistics (class-conditional rates, entropy, co-occurrence) using a statistical encoder; decode with a parallel slot-based decoder to preserve permutation invariance of disjunction; use Product T-norm relaxation to make rule execution differentiable; train end-to-end on prediction accuracy.
AI.result: Demonstrates rule recovery, robustness to label noise and spurious correlations, and zero-shot transfer to real-world benchmarks; code and checkpoint released for reproducibility; supports claim of moving toward foundation-model-like symbolic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: NRI offers a scalable, generalizable approach to rule induction that decouples literal identity from representation, enabling zero-shot transfer and differentiable training. It marks progress toward foundation models for symbolic reasoning, though further work is needed on interpretability, efficiency, and broader benchmarks.

title: Deep Reprogramming Distillation for Medical Foundation Models
summary: Medical foundation models pre-trained on large-scale datasets have shown powerful versatile performance. However, when adapting medical foundation models for specific medical scenarios, it remains the inevitable challenge due to the gap induced by the discrepancy between pre-training and downstream tasks, the real-world computation, and speed constraints. Relevant techniques that probably handle this challenge more or less suffer from some intrinsic limitations. For example, knowledge distillation (KD) assumes that teacher and student models share the same task, training strategy, and model structure family, while prevalent parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) fails to achieve personalized and lightweight deployment. Even the combination of PEFT and KD still struggles to resolve model structures and training strategies inconsistencies between teacher and student models, leading to inefficient knowledge transfer. In this study, we propose a novel framework called Deep Reprogramming Distillation (DRD) to combat the general adaptation challenge. Specifically, DRD introduces the novel reprogramming module that on the one side overcomes the domain and task discrepancy between pretraining and downstream scenarios, and on the other side builds the student-friendly efficient distillation from foundation models to lightweight downstream models. Furthermore, to mitigate variability under different training conditions, we design a centered kernel alignment (CKA) distillation method to promote robust knowledge transfer. Empirical results show that DRD surpasses previous PEFT and KD methods across 18 medical downstream tasks under different foundation models, covering various scenarios including 2D/3D classification and 2D/3D segmentation.

AI.tldr: A novel framework DRD enhances transfer from medical foundation models to lightweight downstream models by using a reprogramming module to bridge task/domain gaps and a centered kernel alignment (CKA) distillation for robust knowledge transfer, outperforming KD/PEFT across 18 medical tasks.
AI.motivation: Medical foundation models face domain/task mismatch and efficiency constraints when adapting to downstream clinical scenarios. Existing KD/PEFT methods have intrinsic limitations in task/structure alignment and personalization, hindering efficient knowledge transfer.
AI.method: Introduce Deep Reprogramming Distillation (DRD) with a reprogramming module to bridge pretraining and downstream tasks and a student-friendly distillation pathway; incorporate centered kernel alignment (CKA) distillation to stabilize knowledge transfer across training variations; evaluate on 18 medical downstream tasks including 2D/3D classification and 2D/3D segmentation using various foundation models.
AI.result: DRD surpasses previous PEFT and KD methods across 18 tasks and multiple foundation models, demonstrating improved performance and robustness in both 2D and 3D medical classification and segmentation tasks.
AI.conclusion: DRD provides an effective, personalized, and lightweight adaptation framework for medical foundation models, addressing domain/task discrepancies and enabling robust knowledge transfer through reprogramming and CKA-based distillation.

title: Efficient Handwriting-Based Alzheimer,s Disease Diagnosis Using a Low-Rank Mixture of Experts Deep Learning Framework
summary: Early and reliable detection of Alzheimer's disease (AD) is crucial for timely clinical intervention and improved patient management. It also supports the evaluation of emerging therapeutic strategies. In this paper, we propose a Low-Rank Mixture of Experts (LoRA-MoE) deep learning framework for Alzheimer's disease diagnosis based on handwriting analysis. Handwriting signals provide a non-invasive and scalable digital biomarker that captures subtle cognitive-motor impairments associated with early AD progression. The proposed architecture allows multiple experts to specialize in different handwriting patterns while sharing a common base network. This design enables efficient learning of general representations while reducing interference between experts. Each expert is equipped with lightweight low-rank adapters. This mechanism significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters compared with standard Mixture of Experts (MoE) models and improves training stability. The proposed framework is evaluated on the Diagnosis AlzheimeR WIth haNdwriting (DARWIN) dataset. Extensive experiments are conducted, including ablation studies on key architectural parameters such as hidden dimension size, number of experts, and LoRA rank. The method is compared with multilayer perceptron (MLP) and conventional MoE architectures. In addition, stacking ensemble strategies (StackMean and StackMax) are investigated to improve robustness and predictive performance. Experimental results show that the LoRA-MoE framework achieves powerful diagnostic performance while activating significantly fewer parameters during inference. These results highlight the potential of the proposed approach as an accurate and computationally efficient solution for handwriting-based Alzheimer's disease screening and digital health applications.

AI.tldr: A Low-Rank Mixture of Experts (LoRA-MoE) framework for Alzheimer's diagnosis from handwriting that achieves strong accuracy with far fewer trainable parameters, evaluated on the DARWIN dataset; stacks ensembles to boost robustness.
AI.motivation: Handwriting-based digital biomarkers offer a non-invasive, scalable means to detect early cognitive impairment. There is a need for efficient, stable learning architectures that can handle heterogeneous handwriting patterns while keeping computational costs low for clinical deployment.
AI.method: A shared-base neural network with multiple expert branches where each expert uses lightweight low-rank adapters (LoRA). The model is a two-tier LoRA-MoE that reduces parameter count and interference between experts. Stacking ensembles (StackMean, StackMax) are explored. Ablation studies vary hidden dimension, number of experts, and LoRA rank. Comparisons are made against MLP and conventional MoE on the DARWIN handwriting dataset.
AI.result: The LoRA-MoE framework delivers strong diagnostic performance while significantly reducing parameters and maintaining training stability. Ensemble methods enhance robustness. Ablation results identify key architectural knobs (hidden size, expert count, LoRA rank). Overall, the approach demonstrates a computationally efficient and accurate solution for handwriting-based AD screening.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-MoE is a promising, resource-efficient architecture for early AD detection from handwriting, with potential for scalable digital health applications and further gains through ensemble strategies and architectural tuning.

title: Dream-MPC: Gradient-Based Model Predictive Control with Latent Imagination
summary: State-of-the-art model-based Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches either use gradient-free, population-based methods for planning, learned policy networks, or a combination of policy networks and planning. Hybrid approaches that combine Model Predictive Control (MPC) with a learned model and a policy prior to leverage the advantages of both paradigms have shown promising results. However, these approaches typically rely on gradient-free optimization methods, which can be computationally expensive for high-dimensional control tasks. While gradient-based methods are a promising alternative, recent works have empirically shown that gradient-based methods often perform worse than their gradient-free counterparts. We propose Dream-MPC, a novel approach that generates few candidate trajectories from a rolled-out policy and optimizes each trajectory by gradient ascent using a learned world model, uncertainty regularization and amortization of optimization iterations over time by reusing previously optimized actions. Our results on 24 continuous control tasks show that Dream-MPC can significantly improve the performance of the underlying policy and can outperform gradient-free MPC and state-of-the-art baselines. We will open source our code and more at https://dream-mpc.github.io.

AI.tldr: Dream-MPC is a gradient-based planning approach that optimizes a few candidate trajectories generated from a policy rollout using a learned world model, incorporating uncertainty regularization and amortized iteration reuse to outperform gradient-free MPC on 24 continuous control tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the computational cost of gradient-free planning in high-dimensional control and the inconsistent performance of pure gradient-based methods; leverage the strengths of MPC, learned dynamics, and policy priors.
AI.method: Generate a small set of candidate trajectories from a rolled-out policy. For each trajectory, perform gradient ascent on a learned world model to optimize the trajectory under an uncertainty-regularized objective. Reuse previously optimized actions across iterations to amortize optimization cost.
AI.result: On 24 continuous control tasks, Dream-MPC significantly improves the performance of the underlying policy and outperforms gradient-free MPC and state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based planning with a learned world model and policy priors can match or exceed gradient-free MPC performance while offering computational advantages via trajectory reuse and amortization; code will be open-sourced.

title: On-line Learning in Tree MDPs by Treating Policies as Bandit Arms
comment: Accepted as a full paper in the Main Track of AAMAS 2026
summary: A Tree Markov Decision Problem (T-MDP) is a finite-horizon MDP with a starting state $s_{1}$, in which every state is reachable from $s_{1}$ through exactly one state-action trajectory. T-MDPs arise naturally as abstractions of decision making in sequential games with perfect recall, against stationary opponents. We consider the problem of on-line learning in T-MDPs, both in the PAC and the regret-minimisation regimes. We show that well-known bandit algorithms -- \textsc{Lucb} and \textsc{Ucb} -- can be applied on T-MDPs by treating each policy as an arm. The apparent technical challenge in this approach is that the number of policies is exponential in the number of states. Our main innovation is in the design of confidence bounds based on data shared by the policies, so that the bandit algorithms can yet be implemented with polynomial memory and per-step computation. We obtain instance-dependent upper bounds on sample complexity and regret that sum a ``gap term'' from every terminal state, rather than every policy. Empirically, our algorithms consistently outperform available alternatives on a suite of hidden-information games.

AI.tldr: Bandit-based online learning in Tree-MDPs via policy-armed LUCB/UCB with shared-data confidence bounds; achieves polynomial complexity and gap-based regret/sample bounds; empirically superior in hidden-information games.
AI.motivation: Address online learning in finite-horizon tree-structured MDPs (T-MDPs) arising as abstractions in sequential games with perfect recall, against stationary opponents; aim for PAC and regret guarantees with scalable algorithms.
AI.method: Treat each policy as an arm in a bandit framework; exploit shared data across policies to build confidence bounds that enable polynomial memory and per-step computation; apply LUCB/UCB; derive instance-dependent bounds summing gaps over terminal states.
AI.result: Theoretical bounds: instance-dependent sample complexity and regret with a terminal-state gap term; empirical results showing the proposed algorithms outperform existing methods on hidden-information games.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and efficiency of bandit algorithms on T-MDPs with exponential policy spaces by leveraging data-sharing confidence bounds; gaps defined per terminal state yield tight performance; empirical validation corroborates.

title: RemoteZero: Geospatial Reasoning with Zero Human Annotations
summary: Geospatial reasoning requires models to resolve complex spatial semantics and user intent into precise target locations for Earth observation. Recent progress has liberated the reasoning path from manual curation, allowing models to generate their own inference chains. Yet a final dependency remains: they are still supervised by human-annotated ground-truth coordinates. This leaves the reasoning process autonomous, but not its spatial endpoint, and prevents true self-evolution on abundant unlabeled remote sensing data. To break this bottleneck, we introduce RemoteZero, a box-supervision-free framework for geospatial reasoning. RemoteZero is motivated by a simple asymmetry: an MLLM is typically better at verifying whether a region satisfies a query than at directly generating precise coordinates. Leveraging this stronger discriminative ability, RemoteZero replaces geometric supervision with intrinsic semantic verification and enables GRPO training without box annotations. The resulting framework further supports iterative self-evolution, allowing the model to improve from unlabeled remote sensing imagery through its own verification signal. Experiments show that RemoteZero achieves competitive performance against strong supervised methods, demonstrating the potential of self-verifying training for geospatial reasoning localization.

AI.tldr: RemoteZero: a box-supervision-free framework for geospatial reasoning that uses discriminative verification to train without box annotations, enabling self-evolution on unlabeled remote sensing data; competitive with supervised methods.
AI.motivation: The bottleneck in geospatial reasoning is reliance on human-annotated coordinates for final spatial endpoints, which prevents fully autonomous learning on unlabeled data. By leveraging the discriminative strength of multilingual LLMs (MLLMs) in verifying region-query satisfaction, the approach aims to remove geometric supervision.
AI.method: Replace geometric supervision with intrinsic semantic verification using MLLM-based verification signals; train GRPO without box annotations; enable iterative self-evolution from unlabeled remote sensing imagery.
AI.result: Experiments show RemoteZero achieves competitive performance against strong supervised methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of self-verifying training for geospatial reasoning localization.
AI.conclusion: Self-verifying training can enable autonomous geospatial reasoning localization without box supervision and facilitate learning from unlabeled remote sensing data.

title: Time series causal discovery with variable lags
summary: Causal Bayesian Networks (CBNs) are a powerful tool for reasoning under uncertainty about complex real-world problems. Such problems evolve over time, responding to external shocks as they occur. To support decision-making, CBNs require a cause-and-effect map of the variables under consideration, known as the network's structure. Learning the graphical structure of a causal model from data remains challenging; learning it from time-series data is even harder because dependencies may arise at different time lags. Existing time-series causal discovery methods often assume a fixed lag window and do not explicitly optimise edge-specific lags. We propose a Tabu-based structure learning algorithm that searches for a time-ordered directed structure (i.e., where every edge respects time) while allowing edge-specific lags up to a specified maximum lag. The approach uses a decomposable BIC-based score with node-specific effective sample sizes and an explicit lag-length penalty encouraging parsimonious delay assignments while preserving efficient local score updates. We provide theoretical guarantees of validity and local optimality, and we also describe a parallel implementation for improved scalability. In simulations, the method recovered graph structure competitively and estimated lags accurately when true adjacencies were recovered. On a real-world UK COVID-19 policy dataset, the learnt structure was dominated by short delays while retaining a substantial minority of longer-lag dependencies, consistent with delayed behavioural and epidemiological effects.

AI.tldr: Tabu-based time-series causal structure learning for time-ordered DAGs with edge-specific lags up to a max lag; uses a decomposable BIC score with lag penalties and node-specific ESS; provides validity guarantees, parallelization, and shows competitive performance on simulations and real COVID-19 data.
AI.motivation: Learning time-lagged causal graphs is challenging due to variable delays and the need to optimize edge-specific lags within a fixed window. A flexible, scalable approach is needed to recover both structure and lag assignments from data.
AI.method: A Tabu-search algorithm over time-ordered directed graphs allowing edge-specific lags up to a specified maximum. It uses a decomposable BIC-based score with node-specific effective sample sizes and an explicit lag-length penalty to encourage parsimonious delays while enabling efficient local score updates. The algorithm has theoretical guarantees of validity and local optimality and supports parallel implementation for scalability.
AI.result: In simulations, the method competitively recovered graph structure and accurately estimated lags when adjacencies were recovered. On a real UK COVID-19 policy dataset, the learned structure was dominated by short delays but retained a substantial minority of longer-lag dependencies, reflecting delayed behavioural and epidemiological effects.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled, scalable approach to learning time-ordered causal graphs with edge-specific lags, with theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness on synthetic and real-world data.

title: Uno-Orchestra: Parsimonious Agent Routing via Selective Delegation
summary: Large language model (LLM) multi-agent systems typically rely on rigid orchestration, committing either to flat per-query routing or to hand-engineered task decomposition, so decomposition depth, worker choice, and inference budget are not jointly optimized under one objective. We introduce Uno-Orchestra, a unified orchestration policy that selectively decomposes a task and dispatches each subtask to an admissible (model, primitive) pair, with both decisions learned together from curated RL trajectories grounded in real worker interactions. Against 22 baselines on a 13-benchmark suite spanning math, code, knowledge, long-context, and agentic tool-use, Uno-Orchestra reaches 77.0% macro pass@1, roughly 16% above the strongest workflow baseline, at roughly an order of magnitude lower per-query cost, advancing the accuracy-efficiency frontier of selective delegation.

AI.tldr: Uno-Orchestra: a unified, learned orchestration policy for LLM multi-agent systems that jointly optimizes task decomposition depth, worker selection, and budget by learning from curated RL trajectories grounded in real worker interactions, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with lower per-query cost.
AI.motivation: To overcome rigid orchestration in LLM multi-agent systems, where task decomposition depth, worker selection, and inference budget are not jointly optimized, and to improve accuracy-cost efficiency through a unified, learnable policy.
AI.method: Introduce Uno-Orchestra, a unified orchestration policy that (i) selectively decomposes tasks and (ii) dispatches each subtask to an admissible (model, primitive) pair. Both decisions are learned jointly from curated RL trajectories grounded in real worker interactions. Evaluation against 22 baselines on a 13-benchmark suite spanning math, code, knowledge, long-context, and agentic tool-use.
AI.result: Achieves 77.0% macro pass@1, about 16% above the strongest workflow baseline, with roughly an order of magnitude reduction in per-query cost, on a 13-benchmark suite across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Uno-Orchestra advances the accuracy-efficiency frontier of selective delegation in LLM multi-agent systems by providing a unified, learned orchestration policy that optimizes task decomposition, worker assignment, and budget within a single objective.

title: StableI2I: Spotting Unintended Changes in Image-to-Image Transition
summary: In most real-world image-to-image (I2I) scenarios, existing evaluations primarily focus on instruction following and the perceptual quality or aesthetics of the generated images. However, they largely fail to assess whether the output image preserves the semantic correspondence and spatial structure of the input image. To address this limitation, we propose StableI2I, a unified and dynamic evaluation framework that explicitly measures content fidelity and pre--post consistency across a wide range of I2I tasks without requiring reference images, including image editing and image restoration. In addition, we construct StableI2I-Bench, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the accuracy of MLLMs on such fidelity and consistency assessment tasks. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that StableI2I provides accurate, fine-grained, and interpretable evaluations of content fidelity and consistency, with strong correlations to human subjective judgments. Our framework serves as a practical and reliable evaluation tool for diagnosing content consistency and benchmarking model performance in real-world I2I systems.

AI.tldr: StableI2I introduces a reference-free, dynamic evaluation framework for image-to-image (I2I) tasks that measures content fidelity and pre--post consistency, plus StableI2I-Bench to benchmark MLLMs on these metrics; it correlates well with human judgments and supports broad I2I scenarios, including editing and restoration.
AI.motivation: Current I2I evaluations emphasize perceptual quality/instruction following but neglect semantic content preservation and spatial structure without references. A need exists for reference-free, content-aware and task-agnostic evaluation to diagnose real-world I2I systems.
AI.method: Develop StableI2I to quantify content fidelity and pre--post consistency across diverse I2I tasks without reference images; create StableI2I-Bench to systematically evaluate MLLMs on fidelity/consistency tasks; conduct extensive experiments to assess accuracy, granularity, interpretability, and correlation with human judgments.
AI.result: StableI2I provides accurate, fine-grained, and interpretable evaluations of content fidelity and consistency; shows strong correlations with human subjective judgments; demonstrates robustness across a wide range of I2I tasks and real-world scenarios; StableI2I-Bench enables systematic benchmarking of MLLMs on fidelity/consistency assessments.
AI.conclusion: StableI2I is a practical and reliable evaluation tool for diagnosing content consistency and benchmarking model performance in real-world I2I systems, applicable to editing, restoration, and beyond.

title: Enhancing the interpretability of spatially variable N2O model predictions with soft sensors during wastewater treatment
summary: Model-based solutions for nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions from wastewater treatment plants (WWTP) are informed by operational datasets designed to control nutrient levels in liquid waste, coupled with dedicated campaigns for N2O measurements. We analysed how machine learning (ML) models predict disturbances to WWT operation and spatially variable N2O emissions. A real dataset was investigated to validate the modelling framework from N2O emissions predicted by four ML models (R2 = 0.79 - 0.89). Monitoring campaigns for N2O were simulated with a plant-wide mechanistic model to include additional sensors, site-level N2O datasets, and wastewater disturbances (n = 16). ML models were highly accurate (0.97 +- 0.02, n = 80), but the feature importance depended on the model, the scenario and the N2O measurement scale (reactor vs. WWTP). We argue that N2O soft sensor model predictions are limited to the measuring location and the methodological uncertainty of the dataset, which affect the interpretability of the model. Lastly, the analysis of the mechanistic model structure exposed interactions between autotrophic and heterotrophic pathways over nitric oxide which can overestimate aerobic nitrite production and bias the N2O pathway contributions.

AI.tldr: ML soft sensors for N2O in WWTPs show high predictive accuracy but are constrained by measurement location and data uncertainty; mechanistic insights reveal potential biases in N2O pathway attribution.
AI.motivation: Assess how ML models predict N2O emissions and how spatial variability and measurement campaigns affect predictions; evaluate the interpretability and reliability of ML-based soft sensors for WWTPs.
AI.method: Train four ML models on real data to predict disturbances and N2O emissions; validate with R^2 0.79–0.89; simulate plant-wide monitoring campaigns with a mechanistic model adding sensors and disturbances (n=16) and evaluate ML performance (0.97±0.02, n=80); analyze feature importance and mechanistic interactions between autotrophic/heterotrophic pathways via nitric oxide.
AI.result: ML models achieved high accuracy across both real and simulated data; feature importance varies by model and context; soft sensors are location-bound and sensitive to data uncertainty; mechanistic analysis suggests possible overestimation of aerobic nitrite production and biased attribution of N2O pathways.
AI.conclusion: N2O soft sensor interpretations must consider spatial measurement scales and dataset uncertainty; mechanistic interactions can bias pathway attribution, indicating need for careful validation and possibly multi-location data fusion.

title: Gaze4HRI: Zero-shot Benchmarking Gaze Estimation Neural-Networks for Human-Robot Interaction
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG 2026)
summary: While zero-shot appearance-based 3D gaze estimation offers significant cost-efficiency by directly mapping RGB images to gaze vectors, its reliability in Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) settings remains uncertain. Existing benchmarks frequently overlook fundamental HRI conditions, such as dynamic camera viewpoints and moving targets in video. Furthermore, current cross-dataset evaluations often suffer from a complexity gap, where methods trained on diverse datasets are tested on significantly smaller and less varied sets, failing to assess true robustness. To bridge these gaps, we introduce Gaze4HRI, a large-scale dataset (50+ subjects, 3,000+ videos, 600,000+ frames) designed to evaluate state-of-the-art performance against critical HRI variables: illumination, head-gaze conflict, as well as the motion of camera and gaze target in video. Our benchmark reveals that all evaluated methods fail in at least one condition, identifying steeply-downward gaze as a universal failure point. Notably, PureGaze trained on the ETH-X-Gaze dataset uniquely maintains resilience across all other conditions. These results challenge the recent focus in the literature on complex spatial-temporal modeling and Transformer-based architectures. Instead, our findings suggest that extensive data diversity, as exemplified by the ETH-X-Gaze dataset, serves as the primary driver of zero-shot robustness in unconstrained environments, while resilience-enhancing frameworks, such as PureGaze's self-adversarial loss for gaze feature purification, provide a substantial further improvement. Ultimately, this study establishes a rigorous benchmark that provides practical guidelines for practitioners as well as reshaping future research. The dataset and codes are available at https://gazeforhri.github.io.

AI.tldr: A new Gaze4HRI benchmark/dataset for zero-shot 3D gaze estimation in HRI, revealing robustness gaps under illumination, head-gaze conflict, and video motion; data diversity (ETH-X-Gaze) improves zero-shot robustness more than model complexity; PureGaze's self-adversarial loss offers further gains.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of HRI-specific benchmarks and realistic dynamic conditions for zero-shot gaze estimation; test cross-dataset robustness under varied illumination, head-gaze conflict, and camera/target motion.
AI.method: Create a large-scale dataset (50+ subjects, 3,000+ videos, 600k+ frames) with diverse HRI-relevant conditions; evaluate state-of-the-art zero-shot methods and cross-dataset transfer; compare results to dataset diversity (ETH-X-Gaze) and specific resilience techniques (PureGaze).
AI.result: All evaluated methods fail in at least one condition; steeply-downward gaze is a universal failure point; PureGaze (trained on ETH-X-Gaze) maintains resilience across conditions; data diversity is the main driver of zero-shot robustness; Transformer-based architectures do not necessarily yield improvements in this setting.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides practical guidelines for practitioners and suggests prioritizing diverse training data to improve zero-shot robustness in unconstrained HRI, with resilience-improving techniques like PureGaze offering additional gains; dataset and code released publicly.

title: Position: Embodied AI Requires a Privacy-Utility Trade-off
summary: Embodied AI (EAI) systems are rapidly transitioning from simulations into real-world domestic and other sensitive environments. However, recent EAI solutions have largely demonstrated advancements within isolated stages such as instruction, perception, planning and interaction, without considering their coupled privacy implications in high-frequency deployments where privacy leakage is often irreversible. This position paper argues that optimizing these components independently creates a systemic privacy crisis when deployed in sensitive settings, thereby advancing the position that privacy in EAI is a life cycle-level architectural constraint rather than a stage-local feature. To address these challenges, we propose Secure Privacy Integration in Next-generation Embodied AI (SPINE), a unified privacy-aware framework that treats privacy as a dynamic control signal governing cross-stage coupling throughout the entire EAI life cycle. SPINE decomposes the EAI pipeline into various stages and establishes a multi-criterion privacy classification matrix to orchestrate contextual sensitivity across stage boundaries. We conduct preliminary simulation and real-world case studies to conceptually validate how privacy constraints propagate downstream to reshape system behavior, illustrating the insufficiency of fragmented privacy patches and motivating future research directions into secure yet functional embodied AI systems. We detail the SPINE framework and case studies at https://github.com/rminshen03/EAI_Privacy_Position.

AI.tldr: Proposes SPINE, a lifecycle-level privacy framework for embodied AI that treats privacy as a dynamic cross-stage control signal, using a multi-criterion classification matrix, with preliminary simulations and real-world case studies to illustrate downstream privacy effects.
AI.motivation: Independent optimization of EAI components (perception, planning, interaction, etc.) risks systemic privacy leakage in sensitive deployments; privacy should be treated as a lifecycle architectural constraint rather than a stage-local feature.
AI.method: Decompose the EAI pipeline into stages; define a multi-criterion privacy classification matrix to govern stage-boundary contextual sensitivity; implement SPINE as a dynamic control signal that orchestrates cross-stage privacy constraints; validate conceptually through preliminary simulations and real-world case studies.
AI.result: Conceptual validation showing how privacy constraints propagate downstream and reshape system behavior; fragmentation patches are insufficient; SPINE and its case studies are detailed and a GitHub resource is provided.
AI.conclusion: Privacy in embodied AI is a lifecycle-level architectural constraint; SPINE offers a unified framework to integrate privacy across stages and across the life cycle, with future work needed on secure yet functional EAI systems.

title: Stream-T1: Test-Time Scaling for Streaming Video Generation
summary: While Test-Time Scaling (TTS) offers a promising direction to enhance video generation without the surging costs of training, current test-time video generation methods based on diffusion models suffer from exorbitant candidate exploration costs and lack temporal guidance. To address these structural bottlenecks, we propose shifting the focus to streaming video generation. We identify that its chunk-level synthesis and few denoising steps are intrinsically suited for TTS, significantly lowering computational overhead while enabling fine-grained temporal control. Driven by this insight, we introduced Stream-T1, a pioneering comprehensive TTS framework exclusively tailored for streaming video generation. Specifically, Stream-T1 is composed of three units: (1) Stream -Scaled Noise Propagation, which actively refines the initial latent noise of the generating chunk using historically proven, high-quality previous chunk noise, effectively establishes temporal dependency and utilizing the historical Gaussian prior to guide the current generation; (2) Stream -Scaled Reward Pruning, which comprehensively evaluates generated candidates to strike an optimal balance between local spatial aesthetics and global temporal coherence by integrating immediate short-term assessments with sliding-window-based long-term evaluations; (3) Stream-Scaled Memory Sinking, which dynamically routes the context evicted from KV-cache into distinct updating pathways guided by the reward feedback, ensuring that previously generated visual information effectively anchors and guides the subsequent video stream. Evaluated on both 5s and 30s comprehensive video benchmarks, Stream-T1 demonstrates profound superiority, significantly improving temporal consistency, motion smoothness, and frame-level visual quality.

AI.tldr: Stream-T1 introduces a streaming, test-time diffusion framework for video generation with three modules that leverage chunk-level synthesis and historical context to reduce computation while improving temporal coherence and visual quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome high candidate exploration costs and weak temporal guidance in test-time diffusion video generation, by shifting focus to streaming, chunk-level synthesis with few denoising steps that enable fine-grained temporal control and lower overhead.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Stream-Scaled Noise Propagation uses historical, high-quality previous chunk noise to refine the current chunk's initial latent noise, establishing temporal dependency via a Gaussian prior; (2) Stream-Scaled Reward Pruning evaluates candidates with immediate short-term aesthetics and sliding-window-based long-term temporal coherence to select an optimal result; (3) Stream-Scaled Memory Sinking routes context evicted from KV-cache along reward-guided pathways, ensuring previously generated visuals anchor subsequent frames.
AI.result: Evaluated on 5s and 30s benchmarks, Stream-T1 achieves significant improvements in temporal consistency, motion smoothness, and frame-level visual quality compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Stream-T1 is a pioneering, comprehensive test-time streaming framework for video generation that reduces computational overhead while enhancing temporal control and quality; its efficacy is demonstrated on longer video benchmarks with notable gains in coherence and stability.

title: AsymmetryZero: A Framework for Operationalizing Human Expert Preferences as Semantic Evals
summary: Much of the focus in RL today is on evaluation design: building meaningful evals that serve simultaneously as benchmarks and as well-defined reward signals for post-training. Yet, many real-world tasks are governed by subjective, procedural, and domain-specific requirements that are difficult to encode as exact-match targets or open-ended preference judgments frequently used in RL pipelines today. In this work, we present AsymmetryZero, a framework for operationalizing human expert preferences as semantic evals. AsymmetryZero represents each task as a stable evaluation contract that makes grading criteria explicit: what is being graded, how each criterion is judged, and how criterion-level decisions are aggregated into a task outcome. The same contract can be executed using Inspect for model-only evaluations, as well as the Harbor Framework for agentic evaluations, enabling comparable scores and shared audit artifacts across both settings. We argue that the central challenge in post-training today is the faithful encoding of expert requirements into the evaluation itself. To that end, we present a study using Harbor that holds task contracts fixed and compares a five-model frontier jury against a five-model compact jury across four frontier-class solvers (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4, Grok-4.20, Gemini-3.1-Pro). We find that criterion-level frontier-vs-compact agreement ranges from $75.9\%$ to $89.6\%$ (strict common-subset agreement: $77.8\%$ to $92.1\%$), while compact juries exhibit substantially higher internal dissent (3--2 split rate $28.7\%$--$32.4\%$) than frontier juries ($6.1\%$--$11.5\%$). Verifier traces further show that compact juries reduce per-criterion judging cost to roughly $4.2\%$--$5.6\%$ of frontier and latency to roughly $21.7\%$--$27.1\%$, even as aggregated task-level outcomes often remain comparatively stable.

AI.tldr: AsymmetryZero formalizes human expert requirements into stable evaluation contracts (semantic evals) usable for both model-only and agentic evaluations; empirical study shows compact juries save cost and latency with some increase in dissent, while task outcomes remain largely stable.
AI.motivation: RL evaluation design struggles to encode subjective, domain-specific requirements into exact targets or open-ended preferences; a stable, explicit evaluation contract is proposed to capture expert criteria and enable cross-setting comparability.
AI.method: Introduce stable evaluation contracts executed via Inspect (model-only) and Harbor (agentic). Conduct a Harbor-fixed-contract study comparing a five-model frontier jury against a five-model compact jury across four frontier-class solvers (Claude Opus 4.6, GPT-5.4, Grok-4.20, Gemini-3.1-Pro). Measure criterion-level frontier-compact agreement, strict common-subset agreement, internal dissent (3-2 splits), per-criterion judging cost, latency, and aggregated task outcomes.
AI.result: Criterion-level frontier-vs-compact agreement ranges from 75.9% to 89.6%, strict common-subset agreement from 77.8% to 92.1%. Compact juries show higher internal dissent (3-2 splits 28.7%–32.4%) than frontier juries (6.1%–11.5%). Verifier traces indicate per-criterion judging cost drops to ~4.2%–5.6% of frontier and latency to ~21.7%–27.1%, while aggregated task-level outcomes remain comparatively stable.
AI.conclusion: Stable, explicit evaluation contracts enable comparable scores and shared audit artifacts across model-only and agentic settings. Compact juries improve efficiency at the cost of increased internal dissent; the core challenge remains faithfully encoding expert requirements into the evaluation design.

title: Koopman Identification of Nonlinear Systems via Reservoir Liftings
summary: Learning tractable linear representations of nonlinear dynamical systems via Koopman operator theory is often hindered by dictionary selection, temporal memory encoding, and numerical ill-conditioning. Inspired by Reservoir Computing (RC) paradigm, this paper introduces the RC-Koopman framework, which interprets reservoir as a stateful, finite-dimensional Koopman dictionary whose temporal depth is explicitly controlled by its spectral radius. We show that the Echo State Property (ESP) guarantees well-posedness and favorable numerical conditioning of the lifted Koopman approximation. A correlation-based spectral radius selection algorithm aligns reservoir memory with dominant system timescales. Analysis reveals how the finite memory of the reservoir determines which Koopman eigenfunctions remain observable from the lifted features. Evaluation on synthetic benchmarks demonstrates that RC-Koopman achieves a favorable balance between reconstruction accuracy of the underlying nonlinear dynamics and dynamical stability, compared to Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) and Hankel-based lifting approaches. Code available at: https://github.com/NEAR-the-future/RC-Koopman.git

AI.tldr: RC-Koopman: a reservoir-based finite-memory Koopman lifting with ESP guarantees, enabling stable, accurate nonlinear dynamics reconstruction, outperforming EDMD and Hankel approaches on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Overcome dictionary selection, memory encoding, and ill-conditioning in Koopman approximations by using reservoir computing to create a tunable, stable, finite-dimensional Koopman dictionary.
AI.method: Model reservoir as a stateful Koopman dictionary; enforce Echo State Property for well-posed lifted dynamics; select spectral radius via correlation with dominant system timescales; analyze observability of Koopman eigenfunctions given finite memory; empirical comparisons with EDMD and Hankel lifting; code release.
AI.result: Achieves favorable reconstruction accuracy and dynamical stability; improved numerical conditioning over EDMD/Hankel baselines on synthetic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: RC-Koopman provides a principled, memory-tuned, stable Koopman lifting with practical benefits; reservoir memory can be configured to align with system dynamics; code available.

title: Executable World Models for ARC-AGI-3 in the Era of Coding Agents
summary: We evaluate an initial coding-agent system for ARC-AGI-3 in which the agent maintains an executable Python world model, verifies it against previous observations, refactors it toward simpler abstractions as a practical proxy for an MDL-like simplicity bias, and plans through the model before acting. The system is intentionally direct: it uses a scripted controller, predefined world-model interfaces, verifier programs, and a plan executor, but no hand-coded game-specific logic. We report results on the 25 public ARC-AGI-3 games. Each recorded playthrough uses a fresh agent instance with no access to previous playthrough-specific files or conversation state. Most games have a single recorded playthrough; for a few games, we report multiple independent fresh-agent playthroughs to expose run-to-run variability. The agent fully solved 7 games, achieved a Relative Human Action Efficiency greater than 75%, on 6 games, and obtained a mean per-game RHAE of 32.58%. Because the system uses no game-specific code, it can serve as a game-general baseline for ARC-AGI-3. Performance on the private validation set remains to be tested. Overall, the results provide preliminary evidence that verifier-driven executable world models are a promising approach for ARC-AGI-3 agents.

AI.tldr: Verifier-driven executable world-model agent baseline for ARC-AGI-3; shows 7 full solvings, 6 games with RHAE>75%, mean RHAE 32.58%; no game-specific code; preliminary but promising.
AI.motivation: Establish a generalizable baseline for ARC-AGI-3 using executable world models and verifiers to enable planning before acting, without hand-coded game logic.
AI.method: Build an agent with a Python world model, verifiers, planner, and scripted controller; verify against observations, refactor toward simpler abstractions (MDL-like bias); evaluate on 25 ARC-AGI-3 games with fresh instances; report per-game metrics.
AI.result: 7 games fully solved; RHAE>75% on 6 games; mean RHAE 32.58%; 25 games tested; results indicate potential; most games single run; private validation pending.
AI.conclusion: Verifier-driven executable world models offer a promising, generalizable approach for ARC-AGI-3; results are preliminary but encouraging; further validation on private set and more diverse tasks recommended.

title: Information Coordination as a Bridge: A Neuro-Symbolic Architecture for Reliable Autonomous Driving Scene Understanding
summary: Reliable autonomous driving requires scene understanding that is semantically consistent across heterogeneous sensors and verifiable at the reasoning stage. However, many recent LLM-driven driving systems attach the language model as a post-processor and force it to reason over redundant or conflicting perception outputs, which can amplify hallucinated entities and unsafe conclusions. This paper proposes InfoCoordiBridge, a BEV-centric neuro-symbolic architecture that inserts an explicit coordination bridge between perception and language reasoning. InfoCoordiBridge comprises (i) a unified multi-agent perception layer that outputs typed structured facts together with modality-focused synopses, (ii) an ICA module that aligns and fuses multi-source outputs into a single SceneSummary, and (iii) an SSRE module that performs SceneSummary-grounded reasoning with verification. Experiments on nuScenes and Waymo show that ICA preserves competitive 3D detection accuracy while substantially improving fusion consistency, reducing redundancy to below 1% and achieving about 98% attribute agreement. On NuScenes-QA and a template-aligned Waymo-QA benchmark, SSRE improves factual grounding and reduces hallucinated entity mentions compared with representative VLM and agentic baselines. Overall, by coordinating multi-sensor outputs into a single conflict-aware SceneSummary before prompting, InfoCoordiBridge prevents redundant and cross-modally inconsistent perception evidence from propagating into high-level reasoning.

AI.tldr: BEV-centric neuro-symbolic InfoCoordiBridge coordinates multi-sensor outputs into a single SceneSummary to enable reliable reasoning and reduce hallucinations in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: LLM-driven driving systems that use perception outputs as-is risk conflicting evidence and hallucinations; there is a need for semantically consistent, verifiable reasoning across heterogeneous sensors.
AI.method: Unified multi-agent perception layer outputs typed structured facts and modality-focused synopses; ICA aligns/fuses multi-source outputs into a single SceneSummary; SSRE performs SceneSummary-grounded reasoning with verification.
AI.result: ICA preserves competitive 3D detection accuracy while substantially improving fusion consistency, reducing redundancy to below 1% and achieving about 98% attribute agreement. On NuScenes-QA and Waymo-QA benchmarks, SSRE improves factual grounding and reduces hallucinated entity mentions compared with representative VLM and agentic baselines.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating multi-sensor outputs into a single, conflict-aware SceneSummary before prompting prevents propagation of conflicting evidence into high-level reasoning, improving reliability of autonomous-driving perception-language systems.

title: FASQ: Flexible Accelerated Subspace Quantization for Calibration-Free LLM Compression
summary: Compressing large language models (LLMs) for deployment on commodity GPUs remains challenging: conventional scalar quantization is limited to fixed bit-widths (e.g., 8/4/3-bit), offers only a few discrete compression points, and typically requires calibration data. We present FASQ (Flexible Accelerated Subspace Quantization), a calibration-free framework that applies product quantization to LLM weight matrices. By tuning two parameters, sub-vector size and codebook cardinality, FASQ exposes a continuous design space spanning 27-49% of the original FP16 model size, filling compression gaps that fixed-bit schemes cannot reach. On Meta-Llama-3-8B, FASQ surpasses 4-bit GPTQ and AWQ in accuracy (67.1-67.7 avg.) at 37-42% model size, with consistent results on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3.5-9B-Base. To make product quantization practical at inference time, we design custom CUDA kernels: a LUT-free direct-compute GEMV for decode and an output-stationary double-buffered LUT GEMM for prefill, both with split-K parallelism. On an RTX~3090, FASQ achieves 45.2 tok/s decode at effective 4-bit (2.56x memory reduction) and 51.8 tok/s at effective 3-bit (2.80x), both surpassing FP16 tensor-core performance (43.9 tok/s) and delivering 1.6 to 1.8x the throughput of AWQ, 2.5 to 2.5x of GPTQ, and 4.3 to 5x of RTN. FASQ is the only compressed method that accelerates decode beyond FP16, offering calibration-free compression, continuous size-quality trade-offs, and real-time inference on a single consumer GPU.

AI.tldr: FASQ is a calibration-free product-quantization framework for LLM weights offering a continuous 27-49% size retention design space, improved accuracy over fixed-bit methods, and real-time decode throughput beyond FP16 on consumer GPUs via custom CUDA kernels.
AI.motivation: Overcome fixed-bit-depth limitations of scalar quantization (discrete compression points, need for calibration) to enable fine-grained, calibration-free weight compression and real-time inference on commodity GPUs.
AI.method: Apply product quantization to LLM weight matrices with tunable sub-vector size and codebook cardinality; design calibration-free codebooks; implement custom CUDA kernels (LUT-free direct-compute GEMV for decode; output-stationary double-buffered LUT GEMM for prefill) with split-K parallelism.
AI.result: On Meta-Llama-3-8B, FASQ achieves 67.1-67.7 avg accuracy at 37-42% model size, outperforming 4-bit GPTQ and AWQ; consistent results on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3.5-9B-Base. On RTX 3090, decode rates of 45.2 tok/s at effective 4-bit (2.56x memory reduction) and 51.8 tok/s at effective 3-bit (2.80x), surpassing FP16 tensor-core performance (43.9 tok/s) and outperforming AWQ, GPTQ, RTN by factors (1.6-1.8x, 2.5x, 4.3-5x respectively).
AI.conclusion: FASQ provides continuous size-quality trade-offs with calibration-free compression and decode-time acceleration on consumer GPUs, enabling practical, real-time LLM deployment on a single consumer GPU.

title: LongSeeker: Elastic Context Orchestration for Long-Horizon Search Agents
summary: Long-horizon search agents must manage a rapidly growing working context as they reason, call tools, and observe information. Naively accumulating all intermediate content can overwhelm the agent, increasing costs and the risk of errors. We propose that effective context management should be adaptive: parts of the agent's trajectory are maintained at different levels of detail depending on their current relevance to the task. To operationalize this principle, we introduce Context-ReAct, a general agentic paradigm for elastic context orchestration that integrates reasoning, context management, and tool use in a unified loop. Context-ReAct provides five atomic operations: Skip, Compress, Rollback, Snippet and Delete, which allow the agent to dynamically reshape its working context, preserving important evidence, summarizing resolved information, discarding unhelpful branches, and controlling context size. We prove that the Compress operator is expressively complete, while the other specialized operators provide efficiency and fidelity guarantees that reduce generation cost and hallucination risk. Building on this paradigm, we develop LongSeeker, a long-horizon search agent fine-tuned from Qwen3-30B-A3B on 10k synthesized trajectories. Across four representative search benchmarks, LongSeeker achieves 61.5% on BrowseComp and 62.5% on BrowseComp-ZH, substantially outperforming Tongyi DeepResearch (43.2% and 46.7%) and AgentFold (36.2% and 47.3%). These results highlight the potential of adaptive context management, showing that agents can achieve more reliable and efficient long-horizon reasoning by actively shaping their working memory.

AI.tldr: Adaptive context management for long-horizon search agents via Context-ReAct; introduces a five-operation framework (Skip, Compress, Rollback, Snippet, Delete) for elastic working memory; shows Compress is expressively complete and LongSeeker outperforms baselines on BrowseComp benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To prevent working-context growth from overwhelming long-horizon reasoning, causing higher costs and errors, by enabling adaptive, detail-aware management of the agent’s trajectory and evidence.
AI.method: Propose Context-ReAct, a general agentic paradigm integrating reasoning, context management, and tool use; define five atomic operations for shaping context; prove theoretical completeness of Compress; instantiate LongSeeker by fine-tuning from Qwen3-30B-A3B on 10k synthesized trajectories; evaluate on BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH benchmarks against baselines.
AI.result: Empirical results show LongSeeker achieving 61.5% and 62.5% on BrowseComp and BrowseComp-ZH, respectively, outperforming Tongyi DeepResearch (43.2%, 46.7%) and AgentFold (36.2%, 47.3%).
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, elastic context management substantially improves reliability and efficiency of long-horizon reasoning by actively shaping working memory, reducing generation cost and hallucination risk.

title: Example-Based Object Detection
summary: In recent years, object detection has achieved significant progress, especially in the field of open-vocabulary object detection. Unlike traditional methods that rely on predefined categories, open-vocabulary approaches can detect arbitrary objects based on human-provided prompts. With the advancement of prompt-based detection techniques, models such as SAM3 can even outperform some category-specific detectors trained on particular datasets without requiring additional training on those datasets. However, despite these advancements, false positives and false negatives still occur. In practical engineering applications, persistent misdetections or missed detections of the same object are unacceptable. Yet retraining the model every time such errors occur incurs substantial costs in terms of human effort, computational resources, and time. Therefore, how to leverage existing false positive and false negative samples to prevent such errors from recurring remains a highly challenging and urgent problem. To address this issue, we propose EBOD (Example-Based Object Detection), which integrates a prompt-based detector (SAM3) with robust feature matching modules (DINOv3 and LightGlue). The proposed framework effectively suppresses the repeated occurrence of false positives and false negatives by leveraging previous error examples, without requiring additional model retraining. Code is available at https://github.com/sunzx97/examples_based_object_detection.

AI.tldr: EBOD uses example-based memory to suppress recurring FP/FN in open-vocabulary detection by integrating SAM3 with DINOv3 and LightGlue, without retraining; code available.
AI.motivation: Persistent misdetections in open-vocabulary detectors and high cost of retraining motivate leveraging previous error exemplars to reduce future errors.
AI.method: Integrates a prompt-based detector (SAM3) with robust feature matching (DINOv3, LightGlue); stores and utilizes error exemplars (FP/FN) to filter or re-rank detections, preventing repeat errors; no additional model training required.
AI.result: The framework effectively reduces repeated FP and FN occurrences using prior error examples; stated without retraining; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Example-based error suppression is a practical, low-cost strategy for improving open-vocabulary object detection in real-world deployments.

title: HERCULES: Hardware-Efficient, Robust, Continual Learning Neural Architecture Search
summary: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) has emerged as a powerful framework for automatically discovering neural architectures that balance accuracy and efficiency. However, as AI transitions from static benchmarks to real-world deployment, the traditional focus on hardware-aware efficiency is no longer sufficient. We observe that modern NAS methods, especially those that target edge AI, are evolving to address a triple objective: Efficiency, Robustness, and Continual Learning. While efficiency ensures feasibility in resource-constrained environments, robustness guarantees reliability under environmental variabilities, and continual learning enables adaptation to sequential tasks without catastrophic forgetting. We propose a taxonomy of NAS approaches through this triple lens, distinguishing between methods targeting resource optimization, environmental resilience, and architectural plasticity. This unified perspective reveals that these axes, though often studied in isolation, are mutually reinforcing. Building on this taxonomy, we map the current landscape of these NAS methods into a new framework called Hardware-Efficient, Robust, and ContinUal LEarning Search (HERCULES). We define the desiderata, the twelve labours of HERCULES, addressing the non-trivial challenge of balancing an adequate search-space exploration with the immense computational costs of a multi-objective NAS, accounting for these crucial objectives of current AI systems. By identifying critical gaps in existing research, this survey outlines a roadmap toward integrated algorithmic, architectural, and hardware-software co-design for truly deployable, lifelong-learning AI systems.

AI.tldr: A survey introducing HERCULES, a unified NAS framework for Hardware-Efficient, Robust, and ContinUal Learning, with a taxonomy along Efficiency, Robustness, and Continual Learning, twelve labours, and a roadmap for integrated co-design for lifelong AI systems.
AI.motivation: Traditional NAS prioritizes accuracy and hardware efficiency; however, real-world AI requires systems that are resource-aware, robust to environmental changes, and capable of continual learning. This motivates a triple-objective NAS and lifelong deployment, especially for edge AI.
AI.method: Proposes a taxonomy of NAS methods across three axes (resource optimization, environmental resilience, architectural plasticity). Maps current NAS work into a new framework (HERCULES), defines desiderata, and outlines twelve labours to balance search-space exploration with multi-objective cost. Provides a roadmap and identifies gaps.
AI.result: A cohesive survey framework that unifies efficiency, robustness, and continual learning in NAS. Delivers a taxonomy, a conceptual framework (HERCULES), and a set of actionable research directions rather than empirical results.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizes that the three axes are mutually reinforcing and advocates integrated algorithmic, architectural, and hardware-software co-design to achieve deployable, lifelong-learning AI systems; highlights critical gaps and a roadmap for future work.

title: A large language model-type architecture for high-dimensional molecular potential energy surfaces
summary: Computing high-dimensional potential energy surfaces for molecular systems and materials is considered to be a great challenge in computational chemistry with potential impact in a range of areas including the fundamental prediction of reaction rates. In this paper, we design and discuss an algorithm that has similarities to large language models in generative AI and natural language processing. Specifically, we represent a molecular system as a graph which contains a set of nodes, edges, faces, etc. Interactions between these sets, which represent molecular subsystems in our case, are used to construct the potential energy surface for a reasonably sized chemical system with 51 nuclear dimensions. For this purpose, a family of neural networks that pertain to the graph-theoretically obtained subsystems get the job done for this 51 nuclear dimensional system. We then ask if this same family of lower-dimensional graph-based neural networks can be transformed to provide accurate predictions for a 186-dimensional potential energy surface. We find that our algorithm does provide accurate results for this larger-dimensional problem with sub-kcal/mol accuracy for the higher-dimensional potential energy surface problem. Indeed, as a result of these developments, here we produce the first efforts towards a full-dimensional potential energy surface for the protonated 21-water cluster (186 nuclear dimensions) at CCSD level accuracy.

AI.tldr: Graph-based neural networks operating on molecular subsystems enable scalable, accurate PESs for high-dimensional systems, achieving sub-kcal/mol accuracy and CCSD-level results for protonated 21-water cluster (186D), advancing toward a full-dimensional PES.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional PESs for molecular systems are computationally expensive; accurate reaction-rate predictions require scalable methods. A graph/subsystem approach with neural networks may reduce dimensionality while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Represent the molecular system as a graph (nodes, edges, faces) with interactions among subsystems. Use a family of graph-based neural networks specialized to these subsystems to construct the PES for a 51D system, then apply the same networks to predict a 186D PES, achieving accurate results.
AI.result: Sub-kcal/mol accuracy for the 186D PES; first CCSD-level full-dimensional PES effort for the protonated 21-water cluster (186 nuclear dimensions).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that subsystem-based graph neural networks can scale to very high dimensions and reach near CCSD accuracy, enabling progression toward full-dimensional PESs for sizable molecular systems.

title: DiffCap-Bench: A Comprehensive, Challenging, Robust Benchmark for Image Difference Captioning
summary: Image Difference Captioning (IDC) generates natural language descriptions that precisely identify differences between two images, serving as a key benchmark for fine-grained change perception, cross-modal reasoning, and image editing data construction. However, existing benchmarks lack diversity and compositional complexity, and standard lexical-overlap metrics (e.g., BLEU, METEOR) fail to capture semantic consistency or penalize hallucinations, which together prevent a comprehensive and robust evaluation of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on IDC. To address these gaps, we introduce DiffCap-Bench, a comprehensive IDC benchmark covering ten distinct difference categories to ensure diversity and compositional complexity.
