Furthermore, we propose an LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation protocol grounded in human-validated Difference Lists, enabling a robust assessment of models' ability to both capture and describe visual changes. Through extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art MLLMs, we reveal significant performance gaps between proprietary and open-source models, highlight the critical importance of reasoning capability, and identify clear limitations in model scaling. Our framework also demonstrates strong alignment with human expert judgments and strong correlation with downstream image editing data construction quality. These findings establish DiffCap-Bench as both a reliable IDC evaluation framework and a practical predictor of downstream utility. The benchmark and code will be made publicly available to support further research.

AI.tldr: DiffCap-Bench proposes a diverse, compositional IDC benchmark with ten difference categories and an LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation protocol, showing gaps between proprietary and open-source MLLMs and strong alignment with human judgments and downstream image-editing data quality; aims to be a reliable IDC evaluator and predictor of practical utility.
AI.motivation: IDC benchmarks currently lack diversity and compositional complexity and rely on lexical-overlap metrics that miss semantic accuracy and hallucinations, hindering robust multimodal model evaluation.
AI.method: Construct DiffCap-Bench with ten difference categories to ensure diversity; develop an LLM-as-a-Judge framework using human-validated Difference Lists to assess model outputs; conduct extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art MLLMs (proprietary vs open-source), analyze reasoning ability and scalability, and validate alignment with human judgments and downstream image-editing data construction quality.
AI.result: Notable performance gaps between proprietary and open-source models; reasoning capability and model scaling emerge as critical factors; the framework achieves strong alignment with human expert judgments and correlates with downstream data quality for image editing; DiffCap-Bench demonstrates predictive utility for downstream tasks and will be publicly released.
AI.conclusion: DiffCap-Bench provides a reliable IDC evaluation framework and a practical predictor of downstream utility, with public availability enabling broader research and benchmarking of MLLMs on image-dine difference captioning tasks.

title: Learning reveals invisible structure in low-rank RNNs
summary: Learning in neural systems arises from synaptic changes that reshape the representations underlying behavior. While low-rank recurrent neural networks (RNNs) have emerged as a powerful framework for linking connectivity to function, a theoretical understanding of their learning process remains elusive. Here, we extend the low-rank framework from activity to learning by deriving gradient-descent dynamics directly in a reduced overlap space. We formulate a closed-form, low-dimensional system of ODEs that governs learning in this space, exact for linear RNNs and asymptotically exact for nonlinear RNNs in the large-$N$ Gaussian limit. Central to our analysis is a distinction between two classes of overlaps: loss-visible overlaps, which fully determine network activity, output, and loss, and loss-invisible overlaps, which do not affect function but are required to describe learning. We illustrate the consequences of this decomposition through two phenomena. First, we show that learning can serve as a perturbation that exposes differences in connectivity between functionally equivalent networks. Second, we show that loss-invisible overlaps can act as memory variables that encode training history, and characterize the conditions under which this occurs. Finally, we present several testable predictions for biological learning experiments derived from our theory.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework that reduces learning dynamics in low-rank RNNs to a small set of ODEs over overlaps, separating loss-visible and loss-invisible components to analyze how learning proceeds and encodes training history, with testable biological predictions.
AI.motivation: To achieve a tractable, principled understanding of learning in low-rank RNNs by deriving gradient-descent dynamics in a reduced overlap space, linking connectivity to function.
AI.method: Derive gradient-descent dynamics directly in overlap space, yielding a closed-form, low-dimensional system of ODEs. Exact for linear RNNs and asymptotically exact for nonlinear RNNs in the large-N Gaussian limit. Introduce a dichotomy between loss-visible overlaps (determine activity, output, loss) and loss-invisible overlaps (do not affect function but are needed for learning).
AI.result: A low-dimensional ODE system governs learning in reduced overlap space. Loss-visible overlaps fully determine network activity, output, and loss; loss-invisible overlaps influence learning without affecting function. Learning can reveal connectivity differences between functionally equivalent networks and memory-like behavior can be encoded in loss-invisible overlaps. The framework yields testable predictions for biological learning experiments.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a principled, reduced-dimensional representation of learning in low-rank RNNs, clarifying the roles of observable vs. latent overlaps and offering concrete, testable predictions for neuroscience experiments.

title: Learning Reconstructive Embeddings in Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces via the Representer Theorem
summary: Motivated by the growing interest in representation learning approaches that uncover the latent structure of high-dimensional data, this work proposes new algorithms for reconstruction-based manifold learning within Reproducing-Kernel Hilbert Spaces (RKHS). Each observation is first reconstructed as a linear combination of the other samples in the RKHS, by optimizing a vector form of the Representer Theorem for their autorepresentation property. A separable operator-valued kernel extends the formulation to vector-valued data while retaining the simplicity of a single scalar similarity function. A subsequent kernel-alignment task projects the data into a lower-dimensional latent space whose Gram matrix aims to match the high-dimensional reconstruction kernel, thus transferring the auto-reconstruction geometry of the RKHS to the embedding. Therefore, the proposed algorithms represent an extended approach to the autorepresentation property, exhibited by many natural data, by using and adapting well-known results of Kernel Learning Theory. Numerical experiments on both simulated (concentric circles and swiss-roll) and real (cancer molecular activity and IoT network intrusions) datasets provide empirical evidence of the practical effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: Kernel-based autorepresentation for reconstruction-based manifold learning in RKHS with a vector-valued extension; embedding via kernel alignment to preserve autoreconstruction geometry.
AI.motivation: Uncover latent manifold structure in high-dimensional data by extending representation learning to RKHS and vector-valued data; transfer reconstruction geometry to a low-dimensional embedding.
AI.method: For each observation, reconstruct as a linear combination of others in RKHS using a vectorized Representer Theorem; employ a separable operator-valued kernel to handle vector-valued data; perform kernel alignment to learn a low-dimensional embedding whose Gram matrix matches the reconstruction kernel.
AI.result: Empirical validation on concentric circles, swiss-roll, cancer molecular activity, and IoT intrusion data shows the approach preserves manifold structure and yields effective latent representations.
AI.conclusion: Extends autorepresentation via Kernel Learning Theory; provides a practical, kernel-based framework for reconstruction-based manifold learning with a scalable alignment step; future work may address scalability, kernel choices, and broader data modalities.

title: SpecPL: Disentangling Spectral Granularity for Prompt Learning
summary: Existing prompt learning for VLMs exhibits a modality asymmetry, predominantly optimizing text tokens while still relying on frozen visual encoder as holistic extractor and neglecting the spectral granularity essential for fine-grained discrimination. To bridge this, we introduce Disentangling Spectral Granularity for Prompt Learning (SpecPL), which approaches prompt learning from a novel spectral perspective via Counterfactual Granule Supervision. Specifically, we leverage a frozen VAE to decompose visual signals into semantic low-frequency bands and granular high-frequency details. A frozen Visual Semantic Bank anchors text representations to universal low-frequency invariants, mitigating overfitting. Crucially, fine-grained discrimination is driven by counterfactual granule training: by permuting high-frequency signals, we compel the model to explicitly distinguish visual granularity from semantic invariance. Uniquely, SpecPL serves as a universal plug-and-play booster, revitalizing text-oriented baselines like CoOp and MaPLe via visual-side guidance. Experiments on 11 benchmarks demonstrate competitive state-of-the-art performance, achieving a new performance ceiling of 81.51\% harmonic-mean accuracy. These results validate that spectral disentanglement with counterfactual supervision effectively bridges the gap in the stability-generalization trade-off. Code is released at https://github.com/Mlrac1e/SpecPL-Prompt-Learning.

AI.tldr: SpecPL introduces spectral-granularity disentanglement for prompt learning in VLMs, using a frozen VAE to separate semantic low-frequency and high-frequency details, with counterfactual granule supervision; acts as a universal plug-in booster for text-oriented prompts, achieving strong results across 11 benchmarks (81.51% harmonic mean).
AI.motivation: Tackle modality asymmetry in prompt learning for VLMs by enabling fine-grained visual discrimination beyond the frozen visual encoder, through spectral decomposition and counterfactual supervision.
AI.method: Employ a frozen VAE to decompose visuals into semantic low-frequency and granular high-frequency components. Use a frozen Visual Semantic Bank to anchor text representations to low-frequency invariants. Train with counterfactual granule supervision by permuting high-frequency signals to enforce discrimination between granularity and invariance. SpecPL is designed as a universal plug-in booster that can enhance existing text-centric baselines like CoOp and MaPLe with visual-side guidance.
AI.result: On 11 benchmarks, achieves competitive state-of-the-art performance with a new high of 81.51% harmonic-mean accuracy. Demonstrates improved stability-generalization trade-off via spectral disentanglement and counterfactual supervision.
AI.conclusion: Spectral disentanglement with counterfactual supervision effectively bridges the gap in prompt-learning for VLMs, and SpecPL provides a versatile, plug-and-play enhancement for text-led prompts by leveraging visual spectral information.

title: Simultaneous CNN Approximation on Manifolds with Applications to Boundary Value Problems
summary: This paper develops convolutional neural network (CNN) methods for simultaneous approximation and elliptic boundary value problems on compact Riemannian manifolds. We establish simultaneous Sobolev approximation results for single- and multichannel CNNs, showing that manifold functions and their derivatives can be approximated with rates governed by the intrinsic dimension and the smoothness gap, rather than by the ambient dimension, thereby mitigating the curse of dimensionality. Building on this approximation theory, we propose a physics-informed CNN (PICNN) framework specially designed for boundary value problems. The main numerical issue is a boundary-norm mismatch: standard PINNs usually impose boundary data through low-order, often L2-type, penalties, whereas elliptic stability requires Sobolev trace control. We address this by introducing a spectral boundary loss based on the boundary Laplace-Beltrami operator, which represents trace errors as weighted frequency energies and relates truncation error to boundary eigenvalue decay. This avoids smooth auxiliary constructions required by exact boundary enforcement and singular double integrals arising in Sobolev-Slobodeckij penalties, while enabling implementations based on Fast Fourier Transforms (FFTs) or precomputed spectral bases on structured boundaries. Numerical experiments demonstrate improved accuracy, convergence, and stability over standard PINNs.

AI.tldr: CNN-based simultaneous Sobolev approximation on compact Riemannian manifolds enables manifold-aware PINNs with a spectral boundary loss for elliptic BVPs, reducing the curse of dimensionality and enhancing stability.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of solving elliptic boundary value problems on manifolds with neural networks by achieving Sobolev-accurate approximations while mitigating dimensionality and boundary enforcement issues in PINNs.
AI.method: Establishes simultaneous Sobolev approximation rates for single- and multichannel CNNs on manifolds; introduces a spectral boundary loss based on the boundary Laplace–Beltrami operator to enforce trace control; develops a physics-informed CNN (PICNN) framework that leverages FFTs or precomputed spectral bases on structured boundaries for efficient implementation.
AI.result: Approximation rates depend on the intrinsic dimension and smoothness gap rather than ambient dimension; the spectral boundary loss aligns boundary errors with boundary eigenvalue decay, improving accuracy, convergence, and stability over standard PINNs in elliptic problems.
AI.conclusion: A theoretically grounded, practically efficient PICNN framework for elliptic BVPs on manifolds, combining manifold-aware Sobolev approximation with spectral boundary enforcement to overcome boundary penalties and dimensionality challenges.

title: Modeling Subjective Urban Perception with Human Gaze
summary: Urban perception describes how people subjectively evaluate urban environments, shaping how cities are experienced and understood. Existing computational approaches primarily model urban perception directly from street view images, but largely ignore the human perceptual process through which such judgments are formed. In this paper, we introduce Place Pulse-Gaze, an urban perception dataset that augments street view images with synchronized eye-tracking recordings and individual perception labels. Based on this dataset, we propose a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework to study how gaze behavior contributes to the modeling of subjective urban perception. The framework systematically investigates three complementary settings: gaze-only modeling, gaze fusion with explicit semantic scene representations, and gaze fusion with implicit richer visual representations. Experiments show that gaze alone already carries useful predictive signals for subjective urban perception, and that integrating gaze with scene representations further improves prediction under both semantic and richer visual representations. Overall, our findings highlight the importance of incorporating human perceptual processes into urban scene understanding and open a direction for gaze-guided multimodal urban computing.

AI.tldr: Introduces Place Pulse-Gaze, a dataset augmenting street-view images with synchronized eye-tracking and perception labels, and a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework to evaluate how gaze signals enhance modeling of subjective urban perception across three settings: gaze-only, gaze + explicit semantic representations, and gaze + implicit richer visual representations. Findings show gaze carries predictive information and improves predictions when fused with scene representations, highlighting the role of human perceptual processes in urban understanding and suggesting gaze-guided multimodal urban computing as a new direction.
AI.motivation: Current computational approaches model urban perception directly from images but largely ignore the human perceptual process (how viewers' gaze and attention shape judgments). There is a need to incorporate gaze data to better understand and predict subjective perceptions of urban environments.
AI.method: 1) Construct Place Pulse-Gaze dataset by augmenting Street View imagery with synchronized eye-tracking data and individual perception labels. 2) Propose a Gaze-Guided Urban Perception Framework with three experimental settings: (a) gaze-only modeling, (b) gaze fusion with explicit semantic scene representations, (c) gaze fusion with implicit richer visual representations. 3) Conduct experiments to evaluate how gaze signals alone and in fusion affect predictive performance for subjective urban perception.
AI.result: Gaze alone provides useful predictive signals for subjective urban perception. Integrating gaze with explicit semantic representations and with richer implicit visual representations yields further improvements in prediction across both semantic and richer representations.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating human perceptual processes, via gaze data, is beneficial for urban scene understanding. The work advocates gaze-guided multimodal urban computing as a promising direction and provides a dataset and framework to study gaze contributions to subjective urban perception.

title: Ilov3Splat: Instance-Level Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Understanding in Gaussian Splatting
comment: The International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: We introduce Ilov3Splat, a novel framework for instance-level open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding built on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS). Most prior work depends on 2D rendering-based matching or point-level semantic association, which undermines cross-view consistency, lacks coherent instance-level reasoning, and limits precision in downstream 3D tasks. To address these limitations, our method jointly optimizes scene geometry and semantic representations by augmenting Gaussian splats with view-consistent feature fields. Specifically, we leverage multi-resolution hash embedding to efficiently encode language-aligned CLIP features, enabling dense and coherent language grounding in 3D space. We further train an instance feature field using contrastive loss over SAM masks, supporting fine-grained object distinction across views. At inference time, CLIP-encoded queries are matched against the learned features, followed by two-stage 3D clustering to retrieve relevant Gaussian groups. This enables our framework to identify arbitrary objects in 3D scenes based on natural language descriptions, without requiring category supervision or manual annotations. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that Ilov3Splat outperforms prior open-vocabulary 3D-GS methods in both object selection and instance segmentation, offering a flexible and accurate solution for language-driven 3D scene understanding. Project page: https://csiro-robotics.github.io/Ilov3Splat.

AI.tldr: Ilov3Splat is a framework that enables instance-level open-vocabulary 3D scene understanding by augmenting 3D Gaussian Splatting with view-consistent feature fields, CLIP-based language grounding, and SAM-guided instance features, enabling language-driven object retrieval and segmentation without category supervision.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of 2D-rendering-based matching and point-level semantic associations in 3D, which hinder cross-view consistency, coherent instance-level reasoning, and precision for downstream 3D tasks.
AI.method: Extend 3D Gaussian Splatting (3D-GS) with view-consistent feature fields encoded via multi-resolution hash embeddings of language-aligned CLIP features; train an instance feature field using contrastive loss over SAM masks to separate instances across views; at inference, match CLIP-encoded queries to learned features and apply two-stage 3D clustering to group Gaussian splats into object instances; operate in an open-vocabulary, supervision-free setting for language-driven 3D understanding.
AI.result: The approach outperforms prior open-vocabulary 3D-GS methods on object selection and instance segmentation on standard benchmarks, demonstrating improved language grounding and cross-view consistency in 3D scenes.
AI.conclusion: Ilov3Splat offers a flexible and accurate solution for language-driven 3D scene understanding that does not rely on predefined categories or manual annotations, enabling robust open-vocabulary instance-level reasoning in 3D.

title: Position: the Stochastic Parrot in the Coal Mine. Model Collapse is a Threat to Low-Resource Communities
comment: 13 pages, 1 figure, International Conference on Machine Learning
summary: Model collapse, the degradation in performance that arises when generative models are trained on the outputs of prior models, is an increasing concern as artificially generated content proliferates. Related critiques of large language models have highlighted their tendency to reproduce frequent patterns in training data, their reliance on vast datasets, and their substantial environmental cost. Together, these factors contribute to data degradation, the reinforcement of cultural biases, and inefficient resource use. In this position paper we aim to combine these views and argue that model collapse threatens current efforts to democratize AI. By reducing training efficiency and skewing data distributions away from the tails of their support, model collapse disproportionately impacts low-resource and marginalized communities. We examine both the environmental and cultural implications of this phenomenon, situate our position within recent position papers on model collapse, and conclude with a call to action. Finally, we outline initial directions for mitigating these effects.

AI.tldr: Position paper arguing that model collapse degrades training efficiency by training on prior models' outputs, amplifies data distribution biases, and undermines democratization by harming low-resource communities and increasing environmental costs; calls for action and outlines initial mitigation directions.
AI.motivation: To address rising concerns about model collapse alongside critiques of LLMs, highlighting environmental, cultural, and equity implications and arguing that unchecked collapse could entrench burdens on marginalized groups.
AI.method: The paper is a theoretical/position piece that synthesizes existing literature, frames the issue of model collapse, situates it with respect to recent work, and outlines preliminary mitigation directions and a call to action.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported; the contribution is conceptual, presenting a stance and proposing directions for mitigation and further research.
AI.conclusion: Model collapse threatens democratization by reducing training efficiency and skewing data away from tails; environmental and cultural consequences are discussed; the paper calls for action and outlines preliminary mitigation agendas.

title: From Priors to Perception: Grounding Video-LLMs in Physical Reality
summary: While Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) excel in general understanding, they exhibit systematic deficits in fine-grained physical reasoning. Existing interventions not only suffer from limited generalization but fundamentally conflate generative artifacts with genuine physical fallacies. Furthermore, we find that models fail systematically not only in anti-physics anomalies but also in counter-intuitive scenarios where visual facts contradict statistical expectations. Accordingly, we propose the Unified Attribution Theory: this dual failure stems not from perception deficiency, but from Semantic Prior Dominance -- the reasoning mechanism is deeply hijacked by internal narrative scripts. To address this, we construct the Programmatic Adversarial Curriculum (PACC), the first high-fidelity adversarial video dataset synthesized based on physical laws, thoroughly decoupling visual artifacts from logical errors. Concurrently, we design the Visual-Anchored Reasoning Chain (VARC) to force models to explicitly ground their judgments in low-level visual facts prior to logical adjudication. Experiments demonstrate that without invasive architectural modifications, standard LoRA fine-tuning with the PACC curriculum effectively neutralizes prior interference in state-of-the-art (SOTA) models, yielding a substantial leap in physical reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: Video-LLMs exhibit systematic gaps in fine-grained physical reasoning due to semantic priors hijacking reasoning; presents Unified Attribution Theory and a practical curriculum that decouples artifacts from logic; introduces PACC (a high-fidelity adversarial video dataset) and VARC (grounding in low-level visuals); shows LoRA fine-tuning with PACC yields significant gains without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: To address persistent, generalization-resistant deficits in physical reasoning in Video-LLMs, which are not merely perceptual but rooted in Semantic Prior Dominance and internal narrative scripts.
AI.method: Propose Unified Attribution Theory; construct Programmatic Adversarial Curriculum (PACC) by synthesizing high-fidelity adversarial videos based on physical laws; design Visual-Anchored Reasoning Chain (VARC) to force grounding in low-level visual facts before logical adjudication; apply LoRA fine-tuning with the PACC curriculum to SOTA models; evaluate on tasks requiring physical reasoning.
AI.result: PACC successfully decouples visual artifacts from logical errors; VARC enforces grounding and improves reasoning; with standard LoRA fine-tuning, PACC neutralizes prior interference in SOTA models, yielding a substantial improvement in physical reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Semantic Priors can dominate physical reasoning in Video-LLMs; grounding visual facts through VARC and adversarial curricula like PACC provides a practical route to robust physical reasoning without major architectural changes. The approach suggests broader generalization to other reasoning-intensive multimodal tasks.

title: Constrained Extreme Gradient Boosting for Adapting Reduced-Order Models
summary: High-fidelity simulations, such as computational fluid dynamics and finite element analysis, are essential for modeling complex engineering systems but are often prohibitively expensive for tasks including parametric studies, optimization, and real-time control. Projection-based reduced-order models (ROMs) alleviate this cost by projecting the governing dynamics onto low-dimensional subspaces. However, their performance can deteriorate under parameter variation, motivating the need for adaptive basis construction. In this work, we propose a constrained ensemble learning framework, termed Constrained Extreme Gradient Boosting (cXGBoost), for predicting Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) bases as functions of system parameters. The approach leverages a geometric representation of subspaces on the Grassmann manifold, which are mapped to a Euclidean space to enable efficient regression using gradient boosting trees. A norm constraint is imposed during training to ensure the validity of the inverse mapping and preserve the geometric structure of the predicted subspaces. The proposed method is evaluated on four numerical examples, including fluid dynamics and wave propagation problems, demonstrating its ability to accurately predict parameter-dependent bases while maintaining robustness across nonlinear regimes. These results highlight the potential of combining geometric learning with constrained ensemble methods for scalable and reliable reduced-order modeling of high-dimensional parametric systems.

AI.tldr: Constrained Extreme Gradient Boosting (cXGBoost) learns parameter-dependent POD bases on the Grassmann manifold, enabling accurate and robust reduced-order models across parameter variations.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity simulations are computationally expensive; ROMs must adapt to parameter changes, but performance degrades when basis is fixed. A geometrically-informed, constrained learning approach can produce valid, parameter-dependent bases.
AI.method: Represent POD subspaces as points on the Grassmann manifold and map to a Euclidean space. Use constrained XGBoost to regress bases as functions of system parameters, with a norm constraint to preserve the inverse map and the geometric structure of subspaces.
AI.result: The method is evaluated on four numerical examples, including fluid dynamics and wave propagation, showing accurate prediction of parameter-dependent bases and robustness across nonlinear regimes.
AI.conclusion: Combining geometric learning with constrained ensemble methods yields scalable, reliable ROMs for high-dimensional parametric systems.

title: DALight-3D: A Lightweight 3D U-Net for Brain Tumor Segmentation from Multi-Modal MRI
summary: Automatic brain tumor segmentation from multi-modal MRI remains challenging because volumetric models often incur substantial computational cost. This paper presents DALight-3D, a compact 3D U-Net variant that combines depthwise separable 3D convolutions, identifier-conditioned normalization, cross-slice attention, and adaptive skip fusion. The method is evaluated on the Medical Segmentation Decathlon Task01 BrainTumour benchmark under matched optimization settings against standard 3D U-Net, Attention U-Net, Residual 3D U-Net, and V-Net baselines. In the reported 50-epoch comparison, DALight-3D achieves a mean Dice of 0.727 with 2.22M parameters, compared with 0.710 Dice and 3.20M parameters for Residual 3D U-Net. Component-wise ablations show consistent performance degradation when SepConv, identifier-conditioned normalization, CSA, or SSFB is removed. These results indicate that DALight-3D offers a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off within the present benchmark setting.

AI.tldr: DALight-3D is a compact 3D U-Net variant (2.22M params) that achieves competitive Dice (0.727) on BrainTumour MSCT Task01 with several efficiency-driven design choices; ablations show each component contributes to performance, indicating a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off.
AI.motivation: Reduce computation and memory footprint of volumetric brain tumor segmentation without sacrificing accuracy, enabling practical deployment on multi-modal MRI data.
AI.method: DALight-3D integrates depthwise separable 3D convolutions, identifier-conditioned normalization, cross-slice attention, and adaptive skip fusion within a 3D U-Net framework; evaluated against standard 3D U-Net, Attention U-Net, Residual 3D U-Net, and V-Net on MS Decathlon Task01 BrainTumour with 50-epoch training and matched optimization settings; component-wise ablations test SepConv, identifier-conditioned normalization, CSA, and SSFB.
AI.result: DALight-3D achieves mean Dice = 0.727 with 2.22M params, outperforming Residual 3D U-Net (Dice 0.710, 3.20M params); ablations show performance degradation when any of SepConv, identifier-conditioned normalization, CSA, or SSFB is removed, indicating each contributes to the accuracy-efficiency balance.
AI.conclusion: DALight-3D offers a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for brain tumor segmentation on the benchmark, with design components providing measurable contributions to performance while reducing parameter count.

title: Model synthesis and identifiability analysis of stiff chemical reaction systems with inVAErt networks
summary: We consider the problem of learning data-driven replicas for stiff systems of ordinary differential equations arising in chemical kinetics that can be evaluated with high computational efficiency. We first focus on training emulators for families of reaction equations under varying reaction rates, using conditional residual networks or long-short term memory architectures. We then apply a recently proposed data-driven framework known as ``inVAErt networks'' to address the ill-posed inverse problem of inferring reaction rates, integration time, and possibly initial conditions from a target set of species concentrations - a problem that has received relatively little attention in the literature. The proposed approach is demonstrated on chemical systems with reversible and irreversible kinetics, spanning 2 to 20 differential equations, 3 to 20 chemical species, and 3 to 25 reaction rate parameters. Relative root mean squared errors produced by the proposed emulators range from $10^{-5}$ for lower-dimensional systems to $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-3}$ for an air pollution model and a hydrogen-air reaction system, respectively. Manifolds of non-identifiable reaction rates recovered by the proposed approach can be analytically verified for simple systems and are consistent with local identifiability analysis in higher dimensions.

AI.tldr: A data-driven surrogate framework for stiff chemical kinetics that learns emulators across varying reaction rates and uses inVAErt networks to solve ill-posed inverse problems of inferring rates, time horizon, and initial conditions from target concentrations; achieving high accuracy and identifying non-identifiable manifolds.
AI.motivation: Stiff ODE systems in chemical kinetics require fast, accurate surrogates and robust inverse solutions. The work combines parametric surrogates across rate variations with a principled inverse problem framework (inVAErt) to tackle non-uniqueness in inferring rates, time, and initial conditions from concentration data.
AI.method: (1) Train emulators for families of reaction equations under varying reaction rates using conditional residual networks or long-short term memory (LSTM) architectures. (2) Apply inVAErt networks to address the ill-posed inverse problem of inferring reaction rates, integration time, and possibly initial conditions from a target set of species concentrations. (3) Demonstrate on chemical systems with reversible and irreversible kinetics, spanning 2–20 ODEs, 3–20 species, and 3–25 rate parameters.
AI.result: Relative RMSEs range from 1e-5 for low-dimensional systems to 1e-3 for a hydrogen–air system and ~1e-4 for an air-pollution model. The method reveals manifolds of non-identifiable reaction rates that can be analytically verified for simple systems and are consistent with local identifiability analyses in higher dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides accurate, data-driven emulators for stiff chemical kinetics across parameter regimes and leverages inVAErt to solve inverse problems, with identifiable/non-identifiable structures aligning with classical identifiability theory and enabling principled uncertainty quantification and design under data limitations.

title: High-Fidelity Single-Image Head Modeling with Industry-Grade Topology
summary: We present a single-image head mesh reconstruction framework that addresses the longstanding challenge of simultaneously preserving facial identity and producing industry-grade topology. Our framework adopts a coarse-to-fine optimization pipeline that refines a rigged template across three stages -- rig, joint, and vertex -- achieving stable convergence and consistent topology. To mitigate the ill-posed nature of single-image 3D face reconstruction and ensure identity preservation, we employ a normal consistency objective jointly with landmark alignment. To further preserve local surface structure and enforce topological regularity, we introduce geometry-aware constraints based on Gaussian curvature and conformal consistency, along with auxiliary regularizations that correct fine artifacts such as lip seams and eyelid discontinuities. Our hierarchical optimization with geometry-aware regularization yields meshes with semantically meaningful edge flow and industry-grade topology. After geometry reconstruction, we extract UV-space texture and normal maps to preserve appearance details for visualization and downstream use. In a user study with 22 professional technical artists, our results were assessed as approaching industry-grade usability, and 95% of participants ranked our method as the top-performing approach, underscoring its effectiveness for real-world digital human production.

AI.tldr: A single-image head mesh reconstruction framework uses a coarse-to-fine, three-stage pipeline (rig, joint, vertex) with geometry-aware regularization to preserve identity while achieving industry-grade topology; it incorporates a normal-consistency objective with landmark alignment and uses Gaussian curvature and conformal constraints plus artifact-regularizations, producing texture maps and showing strong professional usability in a user study.
AI.motivation: Address the ill-posed nature of single-image 3D face reconstruction with the goal of preserving facial identity while enforcing robust, industry-grade topology and surface structure.
AI.method: Coarse-to-fine optimization over three stages (rig, joint, vertex) with a normal-consistency objective and landmark alignment; geometry-aware constraints based on Gaussian curvature and conformal consistency; auxiliary regularizations to fix artifacts; UV-space texture/normal map extraction; evaluation via a user study.
AI.result: Meshes achieve semantically meaningful edge flow and industry-grade topology; textures/normal maps extracted; user study with 22 professionals: 95% ranked top-performing; results approach industry-grade usability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed geometry-aware, staged optimization yields high-quality, identity-preserving head meshes with stable topology suitable for real-world digital human production, validated by expert user study.

title: Enabling Real-Time Training of a Wildfire-to-Smoke Map with Multilinear Operators
summary: Wildfires are a major producer of fine particulate matter, impacting human health and the electrical grid. Accurately forecasting smoke impacts over long time scales incorporates fuel treatment strategies, natural fuel succession, and stochastic events like lightning strikes. However, predicting smoke for each fuel distribution with a forward simulation of a coupled fire-atmosphere model is computationally infeasible. Moreover, relatively simple fire models are tractable to run in many long-time scenarios but do not capture smoke transport. We use data-driven multilinear operators to predict a smoke concentration field from knowledge of the time since ignition for two quantities of interest: aerosol optical depth and smoke detection. Our method first computes the principal components of time-since-ignition and smoke concentration fields and then learns a map from powers of the input coefficients to the output coefficients. We apply our learned operator to smoke prediction in the Upper Rio Grande Watershed. After collecting training data, learning the approximation weights on a CPU takes less than 30 seconds, and each forward call takes less than 1 ms. On a proxy for aerosol optical depth, we obtain equal accuracy to Monte Carlo sampling with fewer than half as many coupled model calls. For smoke detection, we obtain an intersection-over-union (IoU) of 65% and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.95 on holdout data. Our method is significantly more accurate than the most similar published smoke classifier, which obtains an IoU and AUC of 0.15 and 0.61, respectively, on a 2015 bushfire in Australia.

AI.tldr: Data-driven multilinear operators predict smoke concentration from time-since-ignition fields, enabling fast, accurate surrogate forecasts for smoke (IoU 65%, AUC 0.95) with CPU training under 30s and per-forward costs <1 ms.
AI.motivation: Long-time-scale smoke forecasting requires balancing physics fidelity with computational tractability; forward coupled fire-atmosphere models are too expensive for many fuel distributions and scenarios, necessitating fast surrogates that preserve key transport dynamics.
AI.method: Compute principal components of time-since-ignition and smoke concentration fields; learn a map from powers of input coefficients to output coefficients using data-driven multilinear operators; train on region data (Upper Rio Grande Watershed); fast CPU training (<30 s) and extremely fast forward evaluation (<1 ms).
AI.result: Surrogate matches Monte Carlo accuracy for aerosol optical depth with fewer than half the coupled-model calls; smoke-detection performance: IoU 0.65, AUC 0.95 on holdout; outperforms a prior smoke classifier (IoU 0.15, AUC 0.61) on a 2015 bushfire in Australia.
AI.conclusion: The data-driven multilinear operator provides a computationally efficient yet accurate surrogate for smoke forecasting over long horizons, enabling integration into management and decision-support workflows with substantially reduced model calls.

title: Velox: Learning Representations of 4D Geometry and Appearance
summary: We introduce a framework for learning latent representations of 4D objects which are descriptive, faithfully capturing object geometry and appearance; compressive, aiding in downstream efficiency; and accessible, requiring minimal input, i.e., an unstructured dynamic point cloud, to construct. Specifically, Velox trains an encoder to compress spatiotemporal color point clouds into a set of dynamic shape tokens. These tokens are supervised using two complementary decoders: a 4D surface decoder, which models the time-varying surface distribution capturing the geometry; and a Gaussian decoder, which maps the tokens to 3D Gaussians, helping learn appearance. To demonstrate the utility of our representation, we evaluate it across three downstream tasks -- video-to-4D generation, 3D tracking, and cloth simulation via image-to-4D generation -- and observe strong performances in all settings.

AI.tldr: A framework for learning latent dynamic 4D representations from unstructured dynamic point clouds, using an encoder to produce dynamic shape tokens and dual decoders (4D surface for geometry; Gaussian for appearance). Demonstrates utility on downstream tasks: video-to-4D generation, 3D tracking, and cloth simulation via image-to-4D generation; achieves strong performance across tasks.
AI.motivation: Create compact, descriptive, and accessible 4D representations from sparse, unstructured point-cloud data to improve downstream efficiency and enable versatile applications.
AI.method: Train an encoder to compress spatiotemporal color point clouds into dynamic shape tokens. Use two complementary decoders: (1) a 4D surface decoder that models the time-varying surface distribution for geometry; (2) a Gaussian decoder that maps tokens to 3D Gaussians to capture appearance. Evaluate on tasks including video-to-4D generation, 3D tracking, and cloth simulation via image-to-4D generation.
AI.result: The representation yields strong performance across all three downstream tasks, demonstrating its utility and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic 4D tokens learned via a dual-decoder framework provide a descriptive, compressive, and accessible representation of 4D objects, enabling effective video-to-4D generation, tracking, and cloth simulation.

title: A Provably Convergent and Practical Algorithm for Gromov--Wasserstein Optimal Transport
summary: Gromov--Wasserstein optimal transport (GWOT) aligns metric measure spaces by matching their within-domain relational structures, but large-scale GWOT remains challenging because its objective is nonconvex and projection onto the transport polytope is often solved only approximately in practice. This leads to a gap between practical projected-gradient implementations and convergence theory, which typically assumes exact projections. For squared-loss GWOT, we propose an inexact projected-gradient framework with a verifiable feasibility-residual-based inexact condition for the projection subproblem. This condition is directly computable and avoids unknown quantities such as the exact projection point. Under this implementable condition, we prove subsequential convergence to stationary points and, with a mild tolerance-decay condition, convergence of the whole sequence. The resulting method retains the simplicity and sparsity of projected-gradient schemes while providing rigorous convergence guarantees, turning projected-gradient methods into a principled and scalable approach for GWOT with provable reliability.

AI.tldr: Introduces an inexact projected-gradient framework for squared-loss GWOT with a computable feasibility-residual condition on projections, proving subsequential convergence and full-sequence convergence under tolerance decay, enabling scalable and reliable optimization.
AI.motivation: Gromov–Wasserstein optimal transport (GWOT) uses nonconvex objectives and often relies on approximate projections; theory typically assumes exact projections, causing a gap between practice and convergence results. A verifiable inexact projection criterion is needed for reliability in large-scale GWOT.
AI.method: Propose an inexact projected-gradient algorithm for squared-loss GWOT with a feasibility-residual-based inexact condition for the projection subproblem. The condition is computable from current iterates and avoids requiring the exact projection. Analyze convergence under this implementable criterion.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves subsequential convergence to stationary points. Under a mild tolerance-decay condition, the entire sequence converges. It preserves the simplicity and sparsity of standard projected-gradient methods while providing convergence guarantees.
AI.conclusion: This framework makes projected-gradient methods a principled and scalable approach for GWOT with provable reliability, closing the gap between practical implementations and theoretical convergence guarantees.

title: Reward-Guided Semantic Evolution for Test-time Adaptive Object Detection
summary: Open-vocabulary object detection with vision-language models (VLMs) such as Grounding DINO suffers from performance degradation under test-time distribution shifts, primarily due to semantic misalignment between text embeddings and shifted visual embeddings of region proposals. While recent test-time adaptive object detection methods for VLM-based either rely on costly backpropagation or bypass semantic misalignment via external memory, none directly and efficiently align text and vision in a training-free manner. To address this, we propose Reward-Guided Semantic Evolution (RGSE), a training-free framework that directly refines the text embeddings at test time. Inspired by evolutionary search, RGSE treats text embedding adaptation as a semantic search process: it perturbs text embeddings as candidate variants, evaluates them via cosine similarity with current and historical high-confidence visual proposals as a reward signal, and fuses them into a refined embedding through reward-weighted averaging. Without any backpropagation, RGSE achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple detection benchmarks while adding minimal computational overhead. Our code will be open source upon publication.

AI.tldr: Training-free, reward-guided semantic evolution (RGSE) aligns text and vision for open-vocabulary object detection, achieving state-of-the-art results with minimal overhead by iteratively evolving text embeddings at test time without backpropagation.
AI.motivation: Semantic misalignment between text embeddings and shifted visual embeddings causes performance drop under test-time distribution shifts in VLM-based detectors; existing test-time methods rely on costly backprop or external memory, and none offer a truly training-free solution.
AI.method: RGSE treats text embedding adaptation as a semantic search: at test time, perturb text embeddings to generate candidate variants, compute cosine similarities with current and historical high-confidence visual proposals as a reward signal, and fuse variants into a refined embedding via reward-weighted averaging; no backpropagation is used, resulting in low computational overhead.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance across multiple detection benchmarks under distribution shifts, with minimal computational overhead and a training-free approach; code will be open source upon publication.
AI.conclusion: RGSE provides an effective, training-free mechanism to align text and vision in VLM-based detectors at test time, mitigating semantic misalignment and improving robustness with negligible overhead.

title: Angle-I2P: Angle-Consistent-Aware Hierarchical Attention for Cross-Modality Outlier Rejection
summary: Image-to-point-cloud registration (I2P) is a fundamental task in robotic applications such as manipulation,grasping, and localization. Existing deep learning-based I2P methods seek to align image and point cloud features in a learned representation space to establish correspondences, and have achieved promising results. However, when the inlier ratio of the initial matching pairs is low, conventional Perspective-n-Points (PnP) methods may struggle to achieve accurate results. To address this limitation, we propose Angle-I2P, an outlier rejection network that leverages angle-consistent geometric constraints and hierarchical attention. First, we design a scale-invariant, crossmodality geometric constraint based on angular consistency. This explicit geometric constraint guides the model in distinguishing inliers from outliers. Furthermore, we propose a global-tolocal hierarchical attention mechanism that effectively filters out geometrically inconsistent matches under rigid transformation, thereby improving the Inlier Ratio (IR) and Registration Recall (RR). Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the 7Scenes, RGBD Scenes V2, and a self-collected dataset, with consistent improvements across all benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Angle-I2P introduces an angle-consistent, scale-invariant cross-modality constraint and a global-to-local hierarchical attention to robustly reject outliers in image-to-point-cloud registration, achieving state-of-the-art results under low inlier ratio.
AI.motivation: Low inlier ratios in initial image-to-point-cloud matches degrade PnP-based registration; robust outlier rejection is needed to improve accuracy and reliability in cross-modality registration.
AI.method: A two-part approach: (1) a scale-invariant, cross-modality geometric constraint based on angular consistency to separate inliers from outliers; (2) a global-to-local hierarchical attention mechanism that filters geometrically inconsistent matches under rigid transformation, improving inlier ratio and registration recall.
AI.result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on 7Scenes, RGBD Scenes V2, and a self-collected dataset, with consistent improvements in inlier ratio and registration recall across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Angle-I2P effectively enhances I2P robustness to outliers by combining angular-consistency constraints with hierarchical attention, enabling more accurate and reliable cross-modality registration in low inlier ratio scenarios.

title: Deep Wave Network for Modeling Multi-Scale Physical Dynamics
summary: Performance of deep learning models is strongly governed by architectural capacity, with width and depth as primary controls. However, in physical-science applications, models are often compared at a single fixed size or by separating accuracy and computational cost, which can be misleading since architectures exhibit different accuracy-cost scaling as width and depth vary. This issue is particularly relevant for U-Net-type encoder-decoder models, widely used for multi-scale gas, fluid, and plasma dynamics due to their ability to represent features across spatial scales. A U-Net constructs a multi-resolution representation via an encoder that progressively reduces spatial resolution, followed by a decoder that restores it for prediction. Skip connections link corresponding encoder and decoder features, preserving fine-scale information and improving optimization. In practice, U-Net width is routinely tuned, while depth is typically kept fixed (a set number of down/up-sampling stages with few convolutions per stage), limiting systematic exploration of depth for improving the accuracy-cost trade-off. We address this limitation by increasing effective depth through stacking multiple encoder-decoder "waves" in series, with skip connections both within and across waves to enable progressive cross-scale refinement. We call this architecture a Deep Wave Network (DW-Net). Training data, optimization, and schedules are kept identical across models. Instead of evaluating single configurations, we train multiple width variants of each architecture and compare accuracy vs. GPU time Pareto fronts. Across several 2D and 3D flow benchmarks, DW-Net models consistently improve the Pareto frontier over single-wave U-Nets, achieving higher accuracy at matched cost or similar accuracy at reduced cost, and reaching low-error regimes with up to 3x less training time under identical training settings.

AI.tldr: DW-Net stacks multiple encoder-decoder waves with cross-wave skip connections, yielding deeper models that beat single-wave U-Nets on accuracy-cost trade-offs for 2D/3D flow tasks; can reach low-error regimes with up to 3x faster training under identical settings.
AI.motivation: In physical-science modeling, evaluating architectures at fixed size or separating accuracy and cost can obscure true efficiency, because width/depth scaling affects accuracy-cost nonlinearly. There's a need to systematically explore depth in U-Net-like encoders to improve accuracy-cost trade-offs.
AI.method: Propose Deep Wave Network by stacking multiple encoder-decoder waves in series, with internal skip connections and cross-wave connections to enable progressive cross-scale refinement. Keep training data, optimization, and schedules identical across models. Train multiple width variants and compare accuracy vs GPU time Pareto fronts across several 2D/3D flow benchmarks.
AI.result: DW-Net consistently improves the Pareto frontier over single-wave U-Nets, yielding higher accuracy at the same cost or similar accuracy at reduced cost; can reach low-error regimes with up to 3x less training time under identical training settings.
AI.conclusion: Deepening U-Net-style architectures via multi-wave stacking yields superior accuracy-cost scaling in physical-science simulations, offering practical gains in efficiency without altering training protocols.

title: InterMesh: Explicit Interaction-Aware End-to-End Multi-Person Human Mesh Recovery
summary: Humans constantly interact with their surroundings. Existing end-to-end multi-person human mesh recovery methods, typically based on the DETR framework, capture inter-human relationships through self-attention across all human queries. However, these approaches model interactions only implicitly and lack explicit reasoning about how humans interact with objects and with each other. In this paper, we propose InterMesh, a simple yet effective framework that explicitly incorporates human-environment interaction information into human mesh recovery pipeline. By leveraging a human-object interaction detector, InterMesh enriches query representations with structured interaction semantics, enabling more accurate pose and shape estimation. We design lightweight modules, Contextual Interaction Encoder and Interaction-Guided Refiner, to integrate these features into existing HMR architectures with minimal overhead. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on 3DPW, MuPoTS, CMU Panoptic, Hi4D, and CHI3D datasets, demonstrating remarkable improvements over state-of-the-art methods. Notably, InterMesh reduces MPJPE by 9.9% on CMU Panoptic and 8.2% on Hi4D, highlighting its effectiveness in scenarios with complex human-object and inter-human interactions.

AI.tldr: InterMesh introduces explicit human–environment interaction modeling into multi-person human mesh recovery by injecting structured interaction semantics from a human–object interaction detector into query representations, via Contextual Interaction Encoder and Interaction-Guided Refiner, yielding notable MPJPE improvements on multiple datasets with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Current end-to-end DETR-based HMR methods model interactions implicitly through self-attention and lack explicit reasoning about human–object and human–human interactions, limiting pose/shape accuracy in cluttered or interactive scenes.
AI.method: Incorporate a human–object interaction detector to produce structured interaction semantics and enrich human queries. Introduce two lightweight modules: Contextual Interaction Encoder to fuse interaction cues into query representations, and Interaction-Guided Refiner to adapt decoders. Integrate into existing HMR architectures with minimal overhead, enabling improved pose/shape estimation in multi-person scenes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across 3DPW, MuPoTS, CMU Panoptic, Hi4D, and CHI3D datasets shows consistent improvements over state-of-the-art HMR methods, with notable MPJPE reductions (e.g., 9.9% on CMU Panoptic, 8.2% on Hi4D), especially in scenes with complex human–object and inter-human interactions.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling human–environment interactions via a detector-guided, lightweight integration framework yields significant accuracy gains in multi-person HMR and is compatible with existing DETR-based pipelines, suggesting robust, scalable enhancements for interactive scene understanding.

title: Sequential Strategic Classification with Multi-Stage Selective Classifiers
comment: Shorter version presented as a poster at GameNets 2026
summary: Strategic classification studies the problem where self-interested individuals or agents manipulate their response to obtain favorable decision outcomes made by classifiers, typically turning to dishonest actions when they are less costly than genuine efforts. Prior works have demonstrated a fundamental inability to get out of this conundrum by only focusing on the design of a classifier. We note that prior work also heavily focuses on either one-shot settings or repeated interaction with the same classifier. Real-world decision making is often multi-stage, involving a sequence of potentially different classifiers as an agent progresses. This paper introduces a sequential, stochastic, multi-stage model of strategic classification, by capturing how agents adapt their behavior, through improvement actions (enhancing both observable features and true attributes) and gaming actions (enhancing only observable features), over multiple levels of classification with increasing difficulty as well as reward. For each level, we adopt a selective classifier that can abstain from making a prediction at low confidence. Consequently, a positive (resp. negative) outcome leads to promotion (resp. demotion) of the agent to the next higher (resp. lower) level, while abstention keeps the agent at the same level. We fully characterize the agent's optimal instantaneous action under selective classifiers and compare the long-term properties and utility of the agent repeatedly following an optimal myopic policy of either no-improvement (never choose the improvement action) or no-gaming (never choose the gaming action). We further examine design principles over the sequence of classifiers that yield higher long-term utility for the latter policy, thereby effectively incentivizing genuine effort in the long run.

AI.tldr: Introduces a sequential, stochastic, multi-stage model of strategic classification with abstaining (selective) classifiers; analyzes agent incentives under two myopic policies and derives design principles to promote genuine effort.
AI.motivation: Extends prior one-shot/repeated settings to multi-stage decision processes where agents may improve or game across levels; aims to design classifier sequences that incentivize genuine effort in the long run.
AI.method: Model: agents traverse levels with increasing difficulty and reward; actions: improvement (observable+true attributes) or gaming (observable only); classifiers abstain when uncertain; outcomes move agents up/down; analysis obtains the agent's optimal instantaneous action under selective classifiers; compare long-term utilities of no-improvement vs no-gaming policies; derive design principles to boost long-term utility for no-gaming policy.
AI.result: Fully characterizes the agent's optimal instantaneous action under selective classifiers; analyzes long-term properties under two policies; proposes design principles that increase long-term utility, effectively incentivizing genuine effort.
AI.conclusion: In multi-stage selective classification, long-term incentives can be shaped to promote genuine effort; the framework generalizes prior findings and provides principled design guidance for sequences of classifiers.

title: Efficient Geometry-Controlled High-Resolution Satellite Image Synthesis
comment: 2026 IEEE International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium (IGARSS)
summary: High-resolution satellite images are often scarce and costly, especially for remote areas or infrequent events. This shortage hampers the development and testing of machine learning models for land-cover classification, change detection, and disaster monitoring. In this paper, we tackle the problem of geometry-controlled high-resolution satellite image synthesis by adding control over existing pre-trained diffusion models. We propose a simple yet efficient method for controlling the synthesis process by leveraging only skip connection features using windowed cross-attention modules. Several previously established control techniques are compared, indicating that our method achieves comparable performance while leading to a better alignment with the geometry control map. We also discuss the limitations in current evaluation approaches, amplifying the necessity of a consistent alignment assessment.

AI.tldr: Proposes geometry-controlled high-res satellite image synthesis by gating a pre-trained diffusion model with skip-connection features via windowed cross-attention. Shows competitive results with existing controls and emphasizes better alignment with geometry control maps; notes evaluation alignment issues.
AI.motivation: Scarcity and cost of high-res satellite imagery hampers ML model development for land-cover, change detection, and disaster monitoring; need for controllable synthesis to simulate geometries reliably for testing.
AI.method: Leverage skip connection features in pre-trained diffusion models, integrate windowed cross-attention modules to inject geometry/control maps into synthesis without full retraining; compare with prior control methods.
AI.result: Comparable performance to existing control techniques; improved alignment with geometry control maps; suggests current eval methods insufficient for assessing geometry alignment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach is simple and efficient, achieves competitive control with better geometry alignment; highlights the necessity for standardized alignment evaluation in synthesis tasks.

title: Climate-based Pre-screening of Self-sustaining Regreening Opportunities in Drylands: A Case Study for Saudi Arabia
summary: Large-scale restoration in drylands is widely promoted to address land degradation and biodiversity loss, yet many efforts rely on long-term irrigation, limiting sustainability in water-scarce regions. A key challenge is identifying locations where native vegetation can persist without intensive management while minimizing costly field campaigns. A scalable pre-screening framework is presented that integrates climate and remote sensing data to enable cost-efficient site selection in arid environments using Saudi Arabia as a case study. A Climate Suitability Score (CSS), derived from machine learning models trained on expert-curated reference sites, captures complex climatic dependencies on vegetation persistence. Using multi-year ERA5-Land data for Saudi Arabia, national-scale prediction maps are generated and combined with vegetation indices to identify areas where climate is favorable, but vegetation remains underdeveloped. Multi-criteria screening reduces candidates to thirteen priority locations. Climatically analogous intact ecosystems provide benchmarks for restoration targets and indicate that an average 2.5 fold increase in vegetation coverage is a realistic target for restoration efforts. Overall, this approach narrows the search space, reduces costs, and supports resilient ecosystem recovery planning in water-limited regions.

AI.tldr: Scalable pre-screening framework using Climate Suitability Score (CSS) and multi-criteria screening to locate underdeveloped but climate-favorable sites for restoration in Saudi Arabia; reduces field campaigns and identifies 13 priority locations, with benchmarks suggesting a ~2.5x potential increase in vegetation.
AI.motivation: Address land degradation and biodiversity loss in drylands under water scarcity by enabling cost-efficient, scalable site selection without heavy irrigation or extensive field campaigns.
AI.method: Train ML models on expert-curated reference sites to derive a Climate Suitability Score (CSS) that captures climatic dependencies for vegetation persistence. Use multi-year ERA5-Land data to create national-scale climate suitability maps, which are combined with vegetation indices. Apply multi-criteria screening to narrow candidates to a small set of priority sites and use climatically analogous intact ecosystems as restoration benchmarks to set targets.
AI.result: Identified climate-favorable but underdeveloped areas; reduced the candidate pool to 13 priority locations; provided restoration benchmarks indicating an average ~2.5-fold increase in vegetation coverage as a realistic target.
AI.conclusion: The framework narrows the search space, lowers implementation costs, and supports resilient ecosystem recovery planning in water-limited regions, demonstrated here with Saudi Arabia as a case study.

title: SAMIC: A Lightweight Semantic-Aware Mamba for Efficient Perceptual Image Compression
summary: Perceptual image compression focuses on preserving high visual quality under low-bitrate constraints. Most existing approaches to perceptual compression leverage the strong generative capabilities of generative adversarial networks or diffusion models, at the cost of substantial model complexity. To this end, we present an efficient perceptual image compression method that exploits the long-range modeling capability and linear computational complexity of state space models, with a particular focus on Mamba. Unlike existing methods that rely on an inherently fixed scanning order and consequently impair semantic continuity and spatial correlation, we develop a semantic-aware Mamba block (SAMB) to enable scanning guided by dynamically clustered semantic features, thereby alleviating the strict causality constraints and long-range information decay inherent to Mamba. Inspired by singular value decomposition, we design an SVD-inspired redundancy reduction module (SVD-RRM) that performs a low-rank approximation on the latent features by introducing a learnable soft threshold, leading to channel-wise redundancy information reduction. The proposed SAMB is integrated into both the encoder and decoder of the compression framework, whereas the SVD-RRM is incorporated only in the encoder. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method performs favorably against state-of-the-art approaches in terms of rate-distortion-perception tradeoff and model complexity. The source code and pretrained models will be available at https://github.com/Jasmine-aiq/SAMIC.

AI.tldr: Efficient perceptual image compression using a semantic-aware Mamba block (SAMB) and an SVD-inspired redundancy reduction module (SVD-RRM) within a state-space model framework, achieving favorable rate-distortion-perception with lower model complexity.
AI.motivation: Perceptual image compression methods often rely on heavy generative models (GANs/diffusion) leading to high complexity. There is a need for scalable, long-range modeling with linear computational complexity and improved semantic continuity during scanning.
AI.method: Propose SAMB to guide scanning by dynamically clustered semantic features, addressing causality and long-range decay in Mamba. Introduce SVD-RRM to perform low-rank latent feature reduction with a learnable soft threshold. Integrate SAMB into both encoder and decoder, and place SVD-RRM only in the encoder.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show favorable performance on rate-distortion-perception tradeoff and reduced model complexity compared to state-of-the-art methods; code and pretrained models to be released.
AI.conclusion: The combination of semantic-aware scanning (SAMB) and low-rank redundancy reduction (SVD-RRM) within a linear-complexity state-space framework yields efficient perceptual compression with improved perceptual quality and reduced redundancy.

title: Predict-then-Diffuse: Adaptive Response Length for Compute-Budgeted Inference in Diffusion LLMs
summary: Diffusion-based Large Language Models (D-LLMs) represent a promising frontier in generative AI, offering fully parallel token generation that can lead to significant throughput advantages and superior GPU utilization over traditional autoregressive paradigm. However, this parallelism is constrained by the requirement of a fixed-size response length prior to generation. This architectural limitation imposes a severe trade-off: oversized response length results in computational waste on semantically meaningless padding tokens, while undersized response length cause output truncation requiring costly re-computations that introduce unpredictable latency spikes. To tackle this issue, we propose Predict-then-Diffuse, a simple and model-agnostic framework, that enables compute-budgeted inference per input query by first estimating the response length and then using it to run inference with D-LLM. At its core lies a Adaptive Response Length Predictor (AdaRLP) auxiliary predictor that predicts the optimal response length given an input query. As a measure against under-predicting the response length and re-running inference with a higher response length, we introduce a data-driven safety mechanism, which trades a negligible padding overhead. As a whole, our framework limits the significant waste of computation on padding tokens and preserves output quality. Experimental validation on multiple datasets demonstrate that Predict-then-Diffuse significantly reduces computational costs (FLOP) compared to the default D-LLM inference mechanism and baselines based on heuristics, while being robust to skewed data distributions.

AI.tldr: A framework (Predict-then-Diffuse) to make diffusion-based LLMs compute-budget aware by predicting optimal response length (AdaRLP) before inference; includes a safety padding strategy; yields substantial FLOP reductions while maintaining quality across datasets.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based LLMs generate tokens in parallel, but require a fixed response length. Oversized lengths waste computation on padding; undersized lengths cause truncation and costly re-computation with unpredictable latency. A compute-budgeted inference approach is needed.
AI.method: Introduce an Adaptive Response Length Predictor (AdaRLP) as an auxiliary predictor that estimates the optimal response length from the input query. Incorporate a data-driven safety mechanism to prevent under-prediction, trading a small padding overhead. Integrate into the D-LLM inference pipeline and evaluate on multiple datasets.
AI.result: Significant reduction in FLOPs compared to standard D-LLM inference and heuristic baselines, while maintaining output quality. The method is robust to skewed data distributions and reduces wasted computation due to padding or re-computation.
AI.conclusion: Predict-then-Diffuse enables compute-budgeted inference for D-LLMs by accurately predicting response length and safely guarding against under-prediction, yielding efficiency gains without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Open-Source Image Editing Models Are Zero-Shot Vision Learners
summary:
Recent studies have shown that large generative models can solve vision tasks they were not explicitly trained for. However, existing evidence relies on closed-source models~(Veo~3, Nano Banana Pro) or requires task-specific instruction tuning, leaving open whether publicly available image-editing models possess zero-shot vision abilities out of the box.
  We conduct a systematic evaluation of three open-source image-editing models -- Qwen-Image-Edit, FireRed-Image-Edit, and LongCat-Image-Edit -- on dense visual prediction tasks \emph{without any fine-tuning}. We benchmark monocular depth estimation on NYUv2 and DIODE, surface normal estimation on NYUv2, and semantic segmentation on Cityscapes, covering both geometric and semantic scene understanding.
  Results show that open-source image-editing models exhibit non-trivial zero-shot visual understanding. On NYUv2 surface normals, FireRed-Image-Edit achieves a mean angular error of $17.69^\circ$, surpassing the fine-tuned Marigold ($20.86^\circ$) and matching the instruction-tuned Vision Banana ($17.78^\circ$) without any task-specific training. On NYUv2 depth estimation, LongCat-Image-Edit obtains $δ_1{=}0.822$ with affine alignment, and Qwen-Image-Edit leads on DIODE Indoor ($δ_1{=}0.868$). On Cityscapes semantic segmentation, Qwen-Image-Edit reaches 25.7 mIoU at the 19-class level and 49.5 mIoU at a coarser 7-category level. By comparing three independently trained editors, we test whether zero-shot vision ability is an emergent property of image-editing pretraining rather than a model-specific artifact. Code, evaluation scripts, and all results are publicly released to serve as a reproducible baseline for future work.

AI.tldr: Open-source image-editing models show non-trivial zero-shot vision abilities on dense tasks without fine-tuning, across three editors and multiple datasets; some metrics rival or exceed fine-tuned or instruction-tuned baselines; code and results released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Existing evidence of zero-shot vision comes from closed-source models or task-specific instruction tuning; it remains unclear whether publicly available image-editing models have zero-shot vision capabilities out of the box.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of three open-source image-editing models (Qwen-Image-Edit, FireRed-Image-Edit, LongCat-Image-Edit) on monocular depth estimation (NYUv2, DIODE), surface normal estimation (NYUv2), and semantic segmentation (Cityscapes) without any fine-tuning; comparisons to tuned baselines; analysis across three independently trained editors to assess emergent properties; metrics include δ1, mean angular error, and mIoU.
AI.result: FireRed-Image-Edit achieves 17.69° mean angular error on NYUv2 surface normals, surpassing the fine-tuned Marigold (20.86°) and matching the instruction-tuned Vision Banana (17.78°). LongCat-Image-Edit attains δ1=0.822 on NYUv2 (affine alignment); Qwen-Image-Edit leads on DIODE Indoor with δ1=0.868. For Cityscapes, Qwen-Image-Edit reaches 25.7 mIoU (19-class) and 49.5 mIoU (7-class). Across three independently trained editors, the study tests whether zero-shot vision ability is an emergent property of image-editing pretraining rather than a model-specific artifact; code and results are publicly released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot vision capabilities can arise from image-editing pretraining in open-source models, indicating that such abilities are not solely the result of task-specific fine-tuning or proprietary models. This suggests a broader, emergent phenomenon in vision-language or vision-editing pretraining; the released resources enable reproducibility and further exploration.

title: Jordan-RoPE: Non-Semisimple Relative Positional Encoding via Complex Jordan Blocks
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables; code available at https://github.com/ybzhang-nxu/jordan_rope
summary:
Relative positional encodings determine which functions of query-key lag can enter the primitive attention logit. RoPE supplies a rotary phase, while ALiBi supplies an additive distance bias. Motivated by group-theoretic views of linear translation-invariant positional encodings, we study a non-semisimple case in which a complex rotary eigenvalue and a nilpotent response live in the same defective Jordan block. The resulting relative operator generates oscillatory-polynomial features such as $e^{-γd}\cos(ωd)$, $e^{-γd}\sin(ωd)$, $d e^{-γd}\cos(ωd)$, and $d e^{-γd}\sin(ωd)$, for causal lag $d=i-j\geq 0$. Thus the construction realizes a distance-modulated phase basis $d e^{iωd}$, rather than merely adding a separate distance channel to RoPE.
  We formulate Exact Jordan-RoPE as a non-semisimple one-parameter representation, give its real block form, and specify the contragredient query action required by non-orthogonal positional maps. We also distinguish this exact representation from stabilized variants whose bounded shear improves numerical behavior but breaks the exact group law. Kernel-level diagnostics and a Jordan-friendly synthetic language-model task show that the coupled Jordan basis is useful when the target contains distance-modulated phase interactions. On a small WikiText-103 byte language model, a scaled-exact variant improves over RoPE and direct-sum baselines within the Jordan family, while RoPE+ALiBi remains strongest overall. The evidence is structural rather than a broad performance claim.

AI.tldr: Introduces Exact Jordan-RoPE, a non-semisimple relative-position encoding using a Jordan block to realize distance-modulated phase basis, yielding oscillatory-polynomial features; shows structural benefits but not universal performance; ALiBi remains strong overall.
AI.motivation: Explore non-semisimple representations for relative positional encodings to capture distance-modulated phase interactions; bridge RoPE and distance biases; understand exact group law versus stabilized variants.
AI.method: Model uses a non-semisimple one-parameter representation with a complex rotary eigenvalue and nilpotent response within a single Jordan block; derive Exact Jordan-RoPE, its real block form, and contragredient query action; contrast with stabilized variants; implement kernel-level diagnostics and a Jordan-friendly synthetic language-model task; evaluate on a WikiText-103 byte-level LM.
AI.result: Relative operator yields oscillatory-polynomial features: e^{-γ d} cos(ω d), e^{-γ d} sin(ω d), d e^{-γ d} cos(ω d), d e^{-γ d} sin(ω d); scaled-exact variant outperforms RoPE and direct-sum baselines within the Jordan family on a small WikiText-103; RoPE+ALiBi remains strongest overall; evidence is structural rather than broad performance claim.
AI.conclusion: Exact Jordan-RoPE offers a principled non-semisimple representation enabling distance-modulated phase interactions; benefits are task- and structure-dependent; stabilization improves numerical behavior but sacrifices exact group law; further work to explore broader tasks and longer sequences.

title: Lightning Unified Video Editing via In-Context Sparse Attention
summary: Video editing has evolved toward In-Context Learning (ICL) paradigms, yet the resulting quadratic attention costs create a critical computational bottleneck. In this work, we propose In-context Sparse Attention (ISA), the first near-lossless empirical sparse framework tailored for ICL video editing.
