Our design is grounded in two key insights: first, context tokens exhibit significantly lower saliency than source tokens; second, we theoretically prove and empirically validate that Query sharpness correlates with approximation error. Motivated by these findings, ISA implements an efficient pre-selection strategy to prune redundant context, followed by a dynamic query grouping mechanism that routes high-error queries to full attention and low-error ones to a computationally efficient 0-th order Taylor sparse attention. Furthermore, we build \textbf{\texttt{LIVEditor}} , a novel lightning video editing model via ISA and a proposed video-editing data pipeline that curated a 1.7M high-quality dataset. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LIVEditor achieves a $\sim$60% reduction in attention-module latency while surpassing state-of-the-art methods across EditVerseBench, IVE-Bench, and VIE-Bench, delivering near-lossless acceleration without compromising visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: Near-lossless empirical sparse attention (ISA) for ICL video editing reduces attention latency by ~60% via pre-selection and dynamic query routing; introduces LIVEditor and a 1.7M-video dataset; achieves SOTA on benchmarks with preserved visual fidelity.
AI.motivation: Quadratic attention costs in in-context learning for video editing cause a computational bottleneck; need efficient methods that preserve fidelity. Findings: context tokens are less salient; query sharpness correlates with approximation error; motivate sparse, dynamic attention.
AI.method: ISA prunes redundant context through pre-selection; computes query sharpness to route high-error queries to full attention and low-error ones to 0-th order Taylor sparse attention. Builds LIVEditor by integrating ISA with a video-editing data pipeline and curates a 1.7M dataset for training/evaluation.
AI.result: ~60% reduction in attention-module latency; outperforms state-of-the-art on EditVerseBench, IVE-Bench, and VIE-Bench; achieves near-lossless acceleration with preserved visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: ISA delivers near-lossless sparse attention for ICL video editing; LIVEditor demonstrates practical speedups and fidelity. The 1.7M dataset provides a valuable resource for evaluation and future research.

title: Capabilities of Auto-encoders and Principal Component Analysis of the Reduction of Microstructural Images; Application on the Acceleration of Phase-Field Simulations
comment: 21 pages, 8 figures. Preprint version of article published in Computational Materials Science
summary: In this work, a data-driven framework based on Phase-Field simulations data is proposed to highlight the capabilities of neural networks to ensure accurate low dimensionality reduction of simulated microstructural images and to provide time-series analysis. The dataset was indeed constructed from high-fidelity Phase-Field simulations. Analyses demonstrated that the association of auto-encoder neural networks and principal component analyses leads to ensure efficient and significant dimensionality reduction: 1/196 of reduction ratio with more than 80% of accuracy. These findings give insight to apply analyses on data from the latent dimension. Application of Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks showed the possibility of making next frame predictions; that makes possible the acceleration of Phase-Field simulation without the need of high computing resources. We discussed the application of such a framework on various areas of research. Different methods are proposed from the conducted analyses, in order to ensure dimensionality reduction, including auto-encoders, principal component analysis and Artificial Neural Networks, and time-series analysis, including LSTM and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU).

AI.tldr: A data-driven framework combining auto-encoders, PCA, and time-series models (LSTM/GRU) on Phase-Field simulation data for efficient latent-space analysis and next-frame forecasting; achieves around 196× dimensionality reduction with >80% reconstruction accuracy, enabling accelerated simulations and broad applicability.
AI.motivation: To reduce computational costs and enable predictive analytics for microstructural evolution by learning compact latent representations from high-fidelity Phase-Field simulations and forecasting future frames.
AI.method: Data come from high-fidelity Phase-Field simulations. The approach fuses nonlinear dimensionality reduction via auto-encoders with linear reduction via PCA, operating on latent representations. Time-series forecasting is performed with LSTM and GRU networks to predict subsequent frames, enabling acceleration of simulations without heavy computing resources.
AI.result: Dimensionality is reduced by approximately 196× with reconstruction accuracy exceeding 80%. LSTM/GRU-based forecasts of next frames demonstrate potential to accelerate Phase-Field simulations by operating in latent space, reducing computational demands.
AI.conclusion: Combining auto-encoders, PCA, and recurrent neural networks provides effective latent-space analysis and reliable time-series forecasting, with broad applicability beyond Phase-Field data. The framework supports efficient dimensionality reduction and future-frame prediction, suggesting avenues for faster simulations and cross-domain adoption.

title: VL-UniTrack: A Unified Framework with Visual-Language Prompts for UAV-Ground Visual Tracking
summary: UAV-ground visual tracking (UGVT) aims to simultaneously track the same object from both the UAV and the ground view. However, existing two-stream methods suffer from isolated feature extraction and rely heavily on implicit appearance matching, which struggles to establish reliable correspondence under drastic view differences, leading to tracking unreliability. To address these limitations, we propose VL-UniTrack, a fully unified framework enhanced by visual-language prompts. By encoding features from both views within a single shared encoder, our method breaks the barrier of feature isolation to facilitate sufficient cross-view interaction. To overcome the ambiguity caused by relying solely on appearance matching, we design visual-language geometric prompting module, which fuses language descriptions with visual features to generate learnable prompts. These prompts are then fed into our prompt-guided cross-view adapter module to enable sufficient cross-view feature interaction and to guide the learning of view-specific feature representations. Furthermore, a confidence-modulated mutual distillation loss is proposed to regularize the training by mitigating noise propagation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the latest benchmark. The code can be downloaded in https://github.com/xuboyue1999/VL-UniTrack.git

AI.tldr: A unified visual-language prompting framework for UAV-ground visual tracking, VL-UniTrack, that enables cross-view interaction via a shared encoder and prompt-guided adapters, with a confidence-modulated mutual distillation loss, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To overcome isolated feature extraction and unreliable cross-view matching caused by drastic view differences in UAV-ground tracking, by fostering cross-view interaction and incorporating language-guided prompts.
AI.method: Use a single shared encoder to encode both UAV and ground-view features, introduce a visual-language geometric prompting module to fuse language descriptions with visual features into learnable prompts, employ a prompt-guided cross-view adapter to enforce cross-view interaction and learn view-specific representations, and apply a confidence-modulated mutual distillation loss to regularize training.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance on the latest UAV-ground tracking benchmark; code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VL-UniTrack framework effectively integrates language-guided prompts with unified cross-view features to improve robustness in UAV-ground tracking, with the prompt-guided adapter facilitating cross-view interaction and the distillation loss mitigating noise during training.

title: Layerwise LQR for Geometry-Aware Optimization of Deep Networks
summary: Geometry-aware optimizers such as Newton and natural gradient can improve conditioning in deep learning, but scalable variants such as K-FAC, Shampoo, and related preconditioners usually impose structural approximations early, often discarding cross-layer interactions induced by the network computation. We introduce Layerwise LQR (LLQR), a framework for learning structured inverse preconditioners under a global layerwise optimal-control objective. The starting point is an exact equivalence: the steepest-descent step under a broad class of divergence-induced quadratic models--including Newton, Gauss-Newton, Fisher/natural-gradient, and intermediate-layer metrics--can be written as a finite-horizon Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) problem. This formulation serves as a reference that exposes the layerwise dynamics and cost matrices encoding the original dense geometry. We then derive a scalable relaxation that learns diagonal, (E-)Kronecker-factored, or other structured inverse preconditioners by minimizing the LQR objective and reusing them across iterations. The resulting optimizer wraps standard methods while retaining a principled connection to second-order geometry, without forming or inverting the global curvature matrix. Experiments on ResNets and Transformers show that LLQR improves optimization dynamics and often translates these gains into improved final test performance, while adding only modest wall-clock overhead. It establishes LLQR as a practical framework for geometry-aware second-order methods and a reference for evaluating scalable approximations.

AI.tldr: LLQR learns structured, layerwise inverse preconditioners by reformulating the gradient step as a finite-horizon LQR, enabling diagonal or (E-)Kronecker-factored approximations that reuse across iterations without forming the full curvature. It improves optimization dynamics with modest overhead.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of scalable geometry-aware optimizers that discard cross-layer interactions and rely on restrictive structural approximations, by introducing a principled, layerwise optimal-control view that preserves second-order geometry while remaining scalable.
AI.method: Establish an exact equivalence: the steepest-descent step under divergence-induced quadratic models (Newton, Gauss-Newton, Fisher/natural gradient, etc.) equals a finite-horizon LQR. Use this as a reference to expose layerwise dynamics and cost. Propose a scalable relaxation that learns structured inverse preconditioners (diagonal, (E-)Kronecker, etc.) by minimizing the LQR objective and reusing across iterations, wrapping standard optimizers without forming/inverting the global curvature matrix.
AI.result: Experiments on ResNets and Transformers show LLQR improves optimization dynamics and often final test performance, with modest wall-clock overhead, and it serves as a practical geometry-aware framework and benchmark for scalable approximations.
AI.conclusion: LLQR provides a practical, geometry-aware second-order framework that preserves a connection to the underlying curvature geometry, while enabling scalable, structured preconditioners and offering a reference for evaluating approximations.

title: GTF: Omnidirectional EPI Transformer for Light Field Super-Resolution
summary: Light field (LF) image super-resolution benefits from Epipolar Plane Images (EPIs), whose line slopes explicitly encode disparity. However, existing Transformer-based LF SR methods mainly attend to horizontal and vertical EPIs, leaving diagonal epipolar geometry underexplored. We present GTF, an omnidirectional EPI Transformer that explicitly models horizontal, vertical, 45-degree, and 135-degree EPIs within a unified reconstruction framework. GTF combines directional EPI processing, MacPI-based prior injection, adaptive directional fusion, and a topology-preserving feed-forward network to better exploit LF geometry. For the NTIRE 2026 fidelity tracks, we use GTF as the main model, while a lightweight GTF-Tiny variant targets the efficiency track. On five standard LF SR benchmarks covering both real-captured and synthetic scenes, GTF reaches 32.78 dB without inference-time enhancement, and stronger inference settings with EPSW and test-time augmentation further improve performance. Under the NTIRE 2026 efficiency constraint, GTF-Tiny attains 32.57 dB with only 0.915M parameters and 19.81 GFLOPs. In the NTIRE 2026 Light Field Image Super-Resolution Challenge, our submissions rank 3rd on Track 1 and Track 3 and 4th on Track 2. Architecture-evolution, channel-width, and inference analyses further support the effectiveness of diagonal EPI modeling, directional fusion, and the lightweight design.

AI.tldr: Introduces GTF, an omnidirectional EPI Transformer for light-field SR that models horizontal, vertical, and diagonal (45°,135°) EPIs within a unified framework; achieves strong fidelity and efficiency, with a lightweight GTF-Tiny under NTIRE 2026 constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing Transformer-based LF SR methods focus mainly on horizontal/vertical EPIs, underutilizing diagonal EPI geometry. Exploiting omnidirectional EPIs can better capture LF geometry and disparity, improving reconstruction quality while maintaining efficiency.
AI.method: GTF processes four EPI orientations (H/V/45°/135°) via directional EPI processing. It includes MacPI-based prior injection, adaptive directional fusion, and a topology-preserving feed-forward network to preserve LF geometry. A lightweight GTF-Tiny variant adapts the model for efficiency tracks. EPSW and test-time augmentation are used to push performance.
AI.result: On five standard LF SR benchmarks (real and synthetic scenes), GTF achieves 32.78 dB without inference-time enhancement; with inference-time strategies (EPSW, TTA), performance improves further. Under NTIRE 2026 efficiency constraints, GTF-Tiny achieves 32.57 dB with 0.915M parameters and 19.81 GFLOPs. Submissions rank 3rd on Track 1 and Track 3 and 4th on Track 2 in the NTIRE 2026 challenge. Ablations/analyses support the effectiveness of diagonal EPI modeling, directional fusion, and lightweight design.
AI.conclusion: Modeling all four EPI orientations within a unified, topology-aware framework yields tangible gains in LF SR quality while enabling compact variants suitable for efficiency-limited settings. The results substantiate the importance of diagonal epipolar geometry and adaptive fusion in exploiting LF structure.

title: Adaptive Consensus in LLM Ensembles via Sequential Evidence Accumulation: Automatic Budget Identification and Calibrated Commit Signals
summary: Large Language Model ensembles improve reasoning accuracy up to a performance boundary; beyond it, additional deliberation degrades accuracy. Static-budget methods cannot detect this boundary. Extended-thinking architectures compound the problem: a wrong answer after 120k tokens is indistinguishable from a correct one. We introduce DASE (Deliberative Adaptive Stopping Ensemble), a stopping heuristic for iterative ensemble deliberation that commits early on genuine consensus and applies a global-frequency fallback on fragmented evidence. Two configurations are evaluated: a persistence heuristic and DASE-Spatial (arena half-width W). Three contributions. (1) DASE produces a commit-type routing partition complementary to verbalized single-call confidence. On a contamination-controlled corpus (AIME 2010-2023, N=254, 3 seeds), a 120B ensemble achieves a 24.8 pp routing gap (right-wall 97.1% vs. left-wall 73.6%), statistically equivalent to Opus 4.6 Standard verbalized confidence at coverage-matched threshold (25.7 pp gap; bootstrap CI on difference: [-12.0, +10.3] pp, p=0.873). The two mechanisms disagree on 27% of routing assignments, establishing them as complements rather than substitutes; every DASE decision is accompanied by a machine-readable deliberation record. (2) Adaptive stopping, not injection bandwidth, drives accuracy gains. On AIME-300, bandwidth accounts for only 0.3 pp (ns); on GPQA-Extended, 4.4 pp bandwidth versus 5.0 pp stopping effect. DASE-Spatial ties Debate-Dense at its optimal budget using one-tenth the injection bandwidth and identifies that budget automatically; W=8 (65.0%) significantly outperforms W=4 (59.3%) on AIME-300 (adj p=0.0042). (3) Injection-based methods exhibit a retrospective accuracy-vs-inference inverted-U on both benchmarks; this pattern is hypothesis-generating for future work.

AI.tldr: DASE: a deliberative adaptive stopping ensemble that commits on consensus and uses a global fallback to handle fragmented evidence. It yields substantial routing gains and reveals adaptive stopping as the primary driver of accuracy over mere bandwidth, while exposing complementarity with verbalized confidence and an inverted-U pattern for injection-based methods.
AI.motivation: Address the performance boundary in large-language-model ensembles where extra deliberation can reduce accuracy, and overcome static-budget methods' inability to detect when continued deliberation is harmful.
AI.method: Introduce DASE with two configurations (Persistence heuristic and DASE-Spatial). Use commit-type routing partitions and a global-frequency fallback. Evaluate on contamination-controlled AIME datasets with large ensembles (e.g., 120B) and compare routing and confidence to verbalized confidence; analyze bandwidth versus stopping effects and the impact of spatial budget (W) on AIME-300; assess retroactive accuracy-vs-inference trends.
AI.result: DASE achieves a 24.8 percentage-point routing gap on a contamination-controlled corpus (right-wall 97.1% vs left-wall 73.6%), statistically equivalent to Opus 4.6-standard verbalized confidence at a matched coverage threshold (25.7 pp gap; bootstrap CI [-12.0, +10.3] pp, p=0.873). The two mechanisms disagree on 27% of routing assignments, indicating complementarity and resulting machine-readable deliberation records for each decision. Adaptive stopping yields most gains: bandwidth contributes minimally (0.3 pp on AIME-300; ns) while stopping contributes more (GPQA-Extended: 4.4 pp bandwidth vs. 5.0 pp stopping). DASE-Spatial (W=8) outperforms W=4 on AIME-300 (65.0% vs 59.3%; adj p=0.0042). Injection-based methods exhibit an inverted-U in retrospective accuracy vs input effort across benchmarks, suggesting boundary-detection and budget-aware strategies.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive stopping is the primary driver of accuracy gains in deliberative ensembles, with bandwidth being limited in impact. DASE provides a practical, interpretable routing mechanism with machine-readable deliberation traces and reveals complementarities with verbalized confidence. The observed inverted-U trend for injection-based methods motivates future work on boundary-aware deliberation and adaptive budgeting.

title: From Diffusion to Rectified Flow: Rethinking Text-Based Segmentation
summary: Text-based image segmentation aims to delineate object boundaries within an image from text prompts, offering higher flexibility and broader application scope compared to traditional fixed-category segmentation tasks. Recent studies have shown that diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) can provide rich multimodal semantic features, leading to studies of using diffusion models as feature extractors for segmentation tasks. Such methods, however, inherit the generative natures of diffusion models that are harmful to discriminative segmentation tasks. In response, we propose RLFSeg, a novel framework that leverages Rectified Flow to learn direct mapping from the image to the segmentation mask within the latent space. The model is thus freed from the noise-denoise process and the need to optimize the time step of diffusion models, resulting in substantially better performance than previous diffusion-based methods, especially on zero-shot scenarios. By introducing label refinement and an Adaptive One-Step Sampling strategy, the model achieves higher accuracy even on a single inference step. The framework redirects a pretrained generative model to the discriminative segmentation task with zero modification to model structure, thus reveals promising application potential and significant research value.

AI.tldr: RLFSeg maps images to segmentation masks in latent space using Rectified Flow, avoiding diffusion denoising and time-step optimization; achieves superior zero-shot segmentation and single-step sampling.
AI.motivation: To overcome the generative nature of diffusion models when used for discriminative segmentation and to improve zero-shot performance.
AI.method: Use Rectified Flow to learn a direct image-to-mask mapping in latent space; remove noise-denoise process and time-step optimization; add label refinement and Adaptive One-Step Sampling; keep model structure unchanged.
AI.result: Outperforms prior diffusion-based methods, particularly in zero-shot scenarios; high accuracy with a single inference step.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that pretrained generative models can be repurposed for discriminative segmentation without architectural modification, unlocking potential and research value.

title: DiCLIP: Diffusion Model Enhances CLIP's Dense Knowledge for Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation
comment: This work is accepted by IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
summary: Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation (WSSS) with image-level labels typically leverages Class Activation Maps (CAMs) to achieve pixel-level predictions. Recently, Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) has been introduced to generate CAMs in WSSS. However, previous WSSS methods solely adopt CLIP's vision-language paired property for dense localization, neglecting its inherently limited dense knowledge across both visual and text modalities, which renders CAM generation suboptimal. In this work, we propose DiCLIP, a novel WSSS framework that leverages the generative diffusion model to enhance CLIP's dense knowledge across two modalities. Specifically, Visual Correlation Enhancement (VCE) and Text Semantic Augmentation (TSA) modules are proposed for dense prediction enhancement. To improve the spatial awareness of visual features, our VCE module utilizes diffusion's reliable spatial consistency to mitigate the over-smoothing issue in CLIP's attention. It designs the Attention Clustering Refinement (ACR) module to reliably extract diverse correlation maps from the diffusion model. The correlation maps act as a diversity bias for CLIP's self-attention, recursively pushing its visual features towards a more discriminative dense distribution. To augment the semantics of text embeddings, our TSA module argues that a single text modality is insufficient to encompass the variability of visual categories. Thus, we leverage diffusion's generative power to maintain a dynamic key-value cache model, shifting CAM generation from a patch-text matching mechanism to a novel visual knowledge retrieval paradigm. With these enhancements, DiCLIP not only outperforms state-of-the-art methods on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO but also significantly reduces training costs. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/zwyang6/DiCLIP.

AI.tldr: DiCLIP leverages diffusion models to enhance CLIP-based weakly supervised semantic segmentation by introducing Visual Correlation Enhancement (VCE) and Text Semantic Augmentation (TSA), yielding better CAM quality, improved segmentation on VOC/COCO, and reduced training cost.
AI.motivation: CLIP-based CAMs offer weak dense localization due to limited cross-modal dense knowledge; diffusion models provide reliable spatial consistency and richer semantic representations across visual and textual modalities, enabling more accurate and efficient WSSS.
AI.method: Two modules: Visual Correlation Enhancement (VCE) with Attention Clustering Refinement (ACR) to extract diverse diffusion-derived correlation maps that bias CLIP self-attention toward discriminative dense features; Text Semantic Augmentation (TSA) to maintain a dynamic diffusion-driven key-value cache, enabling a retrieval-based visual knowledge augmentation and expanding text semantics beyond a single modality.
AI.result: DiCLIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO for WSSS, while significantly reducing training costs; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-assisted cross-modal enhancement (DiCLIP) effectively enriches both visual and textual representations for dense semantic segmentation, delivering superior CAMs and efficient training.

title: Hierarchical Support Vector State Partitioning for Distilling Black Box Reinforcement Learning Policies
comment: Accepted for poster presentation at HHAI 2026
summary: We introduce State Vector Space Partitioning (SVSP), a novel method to mimic a black box reinforcement learning policy using a set of human-interpretable subpolicies. By partitioning a distillation dataset of state action pairs with linear support vector machine splits, SVSP constructs a compact and structured representation of the original policy. Our method improves mean return by +7.4\% over previous critic driven state partitioning attempts such as Voronoi State Partitioning (VSP) and +2.8\% over the original TD3 policy, while reducing the number of required subpolicies against VSP by 82.1\%. Our results pave the path towards a more flexible form of distillation where both the decision boundary and surrogate models can be chosen within a margin of the original black box behavior.

AI.tldr: A new SVSP method uses linear SVM partitions to distill a black-box RL policy into interpretable subpolicies, improving performance and reducing subpolicies versus VSP, enabling flexible distillation.
AI.motivation: Improve interpretability and efficiency of policy distillation by forming a compact, structured surrogate that preserves behavior close to the original black-box policy.
AI.method: Partition a distillation dataset of state-action pairs with linear SVM boundaries to create a set of interpretable subpolicies, resulting in a compact representation. Compare against Voronoi State Partitioning (VSP) and the original TD3 policy.
AI.result: Mean return improvements: +7.4% over VSP; +2.8% over TD3. Subpolicy count reduced by 82.1% relative to VSP.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a flexible distillation framework where both decision boundaries and surrogate models can be selected within a margin of the original black-box behavior, enabling scalable interpretable RL.

title: Reference-based Category Discovery: Unsupervised Object Detection with Category Awareness
summary: Traditional one-shot detection methods have addressed the closed-set problem in object detection, but the high cost of data annotation remains a critical challenge. General unsupervised methods generate pseudo boxes without category labels, thus failing to achieve category-aware classification. To overcome these limitations, we propose Reference-based Category Discovery (RefCD), an unsupervised detector that enables category-aware\footnotemark[1] detection without any manually annotated labels. It leverages feature similarity between predicted objects and unlabeled reference images. Unlike previous unsupervised methods that lack category guidance and one-shot methods which require labeled data, RefCD introduces a carefully designed feature similarity loss to explicitly guide the learning of potential category-specific features. Additionally, RefCD supports category-agnostic detection without reference images, serving as a unified framework. Comprehensive quantitative and qualitative analysis of category-aware and category-agnostic detection results demonstrates its effectiveness, and RefCD can learn category information in an unsupervised paradigm even without category labels.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised detector with category-aware discovery using reference-based feature similarity (RefCD); learns category-specific features without labels and also supports category-agnostic detection within a unified framework using a feature similarity loss.
AI.motivation: To address the high annotation cost in one-shot/object detection and the limitation that existing unsupervised methods lack category guidance, by enabling category-aware detection without manual labels.
AI.method: Introduce RefCD, which leverages feature similarity between predicted objects and unlabeled reference images. Propose a feature similarity loss that guides learning toward category-specific features. Also design a category-agnostic detection pathway that operates without reference images, yielding a unified framework.
AI.result: Extensive quantitative and qualitative analyses demonstrate effectiveness for both category-aware and category-agnostic detection, showing RefCD can learn category information in an unsupervised setting without labels.
AI.conclusion: RefCD enables category-aware unsupervised detection and a unified category-agnostic detection framework, achieving category information discovery without category labels and outperforming prior unsupervised methods lacking guidance.

title: HUGO-CS: A Hybrid-Labeled, Uncertainty-Aware, General-Purpose, Observational Dataset for Cold Spray
summary: Cold spraying is an increasingly common approach for repairing and manufacturing components due to its solid-state manufacturing capabilities. However, process optimization remains difficult due to many interdependent parameters and the lack of large-scale, machine-readable data to support modeling. While the scientific literature contains many relevant experiments, results are inconsistently reported (often in tables and figures) and use non-uniform units, limiting utilization at scale. To address these limitations, this work presents HUGO-CS, a literature-derived dataset of 4,383 cold-spray experiments with 144 features from 1,124 sources, exceeding the previous largest dataset (137 samples) by 30x. With completely manual extraction requiring an average of 91 minutes per document, this work designs and leverages a Hybrid-labeled, Uncertainty-aware, General-purpose, Observational extraction framework, called HUGO, to support this extraction. HUGO combines automated LLM-based labeling with targeted manual label refinement to handle this experimental result extraction process from scientific literature. To balance labeling efficiency with extraction accuracy, HUGO introduces a Hierarchical Risk Mitigation (HRM) to route LLM outputs with a high risk of potential errors for manual review, while retaining low-risk records as auto-labeled. Lastly, HUGO post-processing consolidates categorical descriptors, maps reported feedstock chemistries into structured continuous compositions, and normalizes units across sources. Of the 4,383 reported experiments, 1,765 are hand-labeled, providing a high-quality labeled subset for benchmarking, error analysis, and higher-fidelity data points. All code to replicate this work, along with the complete HUGO-CS dataset, are released under a CC-BY license at https://github.com/sprice134/HUGO.

AI.tldr: HUGO-CS is a large, manually-augmented literature-derived dataset for cold-spray experiments, built with a Hybrid-labeled, Uncertainty-aware framework (HUGO) and Hierarchical Risk Mitigation to maximize labeling efficiency and accuracy; dataset size (4,383 experiments, 144 features, 1,124 sources) surpasses prior work; code and data released CC-BY.
AI.motivation: Address the shortage of large-scale, machine-readable, uniformly reported data in cold-spray literature to enable modeling and optimization.
AI.method: Combine automated LLM-based labeling with targeted manual refinement via HRM; manual labeling for subset; post-processing to harmonize units, descriptors, and chemistry to structured formats; dataset release and replication materials.
AI.result: 4,383 experiments extracted from 1,124 sources; 1,765 hand-labeled cases; 144 features; 30x larger than prior largest dataset; improved data quality and traceability; code and data released.
AI.conclusion: HUGO framework enables scalable, uncertainty-aware extraction of science results from literature, delivering a reusable dataset for modeling and benchmarking in cold spraying.

title: Advancing Aesthetic Image Generation via Composition Transfer
summary: Composition is a cornerstone of visual aesthetics, influencing the appeal of an image. While its principles operate independently of specific content, in practice, composition is often coupled with semantics. As a result, existing methods often enhance composition either through implicit learning or by semantics-based layout control, rather than explicitly modeling composition itself. To address this gap, we introduce Composer, a framework rooted in aesthetic theory, designed to model composition in a semantic-agnostic manner. First, it supports composition transfer by extracting key composition-aware representations from a reference image and leveraging a tailored conditional guidance module to control composition based on pre-trained diffusion models. Second, when users specify only text themes without a composition reference, Composer supports theme-driven composition retrieval by leveraging the in-context learning capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), achieving explicit composition planning. To enhance composition in a reference-free mode, we conduct text-to-composition fine-tuning on the trained control module to enable implicit composition planning. Furthermore, we curated a high-quality dataset comprising 2 million image-text pairs using state-of-the-art generative models to support model training. Experimental results demonstrate that Composer significantly enhances aesthetic quality in text-to-image tasks and facilitates personalized composition control and transfer, offering users precision and flexibility in the creative process.

AI.tldr: Composer is a semantic-agnostic framework for explicit composition modeling in diffusion-based image synthesis, enabling reference-based transfer, theme-driven planning via LVLMs, and data-driven fine-tuning, plus a 2M-pair dataset; it improves aesthetic quality and user control in text-to-image tasks.
AI.motivation: Current approaches tie composition to semantics, either implicitly or through semantics-based layouts, leaving explicit, content-agnostic composition modeling underexplored. The work aims to formalize composition as an explicit controllable factor grounded in aesthetic theory.
AI.method: Three-part design: (1) composition transfer via extracting composition-aware representations from a reference image and a tailored conditional guidance module in pretrained diffusion models; (2) theme-driven composition retrieval using LVLMs to enable explicit composition planning from text themes through in-context learning; (3) text-to-composition fine-tuning of the control module to enable implicit composition planning in reference-free scenarios. Also, a 2M-image-text dataset is curated using state-of-the-art generative models to support training.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial improvement in aesthetic quality for text-to-image tasks and enable personalized composition control and transfer, offering precision and flexibility in creative workflows.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an aesthetic-theory–driven, semantic-agnostic approach to modeling composition, enabling explicit transfer, LVLM-guided planning, and fine-tuning within diffusion-based generation, backed by a large training dataset.

title: Explaining and Preventing Alignment Collapse in Iterative RLHF
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) typically assumes a static or non-strategic reward model (RM). In iterative deployment, however, the policy generates the data on which the RM is retrained, creating a feedback loop. Building on the Stackelberg game formulation of this interaction, we derive an analytical decomposition of the policy's true optimization gradient into a standard policy gradient and a parameter-steering term that captures the policy's influence on the RM's future parameters. We show that standard iterative RLHF, which drops this steering term entirely, suffers from alignment collapse: the policy systematically exploits the RM's blind spots, producing low-quality, high-reward outputs whose feedback reinforces the very errors it exploits. To mitigate this, we propose foresighted policy optimization (FPO), a mechanism-design intervention that restores the missing steering term by regularizing the policy's parameter-steering effect on RM updates. We instantiate FPO via a scalable first-order approximation and demonstrate that it prevents alignment collapse on both controlled environments and an LLM alignment pipeline using Llama-3.2-1B.

AI.tldr: Stackelberg-based RLHF analysis reveals a missing steering term; introduces foresighted policy optimization (FPO) to regularize RM updates; demonstrates effectiveness including Llama-3.2-1B.
AI.motivation: In iterative RLHF, the policy shapes the data used to retrain the reward model, creating a feedback loop that standard RLHF ignores, leading to alignment collapse.
AI.method: Analytical decomposition of the policy's gradient into standard policy gradient plus a parameter-steering term; propose FPO as a mechanism-design intervention; implement via scalable first-order approximation.
AI.result: FPO prevents alignment collapse in controlled environments and an LLM alignment pipeline using Llama-3.2-1B.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating the steering term via FPO stabilizes RLHF by accounting for the policy's influence on RM updates; the approach is scalable and effective across settings.

title: Temporal Structure Matters for Efficient Test-Time Adaptation in Wearable Human Activity Recognition
summary: Wearable human activity recognition (WHAR) models often suffer from performance degradation under real-world cross-user distribution shifts. Test-time adaptation (TTA) mitigates this degradation by adapting models online using unlabeled test streams, yet existing methods largely inherit assumptions from vision tasks and underexploit the inherent inter-window temporal structure in WHAR streams. In this paper, we revisit such temporal structure as a feature-conditioned inference signal rather than merely an output-space smoothing prior. We derive the insight that temporal continuity and observation-induced feature deviations provide complementary cues for determining when to preserve or release temporal inertia and where to route prediction refinement during likely transitions. Building upon this insight, we propose SIGHT, a lightweight and backpropagation-free TTA framework for WHAR, enabling real-time edge deployment. SIGHT estimates predictive surprise by comparing the current feature with a prototype-based expected state, and then uses the resulting feature deviation to guide geometry-aware transition routing based on prototype alignment and stream-level marginal habit tracking. Evaluations on real-world datasets confirm that SIGHT outperforms existing TTA baselines while reducing computational and memory costs.

AI.tldr: SIGHT is a lightweight, backpropagation-free TTA framework for wearable HAR that exploits feature-level temporal structure via predictive surprise and prototype-based routing to improve accuracy with lower compute/memory, enabling real-time edge deployment.
AI.motivation: WHAR models suffer from cross-user distribution shifts; existing TTA largely copies vision-based assumptions and underutilizes intra-window temporal structure. The motivation is to treat temporal dynamics as a feature-conditioned inference signal to guide adaptation.
AI.method: Introduce SIGHT: compute predictive surprise by comparing current feature to a prototype-based expected state, derive a feature deviation, and use it to perform geometry-aware transition routing guided by prototype alignment and stream-level marginal habit tracking. Backpropagation-free, lightweight architecture designed for real-time edge deployment.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world WHAR datasets shows that SIGHT outperforms existing TTA baselines while reducing computational and memory costs, enabling real-time edge deployment.
AI.conclusion: Temporal structure can be effectively leveraged as a feature-conditioned signal for TTA in WHAR. SIGHT demonstrates accurate, efficient adaptation and offers potential applicability to other streaming, edge-constrained domains.

title: QUIVER: Cost-Aware Adaptive Preference Querying in Surrogate-Assisted Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization
comment: Accepted at Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference (GECCO '26)
summary:
Interactive multi-objective optimization systems face a budget allocation dilemma: one can spend resources on expensive objective evaluations or on eliciting decision-maker preferences that identify the relevant region of the Pareto set. Moreover, preference elicitation itself spans modalities with different information content and cognitive burden, ranging from cheap, noisy pairwise preference statements (PS) to richer but costlier indifference adjustments (IA).
  We study cost-aware optimization under an unknown scalarization and introduce QUIVER (Query-Informed Value Estimation for Regret), a surrogate-assisted evolutionary multi-objective optimizer that adaptively chooses between objective evaluations and heterogeneous preference queries. At each step, QUIVER selects the next action by maximizing the expected decision-quality improvement per unit total cost. Across DTLZ and WFG benchmarks under synthetic decision-maker models, QUIVER achieves the lowest final utility regret on challenging WFG problems (utility regret of 2.14 on WFG4, 2.82 on WFG9: a 25% improvement over baselines), outperforming all single-modality baselines. We analyze how the optimal mix of PS and IA adapts to problem difficulty: on easy problems (DTLZ2), QUIVER selects 80\% PS queries; on hard problems (WFG9), it shifts to 35% IA queries. This adaptive modality selection demonstrates cost-aware preference learning in action.

AI.tldr: A cost-aware interactive MO optimizer (QUIVER) adaptively allocates budget between expensive objective evaluations and heterogeneous preference queries, achieving superior final utility on benchmarks by learning preferences and objectives jointly.
AI.motivation: Budget-limited, unknown scalarization in multi-objective optimization requires balancing evaluation costs and information gain from user preferences across modalities with varying cognitive burden.
AI.method: Introduce QUIVER, a surrogate-assisted evolutionary MO optimizer that at each step selects the next action (objective evaluation or PS/IA query) by maximizing expected decision-quality improvement per unit cost; uses unknown scalarization and benchmarks on DTLZ/WFG with synthetic decision-makers.
AI.result: QUIVER attains the lowest final utility regret on hard WFG problems (e.g., 2.14 on WFG4, 2.82 on WFG9; ~25% better than baselines); demonstrates adaptive modality mix (80% PS on easy DTLZ2, 35% IA on hard WFG9), outperforming single-modality baselines.
AI.conclusion: Cost-aware, adaptive fusion of objective evaluations and preference elicitation enables efficient learning of decision preferences and Pareto-front optimization, with substantial gains on benchmark problems.

title: UniPCB: A Generation-Assisted Detection Framework for PCB Defect Inspection
summary: Printed Circuit Board (PCB) defect inspection faces two compounding challenges: scarce and imbalanced defect samples that limit model training, and insufficient feature representation under complex circuit backgrounds. Existing generation methods rely on single-modality conditions with coarse structural control, while detection methods improve architectures without addressing the data bottleneck. To resolve both challenges jointly, we propose a generation-assisted PCB defect inspection framework that integrates controlled defect synthesis with task-specific defect detection. On the generation side, a Multi-modal Condition Generator extracts complementary edge, depth, and text conditions in parallel. A ScaleEncoder then embeds these conditions into the diffusion U-Net at four resolutions, and a Condition Modulation applies FiLM-style spatially-adaptive modulation at each scale, enabling structurally aligned and defect-aware sample synthesis. On the detection side, an Inverted Residual Shift Attention couples self-attention with shift-wise convolution to jointly capture global context and local texture, and a Cross-level Complementary Fusion Block generates pixel-level gates for selective cross-level feature fusion. The synthesized samples directly enrich the detection training set, so that improvements in generation compound with improvements in detection. Extensive experiments on DsPCBSD+ demonstrate that UniPCB achieves mAP@0.5 of 98.0% and mAP@0.5:0.95 of 61.8% on defect detection, surpassing all compared methods, while the generation branch attains an FID of 129.61 and SSIM of 0.619, outperforming existing conditional generation approaches.

AI.tldr: Jointly addresses data scarcity and feature representation in PCB defect inspection with a diffusion-based generation branch and a specialized detector. The Multi-modal Condition Generator with FiLM-like modulation guides defect synthesis; the detector uses Inverted Residual Shift Attention and Cross-level Complementary Fusion to fuse multi-scale features. Reports strong detection metrics (mAP@0.5 98.0%, mAP@0.5:0.95 61.8%) and competitive generation metrics (FID 129.61, SSIM 0.619) on DsPCBSD+ dataset.
AI.motivation: SCARCE/imbalanced defect samples and challenging complex PCB backgrounds hinder training and generalization. Existing work typically tackles generation or detection in isolation; a joint framework could leverage synthetic data to improve detection while maintaining structural realism.
AI.method: Generation: Multi-modal Condition Generator extracts edge, depth, and text cues in parallel. ScaleEncoder embeds these cues into diffusion U-Net across four resolutions. Condition Modulation applies FiLM-style spatially-adaptive modulation at every scale for structurally aligned, defect-aware synthesis. Detection: Inverted Residual Shift Attention blends self-attention with shift-convolution to capture global and local cues. Cross-level Complementary Fusion Block produces pixel-level gates for selective cross-scale feature fusion. Training uses synthesized samples to augment detection data.
AI.result: On DsPCBSD+ dataset, UniPCB achieves mAP@0.5 of 98.0% and mAP@0.5:0.95 of 61.8%, outperforming baselines. The generation branch yields an FID of 129.61 and SSIM of 0.619, surpassing existing conditional generation methods.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing generation and detection yields compounding gains: better data quality through conditioned synthesis improves detection performance, while a strong detector architecture better exploits augmented data. Potential for broader application in data-limited industrial vision tasks.

title: A Mean Curvature Approach to Boundary Detection: Geometric Insights for Unsupervised Learning
summary: Accurate boundary detection in high-dimensional data remains a central challenge in unsupervised learning, particularly in the presence of non-linear structures and heterogeneous densities. In this work, we introduce Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP), a novel geometric framework grounded in Geometric Machine Learning that departs from traditional density-based approaches by explicitly modeling the intrinsic curvature of the data manifold. The method relies on a discrete approximation of the shape operator, estimated from local k-nearest neighbor patches, to compute pointwise mean curvature without requiring explicit manifold parametrization. The key insight of MCBP is to use mean curvature as a principled descriptor of boundary structure: high-curvature regions naturally correspond to transitions between clusters, geometric irregularities, and low-density interfaces. This yields a unified geometric interpretation of boundary, outlier, and transition points. We further introduce an adaptive percentile-based thresholding scheme that enables multiscale boundary extraction without relying on ad hoc density parameters. Beyond detection, we propose a curvature-driven data decomposition that separates samples into smooth (low-curvature) and boundary (high-curvature) subsets, effectively acting as a non-linear geometric filtering mechanism. This representation enhances cluster separability and improves the robustness of downstream unsupervised algorithms. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that MCBP consistently improves clustering performance, particularly in complex and high-dimensional scenarios. These results position MCBP as a concrete contribution to Geometric Machine Learning, highlighting the potential of curvature-aware analysis as a unifying paradigm bridging differential geometry and data-driven modeling.

AI.tldr: Introduces Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP), a curvature-based geometric framework for boundary detection in high-dimensional data using a discrete shape operator estimated from local patches; includes adaptive multiscale thresholding and curvature-driven data decomposition, yielding improved clustering in complex data.
AI.motivation: Density-based boundary detection struggles with nonlinear manifolds, heterogeneous densities, and high dimensionality. A curvature-based descriptor (mean curvature) can capture intrinsic geometry, unify boundary/outlier/transition points, and improve downstream clustering.
AI.method: Estimate the discrete shape operator from local k-NN patches to compute pointwise mean curvature without explicit manifold parametrization. Use adaptive percentile-based thresholding for multiscale boundary extraction. Introduce curvature-driven data decomposition to separate low-curvature (smooth) from high-curvature (boundary) samples, acting as a nonlinear geometric filter.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets show consistent improvements in clustering performance, especially in complex/high-dimensional settings, validating curvature-aware boundary detection as a unifying geometric framework.
AI.conclusion: MCBP offers a concrete geometric framework in Geometric Machine Learning, positioning curvature-aware analysis as a unifying paradigm that links differential geometry with data-driven modeling and enhances boundary, outlier, and transition identification for unsupervised learning.

title: CAST: Mitigating Object Hallucination in Large Vision-Language Models via Caption-Guided Visual Attention Steering
summary: Although Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance on downstream tasks, they frequently produce contents that deviate from visual information, leading to object hallucination. To tackle this, recent works mostly depend on expensive manual annotations and training cost, or decoding strategies which significantly increase inference time. In this work, we observe that LVLMs' attention to visual information is significantly enhanced when answering caption queries compared to non-caption queries. Inspired by this phenomenon, we propose Caption-guided Visual Attention Steering (CAST), a training-free, plug-and-play hallucination mitigation method that leverages the attention activation pattern corresponding to caption queries to enhance LVLMs' visual perception capability. Specifically, we use probing techniques to identify attention heads that are highly sensitive to caption queries and estimate optimized steering directions for their outputs. This steering strengthens LVLM's fine-grained visual perception capabilities, thereby effectively mitigating object hallucination. CAST reduced object hallucination by an average of 6.03% across five widely used LVLMs and five benchmarks including both discriminative and generative tasks, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance while adding little inference cost and preserving other foundational capabilities.

AI.tldr: CAST is a training-free attention-steering method that reduces object hallucination in LVLMs by leveraging caption-query–driven visual attention, achieving ~6% average improvement across multiple models and tasks with minimal cost.
AI.motivation: Mitigate object hallucination in LVLMs without expensive annotations or inference-time decoding strategies.
AI.method: Training-free, plug-and-play approach that probes for caption-sensitive attention heads and estimates steering directions for their outputs, then applies steering during inference to enhance fine-grained visual perception.
AI.result: Average reduction of object hallucination by 6.03% across five LVLMs and five benchmarks (both discriminative and generative), achieving state-of-the-art performance with low inference overhead and preserved capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Attention-aware, training-free steering can effectively mitigate object hallucination in LVLMs with minimal cost, suggesting a generalizable approach for improving visual grounding.

title: Gradient Flow Structure and Quantitative Dynamics of Multi-Head Self-Attention
summary:
Transformer self-attention can be interpreted as a gradient flow on the unit sphere, in which tokens evolve under softmax interaction potentials and tend to form clusters. While prior work has established clustering behavior for single-head attention, the multi-head setting remains less understood due to geometric interference between heads, which invalidates standard monotonicity arguments.
  In this work, we develop a theoretical framework for multi-head self-attention dynamics and resolve several open questions. We show that, under suitable conditions on the score matrices, a natural multi-head energy functional is non-decreasing along both flat and spherical dynamics. We identify the key obstruction to per-head monotonicity as radial shadow terms, which are projections of each head's output onto token directions, persisting even under orthogonality assumptions. We introduce a sufficient condition ensuring monotonicity and establish robustness to approximate orthogonality.
  In a simplified scalar-head regime with equiangular token configurations, we derive a closed-form expression for the critical inverse temperature governing clustering behavior, and show that heterogeneous heads exhibit super-additive clustering rates. In this regime, we also prove a separation in clustering time between ReLU and softmax attention in the linearized dynamics. Finally, we establish an entropy production identity and show that attention entropy increases monotonically toward equilibrium as clustering progresses.
  Our results provide a unified perspective on the dynamics of multi-head attention and clarify the mechanisms underlying clustering and stability in transformer models.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for multi-head transformer dynamics shows a non-decreasing energy functional under suitable score-matrix conditions, explains why per-head monotonicity fails due to radial shadow terms, and establishes robustness and clustering behavior across heads, including a scalar-head regime with a closed-form inverse temperature and entropy growth toward equilibrium.
AI.motivation: Extend single-head analyses of attention dynamics to multi-head settings, explaining clustering, stability, and the role of head interactions and geometry in transformers.
AI.method: Develop a gradient-flow interpretation of multi-head self-attention on the unit sphere. Define a multi-head energy functional and analyze its monotonicity along flat and spherical dynamics. Identify radial shadow terms as the obstruction to per-head monotonicity. Propose a sufficient condition for monotonicity and assess robustness to approximate orthogonality. In a scalar-head regime with equiangular tokens, derive a closed-form critical inverse temperature, compare clustering rates across heterogeneous heads, and contrast ReLU vs softmax in the linearized dynamics. Prove an entropy production identity showing attention entropy increases toward equilibrium.
AI.result: (1) Multi-head energy is non-decreasing under stated conditions; radial shadow terms explain obstruction to per-head monotonicity. (2) A sufficient monotonicity condition is provided, with robustness to approximate orthogonality. (3) In a simplified scalar-head, equiangular setting, a closed-form critical inverse temperature is derived; heterogeneous heads exhibit super-additive clustering rates; separation of clustering times between ReLU and softmax shown in linearized dynamics. (4) An entropy production identity demonstrates monotonic increase of attention entropy toward equilibrium during clustering.
AI.conclusion: The work unifies the dynamics of multi-head attention, clarifying how clustering and stability arise from the geometry and interactions of heads, and provides practical insights into when and how multi-head attention promotes distinct and stable token clusters.

title: Contact Matrix: Enhancing Dance Motion Synthesis with Precise Interaction Modeling
summary: Generating realistic reactive motions, in which one person reacts to the fixed motions of others, is challenging due to strict interaction constraints and a limited feasible solution space. This paper focuses on a typical scenario: duet dance, where high-quality data is scarce, motion patterns are complex, and the details of human interactions are both intricate and abundant. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel two-stage framework. In the first stage, we introduce a motion VQ-VAE with separate body-part encoders and a joint decoder, enabling specialized codebooks to enhance representation capacity while dynamically modeling dependencies across body parts during decoding, thereby preventing inconsistencies in the generated motions. In the second stage, we propose a contact-aware diffusion model for reactive motion generation that jointly generates motion and a contact matrix between individuals, enabling explicit interaction modeling and providing guidance toward more precise and constrained interaction dynamics during sampling. Experiments show that our method outperforms Duolando with lower $\text{FID}_k$ (8.89 vs. 25.30) and $\text{FID}_{cd}$ (8.01 vs. 9.97), as well as a higher BED (0.4606 vs. 0.2858), indicating improved interaction fidelity and rhythmic synchronization.

AI.tldr: Two-stage framework for reactive duet dance: stage 1 a body-part–aware VQ-VAE; stage 2 a contact-aware diffusion model generating motion and contact matrix, achieving superior metrics vs. Duolando.
AI.motivation: Reactive motion generation under strict interaction constraints faces limited solution space and scarce, complex duet data. The work aims to improve realism and interaction fidelity.
AI.method: Stage 1: VQ-VAE with separate encoders per body part and a joint decoder to diversify codebooks and model cross-part dependencies during decoding. Stage 2: A diffusion model conditioned on both motion and a contact matrix, jointly generating motion and interactions to guide sampling with explicit contact constraints.
AI.result: Outperforms Duolando with lower FID_k (8.89 vs. 25.30) and FID_cd (8.01 vs. 9.97), and higher BED (0.4606 vs. 0.2858), indicating better interaction fidelity and rhythmic synchronization.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage approach effectively captures per-part representations and explicit inter-person interactions, enhancing realism and coordination in duet reactive motion generation.

title: Hardware-Aware Neural Feature Extraction for Resource-Constrained Devices
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication at the IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) Workshops, 2026. \c{opyright}IEEE
summary: Visual SLAM is a core component of spatial computing systems, yet deploying learned local feature extractors on microcontroller-class hardware remains challenging due to memory, bandwidth, and quantization constraints. While modern neural descriptors provide strong robustness, their practical adoption is often hindered by system-level bottlenecks that are not captured by FLOP-based efficiency metrics. In this work, we introduce Gideon, a hardware-aware neural feature extractor explicitly designed for resource-constrained devices. Our approach combines relational knowledge distillation from a SuperPoint teacher with differentiable neural architecture search (DNAS) under strict memory and operator constraints. Unlike conventional design pipelines, we treat quantization stability and dynamic-range compactness as first-class objectives. We show that architectural choices such as replacing Batch Normalization with affine layers significantly improve INT8 robustness, and that descriptor dimensionality directly governs quantization resilience. Deployed on STM32N6, Gideon achieves 9.003 ms inference time (111 fps) while remaining below a 1.5 MB memory footprint. Remarkably, INT8 quantization induces negligible degradation and occasionally matches full-precision performance. These results demonstrate that robust learned feature extraction can be reconciled with embedded hardware constraints through holistic hardware-algorithm co-design.

AI.tldr: Gideon is a hardware-aware neural feature extractor for constrained devices, using relational distillation from a SuperPoint teacher and differentiable neural architecture search under strict memory and operation constraints to enable robust, quantizable descriptors on microcontrollers.
AI.motivation: Deploy robust learned local features for Visual SLAM on MCU-class hardware where memory, bandwidth, and quantization limits prevent standard neural descriptors from being practical; FLOP-based metrics fail to capture system bottlenecks.
AI.method: Relational knowledge distillation from a SuperPoint teacher; differentiable neural architecture search (DNAS) under fixed memory and operator budgets; quantization stability and dynamic-range compactness are explicit optimization objectives; architectural choices include replacing Batch Normalization with affine layers; analysis on descriptor dimensionality and quantization resilience.
AI.result: On STM32N6, Gideon achieves 9.003 ms inference (~111 FPS) with memory footprint < 1.5 MB; INT8 quantization causes negligible degradation and can match full-precision performance in some cases.
AI.conclusion: Hardware-algorithm co-design, combining teacher-guided distillation and memory-aware architecture search, can enable robust learned feature extraction on embedded devices, reconciling accuracy with hardware constraints.

title: Physical Adversarial Clothing Evades Visible-Thermal Detectors via Non-Overlapping RGB-T Pattern
summary: Visible-thermal (RGB-T) object detection is a crucial technology for applications such as autonomous driving, where multimodal fusion enhances performance in challenging conditions like low light. However, the security of RGB-T detectors, particularly in the physical world, has been largely overlooked. This paper proposes a novel approach to RGB-T physical attacks using adversarial clothing with a non-overlapping RGB-T pattern (NORP). To simulate full-view (0$^{\circ}$--360$^{\circ}$) RGB-T attacks, we construct 3D RGB-T models for human and adversarial clothing. NORP is a new adversarial pattern design using distinct visible and thermal materials without overlap, avoiding the light reduction in overlapping RGB-T patterns (ORP). To optimize the NORP on adversarial clothing, we propose a spatial discrete-continuous optimization (SDCO) method. We systematically evaluated our method on RGB-T detectors with different fusion architectures, demonstrating high attack success rates both in the digital and physical worlds. Additionally, we introduce a fusion-stage ensemble method that enhances the transferability of adversarial attacks across unseen RGB-T detectors with different fusion architectures.

AI.tldr: NORP enables robust physical RGB-T (visible-thermal) attacks by using non-overlapping RGB-T patterns on adversarial clothing, optimized with a spatial discrete-continuous method (SDCO) and evaluated across fusion architectures with a fusion-stage ensemble to improve transferability; demonstrates high attack success in digital and physical settings for full-view attacks.
AI.motivation: RGB-T detectors can improve perception, particularly in challenging conditions, but their security in the physical world remains underexplored. The paper addresses this gap by developing an actionable attack framework that leverages non-overlapping patterns and full-view RGB-T modeling to study vulnerabilities and transferability across fusion architectures.
AI.method: 1) Design NORP using distinct visible and thermal materials to avoid overlap and light loss. 2) Build 3D RGB-T models for humans and adversarial clothing to simulate 0°–360° full-view attacks. 3) Optimize NORP on clothing via Spatial Discrete-Continuous Optimization (SDCO). 4) Evaluate against RGB-T detectors with various fusion architectures in digital and physical realms. 5) Propose a fusion-stage ensemble to boost transferability to unseen detectors.
AI.result: Demonstrates high attack success rates across different fusion architectures in both digital simulations and real-world physical setups. The fusion-stage ensemble further improves transferability to unseen detectors.
AI.conclusion: NORP with SDCO constitutes an effective framework for physical RGB-T adversarial attacks, highlighting vulnerabilities in multimodal detectors and underscoring the need for robust defenses. The approach is extendable to other multimodal systems and prompts future work on defense mechanisms and detection of physical adversarial clothing.

title: Probabilistic Classification and Uncertainty Quantification of Sahara Desert Climate Using Feedforward Neural Networks
summary: Climate classification plays a vital role in agricultural planning, hydrological studies, and climate science. One of the most widely used systems for classifying global climate zones is the Köppen-Trewartha (KT) classification. However, the KT classification is fundamentally deterministic, offering discrete labels to spatial locations without accounting for uncertainties in classification. In this paper, we provide a framework for probabilistic modeling of climatic zones. We implement a feedforward artificial neural network (ANN) for classification, allowing for efficient, uncertainty-aware categorization of climatic regions, thereby offering a more nuanced understanding of transitional climate zones compared to traditional deterministic methods. We apply this method to the Sahara Desert region over the 30-year period of 1960 - 1989, using data at more than 400,000 space-time locations from the first 11 years to train our model. We assess the model's short- and long-term classification capabilities to evaluate its stability and accuracy over time. We also compare the probabilistic classification from our model with the traditional KT classification. In addition, we use fluctuation analysis methods to highlight the temporal evolution of climatic zones across the Sahara region and identify areas undergoing significant flux of probabilities of their climate classes, providing insights into broader trends in desertification.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic climate-zone classification via an ANN, replacing KT's deterministic labels; applied to Sahara (1960-1989) with 400k+ spatio-temporal points; evaluates stability, compares with KT, and uses fluctuation analysis to map zones and desertification trends.
AI.motivation: KT classification is deterministic and does not quantify uncertainty, limiting insights into transitional climates. A probabilistic framework can capture uncertainty in zone assignments and reveal flux in climate categories over time.
AI.method: Train a feedforward artificial neural network (ANN) to classify climate zones probabilistically. Use Sahara region data (1960-1989) with >400,000 space-time locations from the first 11 years for training. Evaluate short- and long-term classification stability and accuracy. Compare probabilistic ANN outputs to traditional KT classifications. Apply fluctuation analysis to track temporal evolution of zone probabilities and identify areas with significant probability flux related to desertification trends.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of probabilistic climate-zone classification; provides probabilistic maps of zone membership and their temporal evolution. Shows differences from KT classifications, highlighting transitional zones and areas with significant probability flux, offering a nuanced view of desertification dynamics.
AI.conclusion: A probabilistic ANN framework yields uncertainty-aware climate-zone mappings and temporal dynamics, enabling more nuanced interpretation of climate transitions than deterministic KT classifications. This approach can improve understanding of desertification processes and other regional climate changes.

title: Multi-Level Bidirectional Biomimetic Learning for EEG-Based Visual Decoding
summary: EEG-based visual neural decoding aims to align neural responses with visual stimuli for tasks such as image retrieval. However, limited paired data and a fundamental mismatch between high-fidelity digital images and biological visual perception - distorted by retinotopic mapping and subject-specific neuroanatomy - severely impede cross-modal alignment. To address this, we propose MB2L, a Multi-Level Bidirectional Biomimetic Learning framework that incorporates structured physiological inductive biases into representation learning. Specifically, we propose Adaptive Blur with Visual Priors to mitigate perceptual-structural mismatch by reweighting visual inputs according to retinotopic priors. We further propose Biomimetic Visual Feature Extraction to learn multi-level visual representations consistent with hierarchical cortical processing, enhancing subject-invariant encoding. These modules are jointly optimized via Multi-level Bidirectional Contrastive Learning, which aligns EEG and visual features in a shared semantic space through bidirectional contrastive objectives. Experiments show MB2L achieves 80.5% Top-1 and 97.6% Top-5 accuracy on zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval, significantly outperforming prior methods and demonstrating strong generalization across subjects and experimental settings.

AI.tldr: MB2L introduces a biomimetic, multi-level framework for EEG-to-image retrieval by incorporating retinotopic priors and hierarchical visual features, trained with bidirectional contrastive losses, achieving strong zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and perceptual-structural mismatch between digital images and human visual processing to enable robust EEG–image alignment.
AI.method: Adaptive Blur with Visual Priors to reweight visual inputs; Biomimetic Visual Feature Extraction for multi-level hierarchical representations; Multi-level Bidirectional Contrastive Learning to align EEG and visual features bidirectionally.
AI.result: Zero-shot EEG-to-image retrieval Top-1 80.5%, Top-5 97.6%; outperforms prior methods and generalizes across subjects and settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that incorporating physiological inductive biases and hierarchical representations with bidirectional contrastive learning yields robust cross-modal alignment and strong zero-shot generalization in EEG-to-image retrieval.

title: Leveraging Pretrained Language Models as Energy Functions for Glauber Dynamics Text Diffusion
summary: We present a discrete diffusion-based language model using Glauber dynamics from statistical physics. Our main insight is that instead of trying to train a discrete state space diffusion model using Glauber dynamics with a uniform transition kernel as the forward process, one can set up an ``energy function'' based on pretrained causal/masked language models. When viewed as the stationary distribution, this energy function allows us to significantly improve the quality of the generated text. Incorporating UL2 as the pretrained model into our diffusion pipeline, we outperform prior diffusion based LMs and perform competitively with autoregressive models of comparable model sizes. Furthermore, our models are competitive with or outperform prior diffusion models and GPT-2 style auto-regressive models on zero-shot common sense reasoning tasks as well as planning and search tasks like Sudoku and Zebra puzzles.

AI.tldr: A discrete diffusion model for text uses Glauber dynamics with an energy function derived from pretrained language models (UL2). It yields higher sample quality than prior diffusion LMs, matches autoregressive models of similar size, and excels in zero-shot reasoning and planning puzzles.
AI.motivation: To improve discrete diffusion for text by moving away from a uniform forward process and instead defining a learned energy-based stationary distribution via a strong pretrained LM, enabling higher-quality generation and better reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Construct a discrete diffusion process using Glauber dynamics. Define an energy function based on a pretrained causal/masked LM (UL2) so that the LM defines the stationary distribution. Integrate UL2 into the diffusion pipeline to guide sampling and generation, achieving a text generation model that is diffusion-based rather than autoregressive.
AI.result: The approach yields higher-quality text generation than prior diffusion-based LMs, with competitive performance relative to autoregressive models of similar size. It also achieves competitive or better results on zero-shot common-sense reasoning and planning tasks (e.g., Sudoku, Zebra puzzles).
AI.conclusion: Energy-based discrete diffusion leveraging a pretrained LM as the energy function can significantly improve generation quality and task performance, placing diffusion-based language models on par with autoregressive models for certain sizes and tasks.

title: FaithfulFaces: Pose-Faithful Facial Identity Preservation for Text-to-Video Generation
summary: Identity-preserving text-to-video generation (IPT2V) empowers users to produce diverse and imaginative videos with consistent human facial identity. Despite recent progress, existing methods often suffer from significant identity distortion under large facial pose variations or facial occlusions. In this paper, we propose \textit{FaithfulFaces}, a pose-faithful facial identity preservation learning framework to improve IPT2V in complex dynamic scenes. The key of FaithfulFaces is a pose-shared identity aligner that refines and aligns facial poses across distinct views via a pose-shared dictionary and a pose variation-identity invariance constraint. By mapping single-view inputs into a global facial pose representation with explicit Euler angle embeddings, FaithfulFaces provides a pose-faithful facial prior that guides generative foundations toward robust identity-preserving generation. In particular, we develop a specialized pipeline to curate a high-quality video dataset featuring substantial facial pose diversity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FaithfulFaces achieves state-of-the-art performance, maintaining superior identity consistency and structural clarity even as pose changes and occlusions occur.

AI.tldr: FaithfulFaces introduces a pose-faithful identity-preservation framework for text-to-video synthesis that aligns identities across varying facial poses using a pose-shared aligner and Euler-angle embeddings, achieving state-of-the-art identity consistency in challenging poses and occlusions.
AI.motivation: Current IPT2V methods struggle with identity distortion under large pose variations and occlusions, hindering consistent facial identity in dynamic scenes.
AI.method: Develop a pose-shared identity aligner with a pose-shared dictionary and a pose variation-identity invariance constraint. Map single-view inputs into a global facial pose representation using explicit Euler-angle embeddings to provide a pose-faithful prior. Create a pipeline to curate a high-quality, pose-diverse video dataset to train and validate the model, guiding generative foundations toward robust identity preservation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with superior identity consistency and structural clarity maintained across pose changes and occlusions.
AI.conclusion: FaithfulFaces delivers robust, pose-faithful identity preservation for IPT2V in complex scenes, showing that pose-aware priors and cross-view alignment significantly enhance identity fidelity during video generation.

title: LLMs Uncertainty Quantification via Adaptive Conformal Semantic Entropy
comment: Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of IJCAI 2026, the 35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 12 Pages
summary: LLMs' overconfidence, particularly when hallucinating, poses a significant challenge for the deployment of the models in safety-critical settings and makes a reliable estimation of uncertainty necessary. Existing approaches for uncertainty quantification typically prioritize lexical or probabilistic measures; however, these techniques often ignore the semantic variance of different responses with similar meaning. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Conformal Semantic Entropy (ACSE), a method for estimating prompt-level uncertainty by adaptively measuring semantic dispersion in LLMs outputs. Our uncertainty scoring function is based on clustering semantic entropy of multiple diverse responses to the same prompt. The function adaptively adjusts the uncertainty score based on semantic features of each cluster. To ensure statistical reliability of our score, we use conformal calibration to apply a decision rule to accept/abstain the prompts, providing a finite-sample, distribution-free guarantee such that the error rate among the accepted responses remains bounded by a user-specified tolerance. Our extensive experimental evaluations using different LLMs and datasets, demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification baselines using discriminative performance, conformal guarantees, and probabilistic calibration indicators. As a highlight, for TriviaQA dataset, AUROC of our approach is 0.88 compared to 0.65 produced by the token entropy approach.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Conformal Semantic Entropy (ACSE) estimates prompt-level uncertainty by clustering semantic entropy across diverse LLM responses; it adaptively weights cluster features and uses conformal calibration to provide finite-sample uncertainty guarantees, achieving better discrimination and reliability than baselines (e.g., TriviaQA AUROC 0.88 vs 0.65).
AI.motivation: LLMs often exhibit overconfidence and hallucinations, especially in safety-critical settings. Traditional uncertainty measures focus on lexical or probabilistic cues and neglect semantic variance among responses with similar meanings. There is a need for a semantic-aware, reliable uncertainty quantification method with statistical guarantees.
AI.method: Generate multiple diverse responses to the same prompt, estimate semantic entropy for each response, cluster these responses in semantic space, and compute an adaptive uncertainty score from cluster-level semantic features.
