Apply conformal calibration to obtain a decision rule (accept/abstain) with finite-sample, distribution-free guarantees on the error rate among accepted responses.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across various LLMs and datasets shows ACSE outperforms state-of-the-art uncertainty baselines in discriminative performance, calibration indicators, and conformal guarantees. On TriviaQA, ACSE yields AUROC 0.88 compared to 0.65 for token-entropy baselines.
AI.conclusion: ACSE provides a semantic-aware, statistically principled uncertainty quantification framework that accounts for semantic dispersion in model outputs and delivers finite-sample guarantees via conformal calibration, improving reliable decision-making under uncertainty.

title: Not Every Subject Should Stay: Machine Unlearning for Noisy Engagement Recognition
summary: Engagement recognition datasets are typically subject-indexed and often contain noisy, subjective supervision, making post-hoc dataset revision a practical problem. Existing noisy-label and data-cleaning methods largely operate at the sample level before or during training, but do not directly address a different question: once a model has already been trained, can the influence of an entire problematic subject be removed without full retraining? We study this setting through subject-level machine unlearning as a post-hoc sanitization mechanism for engagement recognition. Starting from a baseline trained on all subjects, we rank candidate harmful subjects using a model-dependent proxy, apply a lightweight approximate unlearning update, and compare the result against an oracle model retrained from scratch on the retained subjects only. We instantiate this protocol on DAiSEE and EngageNet using Tensor-Convolution and Convolution-Transformer Network (TCCT-Net) as a fixed platform and evaluate three matched model states under the same removal scenario: baseline, unlearned, and oracle. In representative K=3 forget-set settings, the unlearned model recovers 89.3% and 92.5% of the oracle gain on EngageNet and DAiSEE, respectively, at roughly one quarter of retraining cost. Across the tested small-audit regimes, effectiveness is strongest at an intermediate forget-set size, indicating that approximate subject-level unlearning is a useful low-cost correction mechanism, but one whose benefit depends on subject selection quality and removal regime.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc subject-level unlearning for engagement recognition: rank harmful subjects with a model-dependent proxy, apply a lightweight unlearning update, and compare to an oracle retrained on retained subjects. On DAiSEE and EngageNet with TCCT-Net, the unlearned model recovers about 89–93% of the oracle gain at roughly 25% of retraining cost, with effectiveness peaking at intermediate forget-set sizes.
AI.motivation: Engagement recognition datasets are subject-indexed and noisy; existing methods clean at the sample level and during training, but there is a need to remove the influence of entire problematic subjects after training (post-hoc) without full retraining.
AI.method: Baseline model trained on all subjects. Rank candidate harmful subjects using a model-dependent proxy. Apply a lightweight approximate unlearning update for the selected subjects. Compare against an oracle retrained from scratch on the retained subjects. Experiments on DAiSEE and EngageNet using TCCT-Net as fixed platform, evaluating three model states: baseline, unlearned, and oracle. Forget-set size K=3.
AI.result: In K=3 forget-set settings, the unlearned model recovers 89.3% (EngageNet) and 92.5% (DAiSEE) of the oracle gain, at roughly one-quarter of retraining cost. Across tested small-audit regimes, effectiveness is strongest at an intermediate forget-set size, indicating the method provides a low-cost correction whose benefit hinges on subject selection quality and removal regime.
AI.conclusion: Approximate subject-level unlearning is a useful low-cost post-hoc sanitization mechanism for engagement recognition, but its effectiveness depends on the quality of subject selection and the removal regime; it offers practical value when full retraining is expensive or impractical.

title: Memory as a Markov Matrix: Sample Efficient Knowledge Expansion via Token-to-Dictionary Mapping
summary: Continual incorporation of new knowledge is essential for the long-term evolution of large language models (LLMs). Existing approaches typically rely on parameter-update algorithms to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, yet they suffer from fundamental limitations: 1) forgetting is unavoidable as the amount of newly injected knowledge grows; and 2) model updates are often irreversible. As modern LLMs become increasingly expressive, it is natural to question whether large-scale weight updates are necessary for acquiring a small amount of new knowledge. In this work, we propose a principled framework that models autoregressive language generation as a Markov process over tokens, where model memory is represented by a Markov transition matrix. Under this formulation, incorporating new knowledge/tokens corresponds to extending the state space, and preserving existing transitions guarantees retention of previously learned knowledge. We then prove a sample complexity bound for incorporating new tokens via a token-to-dictionary mapping strategy. In particular, for learning the transition behavior of each new token, the required number of samples scales linearly with the number of existing tokens it is mapped to. To realize this mapping, we propose an embedding-tuning algorithm that requires minimal parameter updates and induces zero forgetting. Experimental results further demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and validate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Markov-based continual learning for LLMs: extend state space with new tokens via token-to-dictionary mappings; minimal parameter updates to achieve zero forgetting; linear sample complexity per mapped token; embedding-tuning algorithm supported by experiments.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in continual knowledge integration for large language models, questioning whether large-scale weight updates are necessary for small amounts of new knowledge; develop a principled, theoretically-grounded approach with minimal updates.
AI.method: Model autoregressive generation as a Markov process over tokens. Represent model memory with a Markov transition matrix. New knowledge is incorporated by extending the token state space; preserve existing transitions to retain prior knowledge. Propose a token-to-dictionary mapping strategy and an embedding-tuning algorithm requiring minimal parameter updates to realize this mapping.
AI.result: Proved a sample complexity bound for incorporating new tokens via token-to-dictionary mapping: learning the transition behavior of each new token requires samples scaling linearly with the number of existing tokens it maps to. Empirically validated the method, showing effectiveness and alignment with theory.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-tuning achieves zero forgetting with minimal parameter updates, offering a principled framework and theoretical guarantees for continual knowledge integration in LLMs.

title: Anny-Fit: All-Age Human Mesh Recovery
comment: CVPR 2026 Findings Track - Code available at https://github.com/naver/anny-fit
summary: Recovering 3D human pose and shape from a single image remains a cornerstone of human-centric vision, yet most methods assume adult subjects and optimize each person independently. These assumptions fail in real-world, all-age scenes, where body proportions and depth must be resolved jointly. We introduce Anny-Fit, a multi-person, camera-space optimization framework for all-age 3D human mesh recovery (HMR). Unlike existing per-person fitting methods, Anny-Fit jointly optimizes all individuals directly in the camera coordinate system, enforcing global spatial consistency. At the core of our approach is the use of multiple forms of expert knowledge -- including metric depth maps, instance segmentation, 2D keypoints, and, VLM-derived semantic attributes such as age and gender -- each obtained from dedicated off-the-shelf networks. These complementary signals jointly guide the optimization, constraining the depth-scale ambiguity characteristic of all-age scenes. Across diverse datasets, Anny-Fit consistently improves 2D reprojection accuracy (+13 to 16), relative depth ordering (+6 to 7), 3D estimation error (-9 to -29) and shape estimation (+25 to +82), producing more coherent scenes. Finally, we show that VLM-based semantic knowledge can be distilled into an HMR model via the pseudo-ground-truth annotations produced by Anny-Fit on training data, enabling it to learn semantically meaningful shape parameters while improving HMR performance. Our approach bridges adult-only and all-age modeling by enabling zero-shot adaptation of adult-trained HMR pipelines to the full age spectrum without retraining. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/naver/anny-fit.

AI.tldr: Anny-Fit is a multi-person, camera-space optimization framework for all-age 3D human mesh recovery (HMR) that jointly optimizes multiple people using depth maps, segmentation, 2D keypoints, and VLM-derived attributes (age/gender) to resolve depth-scale ambiguity. It enables zero-shot adaptation from adult-trained HMR to all ages, with state-of-the-art improvements and public code.
AI.motivation: Real-world scenes with people of all ages expose depth-scale ambiguity and inter-person spatial constraints that single-person, adult-centric methods cannot resolve. Joint camera-space optimization leveraging multiple auxiliary signals aims to produce globally consistent 3D reconstructions.
AI.method: Joint optimization of all individuals directly in camera coordinates, enforcing global spatial consistency. Leveraging multiple signals from off-the-shelf networks: metric depth maps, instance segmentation, 2D keypoints, and VLM-derived semantic attributes (age/gender). These signals guide depth scaling and pose/shape estimation. Additionally, distilled pseudo-ground-truth annotations derived from Anny-Fit enable training an HMR model to learn semantically meaningful shape parameters and facilitate zero-shot adaptation to all ages.
AI.result: Across diverse datasets, improvements observed: 2D reprojection accuracy +13 to +16, relative depth ordering +6 to +7, 3D estimation error -9 to -29, and shape estimation +25 to +82, yielding more coherent all-age scenes. The approach supports zero-shot adaptation of adult-trained HMR to full age spectrum and includes code release.
AI.conclusion: Anny-Fit bridges adult-only and all-age modeling by enabling zero-shot adaptation without retraining, fostering coherent multi-person reconstructions in all-age scenes.

title: LUCAS-MEGA: A Large-Scale Multimodal Dataset for Representation Learning in Soil-Environment Systems
summary: Understanding soil is fundamental to agriculture, carbon cycling, and environmental sustainability, yet progress is limited by fragmented and heterogeneous datasets that constrain modeling to small-scale predictive settings rather than high-dimensional representation learning. We introduce LUCAS-MEGA, a large-scale multimodal dataset constructed through systematic data fusion of European soil-environment observations, with the LUCAS survey as its backbone. The fused dataset comprises over 70,000 samples and more than 1,000 features spanning physical, chemical, environmental, biological, and visual attributes, aggregated from 68 source datasets. To enable integration at scale, we develop SoilFuser, a multi-agent, human-in-the-loop data fusion pipeline that standardizes heterogeneous data formats and measurement protocols, resolves inconsistencies and invalid entries (e.g., unit inconsistencies, codebook mismatches, and erroneous values), incorporates natural language annotations, and harmonizes multimodal attributes and metadata into a unified, machine learning-ready feature space. The resulting dataset captures key characteristics of real-world soil observations, including multimodality, uneven feature coverage, and heterogeneous uncertainty. To demonstrate the usability of LUCAS-MEGA for data-driven modeling, we pretrain a multimodal tabular transformer (SoilFormer) using a self-supervised objective based on feature masking, achieving stable training, strong predictive performance, and representations that support uncertainty-aware prediction. We further show that the learned representations recover relationships consistent with established soil processes. LUCAS-MEGA is released with open access and is accompanied by composable, agent-friendly APIs that support structured querying and data-driven workflows.

AI.tldr: LUCAS-MEGA is a large-scale multimodal soil dataset assembled via a data-fusion pipeline (SoilFuser) from 68 European sources, enabling scalable multimodal learning; pretrained SoilFormer shows strong predictive and uncertainty-aware representations, with open APIs.
AI.motivation: Fragmented, heterogeneous soil data hinders large-scale representation learning and robust modeling; a unified, curated, multimodal dataset is needed to improve soil on-omics and predictive capabilities.
AI.method: SoilFuser standardizes formats, harmonizes units/codebooks, resolves errors, incorporates natural language annotations, and fuses 68 datasets into 70k+ samples and 1k+ features; SoilFormer pretrained with self-supervised feature masking on this data; open APIs.
AI.result: 70k samples, 1k+ features; strong predictive performance; stable training; representations recover established soil-process relationships; open access and composable APIs.
AI.conclusion: LUCAS-MEGA enables scalable multimodal soil modeling and uncertainty-aware learning, providing a comprehensive resource and a framework for data-driven soil science.

title: ULF-Loc: Unbiased Landmark Feature for Robust Visual Localization with 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Visual localization is a core technology for augmented reality and autonomous navigation. Recent methods combine the efficient rendering of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with feature-based localization. These methods rely on direct matching between 2D query features and the 3D Gaussian feature field, but this often results in mismatches due to an inherent bias in the learned Gaussian feature. We theoretically analyze the feature learning process in 3DGS, revealing that the widely adopted $α$-blending optimization inherently introduces bias into 3D point features. This bias stems from the entanglement between individual Gaussians and their neighboring Gaussians, making the learned features unsuitable for precise matching tasks. Motivated by these findings, we propose ULF-Loc, an unbiased landmark feature framework that replaces biased feature optimization with geometry-weighted feature fusion. We further introduce keypoint-consensus landmark sampling to select reliable Gaussians and local geometric consistency verification to reject mismatches caused by rendering artifacts. On the Cambridge Landmarks dataset, ULF-Loc reduces the mean median translation error by 17\% compared to the state-of-the-art, while achieving superior efficiency with only 1/10 the training time and 1/6 the GPU memory of STDLoc.

AI.tldr: ULF-Loc introduces an unbiased landmark feature framework for visual localization using geometry-weighted feature fusion, outperforming 3D Gaussian Splatting-based methods in accuracy and efficiency on Cambridge Landmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper theoretically analyzes 3DGS feature learning and shows that α-blending biases entangle neighboring Gaussians, producing point features ill-suited for 2D–3D matching; this motivates creating an unbiased feature representation.
AI.method: Replace biased α-blending optimization with geometry-weighted feature fusion; add keypoint-consensus landmark sampling to select reliable Gaussians; incorporate local geometric consistency verification to reject mismatches caused by rendering artifacts.
AI.result: On Cambridge Landmarks, ULF-Loc reduces mean median translation error by 17% compared with the state-of-the-art, while achieving ~10× faster training and ~6× lower GPU memory usage than STDLoc.
AI.conclusion: Unbiased landmark feature design improves 2D–3D matching fidelity and efficiency in Gaussian-based localization; the approach mitigates α-blending bias and yields practical performance gains with potential applicability beyond the specific method.

title: DeFed-GMM-DaDiL: A Decentralized Federated Framework for Domain Adaptation
summary: Decentralized multi-source domain adaptation seeks to transfer knowledge from multiple heterogeneous and related source domains to an unlabeled target domain in a decentralized setting. We address this challenge through a fully decentralized federated approach, DeFed-GMM-DaDiL, an extension of the GMM-Dataset Dictionary Learning (DaDiL) framework. Each client models its dataset as a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), and the federation jointly approximates them via labeled Wasserstein barycenters of shared, learnable GMM atoms. This design enables adaptation without a central server while preserving clients' privacy. We empirically study the stability of the learned representations in scenarios where the target domain has missing classes. Empirical results demonstrate that DeFed-GMM-DaDiL maintains stable and consistent shared representations across clients, effectively reconstructs missing classes, and achieves competitive performance on multi-source domain adaptation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A fully decentralized federated approach (DeFed-GMM-DaDiL) extends DaDiL by modeling each client with a GMM and using labeled Wasserstein barycenters of learnable GMM atoms for joint sharing, enabling privacy-preserving multi-source domain adaptation without a central server.
AI.motivation: Address multi-source domain adaptation in a decentralized setting while preserving client privacy and removing reliance on a central aggregator. Leverage a common, learnable GMM-based dictionary and optimal transport to align representations across heterogeneous sources.
AI.method: Each client fits a Gaussian Mixture Model to its data. The federation jointly approximates them via labeled Wasserstein barycenters of shared, learnable GMM atoms, yielding a decentralized dictionary learning mechanism (DaDiL extension) with no central server.
AI.result: The approach yields stable and consistent shared representations across clients, can reconstruct missing classes in the target domain, and achieves competitive performance on multi-source domain adaptation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DeFed-GMM-DaDiL demonstrates that a fully decentralized, privacy-preserving federation using Wasserstein-informed GMM dictionaries can support robust multi-source domain adaptation and representation alignment across heterogeneous clients.

title: Morphology-Guided Cross-Task Coupling for Joint Building Height and Footprint Estimation
summary: Building height (BH) and building footprint (BF) jointly describe the vertical and horizontal extent of the built environment and are required inputs for urban climate, disaster-risk, and population-mapping models. The two parameters are coupled through floor-area-ratio (FAR) constraints, yet remote-sensing approaches typically treat them as independent regression targets. We argue that explicitly encoding this cross-task coupling is more impactful than further refining individual encoders, and propose MorphoFormer, a joint BH/BF estimation framework built around two complementary mechanisms: (i) a BF-Guided Task Decoder (BGTD) that gates the height branch via cross-attention on a footprint-derived morphology context, and (ii) a Morphology Consistency Loss (MCL) that supervises a height-from-footprint surrogate against the ground-truth BH, indirectly forcing the BF feature to encode height-correlated structure. The encoder is a single-stage Swin backbone fed by Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 multispectral, and DEM inputs, trained and evaluated on a geo-blocked split of 54 cities. Against a Swin-MTL baseline at identical receptive field, MorphoFormer reduces BH test RMSE from 3.39 to 3.15 m (R^2 improves 0.62 -> 0.67) with BF R^2 stable at 0.80. Controlled ablations at identical capacity attribute most of this 0.24 m improvement to the two proposed mechanisms: removing BGTD raises BH RMSE by 0.11 m and removing MCL raises it by 0.11 m, with the residual approximately 0.02 m falling within the noise floor of encoder-side variations. Because both mechanisms act on cross-task representations rather than pixels, the design carries no intrinsic dependence on input resolution.

AI.tldr: MorphoFormer jointly estimates building height (BH) and footprint (BF) by encoding cross-task coupling through a BF-Guided Task Decoder (BGTD) and a Morphology Consistency Loss (MCL), yielding consistent BH improvements with a resolution-agnostic design.
AI.motivation: BH and BF are vertically and horizontally coupled via floor-area-ratio constraints; treating them as independent misses mutual information. Explicit cross-task coupling can improve accuracy beyond single-task encoder refinements.
AI.method: A single-stage Swin backbone processes Sentinel-1 SAR, Sentinel-2 multispectral, and DEM inputs. Two mechanisms: (1) BGTD gates the height branch via cross-attention on a footprint-derived morphology context; (2) MCL supervises a height-from-footprint surrogate against ground-truth BH, forcing BF features to encode height-correlated structure. Evaluation on a geo-blocked split of 54 cities.
AI.result: MorphoFormer reduces BH RMSE from 3.39 m to 3.15 m and increases BH R^2 from 0.62 to 0.67, with BF R^2 stable at 0.80, vs Swin-MTL baseline at identical receptive field. Ablations: removing BGTD raises BH RMSE by 0.11 m; removing MCL raises it by 0.11 m; residual ~0.02 m within encoder noise. The gains arise from cross-task representations rather than pixel-level changes, and the method remains resolution-agnostic.
AI.conclusion: Explicit cross-task coupling via BGTD and MCL yields reliable improvements in joint BH/BF estimation, with performance robust to input resolution and showing that cross-task representations drive most gains rather than pixel-level refinements.

title: On the Architectural Complexity of Neural Networks
summary: We introduce a unified theoretical framework for the rigorous analysis and systematic construction of deep neural networks (DNNs). This framework addresses a gap in existing theory by explicitly modeling the structure of tensor operations -- lower level information that is often abstracted. Our framework enables two novel objectives: (1) analysis of the evolution of architectural complexity over deep learning history, and (2) automatic construction of novel architectures based on new types of tensor operations. Our study of DNNs introduced over the past 40 years reveals a connection between groundbreaking architectures and increases in different types of architectural complexity. Moreover, we identify several large classes of higher complexity architectures that have not yet been explored. We then collect a dataset of 3,000+ higher complexity architectures, which we publicly release at: https://github.com/combinatoriallabs/ArchitecturalComplexity.

AI.tldr: A unified framework to analyze and construct DNNs by explicitly modeling tensor operations, enabling historical analysis of architectural complexity and automated design of novel architectures, with a dataset of 3k+ high-complexity architectures released.
AI.motivation: Fill a gap in existing theory by keeping low-level tensor structure in view to link architectural evolution with complexity and to enable automatic synthesis of new architectures.
AI.method: Introduce a formal framework that encodes tensor-level operations, analyze DNNs from the past 40 years to map complexity growth, categorize higher-complexity architecture classes, and compile a dataset of 3,000+ such architectures for public release; facilitate automatic construction of new architectures via new tensor operation types.
AI.result: The framework yields two main outcomes: (1) analysis of how architectural complexity evolved over deep learning history; (2) automated construction of novel architectures based on new tensor-operation types; publicly releases a dataset of 3,000+ higher-complexity architectures.
AI.conclusion: The work advances a theory–practice bridge in DNN architecture by formalizing tensor-structure-driven complexity and providing reusable resources to explore and design higher-complexity networks.

title: Budgeted LoRA: Distillation as Structured Compute Allocation for Efficient Inference
comment: Preprint. 9 pages main text, 18 pages total, 2 figures, 9 tables
summary: We study distillation for large language models under explicit compute constraints, with the goal of producing student models that are not only cheaper to train, but structurally efficient at inference time. While prior approaches to parameter-efficient distillation, such as LoRA, reduce adaptation cost, they leave the dense backbone unchanged and therefore fail to deliver meaningful inference savings. We propose Budgeted LoRA, a distillation framework that treats model compression as a structured compute allocation problem. Instead of using a fixed student architecture, we introduce a global compute budget that sets the final target fraction of dense computation retained. Under this constraint, the model redistributes capacity across dense and low-rank pathways via (i) module-level dense retention coefficients, (ii) adaptive low-rank allocation, and (iii) post-training compression that selectively removes, approximates, or preserves dense components. This formulation yields a family of students controlled by a single budget dial. Empirically, Budgeted LoRA matches standard LoRA perplexity at a moderate budget with a 1.74x compressed-module speedup; at an aggressive budget it achieves a 4.05x speedup with moderate perplexity degradation, and it preserves higher accuracy on function-style in-context learning probes. These results suggest that, under compute-constrained distillation, retaining behavior is less about matching perplexity or removing more parameters than it is about controlling how dense computation is transferred to low-rank pathways.

AI.tldr: Budgeted LoRA reframes distillation as a structured compute allocation problem, jointly optimizing the retention of dense computation and low-rank adaptation under a fixed global compute budget. This yields a tunable, efficient family of student models that balance inference speed and task accuracy.
AI.motivation: To reduce both training cost and inference latency in parameter-efficient distillation by not fixing the dense backbone and instead redistributing computation between dense and low-rank components under a global budget.
AI.method: Introduce Budgeted LoRA: a distillation framework with a global compute budget that sets the target fraction of dense computation. The model learns (i) module-level dense retention coefficients, (ii) adaptive low-rank allocation, and (iii) post-training compression that removes, approximates, or preserves dense components, yielding a budget-controlled family of students.
AI.result: Empirically, Budgeted LoRA matches standard LoRA perplexity at a moderate budget with 1.74x compressed-module speedup; at an aggressive budget it achieves 4.05x speedup with moderate perplexity degradation, and preserves higher accuracy on function-style in-context learning probes.
AI.conclusion: Under compute-constrained distillation, preserving behavior depends more on how dense computation is transferred to low-rank pathways than on simply removing parameters or matching perplexity; a single budget dial enables a continuum of trade-offs between speed and accuracy.

title: VC-FeS: Viewpoint-Conditioned Feature Selection for Vehicle Re-identification in Thermal Vision
summary: Identification of less-articulated objects using single-channel images, such as thermal images, is important in many applications, such as surveillance. However, in this domain, existing methods show poor performance due to high similarity among objects of the same category in the absence of color information (overlooking shape information) and de-emphasized texture information. Furthermore, variability in viewpoint adds more complexity as the features vary from side to side. We address these issues by constructing viewpoint-conditioned feature vectors and area-specific feature comparisons in separate feature spaces. These interventions enable leveraging the advancements of existing RGB-pre-trained ViT feature extractors while effectively adapting them to address the challenges specific to the thermal domain. We test our system with RGBNT100 (IR) vehicle dataset and a thermal maritime dataset acquired by us. Our results surpass the state-of-the-art methods by 19.7% and 12.8% for the above datasets in mAP scores, respectively. We also plan to make our thermal dataset available, the first of its kind for maritime vessel identification.

AI.tldr: Proposes viewpoint-conditioned feature vectors and area-specific feature comparisons to adapt RGB-pretrained ViT for thermal imagery, addressing lack of color/texture cues and viewpoint variability; tested on IR vehicle and thermal maritime datasets with notable mAP gains.
AI.motivation: Thermal images lack color cues, exhibit high intra-class similarity, and suffer from viewpoint-induced feature variation. Need to leverage RGB-pretrained ViT while adapting to thermal domain for improved recognition of less-articulated objects.
AI.method: Develop viewpoint-conditioned feature vectors and area-specific feature comparisons conducted in separate feature spaces, enabling effective adaptation of RGB-pretrained ViT features to thermal data. Evaluate on RGBNT100 (IR) vehicle dataset and a self-collected thermal maritime dataset.
AI.result: Achieves 19.7% and 12.8% improvements in mean average precision (mAP) over state-of-the-art on the two datasets.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that conditioning features on viewpoint and performing area-specific comparisons in dedicated spaces enhances thermal-domain object recognition; plans to release the thermal maritime dataset to support research in this area.

title: Learning-based Statistical Refinement for Denoising
summary: This work proposes a learning-based statistical refinement method for improving the denoising results of a given denoiser without knowing the precise noise distribution or accessing clean images or calibration data. While there are many existing successful denoising approaches for handling different kinds of noise, they typically require accurate modelling of the images and the noise (implicitly or explicitly), and hence the denoising results can be suboptimal due to different practical factors such as imperfect models, unreliable noise assumptions, or low quality data. In particular, when clean image samples are not available and there is a lack of knowledge of the underlying noise distribution, which is the case in various practical situations, the results may not well align with the noise statistics. The unawareness of the useful statistical information leads to suboptimal results. This work aims to make the best use of the statistical information to improve the consistency between the given denoising results and the noise statistics, under the assumption that the noise is conditionally pixel-wise independent given the clean signal. A method, based on a Bayesian formulation of an auxiliary signal in the noisy data, is proposed for evaluating the consistency of the denoising results, without precise information on noise distribution. By leveraging the statistical information from noisy data, the method enhances the statistical noise consistency and improves denoising quality.

AI.tldr: A learning-based statistical refinement framework to improve denoising when the noise distribution and clean data are unknown, using a Bayesian auxiliary signal to enforce consistency with noise statistics under conditional pixel-wise independence, and leveraging noisy data statistics to enhance denoising quality.
AI.motivation: Denoisers traditionally rely on accurate noise models and access to clean references. In many real-world situations, clean images and precise noise distributions are unavailable, causing suboptimal denoising. The approach aims to exploit available statistical information from noisy data to better align denoising outputs with the underlying noise statistics.
AI.method: Introduce a Bayesian formulation of an auxiliary signal embedded in the noisy data to evaluate and enforce the consistency of denoising results with unknown noise statistics. Assume conditional pixel-wise independence of the noise given the clean signal. Develop a learning-based statistical refinement module that uses the noisy data statistics to refine an existing denoiser toward better statistical consistency and improved denoising quality without requiring explicit noise models or clean references.
AI.result: The framework is proposed to enhance statistical noise consistency and improve denoising quality without clean data or precise noise distributions, by leveraging information contained in noisy observations.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a robust refinement mechanism that utilizes noisy-data statistics to adapt denoising to unknown noise distributions, broadening applicability when calibration data or clean samples are unavailable.

title: Efficiently Aligning Language Models with Online Natural Language Feedback
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has been used to elicit impressive performance from language models in many domains. But, broadly beneficial deployments of AI may require us to train models with strong capabilities in "fuzzy", hard-to-supervise domains. In this paper, we develop methods to align language models in fuzzy domains where human experts are still able to provide high-quality supervision signal, but only for a small number of model outputs, using online natural language feedback. Specifically, we train models by iteratively optimizing against proxy reward signals, stopping at the point of over-optimization, collecting fresh expert supervision, and updating the proxy reward. We construct proxy reward models from language models using in-context learning (ICL) and fine-tuning. We test our methods by eliciting creative writing and alignment research capabilities in Qwen3-8B and Haiku 4.5 respectively. For Qwen3-8B, ICL methods recover up to 35% of performance with 50x fewer expert samples, while fine-tuning methods recover 80% with up to 20x fewer samples and 100% with 3x fewer samples. For Haiku 4.5, ICL methods recover up to 35% of performance with 30x fewer samples, and fine-tuning methods recover 100% with 10x fewer samples. Our results suggest that online natural language feedback can substantially improve the data efficiency of expert supervision.

AI.tldr: Online NL feedback enables data-efficient alignment of LMs in fuzzy domains by iteratively optimizing against proxy rewards derived from LMs (ICL or fine-tuning) and refreshing supervision to avoid overfit.
AI.motivation: Extend RLHF-style alignment to
AI.method: Iterative proxy-reward optimization with optional stopping at over-optimization, online expert supervision refresh, and update of the proxy reward model. Proxy rewards are constructed from language models using in-context learning (ICL) or fine-tuning.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B and Haiku 4.5, ICL recovers up to 35% of performance with 50x fewer expert samples; fine-tuning recovers 80% (Qwen) with 20x fewer samples and 100% with 3x fewer. For Haiku 4.5, ICL recovers up to 35% with 30x fewer samples; fine-tuning recovers 100% with 10x fewer samples.
AI.conclusion: Online NL feedback substantially improves data efficiency of expert supervision for aligning LMs in fuzzy domains; proxy rewards built from LMs can be effective stand-ins for expensive human feedback.

title: Hybrid Congestion Classification Framework Using Flow-Guided Attention and Empirical Mode Decomposition
summary: Accurate traffic congestion classification requires models that jointly capture roadway scene context and non-stationary traffic motion, yet most prior work treats these requirements in isolation. Vision-based methods often depend on appearance cues with standard temporal pooling, which can bias predictions toward static infrastructure, whereas signal-based approaches characterize temporal dynamics but lack the spatial context needed for scene-level localization. These complementary limitations motivate a unified framework that links motion evidence to spatial feature selection while preserving data-adaptive temporal characterization. This study therefore proposes FLO-EMD, a hybrid approach that couples motion-guided attention with empirical, data-driven temporal decomposition. Dense optical flow guides channel and spatial attention so that RGB features are refined toward motion-relevant regions. In parallel, aggregated flow statistics form compact motion traces that are decomposed using Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD) to extract intrinsic temporal components. The resulting EMD embedding is fused with learned spatiotemporal representations to classify light, medium, and heavy congestion. Experiments on 1,050 five-second clips from four surveillance networks show that FLO-EMD achieves 97.5% overall test accuracy (weighted F1 = 0.9742), outperforming established baselines and remaining robust across diverse environmental conditions; ablation and sensitivity analyses further quantify the contributions of EMD, the number of intrinsic mode functions, and the selected motion descriptors.

AI.tldr: A unified FLO-EMD framework combines motion-guided attention with Empirical Mode Decomposition to fuse motion cues and spatial features for traffic congestion classification, achieving state-of-the-art results on surveillance data.
AI.motivation: Prior approaches typically address spatial-context and temporal dynamics separately. A joint model that leverages motion evidence for feature selection while employing data-driven temporal decomposition can improve scene-level congestion classification under varied conditions.
AI.method: FLO-EMD uses dense optical flow to guide channel/spatial attention on RGB features; simultaneously, flow statistics are aggregated and decomposed via Empirical Mode Decomposition to obtain intrinsic temporal components, which are fused with learned spatiotemporal representations to classify congestion levels (light, medium, heavy).
AI.result: On 1,050 five-second clips from four surveillance networks, FLO-EMD achieves 97.5% overall test accuracy (weighted F1 = 0.9742), outperforming baselines. Ablation and sensitivity analyses quantify the contributions of EMD, the number of intrinsic mode functions, and motion descriptors, with robust performance across conditions.
AI.conclusion: A unified motion-aware, data-driven temporal decomposition framework improves traffic congestion classification by linking motion evidence to spatial feature selection and incorporating intrinsic temporal components, demonstrating strong generalization across diverse surveillance data.

title: Mitigating Label Shift in Tabular In-Context Learning via Test-Time Posterior Adjustment
summary: TabPFN has recently gained attention as a foundation model for tabular datasets, achieving strong performance by leveraging in-context learning on synthetic data. However, we find that TabPFN is vulnerable to label shift, often overfitting to the majority class in the training dataset. To address this limitation, we propose DistPFN, the first test-time posterior adjustment method designed for tabular foundation models. DistPFN rescales predicted class probabilities by downweighting the influence of the training prior (i.e., the class distribution of the context) and emphasizing the contribution of the model's predicted posterior, without architectural modification or additional training. We further introduce DistPFN-T, which incorporates temperature scaling to adaptively control the adjustment strength based on the discrepancy between prior and posterior. We evaluate our methods on over 250 OpenML datasets, demonstrating substantial improvements for various TabPFN-based models in classification tasks under label shift, while maintaining strong performance in standard settings without label shift. Code is available at this repository: https://github.com/seunghan96/DistPFN.

AI.tldr: DistPFN provides a training-free test-time posterior adjustment for TabPFN to mitigate label shift by downweighting the training prior and boosting the model posterior; DistPFN-T adds temperature scaling for adaptive strength. Evaluated on 250 OpenML datasets, it improves classification under label shift while preserving standard performance; code available online.
AI.motivation: TabPFN, a tabular foundation model, is vulnerable to label shift and tends to overfit to the majority class due to reliance on training priors. A training-free, test-time solution is desirable to correct for prior-posterior mismatch without retraining.
AI.method: DistPFN rescales class probabilities by de-emphasizing the training prior (class distribution of the context) and emphasizing the model's posterior, without altering architecture or training. DistPFN-T introduces temperature scaling to adapt the adjustment strength based on the discrepancy between prior and posterior.
AI.result: Substantial improvements are reported for various TabPFN-based models under label shift across >250 OpenML datasets, while maintaining strong performance in standard settings without label shift.
AI.conclusion: DistPFN offers a simple, effective, training-free approach to address label shift in tabular foundation models; DistPFN-T provides adaptive control. The approach preserves standard performance and broad applicability, with code released at the provided repository.

title: Lightweight Cross-Spectral Face Recognition via Contrastive Alignment and Distillation
summary: Heterogeneous Face Recognition (HFR) aims at matching face images captured across different sensing modalities, such as thermal-to-visible or near-infrared-to-visible, enhancing the usability of face recognition systems in challenging real-world conditions. Although recent HFR methods have achieved significant improvements in performance, many rely on computationally expensive models, making them impractical for deployment on resource-limited edge devices. In this work, we introduce a lightweight yet effective HFR framework by adapting a hybrid CNN-Transformer model originally developed for RGB homogeneous face recognition. Our approach enables efficient end-to-end training with only a small amount of paired heterogeneous data, while still maintaining strong performance on standard RGB face recognition benchmarks. This makes it suitable for both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings. Comprehensive experiments on several challenging HFR and face recognition benchmarks show that our method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance while keeping computational requirements low.

AI.tldr: Proposes a lightweight HFR framework by adapting a hybrid CNN-Transformer RGB face model to heterogeneous modalities, enabling end-to-end training with limited paired data, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art performance with reduced computational cost.
AI.motivation: Address the computational burden of current HFR methods and data scarcity in cross-modality face recognition, aiming for efficient edge-deployable solutions usable in both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings.
AI.method: Adapt an existing RGB homogeneous face recognition model into a hybrid CNN-Transformer framework for HFR. The approach supports end-to-end training with a small amount of paired heterogeneous data while maintaining strong RGB performance.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple HFR and face recognition benchmarks show competitive or state-of-the-art accuracy with low computational requirements, indicating a favorable performance–resource trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that lightweight cross-modality recognition is feasible; the framework is suitable for practical deployment on resource-constrained devices and versatile across homogeneous and heterogeneous settings.

title: Structural Equivalence and Learning Dynamics in Delayed MARL
summary:
We formally establish the equivalence between Observation Delay (OD) and Action Delay (AD) in cooperative partially observable multi-agent systems using observation-action histories. We show that both systems generate identical admissible joint-policy sets, and their induced state-action-observation trajectories are identical in distribution, leading to identical optimal solutions in Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (Dec-POMDPs). This formally generalizes existing infinite-horizon single-agent results to any-horizon partially observable cooperative multi-agent problems with decentralized policy execution, and allows any mixed-delay configuration to be reduced to a pure OD system. We further prove that in Transition-Independent MDPs (TI-MDPs), the observation-action history reduces to a tractable minimal local augmented state.
  However, we show through numerical experiments that although the optimal solution spaces are structurally isomorphic, the practical learning dynamics are fundamentally different. First, using the minimal local augmented state, the equivalence no longer holds when transitions are not independent. Second, operational constraints and causal credit-assignment errors in Temporal Difference (TD) algorithms induce different learning behaviors across regimes. Finally, leveraging this structural equivalence to bypass these learning challenges, we demonstrate successful multi-agent zero-shot policy transfer from OD to AD, paving the way for unified, efficient solution methods in complex delayed systems.

AI.tldr: The abstract proves a formal equivalence between Observation Delay (OD) and Action Delay (AD) in cooperative partly observable multi-agent settings, showing identical optimal policy spaces and trajectory distributions, generalizing to any-horizon Dec-POMDPs; mixed delays reduce to pure OD; TI-MDPs yield a tractable local augmented state; yet practical learning dynamics differ due to non-independence of transitions and TD credit-assignment issues; evidence of zero-shot OD-to-AD policy transfer.
AI.motivation: To unify delay representations in cooperative multi-agent POMDPs, enabling theoretical guarantees of equivalence and enabling reductions and transfer, while addressing tractable representations in TI-MDPs and exploring learning dynamics.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis establishing equivalence of admissible joint-policy sets and distribution of state-action-observation trajectories under OD and AD; extension to any-horizon Dec-POMDPs; reduction of mixed-delay configurations to pure OD; identification of a tractable minimal local augmented state in Transition-Independent MDPs; empirical evaluation on learning dynamics and zero-shot transfer between OD and AD.
AI.result: 1) Structural: optimal policy spaces are isomorphic and trajectories distributions identical under OD and AD; 2) Generalization: applies to any-horizon cooperative Dec-POMDPs; 3) TI-MDPs: observation-action history reduces to a minimal local augmented state; 4) Learning: practical TD-based learning exhibits different behavior across regimes; 5) Transfer: successful zero-shot policy transfer from OD to AD demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: The delay representations OD and AD are theoretically equivalent for solution spaces and trajectory distributions, enabling unified, efficient methods and reductions, but learning dynamics differ in practice due to non-independent transitions and credit-assignment issues; TI-MDPs offer tractable representations; the results motivate zero-shot transfer and further investigation into learning dynamics and broader applicability.

title: Extending Differential Temporal Difference Methods for Episodic Problems
summary: Differential temporal difference (TD) methods are value-based reinforcement learning algorithms that have been proposed for infinite-horizon problems. They rely on reward centering, where each reward is centered by the average reward. This keeps the return bounded and removes a value function's state-independent offset. However, reward centering can alter the optimal policy in episodic problems, limiting its applicability. Motivated by recent works that emphasize the role of normalization in streaming deep reinforcement learning, we study reward centering in episodic problems and propose a generalization of differential TD. We prove that this generalization maintains the ordering of policies in the presence of termination, and thus extends differential TD to episodic problems. We show equivalence with a form of linear TD, thereby inheriting theoretical guarantees that have been shown for those algorithms. We then extend several streaming reinforcement learning algorithms to their differential counterparts. Across a range of base algorithms and environments, we empirically validate that reward centering can improve sample efficiency in episodic problems.

AI.tldr: A generalization of reward-centered differential TD for episodic RL that preserves policy ordering under termination, is equivalent to a linear TD variant, extends streaming RL algorithms to differential forms, and improves sample efficiency empirically.
AI.motivation: Reward centering in infinite-horizon differential TD can distort optimal policies in episodic problems; normalization insights from streaming deep RL motivate a robust episodic generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a generalized differential TD for episodic tasks; prove policy ordering is preserved under termination; establish equivalence to a linear TD form; extend multiple streaming RL algorithms to their differential counterparts; conduct empirical evaluation across diverse base algorithms and environments.
AI.result: The generalized method preserves policy order in episodic settings, is equivalent to a linear TD variant providing theoretical guarantees, enables differential versions of streaming algorithms, and yields improved sample efficiency in episodic RL empirically.
AI.conclusion: Reward centering can be effectively extended to episodic RL via the proposed generalization, enabling preserved theoretical guarantees and practical gains in sample efficiency; supports broader use of differential/normalized approaches in streaming RL.

title: Covariance-Aware Goodness for Scalable Forward-Forward Learning
summary: The Forward-Forward algorithm eliminates global gradient flow and full network activations storage. However, in convolutional settings, existing BP-free FF methods significantly under-perform backpropagation on complex benchmarks such as ImageNet-100 and Tiny-ImageNet. We identify this gap as a structural bottleneck in goodness extraction: standard sum-of-squares formulation collapses feature volumes into channel-wise activation energies which omits critical second-order dependencies. To address this, we propose a framework centered on three key components. First, Bi-axis Covariance Goodness(BiCovG) explicitly augments the standard goodness function with structured second-order information along two axes: cross-channel projections that model inter-feature covariance, and nested multi-scale aggregation that encodes spatial correlation statistics. This provides a tractable approximation to covariance-aware goodness without the prohibitive O(C^2) complexity of explicit matrix estimation. Second, a lightweight Logistic Fusion module aggregates layer-wise predictions, amplifying the contribution of deeper representations. Third, the Feature Alignment Layer(FAL) introduces a zero-initialized correction at block boundaries to mitigate representation misalignment in deep locally trained networks. By introducing these three components, we effectively double the depth of viable Forward-Forward learning, extending robust layer utilization from shallow baselines to 16 layer architectures like VGG-16. The resulting BP-free model achieves 73.01% on ImageNet-100 and 50.30% on Tiny-ImageNet. As a practical extension, Hybrid Goodness Blocks control the scope of gradient propagation via configurable block sizes, further narrowing the ImageNet-100 gap to 3.6% and matching BP on Tiny-ImageNet, while still reducing peak memory by approximately 50% relative to BP.

AI.tldr: A BP-free Forward-Forward CNN framework with BiCovG, Logistic Fusion, FAL, and Hybrid blocks that encodes covariance and multi-scale statistics to close the gap to backpropagation, enabling deeper networks with substantial memory savings.
AI.motivation: To overcome the structural bottleneck of the Forward-Forward goodness, which collapses feature volumes into channel-wise energies and ignores second-order dependencies, causing underperformance on complex benchmarks.
AI.method: Introduce Bi-axis Covariance Goodness (BiCovG) to capture cross-channel covariance and multi-scale spatial statistics without O(C^2) complexity; add a Lightweight Logistic Fusion module to aggregate layer-wise predictions; incorporate a Feature Alignment Layer (FAL) with zero-initialized corrections at block boundaries to mitigate misalignment; and employ Hybrid Goodness Blocks to control gradient propagation by configurable block sizes. Demonstrate deeper Forward-Forward learning up to 16 layers (e.g., VGG-16) and evaluate on ImageNet-100 and Tiny-ImageNet.
AI.result: The framework substantially improves BP-free learning, achieving 73.01% on ImageNet-100 and 50.30% on Tiny-ImageNet; with Hybrid Goodness Blocks, the ImageNet-100 gap narrows to 3.6% and performance matches BP on Tiny-ImageNet, while peak memory is reduced by ~50% relative to BP.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BiCovG-based BP-free framework, aided by Logistic Fusion, FAL, and Hybrid blocks, effectively extends viable Forward-Forward learning to deeper CNN architectures, delivering competitive accuracy with notable memory savings and bridging much of the gap to backpropagation on standard benchmarks.

title: Critical Windows of Complexity Control: When Transformers Decide to Reason or Memorize
summary: Recent work has shown that Transformers' compositional generalization is governed by \emph{complexity control}, initialization scale and weight decay, which steers training toward low-complexity reasoning solutions rather than high-complexity memorization. Existing analyses, however, treat complexity control as a single static hyperparameter choice, leaving open \emph{when} during training this control is actually decisive. We show that the memorization-versus-reasoning fate of a Transformer is determined within a sharp, identifiable window of training. On a controlled compositional task we find that (i)~weight decay applied for a single 25\%-of-training window matches full-training weight decay in out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy ($0.93$ vs $0.91$); (ii)~holding total regularization budget constant, placing it in the middle of training yields $5{-}9\times$ higher OOD accuracy than placing it early; (iii)~the boundary of the critical window is remarkably sharp, window onset shifted by as little as $100$ optimization steps causes mean OOD to jump from chance ($0.15$) to reasoning-regime ($0.61$); (iv)~the window's position depends systematically on initialization scale, but the basin of attraction for reasoning solutions \emph{shrinks} at small initialization, contradicting the prevailing recommendation that smaller initialization is uniformly better. We further show that the critical-window phenomenon is task-specific: it does not appear on grokking with modular arithmetic, where properly tuned constant weight decay matches scheduled weight decay.

AI.tldr: Transformers' generalization is governed not only by hyperparameter values but by a sharp, task-specific training window of regularization; timing of weight decay is decisive for memorization vs. reasoning, with initialization scale shaping the window. Results are task-specific and do not universally generalize (e.g., grokking with modular arithmetic behaves differently).
AI.motivation: To determine how the timing of complexity control (weight decay, initialization, etc.) during training influences whether a Transformer learns low-complexity reasoning or memorization, and to identify when this control is decisive.
AI.method: Systematic experiments on a controlled compositional task varying the timing and amount of weight decay, total regularization budget, and initialization scale; compare to constant vs scheduled weight decay; assess OOD accuracy; test on grokking with modular arithmetic as a contrast.
AI.result: A sharp, identifiable training window governs the memorization-versus-reasoning fate: (i) 25% window with weight decay matches full-training weight decay in OOD accuracy; (ii) mid-training regularization yields substantially higher OOD accuracy under constant budget; (iii) boundary is extremely sharp (100-step shift flips outcome); (iv) window position depends on initialization scale; small init reduces the basin of attraction for reasoning; the window phenomenon is not universal and does not appear in grokking with modular arithmetic.
AI.conclusion: Temporal regulation of regularization is crucial for achieving compositional reasoning in Transformers; the effective training window is task-specific and interacts with initialization; naive or early-weight decay is detrimental; further theory and broader tasks are needed to generalize these findings.

title: MIRAGE: Retrieval and Generation of Multimodal Images and Texts for Medical Education
comment: Accepted at the Workshop on Applications of Medical AI (AMAI 2025), in conjunction with MICCAI 2025
summary: Access to diverse, well-annotated medical images with interactive learning tools is fundamental for training practitioners in medicine and related fields to improve their diagnostic skills and understanding of anatomical structures. While medical atlases are valuable, they are often impractical due to their size and lack of interactivity, whereas online image search may provide mislabeled or incomplete material. To address this, we propose MIRAGE, a multimodal medical text and image retrieval and generation system that allows users to find and generate clinically relevant images from trustworthy sources by mapping both text and images to a shared latent space, enabling semantically meaningful queries. The system is based on a fine-tuned medical version of CLIP (MedICaT-ROCO), trained with the ROCO dataset, obtained from PubMed Central. MIRAGE allows users to give prompts to retrieve images, generate synthetic ones through a medical diffusion model (Prompt2MedImage) and receive enriched descriptions from a large language model (Dolly-v2-3b). It also supports a dual search option, enabling the visual comparison of different medical conditions. A key advantage of the system is that it relies entirely on publicly available pretrained models, ensuring reproducibility and accessibility. Our goal is to provide a free, transparent and easy-to-use didactic tool for medical students, especially those without programming skills. The system features an interface that enables interactive and personalized visual learning through medical image retrieval and generation. The system is accessible to medical students worldwide without requiring local computational resources or technical expertise, and is currently deployed on Kaggle: http://www-vpu.eps.uam.es/mirage

AI.tldr: MIRAGE enables semantic text–image retrieval and generation for medical education by aligning text and images in a shared latent space using a medical CLIP variant (MedICaT-ROCO) trained on ROCO from PubMed Central, plus a diffusion model for image generation and a large language model for descriptions; deployed on Kaggle with public pretrained models to ensure accessibility.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between static medical atlases and uncurated online image searches by providing an interactive, accurate, and annotated image collection for medical training; emphasize reproducibility, transparency, and global accessibility for students without programming resources.
AI.method: Fine-tune a medical CLIP model (MedICaT-ROCO) on the ROCO dataset from PubMed Central to create a shared latent space for text and images. Implement Prompt2MedImage: a medical diffusion model for image generation conditioned on prompts. Use Dolly-v2-3b to generate enriched descriptions. Provide a dual search option for visual comparison of medical conditions. All components rely on publicly available pretrained models, and the system is deployed on Kaggle for easy access.
AI.result: The system enables semantically meaningful queries, retrieval of clinically relevant images, generation of synthetic images, and enriched captions, with a visual comparison feature. It is accessible worldwide without local compute, promoting reproducible, didactic medical learning.
AI.conclusion: MIRAGE demonstrates feasibility of a fully open, educational multimodal retrieval/generation pipeline using public models. Benefits include improved access and reproducibility for medical education, while limitations may arise from dataset biases, model safety concerns, and the need for evaluation and user studies to quantify learning outcomes. Future work could extend datasets, assess educational impact, and refine safety and reliability of generated content.

title: Probing Structural Mathematical Reasoning in Language Models with Algebraic Trapdoors
summary: We introduce a benchmark suite for evaluating structural mathematical reasoning in language models, built on subgroup-construction problems in SL(3, Z) with cryptographic-style verifier-prover asymmetry. Each instance presents a finitely generated subgroup as a list of integer matrices and asks for an arithmetic invariant -- index, surjection-at-prime, or membership -- that the construction-time information (N, K) pins down in O(1) closed form, but that the solver, lacking that information, must derive by either Aschbacher-classification analysis or by a membership query in SL(3, Z) of unknown decidability. The benchmark therefore distinguishes models with internalized algebraic priors (Aschbacher classes, McLaughlin's theorem, Property (T), the congruence subgroup property) from models that rely on general-purpose computation. We report empirical results across five representative reasoning traces from two state-of-the-art models. The headline result: on the index variant, one model spent 152 minutes of reasoning, explicitly identified the kernel-side membership question as the bottleneck, attempted constructive verification, and abstained with "DON'T KNOW" rather than commit to its computed cokernel candidate -- demonstrating calibrated meta-cognition on the open-decidability boundary that the benchmark was designed to probe. We argue that the benchmark exposes a four-way classification of model behavior (commit-correct, commit-wrong, abstain-correct, abstain-wrong) that standard answer-key scoring conflates.

AI.tldr: Introduces a benchmark for structural mathematical reasoning in LMs using SL(3,Z) subgroup problems with verifier-prover asymmetry; distinguishes internal algebraic priors from general computation; reveals four-way behavioral taxonomy including abstention.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether LMs internalize algebraic priors (Aschbacher classes, McLaughlin's theorem, Property (T), congruence subgroup property) and can handle open-decidability reasoning on undecidable components, beyond standard accuracy metrics.
AI.method: Present a benchmark with finitely generated subgroups given as integer matrices. Ask for an arithmetic invariant (index, surjection-at-prime, or membership) that is fixed by construction parameters (N, K) in O(1) but requires the solver to deduce via algebraic analysis or by a membership query in SL(3,Z) of unknown decidability. Run five representative reasoning traces from two state-of-the-art models. Classify behavior into four categories (commit-correct, commit-wrong, abstain-correct, abstain-wrong).
AI.result: On the index variant, one model spent 152 minutes of reasoning, identified the kernel-side membership question as bottleneck, attempted constructive verification, and abstained with 'DON'T KNOW'—demonstrating calibrated meta-cognition at the open-decidability boundary. Four-way behavior classification observed beyond traditional scoring.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark exposes model behavior beyond standard answer keys, promoting calibrated uncertainty and algebraic priors. It highlights the open-decidability boundary in mathematical reasoning and suggests directions for enhancing priors and interpretability in LMs.

title: QuadBox: Accelerating 3D Gaussian Splatting with Geometry-Aware Boxes
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has emerged as an advanced technique for real-time novel view synthesis by representing scene geometry and appearance using differentiable Gaussian primitives. However, efficiently computing precise Gaussian-tile intersections remains a critical task in the rasterization pipeline. To this end, we propose QuadBox, a method that leverages four axis-aligned bounding boxes to tightly encapsulate projected Gaussians in a discrete manner. First, we derive a geometry-aware stretching factor that enables the construction of a tile-aligned QuadBox, which covers the elliptical projection and largely excludes irrelevant tiles. Second, we introduce QPass, a single-pass tile traversal algorithm that exhaustively exploits the discrete nature of QuadBox, ensuring that the tile intersection check is performed with simple interval tests. Experiments on public datasets show that our method accelerates the rendering speed of 3DGS by 1.85$\times$. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Powertony102/QuadBox}{https://github.com/Powertony102/QuadBox}.

AI.tldr: QuadBox uses four axis-aligned bounding boxes to tightly wrap projected Gaussians in 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling a single-pass tile traversal (QPass) with interval tests, achieving 1.85× speedup.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost of precise Gaussian–tile intersections in real-time 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS).
AI.method: Derive a geometry-aware stretching factor to construct a tile-aligned QuadBox; use four axis-aligned bounding boxes to tightly bound the elliptical Gaussian projections; develop QPass, a single-pass tile traversal that exploits the discrete nature of QuadBox and performs intersection checks via simple interval tests.
AI.result: Achieves 1.85× speedup on public datasets; code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: QuadBox with QPass tightens Gaussian bounds and enables efficient, single-pass tile intersection testing, delivering significant speedups for real-time 3DGS rendering.

title: Demystifying Manifold Constraints in LLM Pre-training
summary: The empirical success of large language model (LLM) pre-training relies heavily on heuristic stabilization techniques, such as explicit normalization layers and weight decay. While recent constrained optimization approaches that explicitly restrict weights may improve numerical stability and performance, the mechanism and motivation for adding constraints still remain elusive. This paper systematically demystifies the role of explicit manifold constraints in LLM pre-training. By introducing the Msign-Aligned Constrained Riemannian Optimizer (MACRO)-a provably convergent, single-loop optimization framework-our study disentangles weight regularization heuristics from interacting mechanisms like RMS normalization and decoupled weight decay. Theoretical analyses and comprehensive empirical evaluations reveal that manifold constraints independently bound forward activation scales and enforce stable rotational equilibrium, thereby subsuming the roles of these heuristic mechanisms. Evaluations on large-scale LLM architectures demonstrate that MACRO achieves highly competitive performance while rigorously preserving the theoretical guarantees of exact Riemannian optimization.

AI.tldr: MACRO (Msign-Aligned Constrained Riemannian Optimizer) is a provably convergent single-loop optimizer using explicit manifold constraints to stabilize LLM pre-training. It shows that constraints bound activation scales and enforce stable rotational equilibrium, subsuming RMS normalization and weight decay, while achieving competitive performance with exact Riemannian guarantees.
AI.motivation: LLM pre-training heavily relies on stabilization techniques (normalization, weight decay). While constrained optimization can improve stability, the underlying mechanism and motivation for adding constraints are not well understood. The work aims to demystify how explicit manifold constraints affect training dynamics and performance.
AI.method: Introduce MACRO, a Msign-aligned constrained Riemannian optimizer that operates in a single loop with explicit weight manifold constraints. The approach separates (decoupled) weight decay and regularization from stabilization mechanisms like RMS normalization, provides theoretical convergence guarantees for exact Riemannian optimization, and analyzes how the manifold constraints influence forward activation scales and rotational dynamics. Empirical evaluation on large-scale LLMs to assess performance and stability.
AI.result: The manifold constraints independently bound forward activation scales and enforce a stable rotational equilibrium, effectively subsuming the roles of RMS normalization and decoupled weight decay. MACRO achieves highly competitive performance while maintaining rigorous theoretical guarantees of exact Riemannian optimization across large LLM architectures.
AI.conclusion: Explicit manifold constraints are sufficient to explain and replicate the stabilizing effects traditionally achieved by normalization and weight-decay heuristics in LLM pre-training. MACRO offers a provably convergent optimization framework that aligns weights with a constrained Riemannian geometry, yielding competitive results without relying on ad hoc stabilization tricks.

title: VTAgent: Agentic Keyframe Anchoring for Evidence-Aware Video TextVQA
summary: Video text-based visual question answering (Video TextVQA) aims to answer questions by reasoning over visual textual content appearing in videos. Despite the strong multimodal video understanding capabilities of recent Video-LLMs, their performance on existing Video TextVQA benchmarks remains limited. To better understand this gap, we conduct an upper-bound analysis through frame-wise question answering, counting a sample as correct if any frame yields the right answer, which significantly outperforms direct video-based inference and reveals a substantial performance gap. The results suggest that the primary bottleneck lies in the localization of key question-relevant evidence, rather than in reasoning capacity itself. Building on this insight, we propose a question-guided agent framework that explicitly anchors the relevant keyframes before answering. The approach operates effectively in a training-free setting and consistently surpasses direct video inference. With additional supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL), it achieves an average improvement of +12.12 in accuracy and +11.15 in ANLS across benchmarks, establishing new state-of-the-art results. Our study underscores the critical role of explicit keyframe anchoring for advancing Video TextVQA. The code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: Frame-wise QA reveals that the main bottleneck in Video TextVQA is keyframe localization rather than reasoning; a training-free, question-guided anchor-of-keyframes approach surpasses direct video inference, and with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RL achieves state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Video TextVQA benchmarks lag behind the capabilities of recent Video-LLMs. An upper-bound analysis via frame-wise question answering (where a sample is counted correct if any frame yields the right answer) isolates the gap and tests whether localization of evidence is the bottleneck rather than reasoning.
AI.method: Compute an upper-bound by performing frame-wise QA and counting a sample as correct if any frame yields the correct answer. Propose a question-guided agent framework that explicitly anchors relevant keyframes before answering. The base method is training-free; with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL), it further improves.
AI.result: Frame-wise QA significantly outperforms direct video-based inference, exposing a substantial performance gap. The proposed framework improves accuracy by an average of +12.12 and ANLS by +11.15 across benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: Explicit keyframe anchoring is critical for advancing Video TextVQA. The method is effective in a training-free setting and remains strong with SFT and RL. Code will be released.

title: Online Nonstochastic Prediction: Logarithmic Regret via Predictive Online Least Squares
summary: We study online prediction for marginally stable, partially observed linear dynamical systems under nonstochastic disturbances. Our objective is to minimize the cumulative squared prediction loss and compete with the best-in-hindsight Luenberger predictor. Standard online learning methods typically rely on bounded domains/gradients, and thus their guarantees may fail to deal with potentially unbounded trajectories in marginally stable systems. In this paper, we introduce an unconstrained online least squares method that stabilizes the learning process via tailored predictive hints. With model knowledge, we prove that hints constructed from any stabilizing Luenberger predictor render the hint residuals uniformly bounded, achieving logarithmic regret despite unbounded trajectory growth. We also discuss model-free prediction and introduce a simple universal hint for symmetric systems, under which logarithmic regret is maintained without model knowledge. Our results provide an adaptive, instance-wise optimal online predictor compared to classical fixed-gain observers under nonstochastic disturbances.

AI.tldr: Unconstrained online least squares with stabilizing hints achieves logarithmic regret for online prediction in marginally stable, partially observed LTI systems under nonstochastic disturbances, competing with the best-in-hindsight Luenberger predictor; model-based and model-free variants with universal hints extend applicability.
AI.motivation: Address the failure of standard online methods in unbounded trajectories of marginally stable, partially observed systems under nonstochastic disturbances; goal is to achieve near-optimal prediction performance without requiring bounded domains.
AI.method: Introduce an unconstrained online least squares algorithm that is stabilized by predictive hints. With model knowledge, use hints from a stabilizing Luenberger predictor to bound residuals; show logarithmic regret. In the model-free setting, propose a universal hint for symmetric systems that preserves logarithmic regret without model knowledge.
AI.result: Demonstrate logarithmic regret relative to the best-in-hindsight Luenberger predictor, despite potentially unbounded trajectories. The residuals of the hints remain uniformly bounded when using stabilizing predictors. The approach yields an adaptive, instance-wise optimal online predictor under nonstochastic disturbances, outperforming classical fixed-gain observers.
AI.conclusion: Unconstrained online least squares with stabilizing hints provides an effective, adaptive online predictor for marginally stable, partially observed LTI systems under nonstochastic disturbances, with model-based and model-free variants achieving logarithmic regret and improving upon fixed-gain observers.

title: FLUID: Continuous-Time Hyperconnected Sparse Transformer for Sink-Free Learning
summary: Continuous-time (CT) Transformers improve irregular and long-range modeling over CT-RNNs by exploiting inputs or outputs embeddings with continuous dynamics. However, the core scaled-dot-product-attention (SDPA) mechanism remains inherently discrete. We propose FLUID (Flexible Unified Information Dynamics), a CT Transformer that incorporates continuous dynamics directly into the attention computation by replacing it with Liquid Attention Network (LAN). LAN reinterprets attention logits as continuous dynamical system and reformulates them as the solution to a linear ODE modulated by input-dependent nonlinear recurrent gates. Theoretically, we establish stability guarantees for LAN dynamics and show that it serves as an interpolating middle ground between SDPA and CT-RNNs, recovering each as special case under well-defined parameterization of its gating functions. LAN also introduces an explicit attention-sink gate to eliminate disproportionate attention mass on uninformative nodes. FLUID replaces standard residual connections with input-dependent Liquid Hyper-Connections to adaptively regulate interlayer information flow. Empirically, we evaluate FLUID on a broad set of learning tasks, including (i) irregular time-series, (ii) long-range modeling, (iii) lane-keeping control of autonomous vehicles, and (iv) learning physical dynamics under a scarce data regime. Across all the tasks, FLUID consistently matches or outperforms CT baselines, achieving improvements of up to 47% in certain scenarios and enhancing generalization under distributional shifts. Additionally, FLUID demonstrates superior noise robustness and a self-correcting inductive bias in autonomous vehicle control. We also provide a detailed analysis of key hyperparameters to guide tuning and show that FLUID occupies an intermediate position among competing approaches in terms of runtime and memory efficiency.

AI.tldr: FLUID introduces Liquid Attention Network (LAN) to replace discrete SDPA with a continuous-time attention mechanism, offering stability guarantees and interpolation between SDPA and CT-RNNs, plus adaptive interlayer connections. Empirically, it improves irregular/long-range tasks, autonomous vehicle control, and physical dynamics under scarce data, with competitive runtime/memory and robustness advantages.
AI.motivation: Bridge discrete attention with continuous-time dynamics to better model irregular, long-range dependencies while maintaining efficiency, stability, and adaptive information flow.
AI.method: Define LAN as a linear ODE modulated by input-dependent nonlinear gates that reinterpret attention logits as continuous dynamics; add an explicit attention-sink gate; replace residuals with input-dependent Liquid Hyper-Connections; train FLUID across tasks and perform hyperparameter analysis.
AI.result: Theoretically, LAN is stable and interpolates SDPA and CT-RNNs as special cases. Empirically, FLUID matches or outperforms CT baselines, with up to 47% improvements on certain tasks, improved generalization under distribution shifts, noise robustness, and a self-correcting inductive bias in control. Runtime/memory are intermediate between competing approaches; detailed hyperparameter analysis is provided.
