AI.conclusion: FLUID is a viable continuous-time Transformer framework that unifies attention and recurrence via LAN and Liquid Hyper-Connections, offering stability, flexibility, and empirical gains across diverse tasks; further work could optimize gating parameterizations and assess on-device feasibility.

title: FairEnc: A Fair Vision-Language Model with Fair Vision and Text Encoders for Glaucoma Detection
summary: Automated glaucoma detection is critical for preventing irreversible vision loss and reducing the burden on healthcare systems. However, ensuring fairness across diverse patient populations remains a significant challenge. In this paper, we propose FairEnc, a fair pretraining method for vision-language models (VLMs) that enables simultaneous debiasing across multiple sensitive attributes. FairEnc jointly mitigates biases in both textual and visual modalities with respect to multiple sensitive attributes, including race, gender, ethnicity, and language. Specifically, for the textual encoder, we leverage a large language model to generate synthetic clinical descriptions with varied sensitive attributes while preserving disease semantics, and employ a contrastive alignment objective to encourage demographic-invariant representations. For the visual encoder, we propose a dual-level fairness strategy that combines mutual information regularization to reduce statistical dependence between learned features and demographic groups, with multi-discriminator adversarial debiasing. Comprehensive experiments on the publicly available Harvard-FairVLMed dataset demonstrate that FairEnc effectively reduces demographic disparity as measured by DPD and DEOdds while achieving strong diagnostic performance under both zero-shot and linear probing evaluations. Additional experiments on the private FairFundus dataset show that FairEnc consistently preserves fairness advantages under cross-domain and cross-modality settings and maintains diagnostic performance within a competitive range. These results highlight FairEnc's ability to generalize fairness under distribution shifts, supporting its potential for more equitable deployment in real-world clinical settings. Our codebase and synthetic clinical notes are available at https://github.com/Mohamed-Elhabebe/FairEnc

AI.tldr: FairEnc is a fair pretraining framework for vision-language models that debiases glaucoma-detection across multiple sensitive attributes in textual and visual modalities; it uses LLM-generated synthetic clinical notes for textual debiasing and a dual-level visual debiasing with mutual information regularization and multi-discriminator adversarial learning; evaluated on Harvard-FairVLMed and private FairFundus datasets, it reduces demographic disparity (DPD, DEOdds) while maintaining diagnostic performance, and generalizes under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Fairness in automated glaucoma detection is challenged by biases across diverse patient populations. The work aims to achieve simultaneous debiasing across multiple sensitive attributes in both text and images, preserving disease semantics while maintaining diagnostic accuracy, and ensuring robustness to distribution shifts for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Textual encoder: employ a large language model to generate synthetic clinical descriptions with varied sensitive attributes while preserving disease semantics; use a contrastive alignment objective to yield demographic-invariant representations. Visual encoder: dual-level fairness using (1) mutual information regularization to minimize dependence between learned features and demographic groups and (2) multi-discriminator adversarial debiasing.
AI.result: On Harvard-FairVLMed, FairEnc reduces demographic disparity (DPD and DEOdds) while achieving strong diagnostic performance in zero-shot and linear probing settings. On the private FairFundus dataset, fairness benefits persist across cross-domain and cross-modality scenarios, with competitive diagnostic performance.
AI.conclusion: FairEnc demonstrates the ability to generalize fairness under distribution shifts, supporting equitable deployment of VLM-based glaucoma detection in real-world clinical settings. The work provides code and synthetic clinical notes for reproducibility.

title: Exploring Clustering Capability of Inpainting Model Embeddings for Pattern-based Individual Identification
summary: In this paper, we explore deep learning techniques for individual identification of animals based on their skin patterns. Individual identification is crucial in biodiversity monitoring, since it enables analysis of decline or growth of populations, or intra-species interactions within populations. Models trained for the task of individual identification often do not focus on the skin pattern of animals, but on background details or body shape details. These characteristics are not individually specific, or can change drastically through time. We focus on techniques that will make machine learning models more responsive to skin pattern structure when extracting individual visual embeddings from images. For this, we explore image inpainting of task-specific masks as an auxiliary task to enhance ML-based individual identification from animal skin patterns. We propose a comparative analysis among four models as an encoder backbone for the individual identification task. We focus on the case study of zebrafish, which is a widely recognized biological model organism, and which exhibits individually identifying skin patterns. To evaluate encoder backbone performance, we present standard metrics for classification accuracy, embedding clustering metrics, and GradCAM visualizations.

AI.tldr: Comparative study of four encoder backbones for zebrafish skin-pattern–based individual identification, using image inpainting of task-specific masks as an auxiliary task to emphasize skin-pattern structure; evaluated with classification accuracy, embedding clustering metrics, and GradCAM visualizations.
AI.motivation: Current animal-identification models often rely on background or body-shape cues rather than skin patterns, which are more stable and individually distinctive. Zebrafish are a well-established model with identifiable skin patterns, making them suitable for studying pattern-focused embeddings for biodiversity monitoring.
AI.method: Four encoder backbones are evaluated as the core for the identification task. An auxiliary inpainting task using task-specific masks is incorporated to steer the learned embeddings toward skin-pattern features. Evaluation uses standard metrics: classification accuracy, embedding clustering metrics, and GradCAM visualizations to assess focus on skin patterns.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Not explicitly stated in the abstract; the work outlines a framework to enhance skin-pattern–driven identification via an inpainting auxiliary task and backbone comparison, with planned evaluation across accuracy, clustering, and interpretability metrics.

title: $p$-adic Manifold Learning and Benchmark Tasks from Impartial Games
summary: We introduce $p$-adic manifold learning, propose an algorithm to solve it, and propose benchmark tasks from impartial games.

AI.tldr: Proposes p-adic manifold learning, an accompanying algorithm, and impartial-game-based benchmark tasks.
AI.motivation: Extend manifold learning to the p-adic setting and establish evaluation benchmarks using impartial games.
AI.method: Formulates a p-adic manifold learning problem and proposes an algorithm to solve it.
AI.result: Introduces benchmark tasks derived from impartial games and an algorithmic framework for p-adic manifold learning.
AI.conclusion: Lays groundwork for p-adic data analysis and evaluation with game-based benchmarks.

title: DART: A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Comprehensive Rope Condition Monitoring
summary: The condition monitoring (CM) of synthetic fibre ropes (SFRs) used in offshore, maritime, and industrial settings demands more than a classifier: inspectors need continuous severity estimates, maintenance recommendations, anomaly flags, deterioration timelines, and automated reports, all from a single inspection image. We present DART (Damage Assessment via Rope Transformer), a vision-language foundation model that addresses the full rope inspection workflow through a unified multi-task architecture. DART extends the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) to the cross-modal domain by coupling a Vision Transformer (ViT-H/14) with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct via a Severity-Conditioned Cross-Modal Fusion (SC-CMF) module. Three architectural innovations drive the model's versatility: (1) HD-MASK, a saliency-guided masking strategy that focuses self-supervised reconstruction on damage-dense patches; (2) per-class learnable severity gates that adaptively weight language grounding by damage category; and (3) a Contrastive Damage Disentanglement (CDD) loss that shapes the embedding space to simultaneously encode damage type, severity ordering, and cross-modal semantics. Trained once on 4,270 images spanning 14 fine-grained rope damage classes, the frozen DART backbone supports downstream tasks without any task-specific fine-tuning: damage classification (93.22 % accuracy, 91.04 % macro-F1, +38.5 pp over a vision-only baseline), continuous severity regression (Spearman rho = 0.94, within-1-ordinal accuracy 99.6 %), few-shot recognition (89.2 % macro-F1 at 20 shots). These results demonstrate that DART functions as a general-purpose CM backbone that goes well beyond classification, providing actionable inspection intelligence from a single shared representation.

AI.tldr: DART is a unified vision-language model for rope-condition monitoring that delivers multi-task outputs (classification, severity regression, few-shot recognition) from a single image, achieving strong cross-modal performance without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: CM of synthetic rope requires continuous severity estimates, maintenance recommendations, anomaly flags, deterioration timelines, and automated reports beyond standard classification.
AI.method: Extend JEPA with cross-modal fusion between ViT-H/14 and Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct using a Severity-Conditioned Cross-Modal Fusion (SC-CMF) module; introduce HD-MASK saliency-guided masking; per-class learnable severity gates; Contrastive Damage Disentanglement (CDD) loss; train on 4,270 images across 14 damage classes; backbone frozen for downstream tasks.
AI.result: No task-specific fine-tuning needed; damage classification 93.22% accuracy, macro-F1 91.04% (+38.5 pp vs vision-only baseline); Spearman 0.94; within-1-ordinal 99.6%; few-shot macro-F1 89.2% at 20 shots.
AI.conclusion: DART functions as a general-purpose condition monitoring backbone, providing actionable inspection intelligence from a single shared representation beyond simple classification.

title: ICPR 2026 Competition on Privacy-Preserving Person Re-Identification from Top-View RGB-Depth Camera (TVRID)
summary: This companion paper reports the ICPR 2026 TVRID competition on privacy-aware top-view person re-identification. We present the competition setting, the released RGB-Depth dataset, and a summary of final results with descriptions of the top entries. TVRID contains 86 identities captured by four synchronized overhead Intel RealSense D455 cameras, with paired RGB/Depth streams and structured geometric variation across flat, ascent, descent, and oblique viewpoints. The evaluation protocol includes three tracks: RGB Re-ID, Depth Re-ID, and RGB$\leftrightarrow$Depth cross-modal retrieval. Submissions are ranked using mAP and CMC-1 under a unified server-side evaluation. The final results show a clear difficulty ordering (RGB $>$ Depth $>$ Cross-Modal), highlighting both the challenge of modality-constrained retrieval and the feasibility of strong performance with modality-invariant learning. By releasing the dataset at https://zenodo.org/records/17909410, the evaluation scripts at https://github.com/RaphaelDel/ICPR-TVRID, and the accompanying documentation, TVRID establishes a reproducible benchmark for top-view, depth-based, and cross-modal person re-id.

AI.tldr: A competition paper introducing the ICPR 2026 TVRID benchmark for privacy-aware top-view re-identification across RGB, depth, and cross-modal retrieval. It uses 4 synchronized overhead cameras (RGB/Depth) with 86 identities, evaluates RGB/Depth/Cross-Modal tracks via mAP and CMC-1, and concludes RGB is the easiest modality, cross-modal the hardest, while providing a reproducible benchmark and release of dataset and scripts.
AI.motivation: To address privacy-aware, top-view person re-identification with multiple modalities, and to provide a reproducible, publicly available benchmark to assess modality-specific and cross-modal performance under a controlled overhead-view setting.
AI.method: Data: 86 identities captured by four synchronized overhead RealSense D455 cameras, with paired RGB and Depth streams; viewpoints include flat, ascent, descent, and oblique. Tracks: RGB Re-ID, Depth Re-ID, and RGB↔Depth cross-modal retrieval. Evaluation: mAP and CMC-1 using a unified server-side evaluation. Release: dataset and evaluation scripts made public (dataset URL, GitHub scripts) for reproducibility.
AI.result: Final results indicate a clear difficulty ordering: RGB most accessible, then Depth, with Cross-Modal being the most challenging. Demonstrates the feasibility of strong modality-invariant learning for top-view re-id and the ongoing challenge of modality-constrained retrieval.
AI.conclusion: TVRID provides a reproducible, top-view, depth-based, and cross-modal benchmark for privacy-aware person re-id, including dataset, evaluation scripts, and documentation to support future research.

title: Attention-Based Chaotic Self-Supervision for Medical Image Classification
summary: Deep learning models for medical image classification usually achieve promising results but typically rely on large, annotated datasets or standard transfer learning from ImageNet. Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) has emerged as a powerful alternative, yet common methods like masked autoencoders (MAEs) may inadvertently destroy fine-grained diagnostic features by using random masking. In this paper, we propose a novel SSL pre-training strategy, the Chaotic Denoising Autoencoder (CDAE). Instead of masking, we apply a chaotic transformation to the input image, tasking an autoencoder to reconstruct the original. We hypothesize this forces the encoder to learn robust, domain-specific features by "inverting the chaos". Furthermore, we propose an attentive fusion mechanism that combines features from our CDAE-trained encoder with a standard encoder, leveraging the strengths of both general and domain-specific representations. Our method is evaluated on two public medical datasets: ISIC 2018 (skin lesions) and APTOS 2019 (diabetic retinopathy). The proposed model achieves high performance, with an accuracy of 0.9221 and an F1-macro of 0.8530 on ISIC 2018, and an accuracy of 0.8644 and F1-macro of 0.7433 on APTOS 2019, demonstrating the efficacy of our approach.

AI.tldr: Introduces Chaotic Denoising Autoencoder (CDAE) for self-supervised pretraining in medical imaging, replacing masking with chaotic transformations and using attentive fusion with a standard encoder; achieves competitive results on ISIC 2018 and APTOS 2019.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised methods like MAEs can disrupt fine-grained diagnostic cues in medical images. A chaotic transformation aimed at requiring reconstruction may encourage learning robust, domain-specific features, while combining with a general encoder captures complementary representations.
AI.method: Propose CDAE that applies a chaotic transformation to input images and trains an autoencoder to reconstruct the original image. Additionally, implement an attentive fusion mechanism to combine features from the CDAE-trained encoder with a standard encoder, leveraging both domain-specific and general representations.
AI.result: On ISIC 2018, accuracy 0.9221 and F1-macro 0.8530; on APTOS 2019, accuracy 0.8644 and F1-macro 0.7433, indicating strong performance and the effectiveness of the proposed SSL pretraining and fusion strategy.
AI.conclusion: CDAE-based SSL with attentive fusion provides robust, domain-aware representations for medical image classification and can outperform or match traditional MAE-based pretraining on the tested datasets, suggesting broader applicability to medical imaging tasks.

title: Beyond Rigid Geometries: The Spline-Pullback Metric for Universal Diffeomorphic SPD Representation Learning
summary: The integration of Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) matrices into deep learning has historically relied on fixed algebraic Riemannian metrics. Analogous to hand-crafted features in classical machine learning, these static formulations impose rigid geometries limiting network expressivity and adaptability. Recent attempts to parameterize these geometries often violate the axioms of primary matrix functions through unconstrained powers or rank-dependent scaling, inviting spatial folding, loss of global surjectivity, and gradient collapse at spectral singularities. In this paper, we introduce the Spline-Pullback Metric (SPM), instantiated as Spectral-SPM and Cholesky-SPM, marking a paradigm shift from static metric selection to universal geometric approximation. By parameterizing the global diffeomorphism via a rank-invariant, monotonically constrained B-spline, SPM acts as a dense universal approximator for strictly increasing $C^1$ diffeomorphisms and theoretically subsumes existing pullback metrics while enabling localized non-linear spectral modelling. Topologically, SPM provides a globally bijective pullback geometry precluding rank-swapping discontinuities and gradient instabilities. Empirically, SPM achieves a state-of-the-art performance across 3 datasets utilizing Linear Probes, SPDNets, and deep Riemannian ResNets.

AI.tldr: Introduces Spline-Pullback Metric (SPM) for SPD matrices in deep learning, parameterizing a global diffeomorphism with a monotone B-spline to create universal, bijective pullback geometries (Spectral-SPM and Cholesky-SPM) that subsume existing metrics and deliver state-of-the-art results on 3 datasets.
AI.motivation: Overcome rigidity and axiomatic violations of fixed algebraic Riemannian metrics used with SPD matrices in neural nets; enable flexible, stable, and globally bijective geometry for SPD learning.
AI.method: Parameterize a rank-invariant, monotonically constrained B-spline to realize a global diffeomorphism; apply as pullback to SPD geometry via spectral or Cholesky representations; ensure universality for strictly increasing C^1 diffeomorphisms; provide global bijection to avoid rank-swaps and gradient issues; integrate into Linear Probes, SPDNets, and deep Riemannian ResNets.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across three datasets on the tested architectures.
AI.conclusion: SPM provides a universal, topologically robust and computationally practical framework for SPD embedding in deep learning, subsuming prior pullback metrics and enabling localized non-linear spectral modeling.

title: Low-Rank Adaptation of Geospatial Foundation Models for Wildfire Mapping Using Sentinel-2 Data
summary: Wildfire burned-area mapping is essential for damage assessment, emissions modeling, and understanding fire-climate interactions across diverse ecological regions. Recent geospatial foundation models provide strong general-purpose representations for satellite imagery, yet there is still no clear understanding of how to efficiently adapt these models for downstream Earth observation tasks, particularly under geographic and temporal domain shift. This study evaluates three state-of-the-art Geospatial Foundation Models (GFMs) - Terramind, DINOv3, and Prithvi-v2 - for burned-area mapping across the United States and Canada using Sentinel-2 data. Leveraging 3,820 wildfire events from 2017-2023, we conduct spatial and temporal generalization tests across diverse biomes. We systematically compare full fine-tuning, decoder-only fine-tuning, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for adapting each model. Across all experiments, LoRA provides the strongest cross-domain generalization while updating less than 1% of parameters, demonstrating a favorable trade-off between accuracy and efficiency. Prithvi-v2 with LoRA achieves the highest overall accuracy and the largest improvement compared to full fine-tuning. These findings indicate that geospatial foundation models, when adapted using lightweight parameter-efficient methods such as LoRA, offer a robust and scalable solution for large-scale burned-area mapping. Code is available at https://github.com/alishibli97/wildfire-lora-gfm.

AI.tldr: LoRA-based fine-tuning of geospatial foundation models (GFMs) achieves best cross-domain burned-area mapping with minimal parameter updates; Prithvi-v2 + LoRA attains the top accuracy across US/Canada with Sentinel-2 data.
AI.motivation: Assess how to efficiently adapt geospatial foundation models for downstream Earth observation tasks under geographic and temporal domain shift, focusing on burned-area mapping across diverse biomes in North America.
AI.method: Evaluate three state-of-the-art GFMs (Terramind, DINOv3, Prithvi-v2) using Sentinel-2 data for 3,820 wildfire events (2017–2023). Conduct spatial and temporal generalization tests across biomes. Compare three adaptation strategies: full fine-tuning, decoder-only fine-tuning, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA).
AI.result: LoRA provides the strongest cross-domain generalization while updating <1% of parameters. Prithvi-v2 with LoRA achieves the highest overall accuracy and the largest improvement compared to full fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Geospatial foundation models, when adapted via lightweight parameter-efficient methods like LoRA, offer a robust and scalable solution for large-scale burned-area mapping; code available at the linked repository.

title: Counterfactual identifiability beyond global monotonicity: non-monotone triangular structural causal models
summary: Structural causal models provide a unified semantics for interventions and counterfactuals, but most identifiability results rely on restrictive assumptions like global monotonicity, which are often violated in embodied interaction, where the same exogenous perturbation can induce opposite responses under different contact contexts. We ask what structure still suffices once global monotonicity is dropped. We introduce non-monotone triangular structural causal models (NM-TM-SCM), which retain triangular recursion but replace global monotonicity with mechanism-wise invertibility and context-independent inverse transport. We prove that these conditions are equivalent to exogenous isomorphism and imply complete counterfactual identifiability, and we give a counterexample showing that local invertibility alone is insufficient. We instantiate the theory in CausalInverter, with triangular invertible layers, orientation gates, and transport-stability regularization. On synthetic non-monotonic mechanisms, the structural bias yields systematic counterfactual gains as non-monotonicity increases. On MuJoCo Door, our model achieves perfect event-level counterfactual recovery, lowers continuous angle error relative to a Transformer baseline, and delivers substantially more stable recovery than Transformer and conditional-flow predictors. On MuJoCo Push, where non-monotonicity is weaker, the same low-data predictors remain competitive or better, consistent with a bias-variance boundary. These results identify a broader identifiable regime between globally monotone triangular models and unconstrained black-box world models.

AI.tldr: Introduce non-monotone triangular SCM (NM-TM-SCM) by replacing global monotonicity with mechanism-wise invertibility and context-independent inverse transport. Prove equivalence to exogenous isomorphism and complete counterfactual identifiability; provide a counterexample to local invertibility sufficiency. Instantiate in CausalInverter and validate on synthetic and MuJoCo tasks, achieving strong counterfactual recovery and improved stability, especially under non-monotonicity. Establish a broader identifiable regime between monotone and unconstrained models.
AI.motivation: Global monotonicity assumptions are often violated in embodied interactions, where the same exogenous perturbation can trigger opposite responses under different contexts. A weaker, pragmatically verifiable set of conditions is needed to enable identifiability of counterfactuals in realistic systems.
AI.method: Define NM-TM-SCM by retaining triangular recursion but substituting global monotonicity with mechanism-wise invertibility and context-independent inverse transport. Prove these conditions are equivalent to exogenous isomorphism and sufficient for complete counterfactual identifiability. Provide a counterexample showing local invertibility is insufficient. Implement CausalInverter with triangular invertible layers, orientation gates, and transport-stability regularization to realize the model. Evaluate on synthetic non-monotonic mechanisms and MuJoCo tasks (Door and Push).
AI.result: The NM-TM-SCM framework attains complete counterfactual identifiability under the stated weaker assumptions. Empirically, increasing non-monotonicity improves counterfactual gains on synthetic data. In MuJoCo Door, the approach achieves perfect event-level counterfactual recovery, lower angle error than a Transformer baseline, and more stable recovery than both Transformer and conditional-flow predictors. In MuJoCo Push, when non-monotonicity is weaker, low-data predictors remain competitive or superior, aligning with a bias-variance perspective. Overall, identifies a broader identifiable regime between globally monotone triangular models and unconstrained black-box models.
AI.conclusion: Weakening monotonicity to mechanism-wise invertibility with context-independent transport yields identifiability of counterfactuals for NM-TM-SCMs. This expands the regime of identifiable models beyond globally monotone SCCMs, providing practical benefits in embodied settings and guiding architecture choices (e.g., CausalInverter) to leverage triangular structure for reliable counterfactual reasoning.

title: Chaotic Contrastive Learning for Robust Texture Classification
summary: Texture classification is a pivotal task in computer vision, presenting unique challenges due to high inter-class similarity and the sensitivity of structural patterns to scale and illumination changes. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and recent Vision Transformers have set performance benchmarks, they often require extensive labeled datasets or struggle to generalize across domains due to an over-reliance on color and shape features. This paper introduces a novel framework that synergizes Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) with deterministic chaotic dynamics. We propose a chaotic contrastive pre-training strategy, where pixel-wise chaotic maps, specifically Logistic, Tent, and Sine maps, act as non-linear data augmentation techniques. These chaotic perturbations, grounded in ergodic theory, force the network to learn topologically robust features by mimicking complex environmental noise and reflectance variations. Furthermore, we introduce an attention-based feature ensemble that fuses high-level semantic representations from a supervised large backbone with low-frequency structural features from a chaos-pretrained tiny encoder. Experimental results on six texture benchmarks (FMD, UMD, KTH-TIPS2-b, DTD, GTOS, and 1200Tex) demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches and achieving promising accuracies on all the analyzed datasets.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised learning framework for texture classification using chaotic dynamics as non-linear data augmentations and a dual-branch attention ensemble to improve robustness and cross-domain generalization across texture datasets.
AI.motivation: Texture classification faces high inter-class similarity and sensitivity to scale/illumination; conventional CNNs/ViTs need large labeled data and may overfit color/shape cues, hindering generalization. The work seeks robust, domain-generalizable texture representations via SSL and chaos-based perturbations.
AI.method: Pixel-wise chaotic maps (Logistic, Tent, Sine) are used as non-linear data augmentations in a chaotic contrastive pre-training regime. The approach enforces learning topology-robust features through ergodic-chaos-inspired perturbations. An attention-based feature ensemble fuses high-level semantic features from a large supervised backbone with low-frequency structural features from a chaos-pretrained tiny encoder.
AI.result: Evaluated on six texture benchmarks (FMD, UMD, KTH-TIPS2-b, DTD, GTOS, 1200Tex); reported superior performance and promising accuracies compared to state-of-the-art methods across all datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SSL framework leveraging chaotic dynamics and a dual-branch attention ensemble improves texture classification robustness and generalization. It demonstrates the value of chaos-inspired augmentations and multi-scale feature fusion for texture tasks.

title: CARD: A Multi-Modal Automotive Dataset for Dense 3D Reconstruction in Challenging Road Topography
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 (Highlight). Project page: https://card.content.cariad.digital
summary: Autonomous driving must operate across diverse surfaces to enable safe mobility. However, most driving datasets are captured on well-paved flat roads. Moreover, recent driving datasets primarily provide sparse LiDAR ground truth for images, which is insufficient for assessing fine-grained geometry in depth estimation and completion. To address these gaps, we introduce CARD, a multi-modal driving dataset that delivers quasi-dense 3D ground truth across continuous sequences rich in speed bumps, potholes, irregular surfaces and off-road segments. Our sensor suite includes synchronized global-shutter stereo cameras, front and rear LiDARs, 6-DoF poses from LiDAR-inertial odometry, per-wheel motion traces, and full calibration. Notably, our multi-LiDAR fusion yields ~500K valid depth pixels per frame, about 6.5x more than KITTI Depth Completion and 10x more on average than other public driving datasets. The dataset spans ~110 km and 4.7 hours across Germany and Italy. In addition, CARD provides 2D bounding boxes targeting road-topography irregularities, enabling accurate benchmarking for both geometry and perception tasks. Furthermore, we establish a standardized evaluation protocol for road surface irregularities on CARD and benchmark state-of-the-art depth estimation models to provide strong baselines. The CARD dataset is hosted on https://huggingface.co/CARD-Data.

AI.tldr: CARD is a multi-modal driving dataset providing quasi-dense 3D ground truth across diverse road surfaces, enabling fine-grained depth estimation and completion benchmarks with ~500k depth pixels per frame and extensive field data.
AI.motivation: Current driving datasets largely cover flat, paved roads and offer sparse LiDAR ground truth, hindering evaluation of fine-grained geometry and road surface irregularities. CARD addresses these gaps by delivering dense multi-modal measurements over varied terrain.
AI.method: CARD collects synchronized global-shutter stereo cameras, front and rear LiDARs, 6-DoF poses from LiDAR-inertial odometry, per-wheel motion traces, and full calibration; multi-LiDAR fusion yields ~500K valid depth pixels per frame; dataset spans ~110 km and 4.7 hours across Germany and Italy; includes 2D bounding boxes for road-topography irregularities; establishes standardized evaluation protocol and benchmarks depth estimation models.
AI.result: The dataset achieves ~500K valid depth pixels per frame (about 6.5x more than KITTI Depth Completion and ~10x more than other public driving datasets), covers diverse topographies (speed bumps, potholes, irregular surfaces, off-road), and enables robust benchmarking for geometry and perception tasks with strong baselines and standardized evaluations.
AI.conclusion: CARD significantly advances driving perception and geometry benchmarking by providing quasi-dense multi-modal ground truth over diverse road surfaces, enabling precise depth estimation and completion, and offering standardized evaluation protocols and baselines.

title: Local Intrinsic Dimension Unveils Hallucinations in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models are prone to generating structural hallucinations - samples that match the statistical properties of the training data yet defy underlying structural rules, resulting in anomalies like hands with more than five fingers. Recent research studied this failure mode from several viewpoints, offering partial explanations to their occurrence, such as mode interpolation. In this work, we propose a complementary perspective that treats hallucinations as instabilities on the model-induced manifold. We begin by showing that a hallucination filter based on such instabilities matches or exceeds the performance of the recently proposed temporal one. By tracing the source of these instabilities, we identify local intrinsic dimension (LID) as their primary driver and propose Intrinsic Quenching (IQ), a direct corrective mechanism that deflates it to alleviate hallucinations. IQ consistently outperforms standard hallucination reduction baselines across a wide array of benchmarks and offers a highly promising solution for enforcing anatomical consistency in downstream medical imaging tasks.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-model hallucinations arise from instabilities on the model-induced manifold, with local intrinsic dimension (LID) as a key driver. The paper introduces Intrinsic Quenching (IQ) to deflate LID, yielding a hallucination filter that matches or surpasses temporal baselines and improves anatomical consistency in medical imaging.
AI.motivation: Address structural hallucinations in diffusion models beyond statistical similarity; leverage a manifold-instability perspective to explain and mitigate hallucinations, aiming to improve anatomical fidelity in downstream medical-imaging tasks.
AI.method: Identify instability-based hallucination signals and compare a filter based on these instabilities to a temporal baseline. Diagnose LID as the primary driver of instabilities and implement Intrinsic Quenching (IQ), a direct mechanism to deflate LID and reduce hallucinations.
AI.result: IQ consistently outperforms standard hallucination reduction baselines across diverse benchmarks, including improvements in anatomical consistency for medical imaging downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Treating hallucinations as model-induced-manifold instabilities and deflating LID with IQ provides a robust, generalizable method for reducing structural errors in diffusion models and enhancing fidelity in medical-imaging applications.

title: Discovering Sparse Counterfactual Factors via Latent Adjustment for Survey-based Community Intervention
summary: Transportation surveys are widely used to understand travel preferences and adoption barriers, yet most survey-based analyses remain descriptive or predictive and rarely provide sparse, policy-feasible intervention strategies. We study sparse counterfactual community intervention from survey responses, where the goal is to shift a target respondent group toward a desired reference group through controllable survey-variable adjustments. We formulate this task as a policy-feasible distributional alignment problem using a fixed-basis nonnegative latent representation that preserves pre/post comparability and provides a stable map from latent factors to original variables. To make latent movement actionable, target-relevant latent factors are identified through Shapley-guided attribution and transferred to controllable variables as intervention priorities. Feasible group-level adjustments are then learned by minimizing an entropy-regularized optimal-transport discrepancy between the post-intervention target distribution and the reference distribution, together with a weighted $\ell_{2,1}$ penalty that promotes shared policy-lever sparsity. Experiments on real-world transportation survey datasets show that the proposed framework produces compact and interpretable policy-feasible interventions with explicit adjustment magnitudes, improves population-level conversion, and preserves intervention sparsity. Code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/pangjunbiao/latent-group-alignment.git

AI.tldr: Sparse, policy-feasible interventions to shift survey respondents toward a reference group via latent-group alignment and sparsity-enabled OT-based reweighting.
AI.motivation: Transportation surveys are plentiful but analyses are often descriptive/predictive; there is a need for actionable, sparse intervention strategies that are feasible to implement at the community level.
AI.method: 1) Use a fixed-basis nonnegative latent representation to ensure pre/post comparability and a stable map to original variables. 2) Identify target latent factors via Shapley-guided attribution and map them to controllable variables as intervention priorities. 3) Learn feasible group-level adjustments by minimizing an entropy-regularized optimal-transport discrepancy between post-intervention target distribution and the reference distribution, with a weighted l2,1 penalty to promote shared policy-lever sparsity.
AI.result: On real-world transportation survey datasets, the framework yields compact, interpretable, policy-feasible interventions with explicit adjustment magnitudes, improves population-level conversion, and maintains intervention sparsity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed latent-group-alignment framework provides actionable, sparse, and interpretable interventions to move populations toward a desired reference group, supported by data-driven attribution and transport-based distribution alignment; code and data are publicly available.

title: Prompt-Anchored Vision-Text Distillation for Lifelong Person Re-identification
summary: Lifelong person re-identification (LReID) aims to train a generalizable model with sequentially collected data. However, such models often suffer from semantic drift, limited adaptability, and catastrophic forgetting as new domains emerge. Existing exemplar-free approaches largely rely on visual-only distillation or parameter regularization, while overlooking the potential of auxiliary modalities, such as text, to preserve semantic stability and enable incremental plasticity. We observe that the frozen text encoder in pretrained vision-language models can serve as a stable semantic anchor across domains. To decouple the roles of vision and text, we propose Prompt-Anchored vision-text Distillation (PAD), an asymmetric vision-text framework for semantic alignment and cross-domain generalization. On the textual side, we distill prompts to preserve vision-text alignment under a fixed semantic space, acting as a global semantic reference rather than a dominant learning signal. On the visual side, an EMA-based teacher with an adaptive prompt pool enables domain-wise adaptation by allocating new slots while freezing past ones. Extensive experiments show that PAD substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods across seen and unseen domains, achieving a strong balance between stability and plasticity. Project page is available at https://github.com/zu-zi/PAD.

AI.tldr: PAD introduces an asymmetric vision-text distillation for lifelong ReID by anchoring semantics with a frozen text encoder and an adaptive, EMA-based visual teacher with a prompt pool, achieving strong cross-domain generalization and stability-plasticity.
AI.motivation: Lifelong person re-identification (LReID) faces semantic drift, limited adaptability, and catastrophic forgetting as new domains arrive. Exemplar-free methods rely on vision-only distillation or regularization and may neglect a stable semantic anchor. Pretrained vision-language models offer a stable text embedding space that can serve as semantic anchor across domains.
AI.method: PAD decouples vision and text. Text side distills prompts to preserve vision–text alignment within a fixed semantic space, acting as a global semantic reference. Visual side uses an EMA-based teacher with an adaptive prompt pool to allocate new slots for domain-specific adaptation while freezing past ones, enabling gradual plasticity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show PAD substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods on both seen and unseen domains, achieving a strong balance between stability and plasticity in LReID.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework validates the effectiveness of a frozen text encoder as a stable semantic anchor and a modular, prompt-based adaptation strategy for lifelong domain generalization in re-identification, with code available at the project page.

title: HeterSEED: Semantics-Structure Decoupling for Heterogeneous Graph Learning under Heterophily
summary: Many real-world heterogeneous graphs exhibit pronounced heterophily, where connected nodes often have dissimilar labels or play different semantic roles. In such settings, standard heterogeneous graph neural networks that aggregate messages along metapaths or meta-relations primarily based on feature similarity can propagate misleading information, since feature similarity may be misaligned with underlying relational semantics. In this paper, we propose HeterSEED, a semantics-structure decoupling framework for heterogeneous graph learning under heterophily. HeterSEED decouples representation learning into a heterogeneous semantic channel that captures type- and relation-aware local semantics and a structure-aware heterophily channel that separates homophilic and heterophilic neighborhoods via pseudo-label-guided partitioning and aggregates them using metapath-based structural weights. A node-level adaptive fusion mechanism then combines the two channels to produce context-dependent node representations. Theoretically, we establish that, on heterogeneous graphs under heterophily, HeterSEED is strictly more expressive than standard heterogeneous graph neural networks that rely primarily on feature similarity and provably reduces the prediction bias introduced by heterophilic neighbors. Experiments on five real-world heterogeneous graphs, including two large-scale networks at the million-node and hundred-million-edge scale, demonstrate that HeterSEED consistently outperforms representative heterogeneous graph neural networks and recent heterophily-aware baselines, especially in strongly heterophilic regimes.

AI.tldr: Semantics-structure decoupling for heterophilic heterogeneous graphs improves expressiveness and reduces bias, with strong empirical gains on large-scale real networks.
AI.motivation: Real-world heterogeneous graphs often exhibit heterophily, where feature similarity-based message passing can propagate misleading information. Standard HGNNs underperform because they rely heavily on feature similarity rather than semantics and structure.
AI.method: HeterSEED introduces a two-channel architecture: (1) a heterogeneous semantic channel that captures type- and relation-aware local semantics; (2) a structure-aware heterophily channel that partitions neighbors into homophilic/heterophilic groups via pseudo-label-guided partitioning and aggregates them with metapath-based structural weights. A node-level adaptive fusion combines the two channels into context-dependent representations.
AI.result: Theoretically, HeterSEED is strictly more expressive than standard HGNNs relying on feature similarity and reduces bias from heterophilic neighbors. Empirically, it outperforms representative HGNNs and heterophily-aware baselines on five real-world heterogeneous graphs, including two large-scale networks (million-node and hundred-million-edge scales), with pronounced gains in strongly heterophilic regimes.
AI.conclusion: HeterSEED provides a principled, scalable solution for learning on heterophilic heterogeneous graphs by decoupling semantics and structure and adaptively fusing them, yielding both theoretical and empirical advantages.

title: Computer-Aided Design Generation by Cascaded Discrete Diffusion Model
summary: Recent deep learning approaches seek to automate CAD creation by representing a model as a sequence of discrete commands and parameters, and then generating them using autoregressive models or continuous diffusion operating in Euclidean embedding space. However, continuous diffusion perturbs representations in a continuous Euclidean domain that does not reflect the inherently discrete and heterogeneous nature of CAD tokens, often producing perturbed representations that map to semantically invalid symbols. To overcome this limitation, we propose a cascaded discrete diffusion framework for CAD generation, which consists of a command diffusion for generating CAD commands and a parameter diffusion conditioned on CAD commands. Unlike isotropic Gaussian perturbation, the forward process of our approach operates directly over categorical token distributions using delicate transition matrices. For commands, we adopt an absorbing-state transition matrix that progressively corrupts tokens to a designated symbol; for parameters, we introduce specific transition matrices tailored to heterogeneous attributes: a Gaussian kernel for coordinate continuity, a scale-invariant kernel for dimensional values, and a prior-preserving kernel for boolean attributes. The reverse process is achieved by two denoising networks: a Transformer-based encoder for command recovery, and a parameter network with extra local self-attention for command-level interaction and cross-attention for conditional injection. Experiments on the DeepCAD dataset show that the proposed approach surpasses existing autoregressive and continuous diffusion models on unconditional generation metrics, while qualitative results validate effective controllability in conditional generation tasks. Source codes will be released.

AI.tldr: Proposes cascaded discrete diffusion for CAD generation: command diffusion on categorical tokens with absorbing-state transitions and parameter diffusion with tailored kernels; two denoisers (Transformer encoder for commands and a parameter network with local/self-attention and cross-attention); outperforms autoregressive and continuous diffusion on DeepCAD for unconditional generation and enables controllable conditional generation; source code to be released.
AI.motivation: Continuous diffusion in Euclidean space inadequately handles the discrete, heterogeneous CAD tokens, often yielding semantically invalid symbols; a discrete, token-aware diffusion framework is needed to better model CAD constructs.
AI.method: Forward processes operate over categorical token distributions with specialized transition matrices: absorbing-state for commands; kernels tailored to attributes for parameters (Gaussian for coordinates, scale-invariant for dimensions, prior-preserving for booleans). Reverse processes use two denoisers: a Transformer-based encoder for command recovery and a parameter network with local self-attention and cross-attention to condition on commands.
AI.result: On the DeepCAD dataset, the method surpasses autoregressive and continuous diffusion models on unconditional generation metrics; qualitative results demonstrate effective controllability in conditional generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The cascaded discrete diffusion framework effectively models CAD generation, leveraging token-level Discrete diffusion with conditional control; source code will be released.

title: A Queueing-Theoretic Framework for Stability Analysis of LLM Inference with KV Cache Memory Constraints
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) has created significant challenges for efficient inference at scale. Unlike traditional workloads, LLM inference is constrained by both computation and the memory overhead of key-value (KV) caching, which accelerates decoding but quickly exhausts GPU memory. In this paper, we introduce the first queueing-theoretic framework that explicitly incorporates both computation and GPU memory constraints into the analysis of LLM inference. Based on this framework, we derive rigorous stability and instability conditions that determine whether an LLM inference service can sustain incoming demand without unbounded queue growth. This result offers a powerful tool for system deployment, potentially addressing the core challenge of GPU provisioning. By combining an estimated request arrival rate with our derived stable service rate, operators can calculate the necessary cluster size to avoid both costly over-purchasing and performance-violating under-provisioning. We further validate our theoretical predictions through extensive experiments in real GPU production environments. Our results show that the predicted stability conditions are highly accurate, with deviations typically within 10%.

AI.tldr: A queueing-theoretic framework that integrates computation and GPU memory constraints for LLM inference, deriving stability conditions to guide GPU provisioning; validated in production with ~10% accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLM inference is bottlenecked not only by compute but also by GPU memory due to KV caching. Traditional analyses ignore memory constraints, hindering reliable provisioning and scheduling for large-scale inference.
AI.method: Develop a queueing-theoretic model that explicitly includes computation and GPU memory limits (KV caching). Derive stability/instability conditions for the inference service, linking arrival rate to a derived stable service rate. Use estimated arrival rate with the stable service rate to compute required cluster size. Validate predictions with experiments in real GPU production environments.
AI.result: Derived stability conditions align with observed performance, with deviations typically within 10%. The framework provides accurate provisioning guidance for avoiding over- or under-provisioning.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a principled tool for deploying LLM inference services, enabling robust GPU provisioning decisions and scalable deployment by predicting stability and informing cluster sizing.

title: Few-Shot Learning Pipeline for Monkeypox Skin Disease Classification Using CNN Feature Extractors
summary: Despite the strong performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in disease classification, their effectiveness often depends on access to large annotated datasets, which is an impractical requirement for emerging or rare conditions such as Monkeypox. To overcome this limitation, we propose a few-shot learning (FSL) framework that employs SimpleShot, a lightweight, non-parametric, inductive classifier, for Monkeypox and pox-like skin disease recognition from limited labeled examples. The proposed pipeline passes the skin lesion images through a frozen, pretrained CNN backbone to obtain feature embeddings, which are then classified via SimpleShot using nearest-centroid comparisons in a normalized embedding space. We systematically benchmark six widely used CNN backbones as feature extractors under consistent experimental settings, enabling fair comparison. Experiments on three publicly available datasets (MSLD v1.0, MSID, and MSLD v2.0) are conducted across 2-way, 4-way, and 6-way tasks with 1-shot, 5-shot, and 10-shot configurations. Among all models, MobileNetV2_100 consistently achieves the highest accuracy. In addition, we present a cross-dataset evaluation for Monkeypox classification, revealing that binary Mpox-vs-Others transfer remains comparatively stable while multi-class performance degrades significantly under domain shift. Together, these results demonstrate the practical utility of combining inductive FSL methods with lightweight CNN backbones and highlight the importance of domain robustness for reliable real-world clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: Few-shot Monkeypox classification using SimpleShot with frozen CNN backbones; MobileNetV2_100 performs best; cross-dataset domain shift hurts multiclass accuracy more than binary transfer; emphasizes lightweight models for practical deployment.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity in emerging/rare skin diseases (e.g., Monkeypox) makes large labeled datasets impractical. A lightweight, non-parametric approach aims to enable reliable recognition from few examples.
AI.method: Frozen pretrained CNN backbones extract feature embeddings; SimpleShot performs nearest-centroid classification in a normalized embedding space. Six backbones are benchmarked on MSLD v1.0, MSID, and MSLD v2.0 across 2/4/6-way tasks and 1/5/10-shot settings.
AI.result: MobileNetV2_100 consistently yields the highest accuracy among backbones. Cross-dataset evaluation shows Mpox-vs-Others transfer remains comparatively stable, while multiclass performance degrades significantly under domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Inductive FSL with lightweight backbones is practically useful for scarce-data clinical applications; domain robustness is crucial for reliable real-world deployment and requires further improvement.

title: Automated Formal Proofs of Combinatorial Identities via Wilf-Zeilberger Guidance and LLMs
summary: Automating formal proofs of combinatorial identities is challenging for LLM-based provers, as long-horizon proof planning is required and unconstrained search quickly explodes. Symbolic methods such as the Wilf-Zeilberger (WZ) method can achieve a mechanized proof of combinatorial identities by constructing special auxiliary functions and demonstrating that they satisfy specific recurrence relations. We propose WZ-LLM, a neuro-symbolic framework that turns WZ proof plans into executable proof sketches in Lean 4 and uses an LLM-based prover to discharge the resulting machine-checkable subgoals. We also train a dedicated WZ-Prover via a Lean-kernel-verified bootstrapping loop with expert-verified iteration, followed by DAPO-based refinement. Experiments show that WZ-LLM achieves a 34% proof success rate on LCI-Test (100 classic combinatorial identities), outperforming strong baselines such as DeepSeek-V3 and Goedel-Prover-V2, and delivering consistent gains on CombiBench and PutnamBench-Comb. These results indicate that our framework provides two complementary strengths: improved direct proving for identities beyond the scope of WZ, and substantially higher end-to-end success when WZ sketches guide a specialized prover.

AI.tldr: WZ-LLM blends Wilf-Zeilberger symbolic proof planning with an LLM-based prover in Lean 4, using a bootstrapped WZ-Prover and DAPO refinement. It yields 34% success on 100 classic identities (LCI-Test), outperforming baselines and improving end-to-end success when WZ sketches guide proving.
AI.motivation: Automating formal proofs of combinatorial identities is difficult due to long-horizon planning and search explosion. Symbolic methods like Wilf-Zeilberger can mechanize such proofs, but require integrating with ML-based provers to handle subgoals efficiently.
AI.method: Turn WZ proof plans into executable Lean 4 proof sketches; employ an LLM-based prover to discharge machine-checkable subgoals; train a dedicated WZ-Prover via a Lean-kernel-verified bootstrapping loop with expert-verified iterations, followed by DAPO-based refinement; evaluate on LCI-Test (100 identities), CombiBench, PutnamBench-Comb, comparing against baselines.
AI.result: WZ-LLM achieves 34% proof success on LCI-Test; outperforms DeepSeek-V3 and Goedel-Prover-V2; gains consistent improvements on CombiBench and PutnamBench-Comb.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides complementary strengths: improved direct proving for identities beyond WZ and substantially higher end-to-end success when WZ sketches guide a specialized prover, indicating a promising neuro-symbolic approach for formal combinatorial proof automation.

title: When Relations Break: Analyzing Relation Hallucination in Vision-Language Model Under Rotation and Noise
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong multimodal performance but remain prone to relation hallucination, which requires accurate reasoning over inter-object interactions. We study the impact of visual perturbations, specifically rotation and noise, and show that even mild distortions significantly degrade relational reasoning across models and datasets. We further evaluate prompt-based augmentation and preprocessing strategies (orientation correction and denoising), finding that while they offer partial improvements, they do not fully resolve hallucinations. Our results reveal a gap between perceptual robustness and relational understanding, highlighting the need for more robust, geometry-aware VLMs.

AI.tldr: VLMs' relational reasoning is fragile under mild visual perturbations; augmentation helps but doesn't fully fix hallucinations, signaling a need for geometry-aware robustness.
AI.motivation: To understand how perceptual perturbations impact relational reasoning in vision-language models and to assess simple mitigation strategies, thereby highlighting gaps between perceptual robustness and relational understanding.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of VLMs on relational tasks under visual perturbations (rotation and noise); assessment of prompt-based augmentation and preprocessing (orientation correction, denoising) as mitigation strategies across multiple models/datasets.
AI.result: Mild distortions significantly degrade relational reasoning across models and datasets; prompt-based augmentation and preprocessing yield partial improvements but do not fully resolve hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: There is a gap between perceptual robustness and relational understanding; robust, geometry-aware VLMs are needed to reduce relation hallucinations.

title: Geometry-Aware Neural Optimizer for Shape Optimization and Inversion
summary: Geometry is central to PDE-governed systems, motivating shape optimization and inversion. Classical pipelines conduct costly forward simulation with geometry processing, requiring substantial expert effort. Neural surrogates accelerate forward analysis but do not close the loop because gradients from objectives to geometry are often unavailable. Existing differentiable methods either rely on restrictive parameterizations or unstable latent optimization driven by scalar objectives, limiting interpretability and part-wise control. To address these challenges, we propose Geometry-Aware Neural Optimizer (GANO), an end-to-end differentiable framework that unifies geometry representation, field-level prediction, and automated optimization/inversion in a single latent-space loop. GANO encodes shapes with an auto-decoder and stabilizes latent updates via a denoising mechanism, and a geometry-injected surrogate provides a reliable gradient pathway for geometry updates. Moreover, GANO supports part-wise control through null-space projection and uses remeshing-free projection to accelerate geometry processing. We further prove that denoising induces an implicit Jacobian regularization that reduces decoder sensitivity, yielding controlled deformations. Experiments on three benchmarks spanning 2D Helmholtz, 2D airfoil, and 3D vehicles show state-of-the-art accuracy and stable, controllable updates, achieving up to +55.9% lift-to-drag improvement for airfoils and ~7% drag reduction for vehicles.

AI.tldr: GANO is a differentiable, geometry-aware neural optimizer for PDE-based shape optimization and inversion that unifies geometry encoding, field prediction, and optimization within a single latent-space loop; it stabilizes updates via denoising and provides geometry-conditioned gradients, enabling part-wise control and remeshing-free processing.
AI.motivation: Geometry and PDE-governed systems require optimization/inversion over shapes, but traditional forward surrogates and differentiable pipelines struggle with gradient flow to geometry, interpretability, and fine-grained control. A unified, end-to-end differentiable framework overcomes these gaps.
AI.method: GANO uses an auto-decoder to encode shapes into a latent space, a denoising mechanism to stabilize latent updates, and a geometry-injected surrogate to supply reliable gradients for geometry updates. It supports part-wise control via null-space projection and employs remeshing-free projection to speed geometry processing, with implicit Jacobian regularization from denoising.
AI.result: On three benchmarks (2D Helmholtz, 2D airfoil, 3D vehicles), GANO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and stable, controllable updates, including up to 55.9% lift-to-drag improvement for airfoils and about 7% drag reduction for vehicles.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an end-to-end differentiable, geometry-aware loop for PDE-based design/inversion that enhances gradient flow, interpretability, and controllability, backed by a denoising-induced implicit Jacobian regularization.

title: Library learning with e-graphs on jazz harmony
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, 2 listings, 1 table, no conference
summary: Humans can acquire a highly structured intuitive understanding of musical patterns, yet these patterns often require multiple iterations of reflection and re-listening to internalize fully. To capture such an internalization process, we present a computational model for the learning of jazz harmonic patterns based on library learning. Given a corpus of harmonic progressions, our model searches over a space of programs composed of primitive harmonic relations in order to discover concise generative explanations of the corpus. The model first enumerates possible programs for each piece, and then jointly learns a library of harmonic patterns and refactored programs. To efficiently navigate the vast joint space of programs and libraries, we integrate deductive parsing with library learning on e-graphs. We explore how well our model captures aspects of human musical pattern learning by evaluating the intuitiveness of both programs and libraries, as well as similarities to human-written harmonic derivations.

AI.tldr: A deductive-programming and library-learning approach on e-graphs learns concise generative explanations of jazz harmony by jointly inferring a library of patterns and refactored programs from a corpus.
AI.motivation: Humans acquire structured musical patterns through iterative listening and reflection; the work aims to model this internalization process computationally for jazz harmonic progressions.
AI.method: Enumerate candidate programs per piece using primitive harmonic relations; jointly learn a library of patterns and refactor programs; navigate the large joint space via deductive parsing combined with library learning on e-graphs.
AI.result: Qualitative evaluation focusing on the intuitiveness of inferred programs and libraries and their similarity to human-written harmonic derivations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a cognitive-like mechanism for learning musical structure, yielding interpretable, reusable harmonic patterns and explanations, and providing a framework to study internalization of musical patterns.

title: Direct Product Flow Matching: Decoupling Radial and Angular Dynamics for Few-Shot Adaptation
summary: Recent flow matching (FM) methods improve the few-shot adaptation of vision-language models, by modeling cross-modal alignment as a continuous multi-step flow. In this paper, we argue that existing FM methods are inherently constrained by incompatible geometric priors on pre-trained cross-modal features, resulting in suboptimal adaptation performance. We first analyze these methods from a polar decomposition perspective (i.e., radial and angular sub-manifolds). Under this new geometric view, we identify three overlooked limitations in them: 1) Angular dynamics distortion: The radial-angular coupling induces non-uniform speed on the angular sub-manifold, leading to regression training difficulty and extra truncation errors. 2) Radial dynamics neglect: Feature normalization discards modality confidence, failing to distinguish out-of-distribution and in-distribution data, and abandoning crucial radial dynamics. 3) Context-agnostic unconditional flow: Dataset-specific information loss during pre-trained cross-modal feature extraction remains unrecovered. To resolve these issues, we propose warped product flow matching (WP-FM), a unified Riemannian framework that reformulates alignment on a warped product manifold. Within this framework, we derive direct product flow matching (DP-FM) by introducing a constant-warping metric, which yields a decoupled cylindrical manifold (i.e., direct product manifold). DP-FM enables independent radial evolution and constant-speed angular geodesic transport, effectively eliminating angular dynamics distortion while preserving radial consistency. Meanwhile, we incorporate classifier-free guidance by conditioning the flow on the pre-trained VLMs' hidden states to inject missing dataset-specific information. Extensive results across 11 benchmarks have demonstrated that DP-FM achieves a new state-of-the-art for multi-step few-shot adaptation.

AI.tldr: Warped product flow matching (WP-FM) reframes cross-modal alignment on a warped product manifold, yielding a decoupled direct product flow (DP-FM) with independent radial and angular dynamics and constant angular speed, plus classifier-free guidance to inject dataset-specific information; achieves state-of-the-art in multi-step few-shot adaptation.
AI.motivation: Existing flow matching (FM) methods are limited by incompatible geometric priors on pre-trained cross-modal features, causing angular distortion, radial neglect, and loss of context-specific information during pre-training.
AI.method: Recast alignment on a warped product manifold and derive DP-FM with a constant-warping metric, yielding a decoupled cylindrical direct product manifold. Implement classifier-free guidance by conditioning the flow on the VLMs’ hidden states to recover dataset-specific information.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 11 benchmarks show DP-FM achieves a new state-of-the-art for multi-step few-shot adaptation.
AI.conclusion: A geometric reformulation to a warped-product (and directly a product) manifold enables decoupled radial and angular flows with stable, constant-speed angular transport, and the injected dataset-specific cues via hidden states further boost performance; the approach demonstrates strong empirical gains.

title: Data-dependent Exploration for Online Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
summary: Online reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a promising paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) by continuously collecting new preference feedback during training. A foundational challenge in this setting is exploration, which requires algorithms that enable the LLMs to generate informative comparisons that improve sample-efficiency in online RLHF. Existing exploration strategies often derive bonuses via on-policy expectations, which are difficult to estimate reliably from the limited historical preference data available during training; as a result, the policy can prematurely down-weight under-explored regions that may contain high-value behaviors. In this paper, we propose data-dependent exploration for preference optimization (DEPO), a simple and scalable method that leverages historical data to construct an extra uncertainty bonus for high-uncertainty regions, encouraging exploration toward potentially high-value data. Theoretically, we provide a data-dependent regret bound for the proposed algorithm, showing that it adapts to the hardness of the learning task itself and can be tighter than worst-case bounds in practice. Empirically, the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines across benchmarks, demonstrating improved sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: DEPO introduces data-dependent exploration for preference optimization in online RLHF by using historical data to build an uncertainty bonus that drives exploration toward under-explored regions with potential high value, yielding data-adaptive regret bounds and improved sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Exploration is hard in online RLHF due to limited and biased historical preference data; on-policy bonuses are unreliable to estimate, causing under-exploration of potentially high-value regions.
AI.method: DEPO constructs a data-dependent uncertainty bonus based on historical data to encourage exploration toward high-uncertainty regions when training with preference data, improving sample efficiency in online RLHF.
AI.result: Theoretical: provides a data-dependent regret bound that adapts to task hardness and can outperform worst-case bounds. Empirical: demonstrates improved sample efficiency and strong performance against baselines on benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DEPO is a simple, scalable approach that leverages historical data to improve exploration in online RLHF, yielding better data efficiency and performance.

title: ScriptHOI: Learning Scripted State Transitions for Open-Vocabulary Human-Object Interaction Detection
summary: Open-vocabulary human-object interaction (HOI) detection requires recognizing interaction phrases that may not appear as annotated categories during training. Recent vision-language HOI detectors improve semantic transfer by matching human-object features with text embeddings, but their predictions are often dominated by object affordance and phrase-level co-occurrence. As a result, a model may predict \textit{cut cake} from the presence of a knife and a cake without verifying whether the hand, tool, target, contact pattern, and object state jointly support the action. We propose \textbf{ScriptHOI}, a structured framework that represents each interaction phrase as a soft scripted state transition. Rather than treating a phrase as a single class token, ScriptHOI decomposes it into body-role, contact, geometry, affordance, motion, and object-state slots. A visual state tokenizer parses each detected human-object pair into corresponding state tokens, and a slot-wise matcher estimates both script coverage and script conflict. These two quantities calibrate HOI logits, expose missing visual evidence, and provide training constraints for incomplete annotations. To avoid suppressing valid but unannotated interactions, we further introduce interval partial-label learning, which constrains unannotated candidates with script-derived lower and upper probability bounds instead of assigning closed-world negatives. A counterfactual script contrast loss swaps individual script slots to discourage object-only shortcuts. Experiments on HICO-DET, V-COCO, and open-vocabulary HOI splits show that ScriptHOI improves rare and unseen interaction recognition while substantially reducing affordance-conflict false positives.

AI.tldr: ScriptHOI presents a structured, slot-based representation of open-vocabulary HOI phrases as soft scripted state transitions, with a visual state tokenizer and slot-wise matcher to calibrate logits, enable partial-label learning, and reduce affordance-conflict wrong positives.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary HOI detection requires recognizing interactions not seen during training; existing methods often rely on object affordances and co-occurrence cues and may fail to verify whether the joint body, tool, target, contact patterns, and object states support the action.
AI.method: Represent each interaction as soft scripts decomposed into body-role, contact, geometry, affordance, motion, and object-state slots. Use a visual state tokenizer to parse detected human–object pairs into state tokens. Employ a slot-wise matcher to estimate script coverage and script conflict, calibrating HOI logits. Introduce interval partial-label learning to bound probabilities for unannotated candidates. Apply a counterfactual script contrast loss by swapping individual script slots to discourage object-only shortcuts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on HICO-DET, V-COCO, and open-vocabulary HOI splits shows improved recognition of rare and unseen interactions and a substantial reduction in affordance-conflict false positives.
AI.conclusion: ScriptHOI provides a structured, interpretable framework for open-vocabulary HOI by decomposing actions into slots, enabling calibrated predictions, robust learning under incomplete annotations, and reducing reliance on object affordances.

title: Height-Guided Projection Reparameterization for Camera-LiDAR Occupancy
summary: 3D occupancy prediction aims to infer dense, voxel-wise scene semantics from sensor observations, where the 2D-to-3D view transformation serves as a crucial step in bridging image features and volumetric representations. Most previous methods rely on a fixed projection space, where 3D reference points are uniformly sampled along pillars. However, such sampling struggles to capture the sparsity and height variations of real-world scenes, leading to ambiguous correspondences and unreliable feature aggregation. To address these challenges, we propose HiPR, a camera-LiDAR occupancy framework with Height-Guided Projection Reparameterization. HiPR first encodes LiDAR into a BEV height map to capture the maximum height of the point cloud. HiPR then adjusts the sampling range of each pillar using the height prior, enabling adaptive reparameterization of the projection space. As a result, the projected points are redistributed into geometrically meaningful regions rather than fixed ranges. Meanwhile, we mask out the invalid parts of the height map to avoid misleading the feature aggregation. In addition, to alleviate the training instability caused by noisy LiDAR-derived heights, we introduce a training-time Progressive Height Conditioning strategy, which gradually transitions the conditioning signal from ground-truth heights to LiDAR heights. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HiPR consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods while maintaining real-time inference. The code and pretrained models can be found at https://github.com/Rayn-Wu/HiPR.

AI.tldr: HiPR proposes a height-guided projection reparameterization for 3D occupancy prediction that adaptively samples the projection space using LiDAR-derived height priors and progressive conditioning, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Fixed 2D-to-3D projection spaces (uniform pillar sampling) fail to capture real-world scene sparsity and height variation, leading to ambiguous correspondences and suboptimal feature aggregation in occupancy prediction.
AI.method: 1) Encode LiDAR into a BEV height map capturing maximum height per cell. 2) Use the height prior to adaptively adjust the sampling range of each projection pillar, reparameterizing the projection space so points are redistributed into geometrically meaningful regions. 3) Mask invalid height map regions to prevent misleading aggregation. 4) Employ Progressive Height Conditioning during training to gradually replace ground-truth heights with LiDAR-derived heights, stabilizing training.
AI.result: Experimental results show HiPR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on 3D occupancy tasks while maintaining real-time inference speed.
AI.conclusion: Height-guided projection reparameterization effectively addresses limitations of fixed projection spaces in 2D-to-3D occupancy pipelines, improving accuracy and robustness, with practical efficiency and code availability.

title: Quadrature-TreeSHAP: Depth-Independent TreeSHAP and Shapley Interactions
summary:
Shapley values are a standard tool for explaining predictions of tree ensembles, with Path-Dependent SHAP being the most widely used variant. Despite substantial progress, existing methods still exhibit trade-offs between depth-dependent runtime, numerical stability, and support for higher-order interactions. To address these challenges, we introduce Quadrature-TreeSHAP, a quadrature-based reformulation of Path-Dependent TreeSHAP that is numerically stable, naturally extends to any-order Shapley interaction values and is practically insensitive to tree depth. Our implementation supports both CPU and GPU and is integrated into XGBoost.
  Our method is based on a weighted-Banzhaf interaction polynomial, which expresses Banzhaf interaction values as expectations under a feature participation probability $p$. Shapley values and any-order interaction values are then recovered by integrating these polynomials over $p$ from 0 to 1. We evaluate these integrals using Gauss-Legendre quadrature, and show that, in practice, only 8 fixed quadrature points are sufficient to reach machine precision. In fact, Quadrature-TreeSHAP with 8 fixed points achieves greater numerical stability than TreeSHAP. This fixed-point formulation removes depth dependence from the inner computation and enables efficient SIMD execution.
  We confirm these advantages empirically. On 12 XGBoost benchmarks, Quadrature-TreeSHAP computes Shapley values 1.06x-10.59x faster than TreeSHAP on CPU and 1.84x-6.95x faster than GPUTreeSHAP on GPU. Shapley pairwise interactions are 3.80x-58.11x faster on CPU, with higher-order interactions achieving speedups of up to 1200x compared to TreeSHAP-IQ.

AI.tldr: Quadrature-TreeSHAP reframes Path-Dependent TreeSHAP via a quadrature-based, depth-insensitive, numerically stable method using a weighted-Banzhaf interaction polynomial; supports any-order Shapley interactions; delivers substantial CPU/GPU speedups and integrates into XGBoost.
AI.motivation: Overcome depth-dependent runtime, numerical instability, and limited support for higher-order interactions in existing Path-Dependent SHAP methods for tree ensembles.
AI.method: Represent Banzhaf interaction values as expectations over a feature participation probability p using a weighted-Banzhaf polynomial; recover Shapley values and interactions by integrating over p from 0 to 1; evaluate integrals with Gauss-Legendre quadrature using a fixed set of 8 points; fixed-point quadrature eliminates depth dependence and enables SIMD execution; implement CPU/GPU version integrated into XGBoost.
AI.result: Using 8 fixed quadrature points yields machine-precision results; Quadrature-TreeSHAP is more numerically stable than TreeSHAP; achieves 1.06x–10.59x CPU speedups and 1.84x–6.95x GPU speedups against TreeSHAP/GPUTreeSHAP on 12 XGBoost benchmarks; Shapley pairwise interactions 3.80x–58.11x faster on CPU; higher-order interactions up to 1200x faster than TreeSHAP-IQ.
AI.conclusion: Quadrature-TreeSHAP delivers a stable, depth-agnostic, scalable framework for Shapley values and interactions in tree ensembles, with practical CPU/GPU efficiency and integration into XGBoost.

title: Exact Dual Geometry of SOC-ICNN Value Functions
summary: Input Convex Neural Networks (ICNNs) are commonly used in a two-stage manner: one first trains a convex network and then minimizes it over its input in a downstream inference problem. Recent second-order-cone ICNNs (SOC-ICNNs) enrich ReLU-based ICNNs with quadratic and conic modules and admit an exact representation as value functions of second-order cone programs (SOCPs). This value-function structure enables an explicit convex-analytic treatment of SOC-ICNN inference. In this paper, we study the exact first-order and local second-order geometry of SOC-ICNNs from the dual viewpoint.
