We show that supporting slopes, subdifferentials, directional derivatives, and local Hessians can be recovered directly from optimal dual variables. These results provide the geometric primitives for white-box SOC-ICNN inference, going beyond black-box automatic differentiation. Numerical experiments validate the exact multiplier readout, the local Hessian formula, and the set-valued behavior at structurally degenerate inputs. We also provide a step-by-step tutorial showing how the readout mechanism instantiates a complete white-box inference loop. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SOC-ICNN-Theory-BEFC/.

AI.tldr: SOC-ICNNs admit an exact SOCP representation; dual variables enable explicit white-box first- and second-order inference.
AI.motivation: Enable transparent, convex-analytic inference for SOC-ICNNs by deriving their geometry from the dual optimization problem.
AI.method: Derive how optimal dual variables recover slopes, subdifferentials, directional derivatives, and local Hessians; develop a dual-based readout mechanism; provide a step-by-step tutorial; validate via numerical experiments and degenerate-input analysis; release code.
AI.result: Exact multiplier readout, explicit local Hessian formula, and correct set-valued behavior at structurally degenerate inputs; demonstration of a complete white-box inference loop.
AI.conclusion: Provides geometric primitives and a practical workflow for white-box SOC-ICNN inference, uniting theory and experiments and enabling transparent decision-making.

title: FlowDIS: Language-Guided Dichotomous Image Segmentation with Flow Matching
summary: Accurate image segmentation is essential for modern computer vision applications such as image editing, autonomous driving, and medical image analysis. In recent years, Dichotomous Image Segmentation (DIS) has become a standard task for training and evaluating highly accurate segmentation models. Existing DIS approaches often fail to preserve fine-grained details or fully capture the semantic structure of the foreground. To address these challenges, we present FlowDIS, a novel dichotomous image segmentation method built on the flow matching framework, which learns a time-dependent vector field to transport the image distribution to the corresponding mask distribution, optionally conditioned on a text prompt. Moreover, with our Position-Aware Instance Pairing (PAIP) training strategy, FlowDIS offers strong controllability through text prompts, enabling precise, pixel-level object segmentation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches both with and without language guidance. Compared with the best prior DIS method, FlowDIS achieves a 5.5% higher $F_β^ω$ measure and 43% lower MAE ($\mathcal{M}$) on the DIS-TE test set. The code is available at: https://github.com/Picsart-AI-Research/FlowDIS

AI.tldr: FlowDIS uses flow matching to cast dichotomous image segmentation as transporting image to mask distributions with optional text conditioning, aided by PAIP training for precise, pixel-level control, achieving state-of-the-art DIS performance.
AI.motivation: DIS is central for high-quality segmentation, but existing DIS methods miss fine details and semantic structure; there is a need for controllable, language-guided segmentation.
AI.method: Proposes FlowDIS, a flow-matching framework learning a time-dependent vector field to morph the image distribution into the binary mask distribution; optionally conditioned on text prompts; introduces Position-Aware Instance Pairing (PAIP) training to improve alignment and controllability.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art DIS methods both with and without language guidance; on DIS-TE, FlowDIS yields 5.5% higher Fβ^ω and 43% lower MAE than the best prior method; code released.
AI.conclusion: FlowDIS provides accurate, controllable dichotomous segmentation with language-guided capabilities and strong empirical gains, contributing a new flow-based paradigm for DIS.

title: Gradient Scaling Effects in Adaptive Spectral PINNs for Stiff Nonlinear ODEs
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 1 table. This work appeared at the ICLR 2026 AI&PDE Workshop on OpenReview
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) often struggle to train reliably on stiff and oscillatory dynamical systems due to poor optimization conditioning. While prior work has emphasized representational remedies such as spectral parameterizations, the optimization implications of initial-condition (IC) embeddings in adaptive spectral PINNs have not been well characterized. In this work, we show that the choice of IC gating function induces explicit time-dependent gradient scaling, which interacts with spectral representations during training. Using a nonlinear stiff spring-pendulum ODE as a controlled benchmark, we compare exponential and linear IC gates in combination with fixed and adaptive Fourier spectral trunks. We observe stiffness-dependent changes in relative dominance for adaptive PINNs: at moderate stiffness ($k=20$), exponential gating often yields lower error but exhibits heterogeneous behavior across random seeds, whereas at higher stiffness ($k=60$), linear gating becomes preferable, with additional reversals observed at larger $k$. These trends hold for both relative $L^2$ error and maximum pointwise error and are confirmed by paired Wilcoxon signed-rank tests with Holm correction. Overall, our results demonstrate that IC embeddings are not a neutral design choice in PINNs: the induced gradient scaling materially shapes optimization conditioning in stiff regimes, with distinct sensitivity patterns in baseline and adaptive spectral models.

AI.tldr: IC embeddings in adaptive spectral PINNs induce time-dependent gradient scaling that affects optimization conditioning in stiff ODEs; exponential vs linear gates have stiffness-dependent performance, with adaptive spectra showing regime-specific dominance and significant seed sensitivity.
AI.motivation: To understand how initial-condition embeddings influence optimization conditioning in PINNs with spectral representations, beyond representational choices.
AI.method: Compare exponential and linear IC gates with fixed and adaptive Fourier spectral trunks on a nonlinear stiff spring-pendulum ODE across different stiffness levels (k=20, k=60), using multiple random seeds; assess performance via relative L2 and max pointwise errors and statistical tests (paired Wilcoxon with Holm correction).
AI.result: At moderate stiffness (k=20), exponential IC gating often yields lower error but with heterogeneous seed behavior; at high stiffness (k=60), linear gating becomes preferable; additional reversals observed at larger k. Trends hold for both error metrics and are statistically validated. IC embeddings influence gradient scaling and optimization conditioning, with stiffness-dependent effects and distinct sensitivity patterns for baseline vs adaptive spectral PINNs.
AI.conclusion: IC embeddings are a non-neutral design choice in PINNs; their induced gradient scaling materially shapes optimization conditioning in stiff regimes, and the optimal gating strategy depends on stiffness and whether spectral representations are fixed or adaptive.

title: Knowledge-Free Correlated Agreement for Incentivizing Federated Learning
summary: We introduce Knowledge-Free Correlated Agreement (KFCA) to reward client contributions in federated learning (FL) without relying on ground truth, a public test set, or distribution knowledge. Under categorical reports and an honest majority, KFCA is strictly truthful, addressing the label-flipping vulnerability of Correlated Agreement (CA). We evaluate KFCA on federated LLM adapter tuning and a real-world PCB inspection task, showing efficient real-time reward computation suitable for decentralized and blockchain-based incentive designs.

AI.tldr: KFCA provides a knowledge-free incentive mechanism in FL that rewards client contributions without ground truth, using correlated agreement; strictly truthful under categorical reports and honest majority; robust to label flipping; evaluated on FL LLM adapter tuning and PCB inspection; supports real-time reward computation for decentralized incentives.
AI.motivation: Incentivize truthful, contribution-focused behavior in federated learning without access to ground-truth labels, public test sets, or distribution knowledge, and without verifying labels, enabling scalable, blockchain-friendly incentives.
AI.method: Introduce KFCA mechanism that relies on correlated agreement under categorical reporting and an honest-majority assumption to ensure truthfulness without external references; compare to Correlated Agreement (CA), address label-flipping vulnerability; implement efficient, real-time reward computation suitable for decentralized/ blockchain-based incentive designs; validate on federated LLM adapter tuning and a real-world PCB inspection task.
AI.result: KFCA is strictly truthful under the stated conditions, mitigates label-flipping vulnerability of CA, and enables efficient real-time reward computation; empirical evaluation on two tasks demonstrates practicality for decentralized incentive designs.
AI.conclusion: KFCA provides a practical, ground-truth-free incentive mechanism for federated learning that supports decentralized and blockchain-based environments; future work may examine robustness to collusion, relaxing honest-majority requirements, privacy-preserving adaptations, and comparisons with alternative incentive schemes.

title: A unified Benchmark for Multi-Frame Image Restoration under Severe Refractive Warping
summary: Video sequence capturing through refractive dynamic media, such as a turbulent air or water surface, often suffer from severe geometric distortions and temporal instability. While recent advances address mild atmospheric turbulence, no existing benchmarks systematically evaluate restoration methods under strong and highly nonuniform refractive conditions. We present a comprehensive benchmark for geometric distortion removal in video, covering a range from turbulence-like mild warping to strong discontinuous refractive deformations. The benchmark includes both laboratory-captured real data and synthetic sequences generated for static scenes via physics-based light refraction modeling across four distortion levels and multiple surface wave types. We evaluate a spectrum of methods from simple baselines and classical registration algorithms to advanced learning-based approaches including DATUM and our proposed diffusion based V-cache for high and extreme distortions regimes. Evaluation uses both pixel-level (PSNR, SSIM), and perceptual (LPIPS, DINO, CLIP) metrics providing the first large scale analysis of geometric distortion removal. Our benchmark establishes a new foundation for developing and evaluating algorithms capable of reconstructing video from highly distorted optical environments. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/iafoss/refractive-mfir-benchmark.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark for restoring video distorted by refractive media, combining real and synthetic data across distortion levels, with comprehensive pixel and perceptual evaluations; includes diffusion-based V-cache method.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate geometric distortion removal under strong, nonuniform refractive distortions, addressing the gap where prior benchmarks cover only mild turbulence.
AI.method: Constructs a benchmark with laboratory-captured real data and physics-based synthetic sequences across four distortion levels and multiple surface wave types; evaluates baselines, classical registration, and learning-based methods including DATUM and diffusion-based V-cache.
AI.result: Provides the first large-scale, multi-metric assessment (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, DINO, CLIP); code and datasets released; establishes a foundation for developing algorithms to reconstruct video from highly distorted optical environments.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark enables rigorous development and evaluation of methods for geometric distortion removal in refractive media, guiding future research.

title: FL-Sailer: Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning for Scalable Single-Cell Epigenetic Data Analysis via Adaptive Sampling
summary: Single-cell ATAC-seq (scATAC-seq) enables high-resolution mapping of chromatin accessibility, yet privacy regulations and data size constraints hinder multi-institutional sharing. Federated learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative, but faces three fundamental barriers in scATAC-seq analysis: ultra-high dimensionality, extreme sparsity, and severe cross-institutional heterogeneity. We propose FL-Sailer, the first FL framework designed for scATAC-seq data. FL-Sailer integrates two key innovations: (i) adaptive leverage score sampling, which selects biologically interpretable features while reducing dimensionality by 80%, and (ii) an invariant VAE architecture, which disentangles biological signals from technical confounders via mutual information minimization. We provide a convergence guarantee, showing that FL-Sailer converges to an approximate solution of the original high-dimensional problem with bounded error. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real epigenomic datasets demonstrate that FL-Sailer not only enables previously infeasible multi-institutional collaborations but also surpasses centralized methods by leveraging adaptive sampling as an implicit regularizer to suppress technical noise. Our work establishes that federated learning, when tailored to domain-specific challenges, can become a superior paradigm for collaborative epigenomic research.

AI.tldr: Proposes FL-Sailer, a privacy-preserving federated learning framework for scATAC-seq analysis that uses adaptive leverage score sampling to reduce dimensionality by 80% and an invariant VAE to disentangle biology from technical confounders, with convergence guarantees and superior performance to centralized methods in synthetic and real datasets.
AI.motivation: Privacy regulations and data size constraints impede multi-institutional sharing of scATAC-seq data. Challenges include ultra-high dimensionality, extreme sparsity, and cross-institutional heterogeneity that hinder collaboration.
AI.method: Two core innovations: (i) adaptive leverage score sampling to select biologically interpretable features and substantially reduce dimensionality (~80%); (ii) an invariant VAE architecture that minimizes mutual information to disentangle biological signals from technical confounders. The approach includes a convergence guarantee showing an approximate solution to the high-dimensional problem with bounded error.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real epigenomic datasets show FL-Sailer enables multi-institutional collaboration that was previously infeasible and outperforms centralized methods. Adaptive sampling acts as an implicit regularizer, suppressing technical noise and improving generalization.
AI.conclusion: Tailoring federated learning to domain-specific challenges can make FL a superior paradigm for collaborative epigenomic research, with FL-Sailer serving as a practical, privacy-preserving framework for scATAC-seq analysis.

title: CPCANet: Deep Unfolding Common Principal Component Analysis for Domain Generalization
summary: Domain Generalization (DG) aims to learn representations that remain robust under out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts and generalize effectively to unseen target domains. While recent invariant learning strategies and architectural advances have achieved strong performance, explicitly discovering a structured domain-invariant subspace through second-order statistics remains underexplored. In this work, we propose CPCANet, a novel framework grounded in Common Principal Component Analysis (CPCA), which unrolls the iterative Flury-Gautschi (FG) algorithm into fully differentiable neural layers. This approach integrates the statistical properties of CPCA into an end-to-end trainable framework, enforcing the discovery of a shared subspace across diverse domains while preserving interpretability. Experiments on four standard DG benchmarks demonstrate that CPCANet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in zero-shot transfer. Moreover, CPCANet is architecture-agnostic and requires no dataset-specific tuning, providing a simple and efficient approach to learning robust representations under distribution shift. Code is available at https://github.com/wish44165/CPCANet.

AI.tldr: CPCANet leverages CPCA and unrolled Flury-Gautschi iterations to learn a shared, interpretable domain-invariant subspace via differentiable layers, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot DG without dataset-specific tuning.
AI.motivation: Domain Generalization (DG) methods struggle to discover a principled, structured, domain-invariant subspace. Second-order statistics via CPCA offer a principled way to identify a common principal subspace across diverse domains, while maintaining interpretability; existing invariant learning and architectural advances lack this CPCA-based structure.
AI.method: Unroll the iterative Flury-Gautschi algorithm as differentiable neural layers to implement CPCA within an end-to-end trainable framework. Enforce discovery of a shared subspace across domains, in a architecture-agnostic and dataset-agnostic manner, with no dataset-specific tuning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on zero-shot DG across four standard benchmarks. Demonstrates architecture-agnosticism and no need for dataset-specific tuning; provides a simple, efficient approach. Code released.
AI.conclusion: A CPCA-based framework (CPCANet) can yield interpretable, robust domain-invariant representations and strong zero-shot generalization across DG tasks, with broad applicability and minimal task-specific tuning.

title: YOTOnet: Zero-Shot Cross-Domain Fault Diagnosis via Domain-Conditioned Mixture of Experts
summary: Mechanical equipment forms the critical backbone of modern industrial production, yet domain shift severely limits the generalization of deep learning based fault diagnosis models across different equipment and operating conditions.Inspired by the success of foundation models in achieving zero-shotgeneralization, we propose YOTOnet (You Only Train Once), a novel architecture specifically designed for cross-domain fault diagnosis in mechanical equipment.YOTOnet comprises three core components: (1) a physics-aware Invariant Feature Distiller that extracts domain-agnostic representations using multi-scale dilated convolutions and FFT-based time-frequency fusion,(2) Domain-Conditioned Sparse Experts (DC-MoE) that adaptively route inputs to specialized processors via learned gating without external meta-data, and (3) a dual-head classification system with auxiliary supervision.Extensive validation on five public bearing datasets (CWRU, MFPT, XJTU,OTTAWA, HUST) through 30 cross-dataset protocols demonstrates the superiority of YOTOnet compared with other state-of-the-art methods. Critically, we observe a clear scaling effect-average test F1 improves from 0.5339(1 training dataset) to 0.705 (4 datasets), with a clear gain when moving from 3 to 4 datasets. These findings provide empirical evidence that foundation model principles can enable robust, train-once deployment for industrial fault diagnosis.

AI.tldr: YOTOnet is a cross-domain fault diagnosis architecture that aims for zero-shot generalization by combining a physics-aware invariant feature distiller, a domain-conditioned sparse expert MoE, and a dual-head classifier, achieving strong cross-dataset results and a notable train-once scalability gain.
AI.motivation: Domain shift across equipment and operating conditions limits the generalization of deep fault diagnosis models. The work seeks to leverage foundation-model ideas to enable robust, train-once deployment for industrial fault diagnostics.
AI.method: Three components: (1) physics-aware Invariant Feature Distiller using multi-scale dilated convolutions and FFT-based time-frequency fusion to extract domain-agnostic features; (2) Domain-Conditioned Sparse Experts (DC-MoE) with learned gating to route inputs to specialized processors without external meta-data; (3) dual-head classifier with auxiliary supervision. Evaluation on five bearing datasets (CWRU, MFPT, XJTU, OTTAWA, HUST) using 30 cross-dataset protocols.
AI.result: YOTOnet outperforms state-of-the-art methods across the 30 cross-dataset protocols. Reported scaling: average test F1 improves from 0.5339 with one training dataset to 0.705 with four datasets, with a notable gain when increasing from three to four datasets.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model-inspired design can enable robust, train-once deployment for industrial fault diagnosis, with clear evidence of cross-domain generalization gains and a scalable data-coverage benefit.

title: Cognitive Twins: Investigating Personalized Thinking Model Building and Its Performance Enhancement with Human-in-the-Loop
summary: This paper presents the Personalized Thinking Model (PTM), a hierarchical and interpretable learner representation designed for AI supported education. PTM organizes evidence from learner journals into a five-layer structure covering behavioral instances, behavioral patterns, cognitive routines, metacognitive tendencies, and self-system values. PTM is grounded in Marzano's New Taxonomy of Educational Objectives and tries to clone learner's thinking model and build cognitive twin. It was constructed using a pipeline that combines large language model inference (Gemini 2.5 Pro), sentence embeddings, dimensionality reduction, and consensus clustering. This paper evaluates PTM fidelity through three methods applied to 40 participants in a seven-week study. First, automatic evaluation using atomic information point matching yielded an overall F1 score of 74.57% before human-in-the-loop (HITL) refinement and 75.48% after refinement. Second, user evaluation using a Likert scale produced mean ratings of 4.26 and 4.30 on a five-point scale for pre and post-HITL conditions respectively. Third, semantic alignment verification showed that topic coherence increased from 0.436 at the behavioral layer to 0.626 at the core value layer, while lexical overlap with journal vocabulary decreased from 0.114 to 0.007 across those same layers. These results suggest that the PTM produces outputs with acceptable fidelity, was generally perceived by users as reflecting their thinking, and showed a pattern consistent with semantic abstraction across layers.

AI.tldr: PTM is a hierarchical, interpretable learner model for AI-assisted education that mirrors learner thinking across five layers and is evaluated via automated, user, and semantic metrics showing acceptable fidelity and perceived reflection of thinking.
AI.motivation: Provide an interpretable, scalable representation of learner thinking (a cognitive twin) to support AI-mediated education, grounded in Marzano's New Taxonomy.
AI.method: Pipeline combines large language model inference (Gemini 2.5 Pro), sentence embeddings, dimensionality reduction, and consensus clustering to construct a five-layer PTM (behavioral, behavioral patterns, cognitive routines, metacognitive tendencies, self-system values). Evaluation uses 40 participants over seven weeks with three approaches: automatic atomic information point matching (F1 ~74.57% pre-HITL, ~75.48% post-HITL), user Likert ratings (pre ~4.26, post ~4.30), and semantic alignment (topic coherence 0.436 to 0.626; lexical overlap 0.114 to 0.007).
AI.result: PTM achieved acceptable fidelity, was generally perceived by users as reflecting their thinking, and showed a coherent pattern of semantic abstraction across layers.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of a cognitive twin for education; HITL improves fidelity; results warrant further validation, especially for generalization, robustness, and longitudinal cognitive tracking.

title: What Matters in Practical Learned Image Compression
summary:
One of the major differentiators unlocked by learned codecs relative to their hard-coded traditional counterparts is their ability to be optimized directly to appeal to the human visual system. Despite this potential, a perceptual yet practical image codec is yet to be proposed.
  In this work, we aim to close this gap. We conduct a comprehensive study of the key modeling choices that govern the design of a practical learned image codec, jointly optimized for perceptual quality and runtime -- including within the ablations several novel techniques. We then perform performance-aware neural architecture search over millions of backbone configurations to identify models that achieve the target on-device runtime while maximizing compression performance as captured by perceptual metrics.
  We combine the various optimizations to construct a new codec that achieves a significantly improved tradeoff between speed and perceptual quality. Based on rigorous subjective user studies, it provides 2.3-3x bitrate savings against AV1, AV2, VVC, ECM and JPEG-AI, and 20-40% bitrate savings against the best learned codec alternatives. At the same time, on an iPhone 17 Pro Max, it encodes 12MP images as fast as 230ms, and decodes them in 150ms -- faster than most top ML-based codecs run on a V100 GPU.

AI.tldr: A practical perceptual-optimized learned image codec developed via multi-criteria optimization including NAS to meet on-device runtime, achieving large bitrate savings against traditional codecs and competitive performance vs other learned codecs; fast on-device encoding/decoding on high-end smartphone.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between perceptual-quality optimization and practical deployment by designing and evaluating a learned codec that is optimized for perceptual quality while meeting real-time on-device constraints.
AI.method: Comprehensive study of modeling choices; joint optimization for perceptual quality and runtime; ablations introducing novel techniques; performance-aware neural architecture search over millions of backbone configurations to meet on-device runtime while maximizing perceptual compression performance.
AI.result: New codec with improved speed-quality tradeoff; subjective user studies show 2.3-3x bitrate savings vs AV1/AV2/VVC/JPEG-AI and 20-40% vs best learned alternatives; on iPhone 17 Pro Max, 12MP encoding in 230 ms and decoding in 150 ms, faster than top ML codecs on V100.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and strong potential of perceptual- and runtime-optimized learned codecs for practical deployment, delivering substantial bitrate savings with on-device performance requirements.

title: From Video-to-PDE: Data-Driven Discovery of Nonlinear Dye Plume Dynamics
summary: Inferring continuum models directly from video is hampered by two facts: the recorded field is uncalibrated image intensity rather than a physical state, and direct numerical differentiation of noisy frames is unstable. We develop a video-to-PDE pipeline that converts grayscale recordings of an ink plume into a normalised scalar field $u(x,y,t)$, isolates a bulk drift $\mathbf{v}(t)$ from intrinsic spreading via the intensity-weighted centroid, and identifies an effective transport law by weak-form sparse regression. Conditioning, threshold-sweep and random-centre diagnostics show that overcomplete libraries are strongly collinear; the search is therefore restricted to compact gradient-based libraries. Coefficients are refined by an inverse physics-informed network and recalibrated against forward rollouts, with a chronological block bootstrap quantifying uncertainty. The selected reduced model $u_t+\mathbf v(t)\!\cdot\!\nabla u = 9.005\,|\nabla u|^{2}+0.666\,Δu$ outperforms advection--diffusion baselines on held-out frames, retains a positive Laplacian coefficient, and admits a Cole--Hopf reduction to a linear advection--diffusion equation. The framework demonstrates that uncalibrated visual data can yield compact, predictive and structurally interpretable continuum models when discovery, calibration and uncertainty are treated as distinct stages.

AI.tldr: Video-to-PDE pipeline that converts uncalibrated grayscale ink-plume videos into a normalized scalar field, extracts a bulk drift, and identifies a compact transport law via weak-form sparse regression, refined by an inverse physics-informed network and forward rollouts; yields a reduced model u_t + v(t)·∇u = 9.005|∇u|^2 + 0.666 Δu with uncertainty quantified, outperforming baselines and admitting a Cole–Hopf reduction.
AI.motivation: Overcome two key obstacles in learning continuum models from video: (1) the recorded field is uncalibrated intensity rather than a physical state, and (2) direct numerical differentiation of noisy frames is unstable. Develop a pipeline that (i) normalizes and converts video to a scalar field, (ii) separates advection (bulk drift) from intrinsic spreading, and (iii) discovers a transport law in a structured, interpretable form with uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: 1) Normalize grayscale video to a scalar field u(x,y,t). 2) Extract bulk drift v(t) from intensity-weighted centroid to separate advection from spreading. 3) Identify transport law using weak-form sparse regression with overcomplete libraries; restrict search to compact gradient-based libraries due to multicollinearity. 4) Refine coefficients with an inverse physics-informed network and recalibrate via forward rollouts. 5) Quantify uncertainty using chronological block bootstrap. 6) Validate by comparing to advection–diffusion baselines on held-out frames; ensure coefficients yield a positive Laplacian and possible Cole–Hopf reduction.
AI.result: The reduced model selected is u_t + v(t)·∇u = 9.005|∇u|^2 + 0.666 Δu. It outperforms advection–diffusion baselines on held-out frames, preserves a positive Laplacian term, and admits a Cole–Hopf reduction to a linear advection–diffusion equation.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that uncalibrated visual data can yield compact, predictive, and structurally interpretable continuum models when discovery, calibration, and uncertainty are treated as distinct, sequential stages.

title: Quantile-Free Uncertainty Quantification in Graph Neural Networks
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) in graph neural networks (GNNs) is crucial in high-stakes domains but remains a significant challenge. In graph settings, message passing often relies on strong assumptions such as exchangeability, which are rarely satisfied in practice. Moreover, achieving reliable UQ typically requires costly resampling or post-hoc calibration. To address these issues, we introduce Quantile-free Prediction Interval GNN (QpiGNN), a framework that builds on quantile regression (QR) to enable GNN-based UQ by directly optimizing coverage and interval width without requiring quantile inputs or post-processing. QpiGNN employs a dual-head architecture that decouples prediction and uncertainty, and is trained with label-only supervision through a quantile-free joint loss. This design allows efficient training and yields robust prediction intervals, with theoretical guarantees of asymptotic coverage and near-optimal width under mild assumptions. Experiments on 19 synthetic and real-world benchmarks show QpiGNN achieves average 22\% higher coverage and 50\% narrower intervals than baselines, while ensuring efficiency and robustness to noise and structural shifts.

AI.tldr: Quantile-free Prediction Interval GNN (QpiGNN) directly optimizes coverage and interval width for graph neural networks without quantile inputs or post-processing, using a dual-head architecture and a quantile-free joint loss.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification in GNNs is challenging due to reliance on exchangeability assumptions and costly resampling/post-hoc calibration; there is a need for efficient, reliable UQ methods that do not depend on quantile inputs.
AI.method: A dual-head GNN architecture that decouples prediction and uncertainty, trained with a quantile-free joint loss and label-only supervision to learn prediction intervals without quantile estimates or post-processing.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 19 synthetic and real-world benchmarks shows approximately 22% higher coverage and 50% narrower intervals compared to baselines, with robustness to noise and structural shifts and efficient training.
AI.conclusion: QpiGNN provides asymptotic coverage guarantees and near-optimal interval width under mild assumptions, offering robust, efficient UQ for GNNs without quantile inputs or post-processing.

title: Aes3D: Aesthetic Assessment in 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: As 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) gains attention in immersive media and digital content creation, assessing the aesthetics of 3D scenes becomes important in helping creators build more visually compelling 3D content. However, existing evaluation methods for 3D scenes primarily emphasize reconstruction fidelity and perceptual realism, largely overlooking higher-level aesthetic attributes such as composition, harmony, and visual appeal. This limitation comes from two key challenges: (1) the absence of general 3DGS datasets with aesthetic annotations, and (2) the intrinsic nature of 3DGS as a low-level primitive representation, which makes it difficult to capture high-level aesthetic features. To address these challenges, we propose Aes3D, the first systematic framework for assessing the aesthetics of 3D neural rendering scenes. Aes3D includes Aesthetic3D, the first dataset dedicated to 3D scene aesthetic assessment, built on our proposed annotation strategy for 3D scene aesthetics. In addition, we present Aes3DGSNet, a lightweight model that directly predicts scene-level aesthetic scores from 3DGS representations. Notably, our model operates solely on 3D Gaussian primitives, eliminating the need for rendering multi-view images and thus reducing computational cost and hardware requirements. Through aesthetics-supervised learning on multi-view 3DGS scene representations, Aes3DGSNet effectively captures high-level aesthetic cues and accurately regresses aesthetic scores. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves strong performance while maintaining a lightweight design, establishing a new benchmark for 3D scene aesthetic assessment. Code and datasets will be made available in a future version.

AI.tldr: Aes3D introduces Aesthetic3D dataset and Aes3DGSNet to quantify aesthetics in 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes, enabling lightweight, rendering-free aesthetic assessment via 3D primitives.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of datasets and methods to evaluate aesthetic attributes (composition, harmony) of 3D neural renders, especially for 3DGS, hindering holistic assessment beyond reconstruction fidelity.
AI.method: Create Aesthetic3D dataset with annotated aesthetics using a proposed strategy; develop Aes3DGSNet that regresses scene-level aesthetics directly from 3D Gaussian primitives, trained with aesthetics-supervised learning on multi-view 3DGS scenes; avoids rendering multi-view images to reduce cost.
AI.result: The model achieves strong performance with a lightweight design, and the dataset serves as a benchmark for 3D scene aesthetics; code/datasets to be released.
AI.conclusion: Aes3D establishes a novel benchmark and baseline for 3D scene aesthetics in 3DGS, enabling efficient, high-level aesthetic assessment and guiding creators; future work includes releasing code and datasets.

title: Power Distribution Bridges Sampling, Self-Reward RL, and Self-Distillation
summary: Recent analyses question whether reinforcement learning (RL) is responsible for strong reasoning in large language models (LLMs). At the same time, distillation and inference-time sampling, including power sampling, have emerged as effective ways to improve LLM performance. However, the relationship among RL, distillation, and sampling remains unclear. In this study, we focus on the power distribution, the target distribution of power sampling, and show that the power distribution bridges sampling, self-reward KL-regularized RL, and self-distillation. From the sampling perspective, we show that inexpensive local approximations cannot reproduce sequence-level power without information about possible suffixes. From the RL perspective, the power distribution is the closed-form optimizer of KL-regularized RL when the model's sequence-level log-probabilities are used as the reward. This identification leads to power self-distillation, an offline distillation surrogate that shares the same target distribution and amortizes the cost of power sampling into supervised training on teacher samples. We further show that power self-distillation can achieve self-reward sharpening, while improvement in a downstream true reward is governed by the covariance between true reward and self-reward under the power distribution. Experiments on reasoning tasks support our analysis: power sampling raises self-reward, true-reward gains depend on alignment with self-reward, and power self-distillation can match or exceed the performance of power sampling at much lower inference cost.

AI.tldr: Power distribution serves as a unifying target that connects sampling, self-reward KL-regularized RL, and self-distillation in LLMs; it is the closed-form optimizer for KL-RL under sequence-level log-prob rewards, enabling a cost-efficient offline self-distillation (power self-distillation) with comparable performance to power sampling; true reward gains depend on alignment with self-reward and on covariance with self-reward.
AI.motivation: Clarify how RL, distillation, and inference-time sampling relate in LLMs, and whether a single target distribution—the power distribution—can unify these approaches and explain observed gains from power sampling and related methods.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis identifying the power distribution as the optimizer of KL-regularized RL when the reward is the model’s own sequence-level log-probabilities; analysis of local approximations vs suffix sensitivity for sequence-level power; propose power self-distillation as an offline surrogate sharing the same target distribution; derive conditions for self-reward sharpening and how true reward depends on covariance with self-reward; empirical evaluation on reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Power distribution links sampling, self-reward KL-regularized RL, and self-distillation; local approximations fail to capture sequence-level power without suffix information; power distribution is the closed-form optimizer for KL-RL with log-prob rewards; power self-distillation can match or exceed power-sampling performance with lower inference cost; improvements in true reward require alignment with self-reward (covariance governs the effect).
AI.conclusion: A unified framework where the power distribution is central to sampling, RL, and distillation for LLMs. Power self-distillation provides an effective offline alternative that preserves the target distribution while reducing inference costs, though gains in true task reward hinge on the alignment between true reward and self-reward.

title: Wasserstein-Aligned Localisation for VLM-Based Distributional OOD Detection in Medical Imaging
summary: Zero-shot anomaly localisation via vision-language models (VLMs) offers a compelling approach for rare pathology detection, yet its performance is fundamentally limited by the absence of healthy anatomical context. We reformulate zero-shot localisation as a comparative inference problem in which anomalies are identified through structured comparison against reference distributions of normal anatomy. We introduce WALDO, a training-free framework grounded in optimal transport theory that enables comparative reasoning through: (i) entropy-weighted Sliced Wasserstein distances for anatomically-aware reference selection from DINOv2 patch distributions, (ii) Goldilocks zone sampling exploiting the non-monotonic relationship between reference similarity and localisation accuracy, and (iii) self-consistency aggregation via weighted non-maximum suppression. We theoretically analyse the Goldilocks effect through distributional divergence, and show that references with moderate similarity minimize a bias-variance trade-off in comparative visual reasoning. On the NOVA brain MRI benchmark, WALDO with Qwen2.5-VL-72B achieves $43.5_{\pm1.6}\%$ mAP@30 (95\% CI: [40.4, 46.7]), representing a 19\% relative improvement over zero-shot baselines. Cross-model evaluation shows consistent gains: GPT-4o achieves $32.0_{\pm6.5}\%$ and Qwen3-VL-32B achieves $32.0_{\pm6.6}\%$ mAP@30. Paired McNemar tests confirm statistical significance ($p<0.01$). Source code is available at https://github.com/bkainz/WALDO_MICCAI26_demo .

AI.tldr: WALDO is a training-free zero-shot anomaly localisation framework that uses entropy-weighted Sliced Wasserstein distances for reference selection, Goldilocks zone sampling, and self-consistency aggregation to compare anomalies against normal anatomy, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot mAP@30 on NOVA brain MRI and demonstrating cross-model gains.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of healthy anatomical context in zero-shot anomaly localisation by reframing detection as comparative inference against normal anatomy distributions; leverage optimal-transport-inspired reference selection for robust localisation.
AI.method: 1) Entropy-weighted Sliced Wasserstein distance to select reference patches from DINOv2 representations; 2) Goldilocks zone sampling exploiting non-monotonic relation between reference similarity and localisation accuracy; 3) self-consistency aggregation via weighted non-maximum suppression; theoretical analysis of the Goldilocks effect via distributional divergence; training-free framework.
AI.result: On the NOVA brain MRI benchmark, WALDO with Qwen2.5-VL-72B achieves 43.5% ±1.6% mAP@30 (95% CI: [40.4,46.7]), a 19% relative improvement over zero-shot baselines. Cross-model gains: GPT-4o 32.0% ±6.5%, Qwen3-VL-32B 32.0% ±6.6% mAP@30. Paired McNemar tests: p<0.01. Source code at https://github.com/bkainz/WALDO_MICCAI26_demo.
AI.conclusion: Training-free, comparative reasoning with normal-anatomy references via optimal-transport concepts yields strong zero-shot anomaly localisation performance, with consistent cross-model gains and a theoretical Goldilocks mechanism explaining the bias-variance trade-off in reference similarity.

title: Event-Based Early Warning of Vineyard Disease Risk from Environmental Time Series
summary: Accurate early warning of vineyard disease risk from environmental observations is essential for timely intervention and more sustainable crop protection. However, many existing studies formulate disease prediction as daily presence classification, which can favor persistence-driven predictions and provide only limited support for actionable short-horizon warning. In this paper, we present an event-based approach for early warning of vineyard disease risk from environmental time series and evaluate it through a vineyard case study. Rather than predicting daily disease status, the task is reformulated to predict transitions into annotated disease-risk periods within a future window of 3-7 days. To reduce fragmentation caused by short interruptions in the binary labels, new events are defined only after a minimum disease-free gap. This formulation encourages models to capture environmental precursors associated with upcoming risk periods instead of merely reproducing temporal persistence. Using multi-year agro-meteorological data, we construct input representations that capture humidity dynamics, rainfall accumulation, temperature variability, and seasonal structure through cyclic temporal encoding. We evaluate representative methods from classical machine learning and deep learning, including XGBoost, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs), using both standard classification metrics and an event-oriented early warning protocol. The results show that the event-based formulation supports practical short-horizon warning, while the compared models exhibit distinct trade-offs between event recall, lead time, and false-alert behavior. Overall, the study underscores the importance of problem formulation in environmental time-series learning and demonstrates the value of event-based prediction for vineyard disease warning systems.

AI.tldr: Reframe vineyard disease prediction as event-based early warning: predict transitions into disease-risk periods within a 3–7 day future window, with minimum disease-free gaps to reduce label fragmentation; using agro-meteorological time-series and cyclic temporal encoding to capture humidity, rainfall, temp variability, and seasonality; compare XGBoost, LSTM, and TCNs under an event-oriented evaluation protocol; finding: event-based formulations enable practical short-horizon warnings but entail trade-offs among recall, lead time, and false alarms; emphasizes problem formulation's importance for environmental time-series learning.
AI.motivation: To provide actionable, short-horizon vineyard disease warnings and avoid persistence-driven predictions common in daily disease-status classifiers.
AI.method: Construct input features from multi-year agro-meteorological time series (humidity dynamics, rainfall accumulation, temperature variability, cyclic temporal encoding). Define events as transitions into annotated disease-risk periods, after a minimum disease-free gap. Evaluate representative ML/DL models (XGBoost, LSTM, Temporal Convolutional Networks) with standard classification metrics and an event-oriented early-warning protocol.
AI.result: Event-based formulation supports practical short-horizon warning; models show varying trade-offs between event recall, lead time, and false-alarm behavior across XGBoost, LSTM, and TCN.
AI.conclusion: Reframing the task as event-based prediction is valuable for vineyard disease warning systems and illustrates that problem formulation significantly shapes predictive performance and practical usefulness.

title: PhysForge: Generating Physics-Grounded 3D Assets for Interactive Virtual World
comment: Accepted by ICML 2026. Project Page: https://hku-mmlab.github.io/PhysForge/
summary: Synthesizing physics-grounded 3D assets is a critical bottleneck for interactive virtual worlds and embodied AI. Existing methods predominantly focus on static geometry, overlooking the functional properties essential for interaction. We propose that interactive asset generation must be rooted in functional logic and hierarchical physics. To bridge this gap, we introduce PhysForge, a decoupled two-stage framework supported by PhysDB, a large-scale dataset of 150,000 assets with four-tier physical annotations. First, a VLM acts as a "physical architect" to plan a "Hierarchical Physical Blueprint" defining material, functional, and kinematic constraints. Second, a physics-grounded diffusion model realizes this blueprint by synthesizing high-fidelity geometry alongside precise kinematic parameters via a novel KineVoxel Injection (KVI) mechanism. Experiments demonstrate that PhysForge produces functionally plausible, simulation-ready assets, providing a robust data engine for interactive 3D content and embodied agents.

AI.tldr: PhysForge proposes a two-stage framework (PhysForge) for physics-grounded 3D asset synthesis, employing PhysDB and a two-stage pipeline: Stage 1 uses a vision-language model as a 'physical architect' to plan a Hierarchical Physical Blueprint; Stage 2 uses a physics-grounded diffusion model with KineVoxel Injection (KVI) to realize the blueprint into geometry and precise kinematics.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D asset generation emphasizes static geometry and neglects interaction physics; functional logic and hierarchical physics are needed to produce simulation-ready assets for interactive worlds and embodied AI.
AI.method: A decoupled two-stage approach: (1) Stage 1 uses a vision-language model as a 'physical architect' to design a Hierarchical Physical Blueprint detailing material, functional, and kinematic constraints. (2) Stage 2 applies a physics-grounded diffusion model to realize the blueprint, generating high-fidelity geometry and precise kinematic parameters via KineVoxel Injection (KVI). Training relies on PhysDB, a dataset of 150k assets with four-tier physical annotations.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments indicate production of functionally plausible, simulation-ready assets, suggesting PhysForge can serve as a robust data engine for interactive 3D content and embodied agents.
AI.conclusion: PhysForge demonstrates how functional logic and hierarchical physics can be integrated into asset synthesis, enabling scalable production of physics-aware 3D assets for interactive environments.

title: Delta-Based Neural Architecture Search: LLM Fine-Tuning via Code Diffs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show strong potential for neural architecture generation, yet existing approaches produce complete model implementations from scratch -- computationally expensive and yielding verbose code. We propose Delta-Code Generation, where fine-tuned LLMs generate compact unified diffs (deltas) to refine baseline architectures rather than synthesizing entire models. Our pipeline iteratively fine-tunes the LLM via LoRA on curated architectures from the LEMUR dataset, with MinHash-Jaccard novelty filtering for structural diversity. We evaluate three 7B-class LLMs -- DeepSeek-Coder-7B, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, and Mistral-7B -- across six datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, MNIST, SVHN, ImageNette, CelebA) using a 22-cycle protocol (1,100 candidates per LLM). All three substantially surpass the full-generation baseline (50.6% valid rate, 42.3% mean first-epoch accuracy): DeepSeek-Coder reaches 75.3% valid rate and 65.8% mean accuracy; Qwen2.5-Coder 72.1%/64.6%; Mistral 66.6%/66.1%. On CIFAR-10, best first-epoch accuracies reach 85.5% (Mistral), 85.2% (DeepSeek), 80.6% (Qwen) -- well above 63.98% full generation and 71.5% for the concurrent approach of Gu et al. Output lengths are 30-50 lines versus 200+ for full generation (75-85% reduction). A 50-epoch study confirms the 1-epoch proxy preserves rankings (Mistral: Spearman $ρ$ = 0.926). Delta-based generation is a token-efficient, multi-domain, LLM-agnostic alternative to full-model synthesis for LLM-driven NAS.

AI.tldr: Delta-Code Generation refines baseline architectures with compact diffs (deltas) rather than full model synthesis, enabling token-efficient, multi-domain LLM-driven NAS that preserves rankings and delivers substantial performance gains across six datasets.
AI.motivation: Reduce the computational cost and verbosity of generating complete neural architectures from scratch in LLM-driven NAS by emitting small, patch-like diffs.
AI.method: Iterative LoRA-based fine-tuning of three 7B-class LLMs (DeepSeek-Coder-7B, Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, Mistral-7B) on curated architectures from the LEMUR dataset, with MinHash-Jaccard novelty filtering to promote structural diversity. A 22-cycle protocol evaluates diff-based generations (1,100 candidates per LLM). Outputs are compact diffs (30–50 lines) instead of 200+ lines for full synthesis.
AI.result: All three LLMs surpass the full-generation baseline (50.6% valid rate, 42.3% mean first-epoch accuracy). DeepSeek-Coder-7B: 75.3% valid, 65.8% mean; Qwen2.5-Coder-7B: 72.1% valid, 64.6% mean; Mistral-7B: 66.6% valid, 66.1% mean. On CIFAR-10, best first-epoch accuracies: 85.5% (Mistral), 85.2% (DeepSeek), 80.6% (Qwen), vs 63.98% full generation and 71.5% for Gu et al. Mitigates verbose code with 30–50 line outputs (vs 200+). A 50-epoch study shows the 1-epoch proxy preserves ranking (Spearman ρ = 0.926).
AI.conclusion: Delta-based, token-efficient diffs offer a scalable, LLM-agnostic alternative to full-model synthesis for LLM-driven NAS, achieving strong accuracy gains with significantly shorter outputs and preserved rankings.

title: Geometry-Aware State Space Model: A New Paradigm for Whole-Slide Image Representation
summary: Accurate analysis of histopathological images is critical for disease diagnosis and treatment planning. Whole-slide images (WSIs), which digitize tissue specimens at gigapixel resolution, are fundamental to this process but require aggregating thousands of patches for slide-level predictions. Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) tackles this challenge with a two-stage paradigm, decoupling tile-level embedding and slide-level prediction. However, most existing methods implicitly embed patch representations in homogeneous Euclidean spaces, overlooking the hierarchical organization and regional heterogeneity of pathological tissues. This limits current models' ability to capture global tissue architecture and fine-grained cellular morphology. To address this limitation, we introduce a hybrid hyperbolic-Euclidean representation that embeds WSI features in dual geometric spaces, enabling complementary modeling of hierarchical tissue structures and local morphological details. Building on this formulation, we develop BatMIL, a WSI classification framework that leverages both geometric spaces. To model long-range dependencies among thousands of patches, we employ a structured state space sequence model (S4) backbone that encodes patch sequences with linear computational complexity. Furthermore, to account for regional heterogeneity, we introduce a chunk-level mixture-of-experts (MoE) module that groups patches into regions and dynamically routes them to specialized subnetworks, improving representational capacity while reducing redundant computation. Extensive experiments on seven WSI datasets spanning six cancer types demonstrate that BatMIL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MIL approaches in slide-level classification tasks. These results indicate that geometry-aware representation learning offers a promising direction for next-generation computational pathology.

AI.tldr: BatMIL: a geometry-aware MIL framework for WSIs that combines hyperbolic-Euclidean dual representations, an S4 backbone, and a chunk-level MoE to model hierarchical tissue structure and regional heterogeneity, achieving state-of-the-art slide-level classification on 7 datasets across 6 cancers.
AI.motivation: WSIs are gigapixel; MIL typically uses Euclidean embeddings; this misses hierarchical organization and regional heterogeneity, limiting performance and generalization.
AI.method: Embed patch features in dual hyperbolic-Euclidean spaces; use S4 backbone to model long-range dependencies with linear complexity; integrate chunk-level MoE to route patches by region to specialized subnetworks; perform MIL in a two-stage framework with patch embedding and slide-level prediction, built on BatMIL architecture.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on seven WSI datasets spanning six cancer types show BatMIL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MIL methods for slide-level classification.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware representation learning, via dual geometric spaces and region-aware MoE, is a promising direction for computational pathology; enhances modeling of tissue hierarchy and cellular detail in WSIs.

title: When Does Gene Regulatory Network Inference Break? A Controlled Diagnostic Study of Causal and Correlational Methods on Single-Cell Data
summary: Despite theoretical advantages, causal methods for Gene Regulatory Network (GRN) inference from single-cell RNA-seq data consistently fail to match or outperform correlation-based baselines in many realistic benchmarks, a persistent puzzle which casts doubt on the value of causality for this task. We argue that existing benchmarks are insufficiently controlled to answer this question because they evaluate on real or semi-real data where multiple pathologies co-occur, confounding failure modes, and obscuring the specific conditions under which different inference methods excel or fail. To address this gap, we introduce a controlled diagnostic framework that isolates seven biologically motivated pathologies (dropout, latent confounders, cell-type mixing, feedback loops, network density, sample size, and pseudotime drift) and measure how six representative methods spanning three inference paradigms degrade as each pathology intensifies. Across 6,120 controlled experiments, we find that causal methods genuinely dominate in clean and structurally favorable regimes, but specific pathologies (notably dropout and latent confounders) selectively neutralize their advantages. We further introduce an error-type decomposition that reveals methods with similar aggregate accuracy commit qualitatively different errors. To probe whether single-pathology effects persist when multiple stressors co-occur, we perform an interaction sweep over the three most impactful pathologies and find that their joint effects are sub-additive, while also exposing density-conditional cross-overs invisible to single-dial analysis. Our findings offer a nuanced understanding of when and why different methods succeed or fail for GRN inference, providing actionable insights for method development and practical guidance for practitioners.

AI.tldr: Controlled diagnostic framework for GRN inference from scRNA-seq; causal methods outperform in clean settings but are mitigated by dropout and latent confounders; reveals error-type differences and sub-additive interactions among pathologies.
AI.motivation: Address inconsistent performance of causal GRN inference methods in real benchmarks by isolating biologically motivated pathologies.
AI.method: Introduce seven pathologies (dropout, latent confounders, cell-type mixing, feedback loops, network density, sample size, pseudotime drift); test six methods across three inference paradigms; conduct 6,120 controlled experiments; perform an error-type decomposition; execute an interaction sweep across top-pathology(s).
AI.result: Causal methods dominate under clean/structurally favorable conditions; dropout and latent confounders selectively neutralize their advantages; error-type decomposition reveals qualitatively different errors among methods with similar aggregate accuracy; joint effects of the three most impactful pathologies are sub-additive, with density causing cross-overs invisible to single-dial analyses.
AI.conclusion: Provides a nuanced understanding of when and why different methods succeed or fail for GRN inference; offers actionable guidance for method development and practical guidance for practitioners.

title: OpenSearch-VL: An Open Recipe for Frontier Multimodal Search Agents
summary: Deep search has become a crucial capability for frontier multimodal agents, enabling models to solve complex questions through active search, evidence verification, and multi-step reasoning. Despite rapid progress, top-tier multimodal search agents remain difficult to reproduce, largely due to the absence of open high-quality training data, transparent trajectory synthesis pipelines, or detailed training recipes. To this end, we introduce OpenSearch-VL, a fully open-source recipe for training frontier multimodal deep search agents with agentic reinforcement learning. First, we curated a dedicated pipeline to construct high-quality training data through Wikipedia path sampling, fuzzy entity rewriting, and source-anchor visual grounding, which jointly reduce shortcuts and one-step retrieval collapse. Based on this pipeline, we curate two training datasets, SearchVL-SFT-36k for SFT and SearchVL-RL-8k for RL. Besides, we design a diverse tool environment that unifies text search, image search, OCR, cropping, sharpening, super-resolution, and perspective correction, enabling agents to combine active perception with external knowledge acquisition. Finally, we propose a multi-turn fatal-aware GRPO training algorithm that handles cascading tool failures by masking post-failure tokens while preserving useful pre-failure reasoning through one-sided advantage clamping. Built on this recipe, OpenSearch-VL delivers substantial performance gains, with over 10-point average improvements across seven benchmarks, and achieves results comparable to proprietary commercial models on several tasks. We will release all data, code, and models to support open research on multimodal deep search agents.

AI.tldr: OpenSearch-VL provides an open-source recipe to train frontier multimodal deep search agents using agentic RL, featuring a reproducible data construction pipeline, two training datasets, a unified tool environment, and a fatal-aware GRPO training algorithm; reported gains of ~10 points across seven benchmarks and competitive performance with proprietary models; data/code/models to be released.
AI.motivation: Address reproducibility and transparency gaps in state-of-the-art multimodal search agents by providing open data, transparent pipelines, and training recipes, mitigating shortcuts and one-step retrieval collapse.
AI.method: Develop a data construction pipeline (Wikipedia path sampling, fuzzy entity rewriting, source-anchor grounding) to create two datasets (SearchVL-SFT-36k and SearchVL-RL-8k); design a diverse tool environment integrating text/image search, OCR, cropping, sharpening, super-resolution, perspective correction; introduce a multi-turn fatal-aware GRPO training algorithm that masks post-failure tokens and uses one-sided advantage clamping to preserve prior reasoning.
AI.result: OpenSearch-VL yields over 10-point average improvements across seven benchmarks and achieves results comparable to proprietary commercial models on several tasks.
AI.conclusion: An open-source, end-to-end recipe for frontier multimodal deep search agents that combines robust data curation, flexible tool use, and agentic RL, enabling broader reproducibility and further research in multimodal AI.

title: LoViF 2026 The First Challenge on Holistic Quality Assessment for 4D World Model (PhyScore)
summary:
This paper reports on the LoViF 2026 PhyScore challenge, a competition on holistic quality assessment of world-model-generated videos across both 2D and 4D generation settings. The challenge is motivated by a central gap in current evaluation practice: perceptual quality alone is insufficient to judge whether generated dynamics are physically plausible, temporally coherent, and consistent with input conditions. Participants are required to build a metric that jointly predicts four dimensions, i.e., Video Quality, Physical Realism, Condition-Video Alignment, and Temporal Consistency. Depart from that, participants also need to localize physical anomaly timestamps for fine-grained diagnosis.
  The benchmark dataset contains 1,554 videos generated by seven representative world generative models, organized into three tracks (text-2D, image-to-4D, and video-to-4D) and spanning 26 categories. These categories explicitly cover physics-relevant scenarios, including dynamics, optics, and thermodynamics, together with diverse real-world and creative content. To ensure label reliability, scores and anomaly timestamps are produced through trained human annotation with an additional automated quality-control pass.
  Evaluation is based on both score prediction and anomaly localization, with a composite protocol that combines TimeStamp_IOU and SRCC/PLCC. This report summarizes the challenge design and provides method-level insights from submitted solutions.

AI.tldr: A holistic evaluation challenge (LoViF 2026 PhyScore) for world-model-generated videos across 2D/4D, requiring a joint metric over Video Quality, Physical Realism, Condition-Video Alignment, and Temporal Consistency, plus anomaly timestamp localization; dataset of 1,554 videos across three tracks and 26 categories; evaluation combines TimeStamp_IOU with SRCC/PLCC; design and method-level insights reported.
AI.motivation: Perceptual quality alone is insufficient to judge physical plausibility, temporal coherence, and input-condition fidelity in dynamic, world-model-generated videos; a joint metric and fine-grained anomaly localization are needed.
AI.method: Participants build a joint metric predicting four dimensions (Video Quality, Physical Realism, Condition-Video Alignment, Temporal Consistency) and localize physical anomaly timestamps. Dataset contains 1,554 videos from seven world generative models, organized into three tracks (text-2D, image-to-4D, video-to-4D) across 26 physics-relevant categories. Label reliability ensured via trained human annotation with automated QC. Evaluation combines score prediction and anomaly localization using TimeStamp_IOU and SRCC/PLCC; the report summarizes challenge design and method-level insights from submissions.
AI.result: As described in the abstract, results are focused on challenge design and methodological insights; no quantitative results are provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The report concludes with a summary of the challenge design and insights derived from submitted solutions.

title: D-OPSD: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Continuously Tuning Step-Distilled Diffusion Models
summary: The landscape of high-performance image generation models is currently shifting from the inefficient multi-step ones to the efficient few-step counterparts (e.g, Z-Image-Turbo and FLUX.2-klein). However, these models present significant challenges for directly continuous supervised fine-tuning. For example, applying the commonly used fine-tuning technique would compromises their inherent few-step inference capability. To address this, we propose D-OPSD, a novel training paradigm for step-distilled diffusion models that enables on-policy learning during supervised fine-tuning. We first find that the modern diffusion model where the LLM/VLM serves as the encoder can inherit its encoder's in-context capabilities. This enables us to make the training as an on-policy self-distillation process. Specifically, during training, we make the model acts as both the teacher and the student with different contexts, where the student is conditioned only on the text feature, while the teacher is conditioned on the multimodal feature of both the text prompt and the target image. Training minimizes the two predicted distributions over the student's own roll-outs. By optimized on the model's own trajectory and under it's own supervision, D-OPSD enables the model to learn new concept, style, etc. without sacrificing the original few-step capacity.

AI.tldr: D-OPSD enables on-policy self-distillation for step-distilled diffusion models to allow supervised fine-tuning without sacrificing few-step inference.
AI.motivation: Efficient few-step diffusion models are difficult to fine-tune with standard supervised methods without increasing inference steps; a fine-tuning paradigm is needed that preserves few-step performance while enabling new concept/style learning.
AI.method: Introduce D-OPSD, a training paradigm where the model acts as both teacher and student in an on-policy setup. The student is conditioned only on text features, while the teacher uses multimodal features (text prompt + target image). The model learns by minimizing two predicted distributions over the student’s own roll-outs, leveraging the encoder’s in-context capabilities to enable self-distillation during supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Not provided in the abstract; the work presents a framework and rationale rather than empirical results.
AI.conclusion: D-OPSD offers a principled on-policy self-distillation approach for fine-tuning step-distilled diffusion models that preserves few-step inference while enabling learning of new concepts and styles.

title: EP-GRPO: Entropy-Progress Aligned Group Relative Policy Optimization with Implicit Process Guidance
summary:
Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), particularly Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has advanced LLM reasoning. However, GRPO suffers from three credit assignment failures: uniform token-level granularity that ignores heterogeneous informational value, uniform polarity that penalizes correct steps and rewards incorrect ones, and zero-variance collapse that erases outcome-driven gradients. We systematically quantify these failures, revealing highly non-uniform token informativeness, widespread step-level polarity misalignment, and substantial training waste.
  To address these limitations, we propose Entropy-Progress Aligned GRPO (EP-GRPO), a framework that mines the model's intrinsic information flow for dense, self-supervised guidance. EP-GRPO integrates entropy-gated modulation to prioritize high entropy decision pivots, implicit process signals from policy divergence anchored to outcome advantages for directional token-level feedback without external reward models, and cumulative entropy mapping that enables progress-aligned advantage normalization, naturally maintaining gradient flow under zero reward variance.
  Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that EP-GRPO achieves superior accuracy and efficiency compared to GRPO and its variants. The code will be available.

AI.tldr: EP-GRPO addresses three credit-assignment flaws in GRPO by leveraging (1) entropy-gated token selection, (2) implicit process signals via policy-divergence anchored to outcome advantages, and (3) cumulative entropy mapping for progress-aligned normalization, enabling self-supervised guidance without external reward models. It yields improved accuracy and efficiency on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: GRPO's credit-assignment is hampered by (i) uniform token-level granularity that ignores informative heterogeneity, (ii) uniform polarity that misweights correct vs. incorrect steps, and (iii) zero-variance collapses that erase outcome-driven gradients. These deficiencies lead to non-uniform token informativeness and wasted training.
AI.method: Introduce Entropy-Progress Aligned GRPO (EP-GRPO): (a) entropy-gated modulation to prioritize high-entropy decision pivots; (b) implicit process signals from policy divergence anchored to outcome advantages for directional token-level feedback without external reward models; (c) cumulative entropy mapping for progress-aligned advantage normalization to preserve gradient flow under zero reward variance.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on mathematical reasoning benchmarks shows EP-GRPO achieving higher accuracy and efficiency than GRPO and its variants; code will be available.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-based, self-supervised guidance effectively remedies GRPO's credit-assignment failures, enabling dense feedback and robust gradient flow, with superior performance on reasoning benchmarks.

title: Taming Outlier Tokens in Diffusion Transformers
summary: We study outlier tokens in Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) for image generation. Prior work has shown that Vision Transformers (ViTs) can produce a small number of high-norm tokens that attract disproportionate attention while carrying limited local information, but their role in generative models remains underexplored. We show that this phenomenon appears in both the encoder and denoiser of modern Representation Autoencoder (RAE)-DiT pipelines: pretrained ViT encoders can produce outlier representations, and DiTs themselves can develop internal outlier tokens, especially in intermediate layers. Moreover, simply masking high-norm tokens does not improve performance, indicating that the problem is not only caused by a few extreme values, but is more closely related to corrupted local patch semantics. To address this issue, we introduce Dual-Stage Registers (DSR), a register-based intervention for both components: trained registers when available, recursive test-time registers otherwise, and diffusion registers for the denoiser. Across ImageNet and large-scale text-to-image generation, these interventions consistently reduce outlier artifacts and improve generation quality. Our results highlight outlier-token control as an important ingredient in building stronger DiTs.

AI.tldr: Outlier tokens in Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) cause artifacts; masking high-norm tokens is ineffective; Dual-Stage Registers (DSR) mitigate outlier effects in both encoder and denoiser, improving image and text-to-image generation.
AI.motivation: High-norm, potentially corrupted tokens can dominate attention and degrade local semantics in ViT-based diffusion models; this issue arises in RAEs-DiT pipelines and harms generation quality.
AI.method: Introduce Dual-Stage Registers (DSR): trained registers when available, recursive test-time registers otherwise for the encoder/RAE components, and diffusion registers for the denoiser; evaluate on ImageNet and large-scale text-to-image generation.
AI.result: DSR reduces outlier artifacts and consistently improves generation quality across evaluated tasks.
AI.conclusion: Controlling outlier tokens is a key factor for stronger DiTs; DSR provides a general, effective intervention that can extend to other ViT-based generative models.

title: FAAST: Forward-Only Associative Learning via Closed-Form Fast Weights for Test-Time Supervised Adaptation
summary: Adapting pretrained models typically involves a trade-off between the high training costs of backpropagation and the heavy inference overhead of memory-based or in-context learning. We propose FAAST, a forward-only associative adaptation method that analytically compiles labeled examples into fast weights in a single pass. By eliminating memory or context dependence, FAAST achieves constant-time inference and decouples task adaptation from pretrained representation. Across image classification and language modeling benchmarks, FAAST matches or exceeds backprop-based adaptation while reducing adaptation time by over 90\% and is competitive to memory/context-based adaptation while saving memory usage by up to 95\%. These results demonstrate FAAST as a highly efficient, scalable solution for supervised task adaptation, particularly for resource-constrained models. We release the code and models at https://github.com/baoguangsheng/faast.

AI.tldr: FAAST is a forward-only, analytical fast-weight adaptation method that compiles labeled examples into fast weights in one pass, achieving constant-time inference and reducing adaptation costs while matching or surpassing backprop-based methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the high training costs of backpropagation and the inference overhead of memory-based or in-context learning in model adaptation, by providing a scalable, efficient alternative.
AI.method: FAAST performs forward-only associative adaptation that analytically compiles labeled examples into fast weights in a single pass, removing dependency on memory or context and enabling constant-time inference; it decouples task adaptation from pretrained representations.
AI.result: On image classification and language modeling benchmarks, FAAST matches or exceeds backprop-based adaptation, reduces adaptation time by over 90%, and remains competitive with memory/context-based methods while saving up to 95% memory usage.
AI.conclusion: FAAST offers a highly efficient, scalable solution for supervised task adaptation, particularly for resource-constrained models; the authors release code and models for reproducibility.

title: Reliable Modeling of Distribution Shifts via Displacement-Reshaped Optimal Transport
summary: Optimal transport (OT) is a central framework for modeling distribution shifts. Because OT compares distributions directly in input space, a well-designed ground metric between observations is essential to ensure that the optimizer does not violate the true geometry of change. We propose Displacement-Reshaped Optimal Transport (ReshapeOT), a method that reshapes the ground metric by integrating observed sample displacements as an additional source of knowledge. Technically, ReshapeOT replaces the Euclidean metric with a Mahalanobis distance estimated from displacement second moments. This effectively carves expressways through the input space, inviting transport solutions that better align with observed displacements. Our method is computationally lightweight, integrates seamlessly into any OT solver that operates on a cost matrix, and can be kernelized for further flexibility. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data show that ReshapeOT achieves substantial gains in transport reliability. We further demonstrate our method's usefulness in two practical use cases.

AI.tldr: ReshapeOT introduces a displacement-informed Mahalanobis ground metric for OT, improving transport reliability by aligning transport with observed displacements while remaining solver-compatible and kernelizable.
AI.motivation: OT is widely used to model distribution shifts, but the choice of ground metric is crucial; Euclidean distance may not reflect the true geometry of observed transport. Incorporating displacement information directly into the metric can steer OT solutions toward more realistic transport paths.
AI.method: Replace the Euclidean ground metric with a Mahalanobis distance estimated from the second moments of observed displacements. This reshapes the input space to favor transport directions consistent with data. The approach is lightweight, works with any OT solver using a cost matrix, and can be kernelized for added flexibility.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world data show substantial improvements in transport reliability when using the reshape metric. Two practical use cases demonstrate the method's applicability and benefits beyond standard OT.
AI.conclusion: ReshapeOT provides a simple, efficient way to inject displacement information into OT through a reshaped ground metric, with broad compatibility and demonstrated gains in reliability and practicality.

title: Syn4D: A Multiview Synthetic 4D Dataset
summary: Dense 3D reconstruction and tracking of dynamic scenes from monocular video remains an important open challenge in computer vision. Progress in this area has been constrained by the scarcity of high-quality datasets with dense, complete, and accurate geometric annotations. To address this limitation, we introduce Syn4D, a multiview synthetic dataset of dynamic scenes that includes ground-truth camera motion, depth maps, dense tracking, and parametric human pose annotations. A key feature of Syn4D is the ability to unproject any pixel into 3D to any time and to any camera. We conduct extensive evaluations across multiple downstream tasks to demonstrate the utility and effectiveness of the proposed dataset, including 4D scene reconstruction, 3D point tracking, geometry-aware camera retargeting, and human pose estimation. The experimental results highlight Syn4D's potential to facilitate research in dynamic scene understanding and spatiotemporal modeling.

AI.tldr: Introduces Syn4D, a large multiview synthetic dataset for dynamic scenes with ground-truth camera motion, depth maps, dense tracking, and parametric human pose annotations; enables unprojection of any pixel to 3D across time/cameras; evaluated on 4D reconstruction, 3D tracking, camera retargeting, and pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of high-quality datasets with dense, complete geometric annotations for dynamic scene understanding and spatiotemporal modeling.
AI.method: Synthetic multiview dataset generation of dynamic scenes with per-pixel depth, camera motion, dense tracking, and parametric human pose annotations; supports unprojection of any pixel to 3D at any time for any camera; comprehensive evaluations on multiple downstream tasks.
