AI.result: Extensive experiments across 4D reconstruction, 3D point tracking, geometry-aware camera retargeting, and human pose estimation demonstrating the utility of Syn4D for dynamic scene understanding.
AI.conclusion: Syn4D provides a valuable resource for advancing dynamic scene understanding and spatiotemporal modeling; the unprojection capability and rich annotations enable new benchmarks and baselines for multi-view and 4D tasks.

title: Threshold-Guided Optimization for Visual Generative Models
summary: Aligning large visual generative models with human feedback is often performed through pairwise preference optimization. While such approaches are conceptually simple, they fundamentally rely on annotated pairs, limiting scalability in settings where feedback is collected as independent scalar ratings. In this work, we revisit the KL-regularized alignment objective and show that the optimal policy implicitly compares each sample's reward to an instance-specific baseline that is generally intractable. We propose a threshold-guided alignment framework that replaces this oracle baseline with a data-driven global threshold estimated from empirical score statistics. This formulation turns alignment into a binary decision task on unpaired data, enabling effective optimization directly from scalar feedback. We also incorporate a confidence weighting term to emphasize samples whose scores deviate strongly from the threshold, improving sample efficiency. Experiments across both diffusion and masked generative paradigms, spanning three test sets and five reward models, show that our method consistently improves preference alignment over previous methods. These results position our threshold-guided framework as a simple yet principled alternative for aligning visual generative models without paired comparisons.

AI.tldr: Threshold-guided alignment using scalar feedback converts unpaired data into a binary decision task with a global data-driven threshold and confidence weighting, improving alignment of visual generative models without requiring paired comparisons.
AI.motivation: Pairwise preference learning for alignment relies on annotated pairs and does not scale well when feedback is available as independent scalar ratings. A scalable method that leverages scalar feedback is needed.
AI.method: Re-examine KL-regularized alignment; show that the optimal policy depends on an instance-specific (intractable) baseline. Replace this with a data-driven global threshold estimated from empirical score statistics. Frame alignment as a binary decision on unpaired data, and add a confidence weighting term that emphasizes samples far from the threshold.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diffusion and masked generative models, three test sets, and five reward models shows consistent improvement in preference alignment over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: The threshold-guided framework offers a simple yet principled alternative for aligning visual generative models using scalar, unpaired feedback without requiring pairwise comparisons, with improved sample efficiency through confidence weighting.

title: Skill Neologisms: Towards Skill-based Continual Learning
summary: Modern LLMs show mastery over an ever-growing range of skills, as well as the ability to compose them flexibly. However, extending model capabilities to new skills in a scalable manner is an open-problem: fine-tuning and parameter-efficient variants risk catastrophic forgetting, while context-based approaches have limited expressiveness and are constrained by the model's effective context. We explore skill neologisms--i.e., soft tokens integrated in the model's vocabulary and optimized to improve capabilities over a specific skill--as a way to selectively extend model capabilities to new skills without weight updates. We first observe that off-the-shelf pre-trained LLMs already demonstrate tokens associated with procedural knowledge. We then show that skill neologisms can be learned to improve model capabilities on specific skills while being composable with out-of-distribution skills, and that independently trained skill neologisms can be composed zero-shot. These results suggest that skill neologisms may provide a scalable path towards skill-based continual learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes skill neologisms—soft tokens embedded in the LLM vocabulary—for scalable skill extension without weight updates; demonstrates learnability, composability with out-of-distribution skills, and zero-shot composition.
AI.motivation: Address the scalability of extending model capabilities without catastrophic forgetting or heavy fine-tuning; seeks a weight-free, composable mechanism to add new skills.
AI.method: Show that pre-trained LLMs already contain procedural tokens; train soft tokens (skill neologisms) to enhance a specific skill; evaluate composability with out-of-distribution skills and zero-shot use of independently trained neologisms.
AI.result: Skill neologisms improve performance on targeted skills; they are composable with other skills including OOD ones; independent neologisms can be composed without additional training.
AI.conclusion: Skill neologisms offer a scalable, weight-free path for skill-based continual learning and modular extension of LLM capabilities.

title: Evidence-based anomaly detection in clinical domains
comment: Published at AMIA Annual Symposium 2007. PDF-only submission; LaTeX source not available (paper was authored in Word)
summary: Anomaly detection methods can be very useful in identifying interesting or concerning events. In this work, we develop and examine new probabilistic anomaly detection methods that let us evaluate management decisions for a specific patient and identify those decisions that are highly unusual with respect to patients with the same or similar condition. The statistics used in this detection are derived from probabilistic models such as Bayesian networks that are learned from a database of past patient cases. We apply our methods to the problem of identifying unusual patient-management decisions in post-surgical cardiac patients.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic anomaly detection for patient management decisions using Bayesian networks to detect unusual decisions among similar patients in post-surgical cardiac care.
AI.motivation: Identify and evaluate management decisions that are highly unusual compared to similar patients to support evidence-based decision-making and quality assurance.
AI.method: Learn Bayesian networks from a database of past patient cases; derive probabilistic statistics to flag unusual decisions; apply to post-surgical cardiac patients.
AI.result: Unusual patient-management decisions can be identified; the approach provides probabilistic anomaly scores and a framework for detecting decisions that deviate from expected practice.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic anomaly detection with learned models can assist evaluation of management decisions and enhance patient safety in cardiac surgery by highlighting atypical practices.

title: Why Geometric Continuity Emerges in Deep Neural Networks: Residual Connections and Rotational Symmetry Breaking
comment: 9 pages of main text, plus appendices. Under review at NeurIPS 2026
summary: Weight matrices in deep networks exhibit geometric continuity -- principal singular vectors of adjacent layers point in similar directions. While this property has been widely observed, its origin remains unexplained. Through experiments on toy MLPs and small transformers, we identify two mechanisms: residual connections create cross-layer gradient coherence that aligns weight updates across layers, and symmetry-breaking nonlinearities constrain all layers to a shared coordinate frame, preventing the rotation drift that would otherwise destabilize weight structure. Crucially, a nonlinear but rotation-preserving activation fails to retain continuity, isolating symmetry breaking -- not nonlinearity itself -- as the active ingredient. Activation and normalization play distinct roles: activation concentrates continuity in the leading singular direction, while normalization distributes it across multiple directions. In transformers, continuity is projection-specific: Q, K, Gate, and Up (which read from the residual stream) develop input-space ($\mathbf{v}_1$) continuity; O and Down (which write to it) develop output-space ($\mathbf{u}_1$) continuity; V alone, lacking an adjacent nonlinearity, develops only low continuity.

AI.tldr: Weight matrices in deep nets show geometric continuity across layers; two mechanisms explain it: cross-layer gradient coherence from residual connections and symmetry-breaking nonlinearities that lock layers into a shared coordinate frame; activation and normalization distribute continuity differently; in transformers, continuity is projection-specific across Q/K/G/U vs O/Down; V develops little continuity.
AI.motivation: To explain the origin of observed cross-layer singular-vector alignment and why weight updates across layers stay coherent despite depth; identify roles of residuals, nonlinearities, activation, and normalization; examine rotation-preserving vs rotation-breaking nonlinearities.
AI.method: Empirical experiments on toy MLPs and small transformers; manipulate architectural components (residual connections, nonlinearities) and activations; analyze leading singular vectors of weight matrices and their continuity across layers; test with rotation-preserving nonlinearities to isolate symmetry-breaking; assess activation vs normalization effects; examine transformer modules Q, K, Gate, Up, O, Down, V and their continuity in input vs output spaces.
AI.result: Two mechanisms: (1) residual connections induce cross-layer gradient coherence aligning weight updates; (2) symmetry-breaking nonlinearities constrain all layers to a shared coordinate frame, preventing rotation drift. Rotation-preserving nonlinearities fail to preserve continuity, indicating symmetry breaking—not nonlinearity—drives continuity. Activation concentrates continuity into the leading singular direction, while normalization distributes it across multiple directions. In transformers, continuity is projection-specific: Q/K/G/U (read from residual stream) show input-space continuity; O/Down (write to residual) show output-space continuity; V shows low continuity due to lacking an adjacent nonlinearity.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry breaking is the key active ingredient for cross-layer continuity; nonlinearity alone is insufficient. Activation and normalization shape how continuity is distributed. In transformers, the pattern of continuity aligns with read/write roles relative to the residual stream; this points to how architectural design controls cross-layer alignment.

title: Feature importance analysis for patient management decisions
comment: Published at MEDINFO 2010. doi:10.3233/978-1-60750-588-4-861. PDF-only submission; LaTeX source not available
summary: The objective of this paper is to understand what characteristics and features of clinical data influence physician's decision about ordering laboratory tests or prescribing medications the most. We conduct our analysis on data and decisions extracted from electronic health records of 4486 post-surgical cardiac patients. The summary statistics for 335 different lab order decisions and 407 medication decisions are reported. We show that in many cases, physician's lab-order and medication decisions can be well predicted from a small subset of all features.

AI.tldr: Physician lab-order and medication decisions can be well predicted from a small subset of clinical features using EHR data from 4,486 post-surgical cardiac patients.
AI.motivation: Identify which data features most influence clinicians' ordering decisions to inform decision-support and reduce unnecessary testing.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of EHR-derived decisions for 335 lab-order instances and 407 medication instances; evaluated predictive power using a subset of features (feature-importance/subset selection).
AI.result: In many cases, decisions can be well predicted from a small subset of all features.
AI.conclusion: Key features drive ordering decisions; a focused feature set could underpin decision-support to streamline lab tests and prescriptions; further work needed to identify the most predictive features and assess generalizability.

title: Federated Learning for Early Prediction of EV Charging Demand
summary: Accurate forecasting of electric vehicle (EV) charging demand is critical for grid stability, infrastructure planning, and real-time charging optimization. In this work, we study the problem of early prediction of charging demand, where the total energy of a session is estimated using only information available at plug-in time and during the first minutes of charging. This enables actionable decisions while the session is still in progress, which is of direct importance for EV network operators. We construct a session-level dataset from the Adaptive Charging Network (ACN), combining session metadata with early-window charging measurements, and derive tabular features capturing user intent, temporal patterns, and initial charging behavior. We focus on a single operational depot, Caltech, and model intra-depot heterogeneity through station-level client partitions while evaluating multiple model families in a federated learning (FL) setting. Our results show that federated models can approach centralized predictive performance while keeping data in-depot, enabling privacy-enhanced training across distributed charging infrastructures. Overall, we demonstrate that reliable demand estimates can be obtained early in the session with minimal data, and that FL provides a practical pathway toward scalable and privacy-aware analytics for EV charging networks. Code is available at https://github.com/Indigma-Innovations/federated-learning-ev-charging-demand.

AI.tldr: Early-session EV charging demand forecasting using federated learning across a single depot; models achieve near-centralized accuracy with privacy benefits, enabling scalable analytics; code released.
AI.motivation: Need actionable, early-demand estimates for EV charging to support grid stability and infrastructure planning while preserving user privacy across distributed charging networks.
AI.method: Construct a session-level dataset from the Adaptive Charging Network (ACN); derive features on user intent, temporal patterns, and initial charging behavior from early-window measurements; model intra-depot heterogeneity with station-level client partitions; evaluate multiple model families in a federated learning setup at Caltech depot; compare with centralized baselines.
AI.result: Federated models approach centralized predictive performance, enabling privacy-preserving training across distributed charging stations; reliable demand estimates can be obtained with minimal data; code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Federated learning offers a practical pathway for scalable, privacy-aware EV charging analytics and early-demand forecasting within depot networks; potential for extension to broader multi-depot deployments.

title: ITBoost: Information-Theoretic Trust for Robust Boosting
summary: Gradient boosting remains a strong and widely used method for tabular data learning, but its performance often degrades when training labels are noisy. This behavior is largely related to the way boosting algorithms emphasize samples with large gradients, without explicitly accounting for whether such errors originate from informative hard cases or from unreliable labels. We address this issue by reconsidering how sample reliability is evaluated during boosting. Instead of relying on instantaneous error, we examine the evolution of each sample's residuals across iterations. Based on this insight, we propose Information-Theoretic Trust Boosting (ITBoost), which uses the Minimum Description Length principle to measure the complexity of residual trajectories. Samples whose residual patterns fluctuate in an irregular manner are treated as less trustworthy and are down-weighted during learning. Theoretically, we derive a tighter generalization bound for ITBoost under label noise. Empirical results on various tabular benchmarks indicate that ITBoost provides improved robustness in noisy environments over leading boosting and deep tabular models, while retaining best average performance on clean data.

AI.tldr: ITBoost introduces an MDL-based measure of residual trajectory complexity to assess sample reliability in boosting, down-weighting samples with irregular residual patterns to improve robustness to noisy labels while maintaining strong performance on clean data.
AI.motivation: Boosting often overemphasizes samples with large instantaneous errors, which can be noisy labels. A reliability criterion that accounts for the trajectory of residuals across iterations is needed to mitigate label noise effects.
AI.method: Track the evolution of each sample's residuals through boosting iterations. Use the Minimum Description Length principle to quantify the complexity of these residual trajectories. Down-weight samples with irregular residual patterns. Provide a theoretical generalization bound under label noise.
AI.result: Empirical results on tabular benchmarks show ITBoost achieves superior robustness to label noise compared with leading boosting and deep tabular models, while preserving best-average performance on clean data.
AI.conclusion: ITBoost offers a principled, MDL-based approach to sample weighting in boosting, yielding improved robustness to noisy labels and strong performance on clean data, supported by a tighter generalization bound.

title: HEXST: Hexagonal Shifted-Window Transformer for Spatial Transcriptomics Gene Expression Prediction
summary: Spatial transcriptomics offers spatially resolved gene expression profiling within tissue sections, but its cost and limited throughput hinder large-scale deployment. To extend this capability to routine practice, recent computational methods aim to infer spatial gene expression directly from ubiquitous hematoxylin and eosin-stained histology slides. However, most existing models assume Cartesian or geometry-agnostic locality, despite the hexagonal sampling of widely used spot-array platforms, and point-wise regression objectives often yield over-smoothed gene expression profiles, obscuring gene-specific spatial heterogeneity. To address these, we propose HEXST, a geometry-aligned Transformer for spatial gene expression prediction from histology. HEXST operates directly on hexagonal spot coordinates to enable efficient local-to-global contextual modeling via tailored shifted-window attention mechanism and hexagonal rotary positional encoding. To enhance gene-wise spatial contrast, HEXST complements point-wise regression with a contrast-sensitive differential objective and transcriptomic priors from a pretrained single-cell foundation model during training. Across seven spatial transcriptomics datasets, HEXST consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, providing accurate and robust spatial gene expression predictions while preserving gene-wise contrast and spatial heterogeneity.

AI.tldr: HEXST is a geometry-aligned Transformer for predicting spatial gene expression from histology by operating on hexagonal spot coordinates with a shifted-window attention and hexagonal rotary positional encoding; it also uses a contrast-sensitive objective and literature priors to preserve gene-wise spatial heterogeneity, outperforming baselines on seven datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for inferring spatial gene expression from H&E slides assume Cartesian locality or geometry-agnostic locality and often produce over-smoothed, gene-agnostic predictions. There is a need to align models to hexagonal sampling patterns and preserve gene-specific spatial heterogeneity.
AI.method: HEXST introduces a geometry-aligned Transformer that operates directly on hexagonal spot coordinates, using tailored shifted-window attention and hexagonal rotary positional encoding. Training includes a contrast-sensitive differential objective and transcriptomic priors from a pretrained single-cell foundation model.
AI.result: HEXST consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models across seven spatial transcriptomics datasets, delivering accurate spatial gene expression predictions while preserving gene-wise contrast and spatial heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: HEXST demonstrates that geometry-aware, hexagon-aligned attention and gene-wise objectives can significantly improve the fidelity of histology-to-spatial gene expression inference, enabling more robust and interpretable spatial transcriptomics analyses.

title: Preference-Based Self-Distillation: Beyond KL Matching via Reward Regularization
summary: On-policy distillation is an efficient alternative to reinforcement learning, offering dense token-level training signals. However, its reliance on a stronger external teacher has driven recent work on on-policy self-distillation, where the same model serves as both teacher and student under different prompt contexts. Yet, existing self-distillation methods largely reduce learning to KL matching toward the context-augmented teacher model. This approach often suffers from training instability and can degrade reasoning performance over time. Moreover, self-distillation from the same model with prompt augmentation lacks the exploratory diversity provided by a genuine external teacher. To address these limitations, we move beyond fixed-teacher KL matching and propose \textbf{P}reference-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{D}istillation (\textbf{PBSD}), which revisits on-policy self-distillation through a reward-regularized perspective. Instead of directly matching the teacher distribution, we derive a reward-regularized objective whose analytic optimum is a reward-reweighted teacher distribution, yielding a target policy provably superior to the original teacher under this objective. Practically, PBSD optimizes preference gaps between teacher and student samples while maintaining on-policy student sampling. We support this framework with a statistical analysis of the induced preference-learning problem, formally establishing when on policy self-distillation is preferable to learning from an external teacher in our setting. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and tool-use benchmarks across multiple model scales demonstrate that PBSD consistently achieves the strongest average performance among comparable baselines, showing improved training stability over prior self-distillation baselines while preserving token efficiency.

AI.tldr: PBSD introduces a reward-regularized on-policy self-distillation that uses reward-weighted teacher distributions to guide the student, improving stability and performance without relying on a fixed external teacher.
AI.motivation: Existing on-policy self-distillation relies on KL matching to a context-augmented teacher, which can cause training instability, degrade reasoning, and lack exploration. An external teacher provides diversity but is not always available; there is a need for a principled, stable, on-policy method that leverages preferences rather than direct distribution matching.
AI.method: Derive a reward-regularized objective for self-distillation; the analytic optimum is a reward-reweighted teacher distribution. PBSD optimizes preference gaps between teacher and student samples while keeping on-policy student sampling, supported by a statistical analysis of when self-distillation is preferable to external-teacher learning.
AI.result: Empirical results on mathematical reasoning and tool-use benchmarks across multiple model scales show PBSD achieves the strongest average performance among baselines, with improved training stability over prior self-distillation methods and preserved token efficiency.
AI.conclusion: PBSD offers a theoretically-grounded, stability-improving self-distillation framework that can outperform external-teacher baselines in the on-policy setting, demonstrated across reasoning and tool-use tasks.

title: Learning Time-Inhomogeneous Markov Dynamics in Financial Time Series via Neural Parameterization
comment: 10 pages, 10 figures and 1 table. Presented at The 2026 ASA Midwest Regional Conference in Statistics and Data Science and the 2026 Undergraduate Symposium at the University of Wisconsin - Madison
summary: Modeling the dynamics of non-stationary stochastic systems requires balancing the representational power of deep learning with the mathematical transparency of classical models. While classical Markov transition operators provide explicit, theoretically grounded rules for system evolution, their empirical estimation collapses due to severe data sparsity when applied to high-resolution, high-noise environments. We explore this statistical barrier using financial time series as a canonical, real-world testbed. To overcome the degeneracy of empirical counting, we introduce a framework that utilizes neural networks strictly as parameterization engines to generate explicit, time-varying Markov transition matrices. By constraining the neural network to output its predictions as a formal stochastic operator, we maintain complete structural interpretability. We demonstrate that these learned operators successfully capture complex regime shifts: the state-conditioned model achieves mean row heterogeneity $\barρ = 0.0073$ while the state-free ablation collapses to exactly zero, and operator row entropy correlates with realized variance at $r = -0.62$ ($p \approx 10^{-251}$), revealing that high-volatility regimes homogenize transition dynamics rather than diversify them. Furthermore, rather than enforcing the Chapman-Kolmogorov equations as a rigid structural requirement, we repurpose them as a localized diagnostic tool to pinpoint specific temporal windows where first-order memory assumptions break down. Ultimately, this framework demonstrates how neural networks can be constrained to make rigorous, classical operator analysis viable for complex real-world time series.

AI.tldr: Neural nets parameterize time-varying Markov transition matrices to address sparsity while preserving interpretability; Chapman-Kolmogorov used diagnostically to locate memory breakdown in financial time series.
AI.motivation: Empirical Markov counting fails in high-resolution, noisy data due to data sparsity; need transparent, scalable models that can capture regime shifts in non-stationary stochastic systems.
AI.method: Neural network outputs a stochastic operator (rows sum to one) to define time-varying Markov transitions; compare a state-conditioned operator with a state-free ablation; use Chapman-Kolmogorov equations as a diagnostic (localization of memory breakdown) rather than a hard constraint; evaluate on financial time series and examine relationships between operator properties (row entropy) and realized variance.
AI.result: State-conditioned model achieves mean row heterogeneity 0.0073; state-free ablation collapses to zero; operator row entropy correlates with realized variance r = -0.62, p ≈ 1e-251; high volatility regimes homogenize transition dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that neural networks can be constrained to produce rigorous, classical operator analyses for complex real-world time series, enabling interpretable, non-stationary dynamic modeling; Chapman-Kolmogorov used as diagnostic to pinpoint memory breakdown windows.

title: Differentiable Chemistry in PINNs for Solving Parameterized and Stiff Reaction Systems
summary: From neural ODEs to continuous-time machine learning, differentiable solvers allow physics, optimization, and simulation to become trainable components within deep learning systems. This has opened the path to a new generation of deep learning frameworks for scientific computing, with many promising applications still emerging. In this paper, we integrate a differentiable chemistry solver into a modified physics-informed neural network to solve parameterized reaction systems that are inherently stiff. The proposed framework introduces several key components required to overcome limitations of standard physics-informed neural networks. These include a differentiable chemistry solver, a network architecture for parameterized solutions, and residual weighting tailored to stiff reactions. We evaluate the framework on a set of differential equations related to hydrogen combustion, which include initial/boundary value problems, inverse parameter identification, and a parameterized partial differential equation. Our results highlight the ability of the proposed approach to extend physics-informed neural networks to stiff chemical systems that were previously inaccessible.

AI.tldr: Integrates a differentiable chemistry solver with a modified physics-informed neural network to solve stiff reaction systems, evaluated on hydrogen combustion problems including initial/boundary value problems, inverse parameter identification, and parameterized PDEs; demonstrates extension of PINNs to stiff chemical kinetics.
AI.motivation: Differentiable solvers enable training within deep learning models for scientific computing. Stiff chemical systems pose limitations for standard physics-informed neural networks (PINNs). The work aims to enable end-to-end trainable solutions for stiff reactions by combining a differentiable chemistry solver with PINNs.
AI.method: Combine a differentiable chemistry solver with a modified physics-informed neural network architecture. Develop a parameterized solution network to handle varying parameters and implement residual weighting calibrated for stiff reactions to improve convergence and accuracy.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the proposed framework extends PINNs to stiff chemical systems previously inaccessible. Evaluated on hydrogen combustion-related differential equations, including initial/boundary value problems, inverse parameter identification, and a parameterized PDE, showing effective handling of stiffness and parameter variability.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables end-to-end trainable solutions for stiff chemical kinetics by integrating a differentiable chemistry solver with PINNs, overcoming limitations of standard PINNs and expanding applicability to complex stiff reaction systems such as hydrogen combustion.

title: Adaptive Learning Strategies for AoA-Based Outdoor Localization: A Comprehensive Framework
summary: Localization in 5G and 6G networks is essential for important use cases such as intelligent transportation, smart factories, and smart cities. Although deep learning has enabled improving localization accuracy, depending on the deployment scenario and the effort required for dataset collection campaigns on a given infrastructure, the training process for localization models can vary significantly. Furthermore, with respect to feature selection, recent works have demonstrated the robustness of angle-of-arrival (AoA) based localization. In view of these two points, we propose an adaptive framework for AoA-based localization that consists of two alternative learning strategies, each suited either for large or small training datasets. The proposed framework is evaluated on a real, massive multiple input multiple output (mMIMO) orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) outdoor channel state information (CSI) dataset. First, we investigate offline learning when large training datasets are available; we propose a hierarchical framework that first distinguishes between line of sight (LoS) and non line of sight (NLoS) regions and then moves to more fine grained localization in the respective region. This approach provides high-performance localization through accumulated batch retraining and an integrated hyperparameter optimization mechanism. Second, when only a small training dataset is available, an online learning framework is proposed, using incremental tree-based and ensemble-based models for handling streaming data and continuously updating mode, as well as an online few-shot learning model for rapidly initializing new classes from a limited labeled support set. These results showcase that highly accurate robust localization can be achieved incrementally during network operation by exploiting online learning, alleviating the need for large dataset collection campaigns.

AI.tldr: An adaptive AoA-based localization framework using offline hierarchical LoS/NLoS classification with region-specific localization and batch retraining for large datasets, plus online incremental/tree/ensemble and few-shot learning for small datasets; validated on real mMIMO-OFDM CSI data to achieve robust, accurate localization with reduced data collection needs.
AI.motivation: Localization in 5G/6G is essential and training data requirements vary by deployment. AoA-based methods offer robustness, but scalable learning strategies are needed to handle different data regimes and minimize data collection campaigns.
AI.method: Two learning strategies. Offline: a hierarchical framework that first classifies LoS vs NLoS, then performs region-specific localization; includes accumulated batch retraining and integrated hyperparameter optimization. Online: incremental tree-based and ensemble-based models for streaming data, plus an online few-shot learning model to quickly initialize new classes from limited labeled support.
AI.result: Demonstrates high-performance localization through batch retraining and hyperparameter optimization in the offline setting. In the online setting, enables accurate, robust localization to be updated incrementally during network operation, reducing reliance on large dataset campaigns.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive dual-strategy framework delivers accurate, robust AoA-based localization across data regimes, combining hierarchical offline processing with online incremental and few-shot learning to suit varying data availability in 5G/6G environments.

title: SPHERE: Mitigating the Loss of Spectral Plasticity in Mixture-of-Experts for Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: In deep reinforcement learning (DRL), an agent is trained from a stream of experience. In a continual learning setting, such agents can suffer from plasticity loss: their ability to learn new skills from new experiences diminishes over training. Recently, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) networks have been reported to enable scaling laws and facilitate the learning of diverse skills. However, in continual reinforcement learning settings, their performance can degenerate as learning proceeds, indicating a loss of plasticity. To address this, building on Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory, we formalize the plasticity loss in MoE policies as a loss of spectral plasticity. We then derive a tractable proxy for spectral plasticity, one expressible in terms of individual expert feature matrices. Leveraging this proxy, we introduce SPHERE, a practical Parseval penalty tailored for MoE-based policies that alleviates the loss of spectral plasticity. On MetaWorld and HumanoidBench, SPHERE improves average success under continual RL by 133% and 50% over an unregularized MoE baseline, while maintaining higher spectral plasticity throughout training.

AI.tldr: SPHERE: a Parseval penalty for MoE-based policies in continual RL that preserves spectral plasticity via a NTK-informed proxy, yielding large gains on MetaWorld and HumanoidBench.
AI.motivation: Continual RL with mixture-of-experts (MoE) policies suffers plasticity loss over time, hindering learning of new skills. A tractable, NTK-inspired measure of spectral plasticity and a regularizer are needed to mitigate this degradation.
AI.method: Formalize plasticity loss with Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory as spectral plasticity loss. Derive a tractable proxy expressed via individual expert feature matrices. Propose SPHERE, a Parseval penalty tailored for MoE-based policies, to preserve spectral energy and mitigate plasticity loss.
AI.result: SPHERE improves average success in continual RL by 133% on MetaWorld and 50% on HumanoidBench compared to an unregularized MoE baseline, while maintaining higher spectral plasticity throughout training.
AI.conclusion: A practical, NTK-grounded regularizer (SPHERE) effectively mitigates spectral plasticity loss in MoE-based policies, improving continual RL performance without sacrificing spectral plasticity.

title: Continual Knowledge Updating in LLM Systems: Learning Through Multi-Timescale Memory Dynamics
summary: LLMs are trained once, then deployed into a world that never stops changing. External memory compensates for this, but most systems manage it explicitly rather than letting it adapt on its own. Biological memory works differently: coupled multi-timescale dynamics make new associations immediately usable, strengthen what repetition confirms, and let the rest fade. We argue that external memory should follow a similar principle. In Memini, this view takes the form of an associative memory that organizes knowledge as a directed graph. Each edge carries two coupled internal variables, one fast and one slow, following the Benna-Fusi model of synaptic consolidation. From this coupling, episodic sensitivity, gradual consolidation, and selective forgetting emerge as facets of a single mechanism, reframing external memory as a learning substrate that reorganizes through its own dynamics.

AI.tldr: Memini proposes an associative, graph-based external memory with edges carrying fast and slow variables (Benna-Fusi). The coupled dynamics yield episodic sensitivity, consolidation, and forgetting, enabling self-organizing memory for LLMs.
AI.motivation: LLMs operate in a non-stationary world; external memory is often managed explicitly. Inspired by biological multi-timescale memory, the work aims to make external memory adapt through intrinsic dynamics.
AI.method: Implement an associative memory as a directed graph where each edge stores two coupled internal variables (fast and slow), following the Benna-Fusi synaptic consolidation model; analyze how this coupling yields emergent memory behaviors.
AI.result: The coupling produces episodic sensitivity, gradual consolidation, and selective forgetting as facets of a single mechanism; external memory reorganizes itself through its own dynamics.
AI.conclusion: External memory should be designed as a learning substrate that reorganizes via endogenous dynamics, exemplified by Memini's multi-timescale, graph-based memory model.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Exploratory Direct Preference Optimization for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has proven to be an effective solution for mitigating hallucination in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by learning from preference pairs. One of its key challenges lies in how to transfer the sequence-level preference into fine-grained supervision on visual fidelity. To safeguard vision-related tokens that are prone to hallucination, existing methods typically allocate training emphasis according to the model's self-assessed visual sensitivity signals. However, such sensitivity, estimated by a model still under training, introduces self-referential bias: reinforcing already well-learned visual cues while neglecting hard-to-perceive but critical details, thereby limiting deeper alignment. In this work, we propose an Uncertainty-aware Exploratory Direct Preference Optimization (UE-DPO) method for MLLMs, which enables the model to uncover its cognitive deficiencies and actively explore for self-correction, guided by token-level epistemic uncertainty. Specifically, we first quantify the uncertainty from the model's failure to ground token predictions in the given image. Then, based on an uncertainty-aware exploration intensity, we encourage more learning pressure on visually deficient tokens in preferred samples, and alleviate the over-penalization of beneficial knowledge in dispreferred samples. Further, we provide a theoretical justification for our method, and extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness and robustness.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty-aware Exploratory Direct Preference Optimization (UE-DPO) improves Direct Preference Optimization for multimodal LLMs by using token-level epistemic uncertainty to guide learning, focusing on visually deficient tokens and reducing self-referential bias; supported by theory and extensive experiments.
AI.motivation: Overcome self-referential bias from self-assessed visual sensitivity that reinforces easy cues and neglects hard but important details, thereby mitigating hallucinations in Multimodal LLMs.
AI.method: Quantify token-level epistemic uncertainty from failures to ground token predictions in the image; adjust exploration intensity based on uncertainty to emphasize learning on visually deficient tokens in preferred samples while reducing penalty on beneficial knowledge in dispreferred samples; provide theoretical justification.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate improved visual grounding alignment, reduced hallucinations, and robustness across different settings in MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-guided exploration enables more effective transfer of sequence-level preferences to token-level supervision for visual fidelity, offering a principled and robust solution to hallucination in MLLMs.

title: Ensuring Reliability in Programming Knowledge Tracing: A Re-evaluation of Attention-augmented Models and Experimental Protocols
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Intelligent Tutoring Systems (ITS 2026). To appear in Springer LNCS
summary: Programming Knowledge Tracing (PKT) has recently advanced through hybrid approaches that integrate attention-based feature modeling for code representation with RNN-based sequential prediction. While these models report strong empirical performance, their reliability can be sensitive to subtle implementation and experimental design choices. This study revisits representative PKT models and shows that reported gains can be substantially influenced by model configuration and sequence construction practices. We identify issues in attention dimension settings that affect performance estimates, and demonstrate that improper ordering of student attempts, such as ignoring ServerTimestamp, can violate temporal causality and lead to overly optimistic results. To ensure consistent evaluation, hyperparameters are selected via grid search guided by a single designated fold and then fixed uniformly across all folds during cross-validation. We further analyze the role of assignment-wise characteristics and systematically explore the impact of maximum sequence length. Using this protocol, we re-evaluate PKT models on the CodeWorkout dataset. Our results show that, under controlled and consistent settings, the performance gap between attention-enhanced models and standard DKT is significantly reduced, and increased architectural complexity does not consistently translate into superior performance. Beyond individual model comparisons, this work provides practical guidance for reliable and comparable evaluation in programming knowledge tracing.

AI.tldr: Under controlled and consistent evaluation, gains from attention-enhanced PKT models over standard DKT are reduced; careful sequence construction, proper temporal causality, and uniform hyperparameter settings negate some reported advantages, suggesting complexity does not guarantee better performance.
AI.motivation: To ensure reliable, reproducible, and fair evaluation of PKT models and to determine whether reported improvements are due to modeling advances or experimental design choices.
AI.method: Critically audit representative PKT models for attention-dimension settings and sequence construction (e.g., ServerTimestamp ordering). Construct sequences to preserve temporal causality. Perform grid search for hyperparameters on a single designated fold and fix them across all folds in cross-validation. Examine assignment-level characteristics and vary maximum sequence length. Re-evaluate on CodeWorkout dataset.
AI.result: With controlled settings, the performance gap between attention-enhanced models and standard DKT narrows; increased architectural complexity does not consistently yield superior performance.
AI.conclusion: Provides practical guidelines for reliable and comparable evaluation in programming knowledge tracing and cautions against over-interpreting improvements that may stem from implementation details rather than modeling advances.

title: On the Wasserstein Gradient Flow Interpretation of Drifting Models
summary: Recently, Deng et al. (2026) proposed Generative Modeling via Drifting (GMD), a novel framework for generative tasks. This note presents an analysis of GMD through the lens of Wasserstein Gradient Flows (WGF), i.e., the path of steepest descent for a functional in the space of probability measures, equipped with the geometry of optimal transport. Unlike previous WGF-based contributions, GMD can be thought of as directly targeting a fixed point of a specific WGF flow. We demonstrate three main results: first, that one algorithm proposed by Deng et al. (2026) corresponds to finding the limiting point of a WGF on the KL divergence, with Parzen smoothing on the densities. Second, that the algorithm actually implemented by Deng et al. (2026) corresponds to a different procedure, which bears some resemblance to the fixed point of a WGF on the Sinkhorn divergence, but lacks certain desirable properties of the latter. Third, the same same idea can be extended to the limiting point of other WGFs, including the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), the sliced Wasserstein distance, and GAN critic functions.

AI.tldr: The abstract analyzes Generative Modeling via Drifting (GMD) through Wasserstein Gradient Flows (WGF), showing (1) a proposed algorithm aligns with the WGF fixed point forKL divergence with Parzen density smoothing; (2) the actually implemented method resembles a fixed point of a WGF on the Sinkhorn divergence but lacks some of its favorable properties; (3) the same idea extends to other WGFs (MMD, sliced Wasserstein) and GAN critics.
AI.motivation: To place GMD within the WGF framework, clarifying how its fixed-point dynamics relate to established divergences and to assess the robustness of GMD's theoretical claims.
AI.method: Analytical mapping: identify GMD iterations as steps toward fixed points of WGFs (KL with Parzen smoothing); compare to Sinkhorn-divergence fixed points; generalize the construction to other WGFs (MMD, sliced Wasserstein) and GAN critics.
AI.result: Three main findings: (i) one GMD algorithm corresponds to the WGF fixed point for KL with Parzen smoothing; (ii) the implemented method aligns with a different WGF-like procedure akin to Sinkhorn fixed points but lacks some beneficial properties; (iii) the same line of reasoning extends to WGFs such as MMD, sliced Wasserstein, and GAN critics.
AI.conclusion: The study clarifies the theoretical connections between GMD and various WGF frameworks, highlighting both consistencies and limitations, and suggesting broader applicability to different divergences and discriminators; it invites further exploration of convergence, stability, and practical implications across divergences.

title: Using Common Random Numbers for Simulation-based Planning with Rollouts
summary: Simulation-based planning with rollouts is a widely-deployed technique for decision making in stochastic environments. The primary instrument of simulation-based planning is a sampling model, which is repeatedly called to generate trajectories and estimate the utilities of available actions. Among the actions thus explored, one with the maximum estimated utility is then executed. In this paper, we examine the effect of using common random numbers in the simulation process. We obtain a simple recipe for (provably) reducing variance in relative utility when simulations invoke a rollout policy beyond some depth. Experiments on synthetic tasks confirm that our scheme improves task performance. The broader significance of our innovation is apparent from two practical applications: (1) single-step lookahead planning in a pension-disbursement task, and (2) a deployment of the well-known UCT algorithm for the game of Ludo.

AI.tldr: Variance reduction for simulation-based planning using common random numbers to stabilize relative utility estimates in rollout planning (beyond some depth); empirical gains on synthetic tasks and two real-world applications: pension-disbursement single-step planning and Ludo UCT deployment.
AI.motivation: Simulation-based planning incurs high variance in estimated utilities from rollout samples, which harms decision quality. Reducing variance in relative utility can improve action selection.
AI.method: Use common random numbers (CRN) during simulations to couple rollout outcomes across actions, yielding a simple, provably variance-reducing recipe for relative utility when rollout depth exceeds a threshold.
AI.result: Synthetic-task experiments show improved performance; practical validations include a pension-disbursement single-step lookahead and a UCT deployment for Ludo, demonstrating real-world relevance.
AI.conclusion: CRN-based variance reduction in rollout planning improves decision quality in stochastic environments and is effective across both synthetic and real-world planning scenarios.

title: Adaptive Policy Selection and Fine-Tuning under Interaction Budgets for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning
summary: In offline-to-online reinforcement learning (O2O-RL), policies are first safely trained offline using previously collected datasets and then further fine-tuned for tasks via limited online interactions. In a typical O2O-RL pipeline, candidate policies trained with offline RL are evaluated via either off-policy evaluation (OPE) or online evaluation (OE). The policy with the highest estimated value is then deployed and continually fine-tuned. However, this setup has two main issues. First, OPE can be unreliable, making it risky to deploy a policy based solely on those estimates, whereas OE may identify a viable policy with substantial online interaction, which could have been used for fine-tuning. Second--and more importantly--it is also often not possible to determine a priori whether a pretrained policy will improve with post-deployment fine-tuning, especially in non-stationary environments. As a result, procedures committing to a single deployed policy are impractical in many real-world settings. Moreover, a naive remedy that exhaustively fine-tunes all candidates would violate interaction budget constraints and is likewise infeasible. In this paper, we propose a novel adaptive approach for policy selection and fine-tuning under online interaction budgets in O2O-RL. Following the standard pipeline, we first train a set of candidate policies with different offline RL algorithms and hyperparameters; we then perform OPE to obtain initial performance estimates. We next adaptively select and fine-tune the policies based on their predicted performance via an upper-confidence-bound approach thereby making efficient use of online interactions. We demonstrate that our approach improves upon O2O-RL baselines with various benchmarks.

AI.tldr: An adaptive policy selection method for O2O-RL using OPE estimates and a UCB-based allocation strategy to fine-tune multiple offline-trained candidates under online interaction budgets, improving efficiency and final performance.
AI.motivation: O2O-RL faces unreliable OPE and the risk of expending online budget on policies unlikely to improve with post-deployment fine-tuning; selecting a single policy is often impractical in non-stationary settings, and exhaustive fine-tuning is infeasible.
AI.method: Train multiple offline RL policies with varying algorithms/hyperparameters; obtain initial performance estimates via OPE; apply an upper-confidence-bound (UCB) approach to adaptively select which policies to fine-tune given a limited online budget, updating estimates as interactions accrue.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows the adaptive method outperforms standard O2O-RL baselines across benchmarks by making more efficient use of online interactions and improving final policy performance.
AI.conclusion: An O2O-RL strategy that combines diverse offline-trained candidates with UCB-guided online fine-tuning yields more reliable and budget-efficient policy improvement in non-stationary environments.

title: OSAQ: Outlier Self-Absorption for Accurate Low-bit LLM Quantization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities. However, their massive parameter scale leads to significant resource consumption and latency during inference. Post-training weight-only quantization offers a promising solution by reducing model size and accelerating token generation through alleviating the memory-bound issue. Nevertheless, the presence of inherent systematic outliers in weights continues to be a major obstacle. While existing methods, such as scaling and rotation, attempt to address this issue, the performance remains unsatisfactory. In this paper, we propose Outlier Self-Absorption Quantization (OSAQ), which performs additive weight suppression guided by the second-order low-rank property for low-bit weight-only quantization of LLMs. Specifically, we observe that the Hessian exhibits low-rank consistency across different inputs, with certain directions consistently showing vanishing curvature. Leveraging this property, we identify a stable null space of the Hessian and then construct an additive weight transformation by linearly combining the vectors within this null space, thereby suppressing weight outliers without affecting the task loss. This additive transformation can be absorbed into the weights offline, requiring no inter-layer transformations and introducing no inference overhead. Moreover, the construction is efficiently achieved by a closed-form solution, without resource-intensive training or iterative procedures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OSAQ effectively suppresses outliers and enhances low-bit quantization performance. For instance, in 2-bit quantization, OSAQ, when integrated with GPTQ, achieves over 40% lower perplexity compared to vanilla GPTQ.

AI.tldr: OSAQ is a post-training weight-only quantization method for LLMs that suppresses weight outliers via a second-order Hessian null-space-based additive transformation, absorbed offline, enabling lower-bit quantization with no extra inference cost and improved perplexity (e.g., 2-bit GPTQ shows >40% perplexity reduction).
AI.motivation: Massive LLMs demand heavy compute; weight-only quantization helps but outliers hinder performance. Existing scale/rotation methods inadequate; need a principled, efficient way to suppress outlier weights without increasing runtime.
AI.method: Identify stable null-space directions of the Hessian (low-rank across inputs). Construct an additive weight perturbation as a linear combination of null-space vectors. This additive transformation reduces weight outliers without changing the loss (to first/second order, and can be folded into the weights offline). Provide a closed-form solution for the null-space-driven transformation, avoiding training or iterative optimization.
AI.result: Empirical results show enhanced low-bit quantization; e.g., 2-bit quantization combined with GPTQ achieves over 40% lower perplexity than vanilla GPTQ, indicating improved quantization performance and robustness to outliers.
AI.conclusion: OSAQ offers a principled, efficient approach to mitigating weight outliers in post-training weight-only quantization by leveraging Hessian low-rank structure, achieving real-world gains without added inference cost and without extra training.

title: Joint Treatment Effect Estimation from Incomplete Healthcare Data: Temporal Causal Normalizing Flows with LLM-driven Evolutionary MNAR Imputation
summary: Target trial emulation (TTE) enables causal questions to be studied with observational data when randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are infeasible. Yet treatment-effect methods often address causal estimation, missingness, and temporal structure separately, limiting their robustness in electronic health records (EHRs), where time-varying confounding and missing-not-at-random (MNAR) biomarkers can reach 50%--80%. We propose a two-stage pipeline for treatment effect estimation from incomplete longitudinal EHRs. First, CausalFlow-T, a directed acyclic graph (DAG)-constrained normalizing flow with long short-term memory (LSTM)-encoded patient history, performs exact invertible counterfactual inference, avoiding approximation errors from variational inference and separating confounding through explicit causal structure. Ablations on four synthetic and one semi-synthetic benchmark with known counterfactuals show that DAG constraints and exact inference address distinct failure modes: neither compensates for the other. Second, because CausalFlow-T requires completed inputs, we introduce an LLM-driven evolutionary imputer that proposes executable imputation operators rather than individual entries, and evaluate it with three large language model (LLM) backends, including two open-source models. Across 30%--80% MNAR missingness, this imputer achieves the best pooled rank over biomarker and causal metrics, leading in point-wise accuracy and temporal extrapolation while preserving average treatment effect (ATE) recovery as statistical baselines degrade. On Swiss primary-care EHRs from adults with type 2 diabetes initiating a GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor, the pipeline estimates a per-protocol weight-loss difference of -0.98 kg [95% CI -1.01, -0.96] favoring GLP-1 receptor agonists, consistent with randomized evidence and obtained from realistically incomplete real-world EHRs.

AI.tldr: A two-stage pipeline combines (1) a DAG-constrained normalizing-flow model (CausalFlow-T) with LSTM-encoded histories for exact counterfactual inference in incomplete longitudinal EHRs, and (2) an LLM-driven evolutionary imputer that generates executable imputation operators to handle MNAR data; the approach improves causal effect estimation and yields results consistent with randomized evidence in T2DM treatment.
AI.motivation: EHRs exhibit time-varying confounding and high MNAR missingness (up to 50–80%), making causal inference challenging. Existing methods often separate estimation of treatment effects, missing data, and temporal structure, risking robustness in target trial emulation (TTE). A unified, exact-inference, structure-aware pipeline could improve validity of causal conclusions from observational data.
AI.method: Stage 1: CausalFlow-T—a DAG-constrained normalizing flow with LSTM-based patient history to enable exact invertible counterfactual inference while explicitly modeling causal structure to separate confounding. Stage 2: an LLM-driven evolutionary imputer that proposes executable imputation operators (not just single-entry imputations); evaluated with three LLM backends (including two open-source models) across 30–80% MNAR missingness, optimized for biomarker and causal metrics.
AI.result: Ablations demonstrate that DAG constraints and exact inference address distinct failure modes, and the imputer achieves best pooled rank across biomarker and causal metrics, with improved point-wise accuracy and temporal extrapolation while preserving ATE recovery. Real-world data (Swiss primary-care EHRs for adults with type 2 diabetes starting GLP-1 RA or SGLT-2 inhibitors) yields a per-protocol weight-loss difference of -0.98 kg (CI -1.01, -0.96) favoring GLP-1 RA, aligning with randomized evidence.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage framework that combines explicit causal structure and exact counterfactual inference with LLM-guided, executable imputations improves treatment-effect estimation in incomplete longitudinal EHRs and produces results congruent with RCTs, even under substantial MNAR missingness.

title: MixINN: Accelerating Plant Breeding by Combining Mixed Models and Deep Learning for Interaction Prediction
summary: Plant breeding underpins global food security through incremental, accumulating improvements in crop yield, quality and sustainability, achieved via repeated cycles of crop ranking, selection and crossing. Climate change disrupts this process by altering local growing conditions, thereby shifting the relative performance of crop genotypes. Predicting these relative changes in yield is critical for food security. Yet, this problem remains an open challenge in plant breeding, and relatively unexplored within the AI community. We propose MixINN, an approach that first isolates high-quality genotype-environment interaction labels using mixed models, and then predicts these interactions for new crop varieties in future environmental conditions with a deep neural network. We evaluate our method on a corn multi-environment trial across the continental United States and show improved prediction of genotype ranking over current plant breeding methods. MixINN demonstrated superior performance in identifying the 20% most productive corn genotypes, leading to a 5.8% higher average yield, which further improved to 7.2% when targeting specific growing environments. These are competitive results for real-world breeding programs, demonstrating the potential of AI research in accelerating the development of climate-adapted crops, and improving future food security under climate change.

AI.tldr: MixINN combines mixed-model extracted genotype-by-environment (G×E) interaction labels with a deep neural network to predict G×E for unseen varieties in future environments, improving corn genotype ranking and yields in multi-environment trials.
AI.motivation: Climate change alters local growing conditions, shifting genotype performance; predicting relative yield changes is critical for breeding resilient crops and food security, an underexplored area in AI.
AI.method: Stage 1: use mixed models to isolate high-quality G×E interaction labels. Stage 2: train a deep neural network to predict these interactions for new varieties under future environmental conditions. Evaluation on a corn multi-environment trial across the continental United States.
AI.result: Improved prediction of genotype ranking over current breeding methods. Identified the top 20% most productive corn genotypes, yielding 5.8% higher average yield, increasing to 7.2% when targeting specific environments.
AI.conclusion: AI-assisted breeding with MixINN shows potential to accelerate climate-adapted crop development and improve future food security under climate change.

title: Superposition Is Not Necessary: A Mechanistic Interpretability Analysis of Transformer Representations for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Transformer architectures have been widely adopted for time series forecasting, yet whether the representational mechanisms that make them powerful in NLP actually engage on time series data remains unexplored. The persistent competitiveness of simple linear models such as DLinear has fueled ongoing debate, but no mechanistic explanation for this phenomenon has been offered. We address this gap by applying sparse autoencoders (SAEs), a tool from mechanistic interpretability, to probe the internal representations of PatchTST. We first establish that a single-layer, narrow-dimensional transformer matches the forecasting performance of deeper configurations across commonly used benchmarks. We then train SAEs on the post-GELU intermediate FFN activations with dictionary sizes ranging from 0.5x to 4.0x the native dimensionality. Expanding the dictionary yields negligible downstream performance change (average 0.214%), with large portions of overcomplete dictionaries remaining inactive. Targeted causal interventions on dominant latent features produce minimal forecast perturbation. Across all evaluated settings, we observe no empirical evidence that the analyzed FFN representations rely on strong superposition. Instead, the representations remain sparse, stable under aggressive dictionary expansion, and largely insensitive to latent interventions. These results demonstrate that superposition is not necessary for competitive performance on standard forecasting benchmarks, suggesting they may not demand the rich compositional representations that drive transformer success in language modeling, and helping explain the persistent competitiveness of simple linear models

AI.tldr: SAE analysis shows PatchTST relies on sparse, non-superpositional FFN representations; a small single-layer transformer matches deeper models, and the ability of linear models to remain competitive is explained by non-compositional representations in time-series forecasting.
AI.motivation: To mechanistically understand whether transformer representations in time-series forecasting reuse NLP-like mechanisms and why simple linear models remain competitive.
AI.method: Train sparse autoencoders on post-GELU FFN activations of PatchTST across dictionary sizes from 0.5x to 4x the native dimensionality; evaluate activity of dictionary units, perform targeted causal interventions on dominant latent features, and compare forecasting performance across settings.
AI.result: Increasing dictionary size yields negligible performance change (average ~0.214%); large portions of overcomplete dictionaries remain inactive; dominant latent feature interventions have minimal forecast effect; FFN representations are sparse, stable under dictionary expansion, and show little evidence of relying on strong superposition.
AI.conclusion: Superposition is not necessary for competitive performance on standard time-series forecasting benchmarks; these tasks may not require rich, compositional representations, helping explain the persistent competitiveness of simple linear models like DLinear.

title: AxMoE: Characterizing the Impact of Approximate Multipliers on Mixture-of-Experts DNN Architectures
comment: accepted at the IEEE Computer Society Annual Symposium on VLSI ISVLSI 2026
summary: Deep neural network (DNN) inference at the edge demands simultaneous improvements in accuracy, computational efficiency, and energy consumption. Approximate computing and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures have each been studied as independent routes towards efficient inference, the former by replacing exact arithmetic with low-power approximate multipliers, the latter by routing inputs through specialized expert sub-networks to enable conditional computation. However, their interaction remains entirely unexplored. This paper presents AxMoE, the first study of the impact of approximate multiplication on MoE DNN architectures. We evaluate three MoE variants: Hard MoE, Soft MoE, and Cluster MoE against dense baselines across three CNN architectures (ResNet-20, VGG11_bn, VGG19_bn) on CIFAR-100 and a Vision Transformer (ViT-Small) on Tiny ImageNet-200 dataset, using eight 8-bit signed multipliers (including one exact baseline) from the EvoApproxLib library. Results show that, without retraining, the Dense baseline is the most resilient topology across all CNN architectures, whereas on ViT-Small, all topologies degrade at comparable rates regardless of routing strategy. After approximate-aware retraining, recovery varies substantially across architectures, topologies, and multipliers. ResNet-20 achieves full recovery across the entire multiplier range, whereas VGG architectures recover at moderate multipliers but fail irreversibly at aggressive ones for all topologies except Cluster MoE on VGG11_bn; on ViT-Small, Hard MoE outperforms Dense under aggressive approximation at equal normalized inference cost. These results pave the way for future approximate MoE hardware-software co-design strategies.

AI.tldr: AxMoE studies the impact of approximate multiplication on Mixture-of-Experts DNNs for edge inference. Dense baselines are most robust without retraining; after approximate-aware retraining, recoverability is highly architecture- and model-dependent, with ResNet-20 fully recoverable, VGGs partly recoverable, and ViT-Small where Hard MoE can outperform dense under aggressive approximation. This motivates approximate MoE hardware-software co-design.
AI.motivation: To understand how approximate computing (low-precision, approximate multipliers) interacts with MoE routing architectures in DNNs and to quantify robustness and retraining potential across CNNs and vision transformers.
AI.method: Evaluate three MoE variants (Hard MoE, Soft MoE, Cluster MoE) and dense baselines across ResNet-20, VGG11_bn, VGG19_bn on CIFAR-100 and ViT-Small on Tiny ImageNet-200. Use eight 8-bit signed multipliers from EvoApproxLib (one exact baseline). Compare performance without retraining and after approximate-aware retraining, across multiplier strengths.
AI.result: Without retraining, Dense baselines are most resilient across CNNs; on ViT-Small, all topologies degrade similarly. After retraining, recovery varies: ResNet-20 fully recovers across the multiplier range; VGGs recover at moderate multipliers but fail at aggressive ones for all topologies except Cluster MoE on VGG11_bn; on ViT-Small, Hard MoE can outperform Dense under aggressive approx at equal normalized cost.
AI.conclusion: Approximate multiplication effects in MoE DNNs are highly architecture- and dataset-dependent. Dense CNNs show robustness, while certain MoE configurations (notably Cluster MoE on specific CNNs and Hard MoE on ViT-Small) can offer advantages under aggressive approximation. These results support pursuing hardware-software co-design for approximate MoE inference.

title: Bilinear Mamba-Koopman Neural MPC for Varying Dynamics
summary:
Koopman-based neural MPC models generate time-varying dynamics from historical data, but preserve convexity by enforcing that the system operator is independent of the current control input. This conditional independence constraint limits adaptation to changing dynamics within a single MPC horizon, particularly under time-varying conditions and under stale-plan execution.
  We propose Bilinear Mamba-Koopman Neural MPC, a minimal extension that introduces control-dependent coupling in the latent dynamics, allowing the effective operator to adapt to the current input. The resulting model is a strict generalization of the standard linear, conditional-independence formulation, adds less than 1% parameters through a low-rank structure, and admits exact model Jacobians that enable efficient Sequential Convex Programming (SCP) with monotone-descent and KKT convergence results under standard trust-region assumptions.
  Across CartPole and RSCP benchmarks in time-invariant and time-varying regimes, the proposed model matches or improves forecasting accuracy on every cell when training noise is averaged out, with strict gains where control-state coupling is structurally present. Its main closed-loop gains appear in the RSCP TV task, where iterative SCP improves adaptation within the horizon and substantially stabilizes training; in CartPole TV, the gains are modest but consistent. In delayed re-planning experiments on the time-varying variants, the bilinear model degrades more gracefully under stale-plan execution, maintaining a consistent advantage on CartPole TV and a substantially larger robustness margin on RSCP TV. These results show that control-dependent latent dynamics provide a simple and effective mechanism for robust MPC under varying conditions.

AI.tldr: Bilinear Mamba-Koopman Neural MPC introduces control-dependent latent dynamics to relax the conditional-independence constraint in Koopman-based MPC, enabling adaptation to changing dynamics with minimal parameter overhead and enabling efficient SCP with convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: Address the convexity-preserving but overly restrictive independence of the system operator from the current control input in standard Koopman-based MPC, which limits adaptation during a single horizon under time-varying and stale-plan conditions.
AI.method: Add a bilinear, low-rank coupling in latent dynamics (control-dependent operator) to the Koopman framework, yielding a strict generalization of the linear, independent-input formulation; preserve exact model Jacobians for SCP; enable monotone-descent SCP with KKT convergence under trust-region assumptions.
AI.result: Forecasting accuracy matches or improves on CartPole and RSCP benchmarks across time-invariant and time-varying regimes (when training noise is averaged). Gains are strongest where control-state coupling is present; in RSCP TV, iterative SCP improves horizon adaptation and stabilizes training; CartPole TV gains are modest but consistent. Under delayed re-planning, the bilinear model degrades gracefully under stale plans, maintaining advantages on CartPole TV and a larger robustness margin on RSCP TV.
AI.conclusion: Control-dependent latent dynamics provide a simple, effective mechanism for robust MPC under varying conditions, achieving improved adaptation and stability with a minimal parameter overhead.

title: Unsat Core Prediction through Polarity-Aware Representation Learning over Clause-Literal Hypergraphs
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026. Camera-ready version coming soon
summary: Graph neural networks have been widely used in Boolean satisfiability (SAT) tasks to learn structural information from SAT formulas. The goal of these studies is to solve SAT instances or to enhance SAT solvers, including tasks such as unsat-core prediction. However, most existing approaches model a SAT formula as a bipartite graph or a directed acyclic graph, which are less expressive in capturing higher-order interactions among literals and clauses. Moreover, these approaches are limited in modeling intrinsic polarity-related properties of SAT, such as the complementary relationship between the positive and negative literals of a variable. To address these limitations, we propose a polarity-aware representation learning framework over clause-literal hypergraphs. We model SAT formulas as clause-literal hypergraphs augmented with a clause incidence graph to capture higher-order structural interactions. We then introduce a polarity-aware decomposed mechanism that separates variable representations into polarity invariant and equivariant components, explicitly modeling the relationship between positive and negative literals, with the resulting literal representations propagated along the hypergraph structure. We further incorporate a polarity-inversion consistency regularization to reinforce polarity-consistent representations during training. Experimental results on multiple SAT datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: A polarity-aware clause-literal hypergraph GNN for SAT that captures higher-order interactions and polarity relationships, with polarity-inversion consistency regularization, improving over standard bipartite/DA-based representations.
AI.motivation: Current SAT graph representations (bipartite clause-form graphs or DAGs) inadequately capture higher-order interactions among literals/clauses and neglect polarity relationships between positive and negative literals. A richer representation could improve learning-based SAT tasks (e.g., unsat-core prediction, SAT solving aids).
AI.method: Model SAT formulas as clause-literal hypergraphs augmented with a clause incidence graph to capture higher-order interactions. Introduce a polarity-aware decomposed mechanism that splits variable representations into polarity-invariant and polarity-equivariant components, explicitly modeling relationships between positive and negative literals. Propagate literal representations along the hypergraph with this decomposition. Apply polarity-inversion consistency regularization to enforce polarity-consistent representations during training.
AI.result: Experimental results on multiple SAT datasets show effectiveness of the proposed approach, outperforming baselines that use simpler graph representations.
AI.conclusion: Polarity-aware hypergraph representation and decomposition effectively capture higher-order and polarity relationships in SAT, with regularization reinforcing consistent polarity representations, suggesting improved performance on SAT-related tasks and potential for enhanced solver inputs.

title: Improving FMQA via Initial Training Data Design Considering Marginal Bit Coverage in One-Hot Encoding
summary: Factorization machine with quadratic-optimization annealing (FMQA) is a black-box optimization method that combines a factorization machine (FM) surrogate with QUBO-based search by an Ising machine. When FMQA is applied to integer or discretized continuous variables via one-hot encoding, uniform random initial sampling can leave many binary variables never active in the initial training data, and the corresponding FM parameters receive no direct gradient updates from the observed responses. We address this by designing the initial training data to achieve complete marginal bit coverage, namely, ensuring that every binary variable obtained by one-hot encoding takes the value one at least once. We use two space-filling sampling methods, Latin hypercube sampling (LHS) and the Sobol' sequence, yielding LHS-FMQA and Sobol'-FMQA. On the human-powered aircraft wing-shape optimization benchmark with 17 and 32 design variables, both proposed methods achieved numerically higher mean final cruising speeds than the baseline FMQA, with the advantage more pronounced on the 32-variable problem.

AI.tldr: FMQA variants with complete marginal bit coverage for one-hot encoded variables; uses LHS and Sobol sampling to initialize training data (LHS-FMQA and Sobol'-FMQA); shows improved mean final cruising speeds vs baseline FMQA on wing-shape optimization, especially with 32 variables.
AI.motivation: In FMQA, one-hot encoding often leaves some binary variables inactive in initial data, blocking direct gradient updates to FM parameters and potentially impairing surrogate accuracy. Ensuring every binary variable is observed as 1 at least once aims to improve initial model quality and optimization performance.
AI.method: Impose complete marginal bit coverage by designing initial data so each binary variable from one-hot encoding takes value 1 at least once. Generate initial samples using space-filling designs: Latin hypercube sampling (LHS) for LHS-FMQA and Sobol sequence for Sobol'-FMQA. Evaluate on wing-shape optimization benchmarks with 17 and 32 design variables.
AI.result: Both LHS-FMQA and Sobol'-FMQA produced higher mean final cruising speeds than baseline FMQA. The performance gain was more pronounced on the higher-dimensional (32-variable) problem.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating complete marginal bit coverage with space-filling initial designs improves FMQA performance, particularly in higher-dimensional discrete/discretized optimization problems.

title: Trustworthy Federated Label Distribution Learning under Annotation Quality Disparity
summary: Label Distribution Learning (LDL) models supervision as an instance-wise probability distribution, enabling fine-grained learning under inherent ambiguity, but its success relies on high-fidelity label distributions that are costly to obtain and thus often noisy.
