Motivated by privacy-sensitive applications, we study Federated Label Distribution Learning (Fed-LDL), where data isolation further induces heterogeneous annotation quality across clients, making local updates unevenly reliable and breaking sample-size-based aggregation (e.g., FedAvg). To address this trust dilemma, we propose FedQual, a quality-aware Fed-LDL framework with two coupled mechanisms: (i) quality-adaptive client training guided by a global semantic anchor that calibrates low-quality clients while preserving high-quality autonomy, and (ii) reliability-aware server aggregation that reweights client contributions by effective reliable information rather than raw sample size. To enable rigorous evaluation, we construct four new Fed-LDL benchmarks (FER-LDL, FI-LDL, PIPAL-LDL, and KADID-LDL) with controlled annotation quality disparity. We further provide a theoretical guarantee showing that under heterogeneous supervision quality, client-specific calibration is strictly better than any uniform calibration. Extensive experiments on the proposed benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of FedQual.

AI.tldr: FedQual introduces a quality-aware federated LDL framework with two mechanisms: quality-adaptive client training guided by a global semantic anchor and reliability-aware server aggregation that weights contributions by effective information rather than sample size. It provides four new benchmarks (FER-LDL, FI-LDL, PIPAL-LDL, KADID-LDL), and a theoretical guarantee that client-specific calibration outperforms any uniform calibration, with extensive experiments confirming effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Label Distribution Learning (LDL) depends on high-fidelity per-instance label distributions, which are costly and often noisy. Federated LDL (Fed-LDL) introduces data isolation and heterogeneous annotation quality across clients, breaking traditional sample-size-based aggregation and challenging reliable learning.
AI.method: Propose FedQual with (i) quality-adaptive client training guided by a global semantic anchor to calibrate low-quality clients while preserving high-quality autonomy, and (ii) reliability-aware server aggregation that weights client contributions by effective reliable information rather than raw sample size. Introduce four benchmarks (FER-LDL, FI-LDL, PIPAL-LDL, KADID-LDL) with controlled annotation quality disparity. Provide a theoretical guarantee showing client-specific calibration is strictly better than uniform calibration under heterogeneous supervision quality.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the proposed benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of FedQual in improving LDL learning under heterogeneous annotation quality and validating the theoretical calibration advantage.
AI.conclusion: FedQual provides a principled, effective solution for Fed-LDL under quality heterogeneity, combining quality-aware training and reliability-based aggregation, with theoretical guarantees and benchmark-driven validation.

title: Concurrence of Symmetry Breaking and Nonlocality Phase Transitions in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models undergo a phase transition in a critical time window during generation dynamics, with two complementary diagnoses of criticality. The symmetry breaking picture views the critical window as when trajectories bifurcate into different semantic minima of the energy landscape, whereas the nonlocality picture views the critical window as when local denoising fails. We study whether two notions of such phase transitions are concurrent in modern diffusion transformers. By evaluating the dynamics and outcomes of the generation trajectory, we observe a near-simultaneous occurrence of the non-locality and symmetry breaking critical times. Our work is the first to unify the two notions of phase transitions in practice: it provides a concrete diagnostic for when and why diffusion models rely on conditioning and global denoising, enabling principled evaluation of model efficiency and guiding the design of architectures and sampling schemes that avoid unnecessary computation.

AI.tldr: Unifies two criticality notions in diffusion-model generation by showing near-simultaneous nonlocal denoising failure and symmetry-breaking trajectory bifurcation in diffusion transformers; offers a practical diagnostic for conditioning/global denoising and informs efficient design of architectures and sampling to reduce computation.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models experience a critical time window during generation. Two complementary diagnostics—nonlocal denoising failure and symmetry breaking into semantic minima—have not been demonstrated as concurrent. Establishing their relationship can yield practical tools for efficiency, diagnosis, and design.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of diffusion-transformer generation trajectories, identifying and timing the occurrences of nonlocal denoising failure and symmetry-breaking bifurcation, and assessing their (near) simultaneity across runs to unify the two notions.
AI.result: The study reports near-simultaneous occurrence of nonlocality and symmetry-breaking critical times, constituting the first practical unification of the two concepts. It provides a concrete diagnostic for when conditioning and global denoising are relied upon, enabling principled evaluation of model efficiency and guiding architectural and sampling choices to reduce computation.
AI.conclusion: A practical framework to diagnose and optimize diffusion-model generation: determine when conditioning/global denoising are necessary and how to design sampling/architectures to minimize unnecessary computation, improving efficiency.

title: Replay-Based Continual Learning for Physics-Informed Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators generally demonstrate strong predictive performance on in-distribution (ID) problems. However, a critical limitation of existing methods is their significant performance degradation when encountering out-of-distribution (OOD) data. To address this issue, this work introduces continual learning into physics-informed neural operators, with particular emphasis on neural operators built upon the Transolver architecture, and proposes a simple yet effective replay-based continual learning strategy. The proposed method is fully physics-informed and does not require labeled data, relying solely on input fields together with physical constraints for training. When new OOD data become available, a small number of past data are incorporated through a distillation-based constraint to preserve previously acquired knowledge and alleviate catastrophic forgetting. Meanwhile, a transfer learning LoRA is employed to enable rapid adaptation to the new data. The proposed framework is systematically validated on three representative physical problems, including the Darcy flow problem in fluid mechanics, a two-dimensional hyperelastic brain tumor problem in biomechanics, and a three-dimensional linear elastic Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces problem in solid mechanics. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting on previously learned data while maintaining fast adaptability to new data. Compared with conventional joint training strategies, the proposed method significantly improves training efficiency while reducing additional memory usage and computational cost.

AI.tldr: Continual learning for physics-informed neural operators using replay-based, distillation-regularized updates with LoRA transfer for rapid OOD adaptation, avoiding labeled data and improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Neural operators struggle with out-of-distribution data; integrate continual learning to preserve prior knowledge while adapting to new OOD scenarios in a purely physics-informed, label-free setting.
AI.method: Replay-based continual learning with a distillation-based constraint to prevent forgetting; physics-informed training using input fields and physical constraints; Transolver-based neural operators; transfer learning via LoRA for fast adaptation; no labeled data required.
AI.result: Mitigates catastrophic forgetting on past data while retaining fast adaptation to new data; improves training efficiency relative to joint training; reduces memory footprint and compute; validated on Darcy flow, 2D hyperelastic brain tumor, and 3D linear elastic Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces.
AI.conclusion: A practical, efficient framework for continual learning in physics-informed neural operators that preserves prior knowledge, enables rapid adaptation to new OOD data, and is applicable across diverse physical domains.

title: Bridging Input Feature Spaces Towards Graph Foundation Models
summary: Unlike vision and language domains, graph learning lacks a shared input space, as input features differ across graph datasets not only in semantics, but also in value ranges and dimensionality. This misalignment prevents graph models from generalizing across datasets, limiting their use as foundation models. In this work, we propose ALL-IN, a simple and theoretically grounded method that enables transferability across datasets with different input features. Our approach projects node features into a shared random space and constructs representations via covariance-based statistics, thus eliminating dependence on the original feature space. We show that the computed node-covariance operators and the resulting node representations are invariant in distribution to permutations of the input features. We further demonstrate that the expected operator exhibits invariance to general orthogonal transformations of the input features. Empirically, ALL-IN achieves strong performance across diverse node- and graph-level tasks on unseen datasets with new input features, without requiring architecture changes or retraining. These results point to a promising direction for input-agnostic, transferable graph models.

AI.tldr: ALL-IN enables cross-dataset transferability for graphs with different input features by projecting features into a shared random space and using covariance-based representations, achieving input-agnostic transfer without retraining.
AI.motivation: Graph learning lacks a shared input space due to dataset-specific feature semantics, value ranges, and dimensionality, which hinders generalization and prevents scalable, foundation-model-like transfer across datasets.
AI.method: Project node features into a shared random space and construct representations via covariance-based statistics. Compute node-covariance operators and node representations, ensuring invariance in distribution to feature permutations; show that the expected operator is invariant to orthogonal transformations of the input features.
AI.result: Empirically, ALL-IN achieves strong performance on diverse node- and graph-level tasks on unseen datasets with new input features, without architecture changes or retraining.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable path toward input-agnostic transferable graph models and motivates further exploration of covariance-based, feature-space-invariant representations.

title: Hybrid Iterative Neural Low-Regularity Integrator for Nonlinear Dispersive Equations
summary: We propose HIN-LRI, a hybrid framework that augments a classical numerical solver with a neural operator trained to correct the solver's structured truncation error. A base low-regularity integrator provides a consistent first-order approximation to nonlinear dispersive PDEs, while a lightweight neural network, operating on a low-dimensional latent manifold, learns the residual defect that analytical methods cannot close. An explicit time-step scaling on the neural correction ensures that its Lipschitz contribution remains $\mathcal{O}(τ)$, yielding a Gronwall stability factor bounded uniformly in the step size and independent of the spatial resolution. The network is trained end-to-end through a solver-in-the-loop objective that unrolls the full iteration and penalises trajectory error in a Bourgain-type norm, aligning learning with multi-step solver dynamics rather than isolated one-step targets. Under stated assumptions, the global error satisfies $C(\varepsilon_{net}+δ)\,τ^γ\ln(1/τ)$, where $\varepsilon_{net}$ measures the network approximation quality and $δ$ the training shortfall. Experiments on three dispersive benchmarks with rough data show that HIN-LRI improves accuracy over analytical integrators, splitting methods, and neural PDE surrogates, with stable spatial refinement, effective out-of-distribution transfer, and modest online overhead.

AI.tldr: Hybrid HIN-LRI combines a low-regularity solver with a latent neural operator to correct truncation error; stabilized by explicit time-step scaling and solver-in-the-loop training, yielding provable error bounds and improved performance on dispersive PDE benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address accuracy and stability gaps in numerical solvers for nonlinear dispersive PDEs with rough data by augmenting traditional methods with data-driven residual learning that respects multi-step dynamics and stability.
AI.method: Base solver: low-regularity integrator; neural operator on a low-dimensional latent manifold learns the residual defect. Explicit time-step scaling keeps Lipschitz contribution O(τ). Training via solver-in-the-loop objective that unrolls iterations and penalizes trajectory error in a Bourgain-type norm. Theoretical bound: ||global error|| ≤ C(ε_net + δ) τ^γ log(1/τ).
AI.result: Empirical results on three dispersive benchmarks show improved accuracy over analytical integrators, splitting methods, and neural surrogates, with stable spatial refinement, good out-of-distribution transfer, and modest online overhead.
AI.conclusion: HIN-LRI delivers stable, accurate integration for nonlinear dispersive PDEs by blending analytical and learned corrections, with generalization to varying resolutions and potential for broader application.

title: Self-Attention as Transport: Limits of Symmetric Spectral Diagnostics
comment: 42 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables; 82-page online supplement (proofs, additional experiments, dataset statistics) as an ancillary file
summary:
Large language models hallucinate in predictable ways: attention routing fails by over-concentrating on a narrow set of positions, or by spreading so diffusely that relevance is diluted, and the shape of the failure carries diagnostic signal. A widely used family of spectral methods analyzes the symmetric component of the degree-normalized attention operator, which governs transport capacity; we prove that every transpose-invariant spectral diagnostic of this operator is structurally orientation-blind (it cannot distinguish an operator from its transpose, and therefore cannot detect information-flow direction), with a quantitative converse establishing the asymmetry coefficient $G$ as the unique control parameter for direction.
  Pairing this with a closed-form bipartite-Cheeger landscape for canonical causal architectures, we show that uniform causal attention satisfies an $n$-independent floor $φ\ge 1/5$ with worst cut at $t^\ast/n \approx 0.32$, while window attention pierces the floor as $O(w/n)$; failure modes are shape-different, not just value-different. The resulting two-axis diagnostic ($φ$ for capacity, $G$ for direction) yields a falsifiable polarity prediction: bottleneck- and diffuse-dominated benchmarks should exhibit opposite polarity. Under length-controlled evaluation, transport features retain interpretable signal (LC-AUROC from 0.62 to 0.84) on tested models up to 8B parameters, with polarity reversing as predicted between HaluEval and MedHallu.

AI.tldr: Spectral diagnostics of attention reveal orientation-blindness in transpose-invariant methods; introduce two-axis φ-G diagnostic to separate capacity and information-flow direction; predict polarity across benchmark types; demonstrate interpretability gains up to 8B models; polarity reversal validates predictions.
AI.motivation: LLM hallucinations exhibit systematic attention-routing failures. Existing spectral diagnostics of the symmetric, degree-normalized attention operator fail to capture directionality. A theory-driven framework is needed to quantify transport capacity and information-flow direction to diagnose and compare attention regimes across architectures and benchmarks.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of the degree-normalized attention operator's symmetric component showing transpose-invariance of spectral diagnostics. Prove that such diagnostics are orientation-blind; identify asymmetry coefficient G as the unique parameter governing direction. Develop a closed-form bipartite-Cheeger landscape for canonical causal architectures to derive a floor φ for uniform causal attention (φ ≥ 1/5) with worst cut at t*/n ≈ 0.32, and show window attention reduces the floor with O(w/n). Propose a two-axis diagnostic (φ for capacity, G for direction) and derive a falsifiable polarity prediction: bottleneck- versus diffuse-dominated benchmarks should exhibit opposite polarity.
AI.result: Theoretical result establishing orientation-blindness of transpose-invariant spectral diagnostics; G as the sole direction-control parameter. Quantitative bounds: φ ≥ 1/5 for uniform causal attention, worst cut t*/n ≈ 0.32, and window attention achieving O(w/n). The two-axis diagnostic predicts polarity across regimes; empirical evaluation shows transport features retain interpretability (LC-AUROC from 0.62 to 0.84) for models up to 8B parameters; polarity reverses between HaluEval and MedHallu, validating the prediction.
AI.conclusion: A two-axis φ-G framework provides falsifiable polarity predictions across bottleneck vs diffuse attention regimes. Transport-based diagnostics remain interpretable under length-controlled evaluation up to 8B params, supporting orientation-aware analysis of attention in LLMs and guiding future design/diagnostic development.

title: Regime-Conditioned Evaluation in Multi-Context Bayesian Optimization
summary: Published transfer-BO comparisons often estimate an average treatment effect of acquisition choice over hidden regime variables, while practitioners need the conditional effect for their specific prior quality, budget ratio, and metric. An audit of 40 transfer-BO papers from NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, AISTATS, UAI, TMLR, JMLR, and AutoML-Conf (2022-2025) finds that 98% never vary B/|A| as a controlled axis. On the same GDSC2 benchmark, changing only the budget reverses the ranking: at B=50, Greedy outperforms UCB by 0.050 Hit@1, while at B=100, UCB outperforms Greedy by 0.035. We capture this transition with the Portable Regime Score PRS=(B/|A|)(1-rho), where rho is the prior rank correlation and can be estimated from pilot contexts before the main comparison. Across 79 conditions spanning chemistry, drug-response biology, and HPO, a hierarchical model gives beta=0.50 (p=1.1e-9), and 19% of conditions fall in an equivalence zone where |advantage|<0.01 Hit@1. In five published reversal cases, PRS predicts the winner from pre-comparison observables. A No-Free-Leaderboard proposition explains why unconditional rankings are unstable: when CATE changes sign across regimes, the reported ATE becomes a function of benchmark mixture. RegimePlanner, which estimates rho online and switches acquisition accordingly, wins all 16 HPO-B search spaces at B=100 and exceeds the matched {Greedy,UCB} per-context oracle on GDSC2 by 18%. Pre-registered predictions achieve 27/40=67.5% overall accuracy and above 90% within EMA prior families. The practical protocol is simple: report B/|A|, rho, K, and metric alongside any claimed acquisition advantage.

AI.tldr: Varying budget-to-action ratio (B/|A|) and regime priors significantly affect transfer-BO performance; unconditional rankings are unstable across regimes; the Portable Regime Score (PRS) and RegimePlanner enable regime-aware evaluation and online adaptation, yielding improved predictive accuracy and decision robustness across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: Current transfer-BO evaluations report average treatment effects over hidden regime variables, masking conditional effects that practitioners care about (prior quality, budget ratio, metric). Studies show that rankings depend on budget and regime, necessitating context-aware analysis.
AI.method: Audit of 40 transfer-BO papers (NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/AISTATS/UAI/TMLR/JMLR/AutoML-Conf, 2022–2025); define PRS=(B/|A|)(1−rho) with rho as prior rank correlation estimated from pilot contexts; hierarchical modeling across 79 conditions in chemistry, drug-response biology, and HPO; examine reversal cases; propose No-Free-Leaderboard; RegimePlanner estimating rho online and switching acquisitions; validation on GDSC2 and HPO-B search spaces; pre-registered predictions.
AI.result: PRS captures budget-driven transitions in algorithm performance (e.g., Greedy vs UCB) with effect sizes; across 79 conditions, beta=0.50 (p=1.1e-9); 19% of conditions in an equivalence zone (|advantage|<0.01 Hit@1); PRS predicts winners in five reversal cases; RegimePlanner wins all 16 HPO-B spaces at B=100 and exceeds the per-context oracle on GDSC2 by 18%; pre-registered predictions achieve 27/40=67.5% overall accuracy and >90% within EMA-prior families.
AI.conclusion: Report B/|A|, rho, K, and metric alongside claimed acquisition advantage; adopt regime-aware evaluation and online rho estimation (RegimePlanner); unconditional rankings are unstable when CATE changes sign across regimes, as ATE becomes a function of benchmark mixture.

title: A geometric relation of the error introduced by sampling a language model's output distribution to its internal state
comment: 12 Pages, 10 Figures, 2 Appendices. To appear in Proceedings of ICML 2026
summary: GPT-style language models are sensitive to single-token changes at generation points where the predicted probability distribution is spread across multiple tokens. Viewing this sensitivity as a geometric property, we derive an $\mathfrak{so}(n)$-valued 1-form that depends only on the geometry of the token embeddings. Despite this purely geometric origin, we show that its curvature is semantically meaningful: On chess reasoning tasks, the curvature couples to the world model of an off-the-shelf instruction-tuned model, with transformations clustering by board region and respecting piece importance. Our findings suggest that token space geometry directly reflects how models internally represent problems.

AI.tldr: Geometric analysis of GPT-style token spaces via an so(n)-valued 1-form; curvature reveals semantically meaningful structure in chess reasoning, linking token geometry to internal world-model representations.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the geometry of token embeddings directly encodes problem representations and how local generation sensitivity relates to global semantic structure.
AI.method: Construct an so(n)-valued 1-form from token embedding geometry; study its curvature; apply to chess reasoning by comparing with an instruction-tuned model's world model, observing clustering by board region and piece importance.
AI.result: The curvature is semantically meaningful: it correlates with the model's world model on chess tasks, with transformations clustering by board region and respecting piece importance.
AI.conclusion: Token-space geometry provides a plausible proxy for internal problem representations in language models; curvature analysis offers a geometric lens for interpreting reasoning processes.

title: Cross-Model Consistency of Feature Importance in Electrospinning: Separating Robust from Model-Dependent Features
summary: Electrospinning is a highly sensitive fabrication process in which small variations in operating parameters can significantly influence fiber morphology and material performance. Machine learning (ML) methods are increasingly employed to model these process-structure relationships and to identify the relative importance of processing variables. However, most existing studies rely on a single ML model, implicitly assuming that the resulting feature importance is robust and reproducible. In this study, the consistency of feature importance across multiple ML model families was systematically evaluated using a curated dataset of 96 polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) electrospinning experiments. Twenty-one ML models representing linear, tree-based, kernel-based, neural network, and instance-based approaches were trained and compared. To provide a unified interpretability framework, SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values were used to calculate feature importance consistently across all models. A rank-based statistical analysis was then performed to quantify inter-model agreement and assess the robustness of parameter rankings. The results demonstrate that predictive performance and interpretive reliability are fundamentally distinct properties. Although several models achieved comparable predictive accuracy, substantial differences were observed in their feature importance rankings. Solution concentration emerged as the most robust and consistently influential parameter (variability = 0), whereas flow rate and applied voltage exhibited high ranking variability (variability > 0.9), indicating strong model dependence. These findings suggest that feature importance derived from a single ML model may be unreliable, particularly for small experimental datasets, and highlight the importance of cross-model validation for achieving trustworthy interpretation in ML-assisted electrospinning research.

AI.tldr: Cross-model SHAP-based analysis across 21 ML models on 96 PVA electrospinning experiments shows predictive performance is not tied to interpretability; solution concentration is robustly influential, while flow rate and applied voltage vary by model, underscoring the need for cross-model validation of feature importance.
AI.motivation: To assess whether feature importance from ML models in electrospinning is robust across different model families, addressing a common bias of relying on a single model for interpretation.
AI.method: Train 21 models spanning linear, tree-based, kernel-based, neural network, and instance-based families on a dataset of 96 PVA electrospinning experiments. Use SHAP values to compute feature importance consistently across models. Apply rank-based statistics to quantify inter-model agreement and robustness of parameter rankings.
AI.result: Predictive accuracy is similar across models, but feature importance rankings differ substantially among models. Solution concentration is the most robust parameter (variability = 0). Flow rate and applied voltage show high ranking variability (variability > 0.9), indicating strong model dependence.
AI.conclusion: Interpretability is not guaranteed by predictive performance; single-model feature importance can be unreliable, especially with small datasets. Cross-model validation using SHAP enhances trustworthiness of ML-assisted electrospinning interpretations.

title: Breaking the Quality-Privacy Tradeoff in Tabular Data Generation via In-Context Learning
summary: Tabular data synthesis aims to generate high-quality data while preserving privacy. However, we find that existing tabular generative models exhibit a clear tradeoff in the small-data regime: improving data quality typically comes at the cost of increased memorization of training samples, thereby weakening privacy protection. This tradeoff arises because small training sets make it difficult for dataset-specific generative models to distinguish generalizable structure from sample-specific patterns. To address this, we propose DiffICL, which formulates tabular data generation as an in-context learning problem. Instead of fitting each dataset from scratch,DiffICL leverages pretrained structural priors learned from a large collection of datasets, enabling it to infer data distributions from limited context rather than memorizing individual samples. We evaluate DiffICL on 14 real-world datasets. Results show that DiffICL improves both data quality and privacy, and generate synthetic data that provides effective data augmentation. Our findings suggest that the quality-privacy tradeoff can be improved through better training paradigms.

AI.tldr: DiffICL reframes tabular data generation as in-context learning using pretrained structural priors to overcome the small-data privacy/quality tradeoff, improving both data quality and privacy while supporting data augmentation.
AI.motivation: In the small-data regime, tabular generative models struggle to distinguish generalizable structure from sample-specific patterns, leading to a tradeoff where higher data quality increases memorization and weakens privacy.
AI.method: DiffICL leverages pretrained structural priors learned from a large collection of datasets and performs in-context learning to infer data distributions from limited context rather than fitting each dataset from scratch.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 14 real-world datasets shows that DiffICL improves both data quality and privacy and yields synthetic data effective for data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: Better training paradigms that leverage priors and in-context learning can mitigate the quality-privacy tradeoff in tabular data synthesis.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Compositional Generalization with Outcome-Level Optimization
summary: Compositional generalization refers to correctly interpret novel combinations of known primitives, which remains a major challenge. Existing approaches often rely on supervised fine-tuning, which encourages models to imitate target outputs. This token-level training paradigm fails to capture the global compositional structure required for generalizing to unseen combinations. In this work, we investigate whether compositional generalization can instead be improved through outcome-level reinforcement learning. We adopt Group Relative Policy Optimization to optimize models based on feedback on their final outputs. Within this framework, we explore both a simple binary outcome reward and a composite reward that provides additional composition feedback. Experiments on multiple compositional benchmarks show that reinforcement learning improves compositional generalization compared to supervised fine-tuning. Further analysis reveals that supervised models tend to overfit frequent training compositions, whereas reinforcement learning improves compositional generalization by reshaping the output distribution, particularly for more complex composition types.

AI.tldr: Outcome-level reinforcement learning with Group Relative Policy Optimization improves compositional generalization over supervised fine-tuning; binary vs composite rewards; RL reshapes the output distribution and mitigates overfitting to frequent compositions, aiding complex compositions.
AI.motivation: Compositional generalization is challenging; token-level supervised fine-tuning imitates targets and lacks global structural incentives, hindering generalization to unseen composition of primitives.
AI.method: Apply Group Relative Policy Optimization to optimize models based on final output feedback. Compare a simple binary outcome reward with a composite reward that provides additional composition feedback. Evaluate on multiple compositional benchmarks and analyze how output distributions shift.
AI.result: Reinforcement learning improves compositional generalization compared to supervised fine-tuning. Supervised models overfit frequent training compositions, whereas RL reshapes the output distribution, particularly for more complex composition types.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-level RL via GRPO is effective for compositional generalization; incorporating composite rewards can further enhance generalization by guiding the model toward proper global structure and reducing overfitting to frequent compositions.

title: Training-Time Batch Normalization Reshapes Local Partition Geometry in Piecewise-Affine Networks
summary: Batch normalization (BN) is central to modern deep networks, but its effect on the realized function during training remains less understood than its optimization benefits. We study training-time BN in continuous piecewise-affine (CPA) networks through the geometry of switching hyperplanes and the induced affine-region partition. Conditioned on a mini-batch, we show that BN defines for each neuron a reference hyperplane through the batch centroid, and that breakpoint-switching hyperplanes are parallel translates whose offsets are expressed in batch-standardized coordinates and are independent of the raw bias. This yields an exact criterion for when a switching hyperplane intersects a local $\ell_\infty$ window and motivates a local region-density functional based on exact affine-region counts. Under explicit sufficient conditions, we show that BN increases expected local partition refinement in ReLU and more general piecewise-affine networks, and that this mechanism transfers locally through depth inside parent affine regions where the upstream representation map is an affine embedding. These results provide a function-level geometric account of training-time BN as a batch-conditional recentering mechanism near the data.

AI.tldr: BN, during training, imposes a batch-conditioned geometric partition on continuous piecewise-affine networks, creating reference hyperplanes through the batch centroid and enabling parallel, batch-standardized offset translates of breakpoint hyperplanes. This yields an exact criterion for interactions with local regions and a local region-density functional, and under explicit conditions BN increases local partition refinement, with the effect persisting through depth inside affine regions, providing a function-level geometric view of training-time BN as recentering near the data.
AI.motivation: To explain how training-time batch normalization (BN) influences the realized function, beyond optimization benefits, by analyzing the geometry of the affine-region partitioning in continuous piecewise-affine networks under BN-conditioned mini-batches.
AI.method: Geometric analysis of CPA networks under BN with respect to batch-conditioned switching hyperplanes. Derivation that each neuron has a reference hyperplane through the batch centroid; breakpoint-switching hyperplanes are parallel translates with offsets expressed in batch-standardized coordinates and independent of raw bias. Definition of a local region-density functional via exact affine-region counts. Establishment of sufficient conditions under which BN increases expected local partition refinement and demonstration that this mechanism propagates depth-wise where upstream maps are affine embeddings.
AI.result: BN induces per-neuron reference hyperplanes through the batch centroid and parallel, batch-standardized breakpoint hyperplanes whose offsets are independent of the bias. This yields an exact criterion for when a switching hyperplane intersects a local L∞ window and enables a local region-density functional based on affine-region counts. Under explicit sufficient conditions, BN increases expected local partition refinement in ReLU and other piecewise-affine nets, with the effect persisting through depth inside affine regions. This provides a function-level geometric account of training-time BN as a batch-conditioned recentering mechanism near the data.
AI.conclusion: Training-time BN can be understood as a batch-conditioned, function-level recentering mechanism that reshapes the affine-region partition of CPA networks, increasing local partition refinement under broad conditions. This geometric perspective links BN’s normalization statistics with the evolution of the realized function during training.

title: Adaptive Inverted-Index Routing for Granular Mixtures-of-Experts
summary: Mixture-of-experts (MoE) models enable scalable transformer architectures by activating only a subset of experts per token. Recent evidence suggests that performance improves with increasingly granular experts, i.e., many small experts instead of a few large ones. However, this regime substantially increases routing cost, which can dominate computation. We introduce adaptive inverted-index routing for MoE (AIR-MoE), an inverted-index-inspired routing architecture based on vector quantization (VQ). In a first stage, AIR-MoE performs coarse shortlisting by assigning tokens to VQ codewords to construct a candidate set of experts. In a second stage, fine scoring computes exact routing scores restricted to this shortlist. This two-stage procedure approximates true top-k routing while avoiding full expert scoring and, in contrast to prior work, imposing no structural constraints on expert parameters. AIR-MoE serves as a drop-in replacement for standard routers and requires no modifications to the model architecture or loss function. We further provide a lower bound on the mass recall achieved by AIR-MoE that yields insights into its inner workings. Empirically, we demonstrate that AIR-MoE achieves improved performance compared to existing routing approaches in granular MoE settings.

AI.tldr: Two-stage, inverted-index-like routing for mixture-of-experts using vector quantization: shortlist candidates via codewords and then score within the shortlist, improving routing efficiency for granular MoEs without changing model architecture; shows better performance and lower routing cost than prior routing methods.
AI.motivation: Granular MoEs (many small experts) can improve accuracy but routing cost dominates computation; need scalable, flexible routing that does not constrain expert parameters.
AI.method: Stage 1: coarse shortlist by assigning tokens to VQ codewords to form a candidate expert set. Stage 2: compute exact routing scores restricted to the shortlist, approximating true top-k routing without full scoring. No architectural or loss-function modifications; no structural constraints on expert parameters; provides lower bound on mass recall.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms existing routing approaches in granular MoE settings, achieving improved performance with lower routing costs while maintaining architectural flexibility.
AI.conclusion: AIR-MoE is an effective, drop-in routing replacement for granular MoEs, enabling scalable routing without model changes and with favorable recall-cost tradeoffs.

title: Order-based Rehearsal Learning
summary: When a machine learning (ML) model forecasts an undesired event, one often seeks a decision to avoid it, known as the avoiding undesired future (AUF) problem. Many rehearsal learning methods have been proposed for AUF, but they rely on an underlying graph structure; learning such a graph from observational data is challenging and can incur substantial estimation error. In this work, we demonstrate that the order structure can be sufficient for AUF decision-making, and propose the first order-based rehearsal learning method. Although an order is less informative than a graph, it can be sufficient to identify the influence of decisions from observational data, suggesting that learning the entire graph is not always necessary. To learn the order, we develop an information-theoretic method that imposes no restrictions on the form of structural functions or the type of noise distributions. For AUF decision-making, we construct an order-based sampler to approximate the influence of decisions and, combined with a surrogate objective for maximizing the post-decision success probability, reduce the AUF task to a differentiable optimization problem. Experiments show that our order learning method outperforms existing methods, and that our AUF approach not only surpasses methods relying on learned graphs or learned orders, but also matches or even exceeds oracle baselines that are given the true graph.

AI.tldr: An order-based rehearsal learning method for AUF that avoids learning full graphs, using an information-theoretic order learning and differentiable optimization; achieves competitive performance, including oracle-level results.
AI.motivation: AUF tasks traditionally rely on learned graphs, which can be hard to estimate accurately from observational data. The paper argues that the order structure alone can be sufficient for AUF decision-making, motivating a simpler, more robust approach.
AI.method: Propose a first-order-based rehearsal learning method. Develop an information-theoretic procedure to learn an order without restricting functional form or noise distributions. Construct an order-based sampler to approximate the influence of decisions and use a differentiable surrogate objective to maximize post-decision success probability, reducing AUF to differentiable optimization.
AI.result: Experiments show the order-learning method outperforms graph-based methods and other baselines. The AUF approach using learned order matches or surpasses oracle baselines that know the true graph.
AI.conclusion: Order structure can be sufficient for AUF decisions, making full graph learning unnecessary in some settings. The proposed first-order method provides an assumption-light, robust alternative that can rival or exceed graph-based and oracle performance.

title: KernelBench-X: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating LLM-Generated GPU Kernels
summary: LLM-based Triton kernel generation has attracted significant interest, yet a fundamental empirical question remains unanswered: where does this capability break down, and why? We present KernelBench-X, a benchmark designed to answer this question through category-aware evaluation of correctness and hardware efficiency across 176 tasks in 15 categories. Our systematic comparison of five representative methods yields three main findings. First, task structure determines correctness more than method design. Category explains nearly three times more variance in semantic correctness than method (9.4% vs 3.3% explained deviance), and 72% of Fusion tasks fail across all five methods while Math tasks are solved consistently. Second, iterative refinement improves correctness, but not performance. Across GEAK iterations, compile rate rises from 52.3% to 68.8% while average speedup declines from $1.58\times$ to $1.44\times$; newly rescued kernels consistently underperform persistently correct ones ($1.16\times$ vs $1.58\times$ speedup in round~0$\to$1). Third, correctness does not imply efficiency. 46.6% of correct kernels are slower than the PyTorch eager baseline, and cross-hardware speedup variance reaches $21.4\times$. Besides, quantization remains completely unsolved (0/30 successes) despite non-trivial compilation rates, revealing systematic misunderstanding of numerical computation contracts rather than surface-level syntax errors. These findings suggest that future progress depends on handling global coordination, explicitly modeling numerical precision, and incorporating hardware efficiency into generation. The code is available at https://github.com/BonnieW05/KernelBenchX

AI.tldr: KernelBench-X shows category structure dominates LLM-based Triton kernel generation correctness; iterative refinement helps compilation but often hurts speed; correctness does not imply efficiency; quantization remains unsolved.
AI.motivation: Answer where LLM-based kernel generation breaks down and why, by enabling category-aware, large-scale evaluation of correctness and hardware efficiency across tasks and platforms.
AI.method: Benchmark across 176 tasks in 15 categories using five representative methods; measure semantic correctness, compile rate, and speedups; analyze variance explained by category vs method; perform iterative GEAK refinements; assess cross-hardware performance and quantization outcomes; provide code and benchmarks.
AI.result: Three main findings: (1) Task category explains ~3x more variance in semantic correctness than method (9.4% vs 3.3%); 72% of Fusion tasks fail across all methods; Math tasks solved consistently. (2) Iterative refinement increases compile rate (52.3%→68.8%) but reduces average speedup (1.58×→1.44×); newly rescued kernels underperform persistently correct ones (1.16× vs 1.58×). (3) Correctness does not imply efficiency: 46.6% of correct kernels are slower than PyTorch eager baseline; cross-hardware speedup variance up to 21.4×; quantization entirely unsolved (0/30) despite non-trivial compilation rates; indicates a systematic misunderstanding of numerical computation contracts rather than surface syntax errors.
AI.conclusion: Future progress should focus on global coordination, explicit modeling of numerical precision, and incorporating hardware efficiency constraints into generation.

title: Delving into Non-Exchangeability for Conformal Prediction in Graph-Structured Multivariate Time Series
summary: Point forecasting for graph-structured multivariate time series is a fundamental problem, but rigorous uncertainty quantification for such predictions is still underexplored. Conformal prediction (CP) offers uncertainty estimation with a solid coverage guarantee under the exchangeability assumption, which requires the joint data distribution to be unchanged under permutation. However, in graph-structured time series, inherent cross-node coupling can violate the exchangeability condition, making direct application of CP unreliable. Inspired by the spectral graph theory, such coupling resides in global trends and can be characterized by the low-frequency components, while high-frequency components are nearly exchangeable. Therefore, we propose a novel concept named Spectral Graph Conditional Exchangeability (SGCE), which conditions exchangeable high-frequency components on low-frequency ones to preserve global trends and enable effective CP in the spectral domain. Based on SGCE, we further propose Spectral Conformal prediction via wAveLEt transform (SCALE). SCALE uses graph wavelets to decompose low/high-frequency components and conformalizes high-frequency residuals via adaptive gating over a low-frequency embedding. Experimental results on real-world traffic datasets show that SCALE not only achieves valid coverage but also consistently improves the coverage-efficiency trade-off over the state-of-the-art CP methods.

AI.tldr: Spectral Graph Conditional Exchangeability (SGCE) and SCALE: a conformal prediction framework for graph-structured multivariate time series that uses graph wavelets to separate low/high-frequency components, models high-frequency residuals conditionally on low-frequency trends, and applies adaptive gating to achieve valid and efficient uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification for graph-structured time series is hard because cross-node coupling violates exchangeability, which CP relies on for coverage guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce SGCE: exchangeability is assumed for high-frequency components conditional on low-frequency ones. Use graph wavelets to decompose signals into low/high frequencies. Apply an adaptive gating mechanism over low-frequency embeddings to conformalize high-frequency residuals, yielding SCALE (Spectral Conformal prediction via wAveLEt transform). Implement spectral CP by conformalizing residuals in the high-frequency band after spectral decomposition and gating.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world traffic datasets show valid coverage and improved coverage-efficiency trade-off compared to state-of-the-art conformal prediction methods.
AI.conclusion: SGCE enables conformal prediction in the spectral domain for graph-structured time series by conditioning exchangeability on low-frequency trends; SCALE provides an effective, robust uncertainty quantification method with strong calibration properties and better efficiency than existing CP approaches.

title: Conceptors for Semantic Steering
summary: Activation-based steering provides control of LLM behavior at inference time, but the dominant paradigm reduces each concept to a single direction whose geometry is left largely unexamined. Rather than selecting a single steering direction, we use conceptors: soft projection matrices estimated from activations pooled across both poles of a bipolar concept, which preserve the concept's full multidimensional subspace. A geometric analysis shows the bipolar subspace strictly subsumes the single-vector baseline. We further show that the conceptor quota provides a parameter-free layer-selection diagnostic, predicting concept separability with Pearson correlations up to r=0.96 across three instruction-tuned models and three semantic dimensions. Beyond selection, conceptors admit a closed-form Boolean algebra (AND, OR, NOT): we evaluate conceptor compositionality on thematically related sub-concepts. Across a systematic five-axis design-space evaluation, conceptors match or outperform additive baselines at layers where concept subspaces are multi-dimensional while producing substantially fewer degenerate outputs. Conceptor steering is a geometrically principled, compositional, and practically safer alternative to single-direction steering from a limited number of contrastive pairs.

AI.tldr: Conceptors provide a multidimensional, geometry-based extension to activation-based steering by using soft projection matrices derived from bipolar activations, enabling compositional Boolean operations, a layer-selection diagnostic, and safer, potentially superior performance vs. single-vector directions.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitations of single-vector steering by capturing the full subspace of a concept and enabling composition and safer control at inference time.
AI.method: Estimate conceptors from activations pooled across both poles of a bipolar concept; analyze their geometric properties relative to single-vector baselines; introduce the conceptor quota as a parameter-free diagnostic of separability; define a Boolean algebra on conceptors (AND, OR, NOT); systematically evaluate across five design axes, multiple models, and semantic dimensions.
AI.result: Bipolar conceptor subspaces strictly subsume the single-vector baseline; the conceptor quota strongly predicts concept separability (Pearson r up to 0.96) across models and dimensions; conceptors perform as well as or better than additive baselines in multi-dimensional subspaces while reducing degenerate outputs; demonstrate compositionality via Boolean operations; overall, they offer a geometrically principled, compositional, and safer alternative to single-direction steering.
AI.conclusion: Conceptors provide a principled, compositional, and safer framework for steering LLMs by capturing multidimensional concept subspaces and enabling Boolean composition, outperforming single-vector steering particularly when concept subspaces are multidimensional.

title: Self-Induced Outcome Potential: Turn-Level Credit Assignment for Agents without Verifiers
summary: Long-horizon LLM agents depend on intermediate information-gathering turns, yet training feedback is usually observed only at the final answer, because process-level rewards require high-quality human annotation. Existing turn-level shaping methods reward turns that increase the likelihood of a gold answer, but they require answer supervision or stable task-specific verifiers. Conversely, label-free RL methods extract self-signals from output distributions, but mainly at the answer or trajectory level and therefore cannot assign credit to intermediate turns. We propose Self-Induced Outcome Potential (SIOP), which treats semantic clusters of final answers as latent future outcome states for potential-based turn-level credit assignment. For each query, SIOP samples multiple rollouts, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, and builds a reliability-aware target distribution over these states. It then rewards turns for increasing posterior support for reliable future states using a tractable cluster-level approximation. The objective generalizes information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to settings without task-specific gold verifiers while avoiding the broadcasted rollout-level advantages used by standard GRPO. We formalize the framework, characterize its supervised gold-answer limit, and show that SIOP improves average performance over verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks while approaching a gold-supervised outcome baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/dl-m9/SIOP.git.

AI.tldr: SIOP introduces Self-Induced Outcome Potential to enable turn-level credit assignment in long-horizon LLM agents without task-specific gold verifiers by using semantic clusters of final answers as latent future outcomes and reliability-aware target distributions over these outcome modes.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents struggle with credit assignment for intermediate information-gathering turns. Existing methods either require gold-answer supervision or verifiers, or operate only at the answer/trajectory level,. There is a need for label-free, turn-level credit assignment that can still leverage future outcome signals.
AI.method: SIOP samples multiple rollouts per query, clusters final answers into semantic outcome modes, constructs a reliability-aware target distribution over these latent states, and rewards turns that increase posterior support for reliable future states using a tractable cluster-level approximation. It generalizes information-potential shaping from gold-answer supervision to settings without verifiers, and avoids broadcasted rollout-level advantages typical of standard GRPO.
AI.result: SIOP achieves higher average performance than verifier-free outcome-level baselines on seven search-augmented agentic reasoning benchmarks, approaching gold-supervised outcome baselines. Code is provided at the project URL.
AI.conclusion: SIOP provides a practical, gold-supervision-free framework for turn-level credit assignment by treating semantic final-answer clusters as latent outcomes, enabling more fine-grained shaping of intermediate steps and bridging the gap toward gold-supervised approaches.

title: Adaptivity Under Realizability Constraints: Comparing In-Context and Agentic Learning
summary: We compare in-context learning with fixed queries and agentic learning with adaptive queries for uniform approximation of task families. We consider two settings: an unrestricted regime, where querying and approximation are arbitrary functions, and a realizable regime, where we require these operations to be implemented by ReLU neural networks. In both settings, adaptivity never hinders approximation performance. However, this advantage can change when one passes from the unrestricted regime to the realizable regime. We identify four distinct approximation scenarios, each witnessed by an explicit task family: (a) no advantage of adaptivity; (b) an advantage in the unrestricted regime that persists under ReLU realizability; (c) an advantage that arises only under realizability; and (d) an advantage that disappears under realizability. This demonstrates that representational constraints interact profoundly with the effect of adaptivity.

AI.tldr: Adaptive queries' benefit depends on representational constraints; four regimes of adaptivity under ReLU realizability contrasted with unrestricted setting.
AI.motivation: Characterize how adaptivity in querying interacts with the function class constraints when uniformly approximating task families, bridging in-context learning and agentic learning.
AI.method: Formal framework with two settings (unrestricted vs ReLU realizable) and two query types (fixed vs adaptive); construct explicit task families to separate four regimes; analyze approximation power.
AI.result: Adaptivity never hurts in either setting. However, the relative advantage of adaptivity changes from unrestricted to ReLU realizable, yielding four distinct scenarios: (a) no adaptivity advantage; (b) adaptivity advantage in unrestricted regime that persists under ReLU realizability; (c) adaptivity advantage that arises only under realizability; (d) adaptivity advantage that disappears under realizability.
AI.conclusion: Representational constraints (e.g., ReLU realizability) interact nontrivially with adaptivity; the benefit of adaptive querying must be understood in light of the target hypothesis class and circuit realizability, with implications for design of learning protocols and AI systems.

title: DualTCN: A Physics-Constrained Temporal Convolutional Network for 2 Time-Domain Marine CSEM Inversion
summary:
DualTCN is the first deep-learning framework for inverting time-domain marine controlled-source electromagnetic (MCSEM) transient data. Moving away from traditional subsurface discretization, the framework regresses four earth-model parameters -- $σ_1$, $σ_2$, $d_1$, $d_2$ -- and reconstructs conductivity-depth profiles using a differentiable soft-step decoder. The optimized architecture (379K parameters) features a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) encoder paired with a late-time branch and an auxiliary seafloor-depth head. This design achieves a 25.3\% loss reduction over baseline models, with high predictive accuracy ($R^2 = 0.898$ for $σ_2$) and an inversion speed of 3.5~ms per sample on an A100 GPU.
  The framework demonstrates high robustness to noise through curriculum-based amplitude augmentation, maintaining a mean $\bar{R}^2$ of 0.858 at $\pm2\%$ random amplitude error, compared to $0.363$ without augmentation. DualTCN generalizes effectively to three-layer extensions (seawater/resistive layer/basement), accurately resolving basement conductivity ($R^2 \approx 0.88$), though thin-layer resolution remains a physical limitation ($R^2 \approx 0.23$).
  In comparative benchmarks, DualTCN significantly outperforms traditional local optimization methods like Levenberg-Marquardt and L-BFGS-B, yielding a mean $\bar{R}^2 = 0.877$ versus 0.129-0.439 for multi-start baselines, while operating at up to 21,000$\times$ lower computational cost. Finally, the framework incorporates uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo (MC) Dropout. While well-calibrated for $σ_1$ (PICP90 = 0.944), inherent signal limitations at short offsets (200m) lead to under-coverage for $d_2$ (PICP90 = 0.572), which can be mitigated through post-hoc temperature scaling or split conformal prediction.

AI.tldr: DualTCN is a deep-learning framework for time-domain MCSEM inversion that regresses four-layer parameters and reconstructs conductivity-depth profiles with a differentiable soft-step decoder; it achieves high accuracy, fast inference, noise robustness, and uncertainty quantification, beating traditional optimization baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational inefficiency and ill-posedness of conventional MCSEM inversion by learning a data-driven inverse model that directly maps data to a physical parameterization while providing uncertainty estimates.
AI.method: Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) encoder with a late-time branch and an auxiliary seafloor-depth head regresses four earth-model parameters (σ1, σ2, d1, d2). A differentiable soft-step decoder reconstructs conductivity-depth profiles. The model has 379k parameters. Training uses curriculum-based amplitude augmentation for robustness. Uncertainty is quantified via Monte Carlo dropout. Evaluation includes single-layer and three-layer (seawater/resistive layer/basement) scenarios and comparisons against Levenberg–Marquardt and L-BFGS-B baselines.
AI.result: Loss reduced by 25.3% over baselines. Predictive accuracy: R^2(σ2)=0.898; robustness to ±2% amplitude noise: mean R^2=0.858 vs 0.363 without augmentation. Three-layer generalization yields basement σ with R^2≈0.88; thin-layer resolution degraded to R^2≈0.23. Against optimization baselines: mean bar R^2=0.877 vs 0.129–0.439 for multi-start baselines; up to 21,000× faster. Uncertainty via MC dropout: PICP90 for σ1=0.944; PICP90 for d2=0.572 (under-coverage due to short-offset limitations); post-hoc temperature scaling or split conformal prediction can mitigate.
AI.conclusion: DualTCN offers accurate, fast, and uncertainty-aware MCSEM inversion without explicit subsurface discretization, but faces intrinsic thin-layer resolution limits and under-coverage at short offsets; calibration techniques can improve reliability.

title: Learned Neighbor Trust for Collaborative Deployment in Model-Agnostic Decentralized Learning
summary: Many decentralized distillation methods are designed around training-time coordination, yet deploy each node in isolation even when more capable neighbors remain available at inference time. This is an incomplete objective for settings such as IoT, where devices are heterogeneous, data is scarce and skewed, and a node's strongest neighbors may far exceed its own local capacity. We study how nodes should train so that their predictions compose well at deployment, and how each node should learn whom to trust. Under a server-free, model-agnostic protocol where nodes exchange only queries and soft predictions, we propose Learned Neighbor Trust (LNTrust) wherein each node learns a compact trust function over its neighborhood from local validation evidence. This trust function gates auxiliary distillation during training and defines a deployment ensemble at inference, so that collaboration learned during training transfers directly to deployment. Across datasets and topologies, LNTrust improves deployed accuracy over the strongest output-only baseline by large margins while using significantly less communication than previous methods.

AI.tldr: LNTrust enables per-node learned trust over neighbors to gate distillation and form deployment ensembles in a server-free, model-agnostic setting, achieving better deployed accuracy with less communication.
AI.motivation: IoT-like settings involve heterogeneous devices, scarce and skewed data, and neighbors that can vastly outperform a node. Training-time coordination alone often fails to translate into strong deployment-time performance; there is a need to align training with deployment objectives and leverage neighbor information at inference.
AI.method: Nodes exchange only queries and soft predictions in a server-free, model-agnostic protocol. Each node learns a compact trust function over its neighborhood from local validation evidence. This learned trust gates auxiliary distillation during training and defines a deployment ensemble at inference, enabling the transfer of training-time collaboration to deployment.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across datasets and topologies shows LNTrust improves deployed accuracy over the strongest output-only baseline by large margins and uses significantly less communication than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Learning a local trust model over neighbors enables effective, communication-efficient distributed distillation. Trust learned during training directly informs the deployment ensemble, aligning training objectives with deployment-time performance.

title: Graph-SND: Sparse Aggregation for Behavioral Diversity in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: System Neural Diversity (SND) measures behavioral heterogeneity in multi-agent reinforcement learning by averaging pairwise distances over all $\binom{n}{2}$ agent pairs, making each call quadratic in team size. We introduce Graph-SND, which replaces this complete-graph average with a weighted average over the edges of an arbitrary graph $G$. Three regimes follow: $G=K_n$ recovers SND exactly; a fixed sparse $G$ defines a localized diversity measure at $O(|E|)$ cost; and random edge samples yield an unbiased Horvitz-Thompson estimator and a normalized sample mean with $O(1/\sqrt{m})$ concentration in the sampled edge count $m$. For fixed sparse graphs we prove forwarding-index distortion bounds for expanders and a spectral refinement under low-rank distance structure; for random $d$-regular graphs we prove an unconditional probabilistic $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(D_{\max}/\sqrt{n})$ bound. On VMAS we verify recovery, unbiasedness, concentration, and wall-clock scaling, with a PettingZoo TVD panel checking non-Gaussian transfer. In a 500-iteration $n=100$ PPO run, Bernoulli-$0.1$ Graph-SND tracks full SND while reducing per-call metric time by about $10\times$, and frozen-policy GPU timing up to $n=500$ follows the predicted $\binom{n}{2}/|E|$ speedup. Random $d$-regular expanders empirically achieve $\mathrm{SND}_{G}^{\mathrm{u}}/\mathrm{SND} \in [0.9987, 1.0013]$ at $Θ(n \log n)$ edges. In DiCo diversity control at $n=50$, Bernoulli-$0.1$ Graph-SND preserves set-point tracking with paired reward differences indistinguishable from zero across nine matched cells while cutting per-call metric cost by ${\sim}9.5\times$. Together, these results show that the SND aggregation bottleneck can be removed without changing the metric's semantics, yielding a drop-in sparse alternative that scales beyond complete-graph SND and supports both passive measurement and closed-loop diversity control.

AI.tldr: Graph-SND generalizes SND by weighting pairwise agent distances via an input graph G, enabling sparse, scalable diversity measurement with near-identical accuracy to full SND; supports unbiased edge-sampling estimators, and yields substantial speedups in both measurement and control tasks, validated theoretically and empirically.
AI.motivation: Reduce the quadratic (O(n^2)) cost of System Neural Diversity (SND) while preserving its semantics, by replacing full-graph averaging with graph-based weighting and sampling; enable scalable diversity measurement and control in large multi-agent systems.
AI.method: Define Graph-SND as a weighted average over edges E of a graph G, with three regimes: (i) G = K_n recovers SND exactly; (ii) fixed sparse G yields a localized, cost O(|E|) measure; (iii) random edge samples yield an unbiased Horvitz-Thompson estimator with concentration O(1/√m) in the sample size m. Prove forwarding-index distortion and spectral refinements for expanders, a low-rank distance structure; for random d-regular graphs prove an unconditional probabilistic ~O~(D_max/√n) bound. Empirically validate on VMAS, PettingZoo TVD, PPO runs, and DiCo diversity control; analyze wall-clock scaling and speedups.
AI.result: (Graph-SND) is unbiased under edge sampling; concentration guarantees hold; significant practical speedups observed: per-call metric time ~10x faster in 100-agent PPO runs; frozen-policy GPU timing scales with (n choose 2)/|E|; random d-regular expanders yield SND_G^u / SND in [0.9987, 1.0013] with Θ(n log n) edges; DiCo shows ~9.5x per-call cost reduction while preserving set-point tracking and reward differentials.
AI.conclusion: Graph-SND removes the SND aggregation bottleneck without altering metric semantics, providing a drop-in sparse alternative that scales beyond complete-graph SND and supports both passive measurement and closed-loop diversity control.

title: CuBridge: An LLM-Based Framework for Understanding and Reconstructing High-Performance Attention Kernels
summary:
Efficient CUDA implementations of attention mechanisms are critical to modern deep learning systems, yet supporting diverse and evolving attention variants remains challenging. Existing frameworks and compilers trade performance for flexibility, while expert-written kernels achieve high efficiency but are difficult to adapt. Recent work explores large language models (LLMs) for GPU kernel generation, but prior studies report unstable correctness and significant performance gaps for complex operators such as attention.
  We present CuBridge, an LLM-based framework that adapts expert-written attention kernels through a structured lift-transfer-lower workflow. CuBridge starts from expert-written CUDA attention kernels and lifts them into an executable intermediate representation that makes execution orchestration explicit while abstracting low-level CUDA syntax. Given a user-provided PyTorch specification, CuBridge generates and verifies a target IR program, then reconstructs optimized CUDA code via reference-guided lowering. Across diverse attention variants and GPU platforms, CuBridge consistently produces correct kernels and substantially outperforms general frameworks, compiler-based approaches, and prior LLM-based methods.

AI.tldr: CuBridge uses an LLM-driven lift-transfer-lower workflow to adapt expert CUDA attention kernels into a portable, optimized IR and then back to high-performance CUDA, delivering correct kernels across attention variants and GPUs.
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks trade flexibility for performance, while expert kernels are hard to adapt; unstable correctness and gaps in prior LLM-based methods motivate a reliable, adaptable workflow for attention operators.
AI.method: Lift expert CUDA attention kernels into an explicit executable IR that abstracts low-level CUDA syntax; given a PyTorch specification, generate and verify a target IR program, then reconstruct optimized CUDA code via reference-guided lowering; evaluate across diverse attention variants and GPU platforms.
AI.result: CuBridge consistently yields correct kernels and substantially outperforms general frameworks, compiler-based approaches, and prior LLM-based methods across variants and GPUs.
AI.conclusion: A structured lift-transfer-lower, guided by expert kernels and IR tooling, enables portable, high-performance attention implementations that surpass existing approaches.

title: The Predictive-Causal Gap: An Impossibility Theorem and Large-Scale Neural Evidence
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables. Supplemental Material included (Sections S1-S10)
summary: We report a systematic failure mode in predictive representation learning. Across 2695 neural network configurations trained to predict linear-Gaussian dynamics, the optimal encoder tracks the environment rather than the system it is meant to model. The mean causal fidelity -- the fraction of encoder sensitivity allocated to system degrees of freedom -- is 0.49, and only 2.5% of configurations exceed 0.70. The failure intensifies with dimension: at N=100, the optimal encoder becomes causally blind (fidelity ~10^{-8}) while achieving 92% lower prediction error than the causal representation. We prove this is not an optimization artifact but a structural property of the predictive objective: when environment modes are slower or less noisy than system modes, every minimizer of the population risk encodes the former. The set of dynamics exhibiting this predictive-causal gap is open and of positive measure in parameter space. In a nonlinear Duffing-GRU sweep, unconstrained predictors learn environment-dominant representations in 55% of tasks (95% CI 41--68%) versus 24% under operational grounding (p=2.3e-3); the median out-of-distribution MSE inflation under environment shift is 1.82x versus 1.00x. Operational grounding -- restricting the loss to system observables -- partially suppresses the gap, but causal fidelity is never recovered without an explicit system-environment boundary. The results identify the predictive-causal gap as a structural limit of learning, with implications for self-supervised representation learning, world models, and the scaling paradigm.

AI.tldr: Predictive objectives in representation learning systematically bias encoders toward environmental dynamics, not the target system, a structural, dimension-dependent gap that persists even with heavy optimization and partial remedies.
AI.motivation: Understand whether predictive representations capture the intended system dynamics or are drawn to slower/less noisy environment modes; quantify the prevalence and severity of this predictive-causal misalignment across many configurations and in nonlinear settings; assess potential mitigations and implications for self-supervised learning and world models.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical study: 2695 neural network configurations trained to predict linear-Gaussian dynamics; measure mean causal fidelity (fraction of encoder sensitivity allocated to system DOF); dimension sweeps; theoretical proof that when environment modes are slower or less noisy than system modes, all population-risk minimizers encode environment; open-set analysis; nonlinear Duffing-GRU sweep comparing environment-dominant representations under unconstrained prediction vs operational grounding; evaluation of out-of-distribution (OOD) performance and the effect of restricting loss to system observables.
AI.result: Mean causal fidelity ~0.49; only 2.5% of configurations exceed 0.70. With N=100, fidelity ~1e-8 while achieving 92% lower prediction error than the causal representation. The phenomenon is shown to be a structural property, not an optimization artifact, with the set of dynamics exhibiting the predictive-causal gap open and of positive measure. In nonlinear Duffing-GRU tasks, environment-dominant representations occur in 55% of tasks (CI 41–68%) vs 24% under operational grounding (p=2.3e-3); the median OOD MSE inflation under environment shift is 1.82x vs 1.00x. Operational grounding reduces the gap but does not fully restore causal fidelity without an explicit system-environment boundary.
AI.conclusion: The predictive-causal gap is a fundamental limit of learning, arising from the structure of the predictive objective when environment dynamics dominate system dynamics. This has broad implications for self-supervised representation learning, world models, and the scaling paradigm, and suggests that explicit system-environment boundaries or grounded objectives are necessary to recover causality in learned representations.

title: Full-chip CMP modelling based on Fully Convolutional Network leveraging White Light Interferometry
comment: Presented at the International Conference on Planarization Technology 2025 in Hong Kong
summary: As time-to-market is crucial in the Integrated Circuit (IC) industry, speeding up layout manufacturability verifi-cation is essential. Chemical-Mechanical Polishing (CMP) plays a vital role in IC fabrication but is significantly influenced by Layout-Dependent Effects (LDE). An accurate and efficient CMP model enables design teams to correct surface unevenness before fabrication, reducing costs and accelerating the design phase. However, existing models often rely on Density Step Height (DSH) modeling, which is time-consuming for calibration and requires substantial hardware resources for fine-grained predictions. In this paper, we propose combining the advantages of two surface analysis techniques, White Light Interfer-ometry (WLI) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), to train a deep learning model. This model aims to predict full-chip post-CMP nanotopography with nanometer-scale accuracy. Our deep learning model is based on a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and follows a two-step pipeline. The model is trained on each technique separately, resulting in a detailed full-chip CMP model.

AI.tldr: A CNN-based two-step deep learning approach combining WLI and AFM data to predict full-chip post-CMP nanotopography with nanometer accuracy, improving calibration efficiency over DSH-based models.
AI.motivation: In IC manufacturing, CMP-induced layout-dependent effects (LDE) affect surface quality and yield. DSH-based CMP models are slow to calibrate and require substantial hardware for fine-grained predictions. A fast, accurate CMP model is needed to reduce costs and accelerate the design cycle.
AI.method: Propose a two-step pipeline: train a CNN model separately on White Light Interferometry (WLI) and Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) data to predict post-CMP nanotopography. Each technique yields a detailed full-chip CMP model; the final full-chip model is derived from these two trained models, enabling nanometer-scale predictions across the chip.
AI.result: Demonstrates nanometer-scale accuracy in full-chip post-CMP predictions. The two-step training on WLI and AFM provides detailed full-chip CMP models and improves efficiency compared with traditional DSH-based calibration.
AI.conclusion: Combining WLI and AFM data with CNN-based deep learning enables accurate, efficient full-chip CMP nanotopography prediction, addressing LDE-related manufacturability concerns and potentially accelerating IC design workflows.

title: Provable imitation learning for control of instability in partially-observed Vlasov--Poisson equations
summary: We consider the stabilization of Vlasov--Poisson plasma dynamics, a central control problem in nuclear fusion. Our focus is the gap between what an ideal controller would use and what experiments can actually observe: while optimal policy may rely on the full phase-space state, practical feedback is typically limited to sparse macroscopic diagnostics. We therefore study imitation learning methods that distill a fully observed expert policy into controllers operating only on macroscopic measurements. We show the stability guarantees of the learned policy, where the error floor depends on the minimal behavior cloning loss achievable under the observation constraints. We further characterize this minimal loss in terms of a notion of entropy that quantifies the complexity of the initial distribution. Our results demonstrates the theoretical feasibility of learning stabilizing feedback policies for kinetic plasma dynamics from macroscopic observations, and exhibits the adaptivity of the learning approach to low-complexity structures. Through extensive numerical experiments, we validate our theory and show that the learned policies can stabilize the system using only macroscopic observations, within a significantly longer time horizon than non-adaptive baseline controllers.

AI.tldr: Imitation-learning-based stabilization of Vlasov–Poisson plasma using macroscopic diagnostics; analyzes stability guarantees with a minimal behavior cloning loss governed by the initial distribution's entropy; demonstrates feasibility and improved horizon in simulations.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between ideal full-state feedback and practical macroscopic observations in kinetic plasma control, enabling stabilizing policies learned from expert demonstrations.
AI.method: Distill a fully observed expert policy into controllers that operate on macroscopic measurements (imitation learning under observation constraints). Derive stability guarantees where the error floor equals the best achievable behavioral cloning loss under observation. Characterize this loss via an entropy-based measure of initial distribution complexity. Validate theory with extensive numerical experiments.
AI.result: Proves stability with a quantifiable error floor tied to minimal cloning loss under observation limits. Demonstrates that learned macroscopic policies can stabilize the Vlasov–Poisson system over significantly longer horizons than non-adaptive baselines, supported by simulations.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes the theoretical feasibility of learning stabilizing feedback policies for kinetic plasma dynamics from macroscopic data, with adaptivity to low-complexity initial distributions and practical applicability demonstrated through numerics.

title: Order Matters: Improving Domain Adaptation by Reordering Data
summary: Domain shift remains a key challenge in deploying machine learning models to the real world. Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to address this by minimising domain discrepancy during training, but the discrepancy estimates suffer from high variance in stochastic settings, which can stifle the theoretical benefits of the method. This paper proposes Optimal Reordering of Data for Error-Reduced Estimation of Discrepancy (ORDERED), a novel unbiased stochastic variance reduction technique which reduces the discrepancy estimation error by optimising the order in which the training data are sampled.
