We consider two specific domain discrepancy losses (correlation alignment and the maximum mean discrepancy), formulate their stochastic estimation error as a function of the data sampling order, and propose a practical optimisation algorithm. Our simulations demonstrate reduced variance compared to related methods, and experiments on two domain shift image classification benchmarks show improved target domain accuracy.

AI.tldr: ORDERED is a variance-reduction technique for unsupervised domain adaptation that reorders data sampling to reduce discrepancy estimation variance for CORAL and MMD, improving target accuracy.
AI.motivation: Domain shift challenges: discrepancy estimates in stochastic UDA have high variance, limiting theoretical benefits. Need unbiased, low-variance estimation of domain discrepancy.
AI.method: Develop ORDERED, an unbiased stochastic variance-reduction method that optimizes the data sampling order to minimize the discrepancy-estimation error for CORAL and MMD. Formulate the estimation error as a function of sampling order and propose a practical optimization algorithm, ensuring unbiasedness of the discrepancy estimates.
AI.result: Simulations show reduced variance compared with related methods; experiments on two domain-shift image classification benchmarks show improved target-domain accuracy.
AI.conclusion: ORDERED reduces variance in discrepancy estimation for UDA and yields practical performance gains; adaptable to multiple domain-discrepancy losses like CORAL and MMD.

title: Gated Multimodal Learning for Interpretable Property Energy Performance Prediction and Retrofit Scenario Analysis
summary: Achieving resilient and sustainable cities requires scalable approaches to decarbonising residential buildings, which account for about 20% of UK greenhouse gas emissions and 25% of energy-related emissions in the European Union. Energy Performance Certificates (EPCs) support regulation and retrofit planning, but their reliance on on-site inspections limits timely city-scale assessment. This study introduces a gated multimodal model to predict Standard Assessment Procedure (SAP) energy efficiency and Environmental Impact (EI) scores by integrating EPC tabular variables, assessor-written free text, and Geographic Information System (GIS)-derived spatial features describing footprint geometry, height, area, and orientation. Sample-wise gating learns property-specific modality weights, while an auxiliary band classification head stabilises training. In a Westminster, London case study, the model predicts SAP and EI scores with MAEs of 4.03 and 4.76 points and R2 values of 0.757 and 0.748, respectively, achieving a mean MAE of 4.39. Ablation results show that full multimodal fusion outperforms unimodal and bimodal baselines for both score prediction and band-level classification. Interpretability analyses provide decision-relevant evidence: gating weights indicate strong reliance on assessor text; SHAP highlights main fuel, built form, and construction age band; text occlusion prioritises roof and wall fields; and spatial attribution is dominated by height and footprint area, with sensitivity to footprint shape. The validated framework is further applied to retrofit scenarios for wall insulation, roof insulation, and window glazing upgrades, indicating projected improvements in SAP, EI, annual energy cost, and equivalent CO2 emissions. Overall, the framework provides scalable property-level evidence for retrofit screening, intervention prioritisation, and net-zero housing transitions.

AI.tldr: A gated multimodal model combines EPC tabular data, assessor text, and GIS features to predict SAP energy efficiency and EI scores, outperforming unimodal baselines; Westminster validation; enables retrofit screening and net-zero housing planning.
AI.motivation: Decarbonizing residential buildings requires scalable city-scale assessment beyond on-site inspections. EPCs offer data but limited by inspections; multimodal fusion aims to improve accuracy, interpretability, and actionable retrofit planning.
AI.method: Proposed gated multimodal architecture with sample-wise gating to weight modalities; auxiliary band classification head for training stability. Inputs: EPC tabular variables, assessor-written free text, GIS-derived spatial features (height, footprint area, orientation). Evaluated via Westminster, London case study with ablations; interpretability analyses using gating weights, SHAP, text occlusion, and spatial attribution; extended to retrofit scenarios (wall/roof insulation, window glazing).
AI.result: SAP and EI scores predicted with MAEs of 4.03 and 4.76; R^2 of 0.757 and 0.748; mean MAE 4.39. Ablation shows full multimodal fusion outperforms unimodal/bimodal baselines for score prediction and band-level classification. Interpretability evidence: gating weights rely on assessor text; SHAP highlights main fuel, built form, construction age; text occlusion emphasizes roof/walls; spatial attribution dominated by height and footprint area; retrofit scenarios indicate improvements in SAP, EI, energy cost, and CO2e.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides scalable property-level evidence for retrofit screening, intervention prioritization, and net-zero housing transitions, enabling policy-relevant city-scale decision support.

title: Unified Framework of Distributional Regret in Multi-Armed Bandits and Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at the Conference of Learning Theory (COLT) 2026
summary: We study the distribution of regret in stochastic multi-armed bandits and episodic reinforcement learning through a unified framework. We formalize a distributional regret bound as a probabilistic guarantee that holds uniformly over all confidence levels $δ\in (0,1]$, thereby characterizing the regret distribution across the full range of $δ$. We present a simple UCBVI-style algorithm with exploration bonus $\min\{c_{1,k}/N, c_{2,k}/\sqrt{N}\}$, where $N$ denotes the visit count and $(c_{1,k},c_{2,k})$ are user-specified parameters. For arbitrary parameter sequences, we derive general gap-independent and gap-dependent distributional regret bounds, yielding a principled characterization of how the parameters control the trade-off between expected performance, tail risk, and instance-dependent behavior. In particular, our bounds achieve optimal trade-offs between expected and distributional regret in both minimax and instance-dependent regimes. As a special case, for multi-armed bandits with $A$ arms and horizon $T$, we obtain a distributional regret bound of order $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{AT}\log(1/δ))$, confirming the conjecture of Lattimore & Szepesvári (2020, Section 17.1) for the first time.

AI.tldr: Unified distributional regret framework for stochastic bandits and episodic RL; introduces a UCBVI-style algorithm with a tunable exploration bonus; provides δ-uniform distributional regret bounds, achieving minimax and instance-dependent optimal trade-offs; for A-armed bandits with horizon T, yields O(√(A T) log(1/δ)) and confirms a prior conjecture.
AI.motivation: To analyze not just the expected regret but the entire distribution across confidence levels δ, enabling a tail-risk aware understanding; unify stochastic bandits and episodic RL under a single distributional regret framework and provide parameter-driven trade-offs between mean performance, tail risk, and instance dependence.
AI.method: Propose a simple UCBVI-style algorithm with exploration bonus min{ c_{1,k}/N, c_{2,k}/√N }, where N is visit count and (c_{1,k}, c_{2,k}) are user-specified. Derive general gap-independent and gap-dependent distributional regret bounds valid uniformly over δ ∈ (0,1], for arbitrary parameter sequences. Analyze minimax and instance-dependent regimes and apply to MAB to obtain δ-uniform bounds that match the conjectured rates.
AI.result: Establish distributional regret bounds that are uniform in δ, providing a principled link between expected regret, tail risk, and instance-dependent behavior. Demonstrate optimal trade-offs between expected and distributional regret in both minimax and instance-dependent settings. In the MAB setting (A arms, horizon T), prove a δ-uniform distributional regret bound of O(√(A T) log(1/δ)), thereby validating the conjecture of Lattimore & Szepesvári (2020).
AI.conclusion: The work presents a unified distributional regret framework with a tunable UCBVI-style algorithm, delivering δ-uniform guarantees that capture the full regret distribution and enable optimal mean–tail trade-offs in both bandits and episodic RL. It resolves the conjectured rate for MAB and provides a versatile tool for tail-risk-aware regret analysis.

title: Rollout Pass-Rate Control: Steering Binary-Reward RL Toward Its Most Informative Regime
summary: SWE-bench-style agentic reinforcement learning relies on expensive stateful trajectories, yet substantial compute is wasted on sampled rollout groups with skewed pass rates, where binary rewards provide a weak contrastive signal. We frame this inefficiency as a pass-rate control problem and show that a 50% pass rate is the most informative operating point: it maximizes reward entropy, the probability of surviving group filtering, RLOO advantage energy under GRPO, and success--failure contrastive structure. Guided by this principle, we propose Prefix Sampling (PS), which replays trajectory prefixes to steer skewed groups toward this regime: successful prefixes serve as head starts for mostly failing groups, while failing prefixes serve as handicaps for mostly passing groups. In stateful agent environments, prefix states are reconstructed through replay while replayed tokens are excluded from the loss, restricting optimization to continuations generated by the current policy. On SWE-bench-style agentic RL, PS delivers end-to-end wall-clock speedups of 2.01x on Qwen3-14B and 1.55x on Qwen3-32B while preserving or improving final verified performance. For 14B, the SWE-bench Verified peak rises from the baseline peak of 0.273 to 0.295 under PS. Additional mathematical reasoning experiments on AIME 2025 show the same pass-rate control pattern and decompose the gains into replay, bidirectional coverage, and adaptive control.

AI.tldr: Proposes Prefix Sampling (PS) to optimize pass-rate at 50% in SWE-bench-style agentic RL, yielding significant wall-clock speedups and maintained/improved performance; validated on Qwen LLMs (14B and 32B) and AIME 2025 with demonstrated gains decomposed into replay, bidirectional coverage, and adaptive control.
AI.motivation: Inefficient compute in stateful, rollout-based agentic RL due to skewed pass-rate groups; a 50% pass-rate maximizes information in rewards and signals, motivating a control strategy to steer groups toward this regime.
AI.method: Introduce Prefix Sampling (PS) that replays trajectory prefixes to steer pass-rate toward 50%. Successful prefixes seed mostly-failing groups; failing prefixes handicap mostly-passing groups. In stateful environments, reconstruct prefix states via replay and exclude replayed tokens from loss, ensuring optimization focuses on continuations generated by the current policy. Evaluations on SWE-bench-style agentic RL show speedups; 14B and 32B models tested (Qwen3-14B, Qwen3-32B).
AI.result: PS achieves end-to-end wall-clock speedups of 2.01x (Qwen3-14B) and 1.55x (Qwen3-32B); SWE-bench Verified peak for 14B rises from 0.273 to 0.295; AIME 2025 experiments show the same pass-rate pattern and decompose gains into replay, bidirectional coverage, and adaptive control.
AI.conclusion: A 50% pass-rate is the most informative operating point for reward-based optimization in this setting. Prefix Sampling provides an effective, generalizable mechanism to align data and learning dynamics with this regime, delivering substantial efficiency gains with or without final performance improvements.

title: How Long Does Infinite Width Last? Signal Propagation in Long-Range Linear Recurrences
summary: We study signal propagation in linear recurrent models at finite width. While existing signal propagation theory relies predominantly on the infinite-width limit, it remains unclear for how long that approximation remains accurate when recurrent depth $t$ grows jointly with width $n$. This question is especially relevant for modern recurrent sequence models, whose natural operating regime involves long input sequences, i.e., large $t$. We derive exact finite-width formulas for the hidden state signal energies in linear recurrences under complex Gaussian initialization. Using these formulas, we identify the joint depth-width scaling regimes that govern signal propagation: (i) a subcritical regime $t=o(\sqrt n)$, in which the infinite-width approximation remains valid; (ii) a critical regime $t\sim c\sqrt n$, in which non-negligible deviations from infinite-width predictions appear and a nontrivial joint scaling limit emerges; and (iii) a supercritical regime $t\gg \sqrt n$, in which finite-width effects dominate. Thus, our results pinpoint the precise recurrent depth scale at which infinite-width theory breaks down in long-range linear recurrences. In turn, this shows when standard initialization schemes, such as Glorot, become unstable. More broadly, our results demonstrate that finite-width effects accumulate more rapidly with depth in recurrent models than in feedforward ones, leading to qualitatively different signal propagation behavior.

AI.tldr: Finite-width corrections in linear recurrent networks scale with depth t relative to width n, revealing three regimes around t ~ sqrt(n): subcritical t=o(sqrt n) where infinite-width theory holds; critical t~c sqrt n with nontrivial joint scaling limit; supercritical t>>sqrt n where finite-width effects dominate. Exact finite-width formulas for hidden-state energies under complex Gaussian initialization show how infinite-width predictions break down and how common initializations (e.g., Glorot) become unstable. Finite-width effects accumulate more rapidly in recurrent models than in feedforward ones, affecting long-sequence propagation.
AI.motivation: To quantify when infinite-width approximations for signal propagation in recurrent networks remain valid as depth grows with width, addressing long-sequence regimes in modern RNN-like models and clarifying initialization stability.
AI.method: Derive exact finite-width expressions for hidden-state signal energies in linear recurrence chains with complex Gaussian initialization. Analyze joint depth-width scaling t and n, identify subcritical, critical, and supercritical regimes, and extract a nontrivial joint scaling limit in the critical regime.
AI.result: Three scaling regimes are established: subcritical (t=o(sqrt n)) where infinite-width predictions hold; critical (t ~ c sqrt n) with non-negligible finite-width deviations and a joint scaling limit; supercritical (t >> sqrt n) where finite-width effects dominate. The work provides exact finite-width corrections and shows breakdown of the infinite-width theory beyond t ~ sqrt n, including instability of Glorot-like initialization.
AI.conclusion: Infinite-width theory is reliable only when recurrence depth grows slower than sqrt(n). The results quantify when and how finite-width corrections become essential, revealing faster accumulation of these effects in RNNs than in feedforward nets and offering guidance for initialization and architecture design for long sequences; the framework may extend to nonlinear/non-Gaussian settings.

title: Manifold Steering Reveals the Shared Geometry of Neural Network Representation and Behavior
summary: Neural representations carry rich geometric structure; but does that structure causally shape behavior? To address this question, we intervene along paths through activation space defined by different geometries, and measure the behavioral trajectories they induce. In particular, we test whether interventions that respect the geometry of activation space will yield behaviors close to those the model exhibits naturally. Concretely, we first fit an activation manifold $M_h$ to representations and a behavior manifold $M_y$ to output probability distributions. We then test the link $M_h \leftrightarrow M_y$ via interventions: we find that steering along $M_h$, which we term manifold steering, yields behavioral trajectories that follow $M_y$, while linear steering -- which assumes a Euclidean geometry -- cuts through off-manifold regions and hence produces unnatural outputs. Moreover, optimizing interventions in activation space to produce paths along $M_y$ recovers activation trajectories that trace the curvature of $M_h$. We demonstrate this bidirectional relationship between the geometry of representation and behavior across tasks and modalities. In language models, we use reasoning tasks with cyclic and sequential geometries as well as in-context learning tasks with more complex graph geometries. In a video world model, we use a task with geometry corresponding to physical dynamics. Overall, our work shows that geometry in neural representation is not merely incidental, but is in fact the proper object for enabling principled control via intervention on internals. This recasts the core problem of steering from finding the right direction to finding the right geometry.

AI.tldr: Neural representations possess a causal geometric structure that shapes behavior; interventions aligned with representation geometry steer behavior along a learned behavior manifold, whereas Euclidean (linear) steering fails; optimizing interventions along behavior paths recovers representation trajectories; this bidirectional link holds across tasks and modalities, recasting steering as geometry-driven control.
AI.motivation: To test whether the geometry of neural activation spaces causally constrains behavior, moving beyond correlation by intervening along geometry-defined paths and observing resulting behavioral trajectories.
AI.method: Fit activation manifold Mh to internal representations and behavior manifold My to output distributions. Perform manifold steering along Mh to induce behavior along My; compare with linear steering that assumes Euclidean geometry. Optimize interventions to produce paths along My and observe whether activation trajectories trace Mh’s curvature. Apply across tasks: language-model reasoning with cyclic/sequential geometries, in-context graph geometries, and a video world model with dynamics geometry.
AI.result: Steering along Mh yields behavioral trajectories that align with My; Euclidean linear steering traverses off-manifold regions and yields unnatural outputs. Optimized interventions to make paths along My recover activation trajectories that trace Mh’s curvature. Bidirectional link between representation geometry and behavior observed across tasks and modalities.
AI.conclusion: Activation-space geometry is not incidental but is the proper object for principled control via internal interventions. Steering should be defined by geometry rather than directions, reframing the core control problem as finding the right geometry.

title: On the Hardness of Junking LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are known to be vulnerable to jailbreak attacks, which typically rely on carefully designed prompts containing explicit semantic structure. These attacks generally operate by fixing an adversarial instruction and optimizing small adversarial components (e.g., suffixes or prefixes). In this setting, prompt structure is fundamental for performance, and recent results show that even simple random search can achieve strong performance when combined with sophisticated prompt design. Recently, it has been observed that harmful behaviors can be elicited even without the adversarial prompt, relying solely on optimized token sequences. This suggests the existence of natural backdoors, i.e., token sequences naturally emerged during LLMs training that trigger unsafe outputs without any meaningful instruction. However, despite these observations, this setting remains largely unexplored, and in particular the hardness of finding natural backdoors has not been assessed yet. In this work, we provide a first proof-of-concept study investigating the hardness of this task, which we refer to as the junking problem. We formalize it as the problem of finding token sequences that maximize the probability of generating a target prefix of harmful responses, propose a greedy random-search method to assess is such sequences can be discovered easily. Our results show that this problem is harder than standard jailbreak attacks, confirming the importance of semantic information in prompt design. At the same time, we find that our simple strategy is sufficient to solve it with a high success rate, suggesting that natural backdoors are present and easily recoverable. Finally, through perplexity analysis, we observe that the discovered token sequences lie in low-probability regions of the model distribution, supporting the hypothesis that they emerged implicitly from the training process.

AI.tldr: Formalizes and studies the 'junking problem': discovering natural backdoors—token sequences that maximize the chance of harmful output without an adversarial prompt. A greedy random-search method is proposed; results show the problem is harder than standard jailbreak but still solvable with a simple strategy, and perplexity analysis suggests these sequences lie in low-probability regions, implying they emerge during training.
AI.motivation: To probe the existence and hardness of natural backdoors in LLMs (token sequences that trigger harmful outputs without explicit adversarial instructions) and to compare this with traditional jailbreak attacks.
AI.method: Define the task as maximizing the probability of a target harmful prefix over token sequences. Propose a greedy random-search algorithm to discover such sequences. Evaluate the approach and perform perplexity analysis to understand where discovered sequences lie in the model distribution.
AI.result: The junking problem is harder than standard jailbreak attacks, indicating the importance of semantic prompt structure. However, the simple greedy random-search is capable of solving it with high success, suggesting natural backdoors are present and recoverable. Perplexity analysis shows discovered sequences reside in low-probability regions, supporting the notion that they emerged implicitly during training.
AI.conclusion: Natural backdoors exist and can be recovered with simple strategies, highlighting the implicit emergence of unsafe sequences during training and the role of semantic information in prompt design. This motivates defenses that account for such backdoors and the distributional placement of risky sequences.

title: Physiologically Grounded Driver Behavior Classification: SHAP-Driven Elite Feature Selection and Hybrid Gradient Boosting for Multimodal Physiological Signals
summary: An interpretable and scalable framework for decoding driving behaviors from multimodal physiological signals is proposed in this study. We utilize multimodal physiological driving behavior large-scale dataset comprising synchronized electroencephalogram (EEG), electromyography (EMG), and galvanic skin response (GSR) signals. Our approach involves rigorous preprocessing followed by a domain-specific feature extraction pipeline targeting time-domain, frequency-domain, and derived physiological indices. To address high dimensionality, we employ SHAP-based elite feature selection, retaining the top 250 features to reduce computational overhead while preserving predictive power. Hyperparameter optimization for extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) and light gradient boosting machine (LightGBM) models is conducted using Bayesian optimization via Optuna. Finally, a weighted soft-voting ensemble is constructed to leverage the complementary strengths of both gradient boosting frameworks. The results demonstrate that the proposed ensemble achieves a test accuracy of 80.91% and a macro-F1 score of 0.79, significantly outperforming single-modality baselines and traditional machine learning models. Ablation studies confirm an 8% performance gain over the best single modality (EEG), validating the necessity of multimodal fusion. SHAP analysis further validates the physiological plausibility of the model, revealing that the EEG contributes the majority of predictive weight, GSR and EMG features provide critical discriminatory signals for high-arousal and motor-intensive maneuvers.

AI.tldr: Multimodal physiological decoding of driving behaviors using EEG, EMG, and GSR with SHAP-based feature selection and an ensemble of XGBoost and LightGBM optimized by Bayesian search; achieves ~81% accuracy and macro-F1 0.79, outperforming single-modality baselines; ablation confirms multimodal fusion; SHAP indicates EEG dominates while GSR/EMG aid high-arousal/movement signals.
AI.motivation: Develop an interpretable, scalable framework to decode driving behaviors from multimodal physiological signals, addressing high dimensionality and ensuring interpretability and competitive accuracy.
AI.method: Rigorous preprocessing; extraction of time-domain, frequency-domain, and derived physiological indices. SHAP-based elite feature selection (top 250). Bayesian hyperparameter optimization (Optuna) for XGBoost and LightGBM. Weighted soft-voting ensemble of both models.
AI.result: Test accuracy 80.91% and macro-F1 0.79; outperforms single-modality baselines and traditional models. Ablation shows an 8% gain over EEG-only; SHAP confirms physiological plausibility with EEG contributing most predictive weight and GSR/EMG adding signals for high arousal and motor maneuvers.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal fusion with SHAP-guided feature selection and ensemble boosting yields robust, interpretable decoding of driving behaviors; EEG is the primary driver, with GSR/EMG providing complementary discriminative information.

title: Conditional outlier detection for clinical alerting
comment: AMIA 2010 Annual Symposium proceedings, pp. 286-290. Homer R. Warner Best Paper Award
summary: We develop and evaluate a data-driven approach for detecting unusual (anomalous) patient-management actions using past patient cases stored in an electronic health record (EHR) system. Our hypothesis is that patient-management actions that are unusual with respect to past patients may be due to a potential error and that it is worthwhile to raise an alert if such a condition is encountered. We evaluate this hypothesis using data obtained from the electronic health records of 4,486 post-cardiac surgical patients. We base the evaluation on the opinions of a panel of experts. The results support that anomaly-based alerting can have reasonably low false alert rates and that stronger anomalies are correlated with higher alert rates.

AI.tldr: Data-driven anomaly detection to flag unusual patient-management actions in EHRs; validated on 4,486 post-cardiac surgery cases with expert evaluation; findings: low false alert rates and stronger anomalies correlate with higher alert rates.
AI.motivation: Enhance patient safety by identifying potentially erroneous management actions through historical EHR data, aiming for informative alerts with acceptable false positives.
AI.method: Develop and apply an anomaly-detection approach using past patient cases from EHRs to score and flag unusual management actions; evaluate via expert panel on 4,486 post-cardiac surgery patients, examining the relationship between anomaly strength and alert frequency.
AI.result: Anomaly-based alerting can achieve reasonably low false alert rates; stronger anomalies are associated with higher alert rates.
AI.conclusion: Anomaly-based alerting is a promising approach for detecting potential errors in patient management from EHR data, suitable for integration into clinical workflows with further refinement.

title: Transformed Latent Variable Multi-Output Gaussian Processes
summary: Multi-Output Gaussian Processes (MOGPs) provide a principled probabilistic framework for modelling correlated outputs but face scalability bottlenecks when applied to datasets with high-dimensional output spaces. To maintain tractability, existing methods typically resort to restrictive assumptions, such as employing low-rank or sum-of-separable kernels, which can limit expressiveness. We propose the Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP), a novel framework that scales MOGPs to a massive number of outputs while preserving the capacity to capture meaningful inter-output dependencies. T-LVMOGP constructs a flexible multi-output deep kernel by mapping inputs and output-specific latent variables into an embedding space using a Lipschitz-regularised neural network. Combined with stochastic variational inference, our model effectively scales to high-dimensional output settings. Across diverse benchmarks, including climate modelling with over 10,000 outputs and zero-inflated spatial transcriptomics data, T-LVMOGP outperforms baselines in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Introduces T-LVMOGP, a scalable MOGP using a transformed latent variable, deep kernel with Lipschitz-regularised neural embedding, and VI for high-dimensional outputs; claims superior predictive performance and efficiency on large-scale benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Scale Gaussian process models to massive output spaces without sacrificing expressiveness; current methods rely on restrictive low-rank or separable kernels that limit interaction modelling.
AI.method: Propose Transformed Latent Variable MOGP (T-LVMOGP) that constructs a multi-output deep kernel by embedding inputs and output-specific latent variables into a shared space via a Lipschitz-regularised neural network. Employ stochastic variational inference to enable scalable training on high-dimensional outputs.
AI.result: Empirically superior predictive accuracy and computational efficiency over baselines across diverse benchmarks, including climate modelling with >10,000 outputs and zero-inflated spatial transcriptomics.
AI.conclusion: T-LVMOGP scales MOGPs to very large numbers of outputs while preserving meaningful inter-output dependencies, enabling tractable and accurate modeling in high-dimensional-output domains.

title: Low-Cost Black-Box Detection of LLM Hallucinations via Dynamical System Prediction
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently generate plausible but non-factual content, a phenomenon known as hallucination. While existing detection methods typically rely on computationally expensive sampling-based consistency checks or external knowledge retrieval, we propose a new method that treats the LLM as a black-box dynamical system. By projecting LLM responses into a high-dimensional manifold via an embedding model, we characterize the resulting vector sequences as observable realizations of the model's latent state-space dynamics. Leveraging Koopman operator theory, we fit the transition operators for both factual and hallucinated regimes and define a differential residual score based on their respective prediction errors. To accommodate varying user requirements and domain-specific sensitivities, we introduce a preference-aware calibration mechanism that optimizes the classification threshold based on a small set of demonstrations. This approach enables low-cost hallucination detection in a single-sample pass, avoiding the need for secondary sampling or external grounding. Extensive testing across three data benchmarks demonstrates that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance with reduced resource overhead.

AI.tldr: A black-box dynamical-systems approach detects LLM hallucinations using Koopman operator theory on embedding trajectories, with a preference-aware one-pass detector that outperforms baselines with lower resource use.
AI.motivation: Hallucination in LLMs remains a challenge and existing detectors rely on costly sampling or external retrieval; a low-cost, adaptable, single-pass detector is needed to meet real-time and domain-specific sensitivity requirements.
AI.method: Embed LLM responses into a high-dimensional manifold; treat the embedding sequence as observations of latent dynamics; estimate separate Koopman transition operators for factual vs. hallucinated regimes; define a differential residual score from their prediction errors; incorporate a preference-aware calibration step to select the decision threshold from a small set of demonstrations, enabling one-pass detection without sampling or grounding.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmarks shows state-of-the-art hallucination detection with reduced resource overhead compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a compact, cost-efficient, and adaptable detection strategy that leverages black-box dynamical modeling and Koopman theory to distinguish factual vs. hallucinated outputs, with user-tailorable thresholds via demonstration-based calibration.

title: Human-AI Co-Mentorship in Project-Based Learning: A Case Study in Financial Forecasting
comment: Accepted for publication in 2026 ASEE Annual Conference & Exposition
summary:
This paper reflects on a AI research project carried out by a team of high-school and early-undergraduate students under the mentorship of graduate researchers and ably assisted by AI tools. We share our experience in not only on the learning experience for the high school students, but also on how AI tools accelerated the process that enabled the high school students to focus on higher order problem formulation and solution. Although the participants entered the project with limited background in both AI and finance, they showed strong enthusiasm for technical market analysis and ETF price prediction. Traditional learning settings would first teach the necessary methods in a classroom setting and only later let students apply them. In contrast, our project emphasized workflow design: students identified the sequence of steps needed to address the problem and then used AI-driven tools to execute each step.
  We note that the high school students developed the necessary code through iterating with the AI tools, and we used our daily stand-ups to debug and answer conceptual questions. Each of the student was able to dig deeper into their area of interest whether computer science or finance, while collaboratively making a significant advance over the summer of 2025. This project was an important pedagogical exercise on how AI tools can be used for mentoring high school students, allowing them to focus on their specific interests and using the daily stand-ups to focus on problem definition and conceptual understanding. Despite their limited technical qualifications, the students were able to leverage AI tools to build meaningful models with real-world application.

AI.tldr: A pedagogical project where high-school and early-undergraduate students used AI tools under mentorship to accelerate learning in AI and finance, emphasizing workflow design and problem formulation over traditional sequencing; iterative AI-assisted coding and daily stand-ups enabled meaningful finance/CS models by summer 2025.
AI.motivation: To explore how AI tools can transform pedagogy by focusing on problem formulation and workflow design, enabling students with limited background to engage in AI/finance projects and produce real-world models.
AI.method: Mentored student team (high-school to early-undergraduate) with graduate researchers; used AI tools to perform and automate analysis steps; emphasized workflow design and iterative coding with AI support; daily stand-ups for debugging and conceptual questions; project spanned summer 2025.
AI.result: Students developed meaningful models with real-world applications in finance/CSS; rapid learning and engagement with AI/finance concepts; demonstrated ability to focus on problem definition and higher-order thinking; successful collaboration and progress within a summer timeframe.
AI.conclusion: AI-assisted mentoring and workflow-centric pedagogy can empower novice students to contribute meaningfully to AI/finance projects, accelerating learning and enabling deeper conceptual understanding through iterative AI-enabled development.

title: Understanding In-Context Learning for Nonlinear Regression with Transformers: Attention as Featurizer
summary: Pre-trained transformers are able to learn from examples provided as part of the prompt without any weight updates, a remarkable ability known as in-context learning (ICL). Despite its demonstrated efficacy across various domains, the theoretical understanding of ICL is still developing. Whereas most existing theory has focused on linear models, we study ICL in the nonlinear regression setting. Through the interaction mechanism in attention, we explicitly construct transformer networks to realize nonlinear features, such as polynomial or spline bases, which span a wide class of functions. Based on this construction, we establish a framework to analyze end-to-end in-context nonlinear regression with the constructed features. Our theory provides finite-sample generalization error bounds in terms of context length and training set size. We numerically validate the theory on synthetic regression tasks.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for in-context nonlinear regression with transformers, constructing nonlinear features via attention (e.g., polynomial/spline bases), yielding finite-sample generalization bounds dependent on context length and data, with validation on synthetic tasks.
AI.motivation: To extend the theoretical understanding of in-context learning (ICL) beyond linear models by addressing nonlinear regression and providing performance guarantees for end-to-end in-context learning.
AI.method: Explicitly design transformer networks where attention interactions realize nonlinear feature bases (polynomials, splines, etc.). Develop a framework for end-to-end in-context nonlinear regression using these constructed features to analyze generalization.
AI.result: Derives finite-sample generalization error bounds that depend on context length and training set size; validates the theory on synthetic regression tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work extends ICL theory to nonlinear regression by constructing nonlinear features via attention, offers finite-sample guarantees, and shows empirical support on synthetic tasks.

title: Estimating the expected output of wide random MLPs more efficiently than sampling
summary: By far the most common way to estimate an expected loss in machine learning is to draw samples, compute the loss on each one, and take the empirical average. However, sampling is not necessarily optimal. Given an MLP at initialization, we show how to estimate its expected output over Gaussian inputs without running samples through the network at all. Instead, we produce approximate representations of the distributions of activations at each layer, leveraging tools such as cumulants and Hermite expansions. We show both theoretically and empirically that for sufficiently wide networks, our estimator achieves a target mean squared error using substantially fewer FLOPs than Monte Carlo sampling. We find moreover that our methods perform particularly well at estimating the probabilities of rare events, and additionally demonstrate how they can be used for model training. Together, these findings suggest a path to producing models with a greatly reduced probability of catastrophic tail risks.

AI.tldr: A non-sampling, cumulant/Hermite-based estimator for MLP outputs with Gaussian inputs; enables accurate mean and tail-probability estimates with far fewer FLOPs than Monte Carlo in wide networks, with potential applications to training and tail-risk reduction.
AI.motivation: Standard Monte Carlo sampling for expected loss estimation is simple but may be inefficient. The paper seeks a cheaper, more accurate way to estimate the expected output and tail-event probabilities of neural networks under Gaussian inputs, particularly for wide networks, and to leverage this for training to reduce catastrophic tail risks.
AI.method: Approximate the activations' distributions at each layer using cumulants and Hermite expansions. Propagate Gaussian inputs through an untrained (randomly initialized) MLP to obtain closed-form or efficiently computable representations of layer-wise activations, avoiding Monte Carlo sampling. Provide theoretical analysis in the wide-network limit and empirical validation, showing reduced FLOPs to achieve a target MSE. Also demonstrate usefulness for estimating rare-event probabilities and potential applications to model training.
AI.result: The estimator achieves the target mean squared error with substantially fewer FLOPs than Monte Carlo sampling in sufficiently wide networks. It performs particularly well at estimating rare-event probabilities and can be used to inform or enable model training. Overall, it provides a practical path to reducing catastrophic tail risks by more efficiently estimating expectations and tail probabilities.
AI.conclusion: A computationally efficient, non-sampling framework for estimating expected outputs and tail probabilities of wide MLPs with Gaussian inputs, leveraging cumulant and Hermite representations. It can decrease reliance on sampling, speed up risk-sensitive metrics, and be integrated into training to mitigate tail risk.

title: Conditional Flow-VAE for Safety-Critical Traffic Scenario Generation
summary: Safety-critical scenarios are essential for the development of autonomous vehicles (AVs) but are rare in real-world driving data. While simulation offers a way to generate such scenarios, manually designed test cases lack scalability, and adversarial optimization often produces unrealistic behaviors. In this work, we introduce a conditional latent flow matching approach for scalable and realistic safety-critical scenario generation. Our method uses distribution matching to transform nominal scenes into safety-critical rollouts. Furthermore, we demonstrate that incorporating both simulation and real-world data enables our framework to efficiently generate diverse, data-driven scenarios. Experimental results highlight that our approach is able to more consistently and realistically generate novel safety-critical scenarios, making it a valuable tool for training and benchmarking AV systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces a conditional latent flow matching framework to generate scalable, realistic safety-critical autonomous vehicle scenarios by distribution-matching nominal scenes to safety-critical rollouts; leverages both simulation and real-world data to produce diverse, data-driven scenarios.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical scenarios are rare in real driving data; manual test cases lack scalability; adversarial optimization can yield unrealistic behaviors. A data-driven, scalable generation method is needed to train and benchmark AV systems.
AI.method: Propose a conditional latent flow matching approach that learns a distribution-matching transformation from nominal scenes to safety-critical rollouts. The model conditions on scene context and safety cues, and integrates both simulated and real-world data to capture realism and diversity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows the method generates novel safety-critical scenarios with higher realism and consistency compared to baselines, while efficiently producing diverse scenarios suitable for AV training and benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: Conditional latent flow matching provides a scalable, data-driven pathway to synthesize safety-critical AV scenarios, combining simulation and real-world data to improve realism and diversity for training and benchmarking.
